# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert to **homogeneous coordinates** — KTU specifically tests this in **Module 1**, and failing to do so will cost **at least 2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using the wrong **order of multiplication** in composite transformations. The correct order for "scale about fixed point" is $T_2 \cdot S \cdot T_1$ (read **right-to-left** as applied to the point). Reversing this is a **fatal error**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Computing $(1 - S_x) \cdot x_f$ as $(S_x - 1) \cdot x_f$ — the sign is critical.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not verifying the **fixed-point invariance** at the end of a numerical problem. KTU examiners award **bonus marks** for this self-check.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]`
**Define scaling transformation in 2D. Write the 2D scaling matrix with respect to the origin in homogeneous coordinates and explain its effect on a unit square.**

**Model Answer:**

Scaling is a geometric transformation used to change the size of a 2D object by multiplying the coordinates of each vertex by scaling factors $S_x$ (along the X-axis) and $S_y$ (along the Y-axis).

The 2D scaling matrix in homogeneous coordinates is:

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Effect on a unit square** with vertices $(0,0)$, $(1,0)$, $(1,1)$, $(0,1)$:

- $(0, 0) \to (0, 0)$ — Origin remains fixed.
- $(1, 0) \to (S_x, 0)$
- $(1, 1) \to (S_x, S_y)$
- $(0, 1) \to (0, S_y)$

If $S_x = 2$ and $S_y = 3$, the unit square becomes a $2 \times 3$ rectangle with vertices $(0,0)$, $(2,0)$, $(2,3)$, $(0,3)$.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Model Q2]`
**What is a fixed point in scaling? Why is fixed-point scaling necessary instead of origin-based scaling?**

**Model Answer:**

A **fixed point** (also called pivot point) is a point in 2D/3D space that remains **unchanged** (invariant) after the scaling transformation is applied. Its coordinates before and after the transformation are identical.

**Why fixed-point scaling is necessary:**

1. **Localization**: In CAD, an engineer often wants to enlarge a specific feature (e.g., a hole) without moving it. Origin-based scaling would shift the entire object.
2. **Engineering Practice**: When zooming into a detail view, the focus should remain at the click point, not at the origin of the drawing sheet.
3. **Animation & Robotics**: Skeletal animation scales limbs about joints (fixed points) rather than the world origin.
4. **Mathematical Generality**: Fixed-point scaling generalizes origin-scaling — when $(x_f, y_f) = (0, 0)$, the formulas reduce to origin scaling.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B questions have an **internal choice**. The student must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Both choices are provided below.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q-A]`

**Derive the 2D fixed-point scaling transformation matrix. A square of side 2 units, with one corner at the origin and sides along the axes, is to be scaled by a factor of 3 in the X-direction and 2 in the Y-direction, with the fixed point at (1, 1). Find the new coordinates of the square.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Fixed-Point Scaling Matrix (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** The fixed-point scaling can be decomposed into three sequential transformations:
- $T_1$ = Translate the fixed point $(x_f, y_f)$ to the origin: $T_1 = T(-x_f, -y_f)$
- $S$ = Perform origin-based scaling: $S = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- $T_2$ = Translate the point back to its original position: $T_2 = T(x_f, y_f)$

**Step 2:** The combined matrix is $M = T_2 \cdot S \cdot T_1$:

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply $S \cdot T_1$ first:

$$
S \cdot T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & -S_x \cdot x_f \\ 0 & S_y & -S_y \cdot y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply $T_2 \cdot (S \cdot T_1)$:

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & x_f - S_x \cdot x_f \\ 0 & S_y & y_f - S_y \cdot y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & (1 - S_x) \cdot x_f \\ 0 & S_y & (1 - S_y) \cdot y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **general 2D fixed-point scaling matrix**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Square vertices: $A(0, 0)$, $B(2, 0)$, $C(2, 2)$, $D(0, 2)$
- Scaling factors: $S_x = 3$, $S_y = 2$
- Fixed point: $F(1, 1)$

**Step 1:** Compute the offset terms.

$(1 - S_x) \cdot x_f = (1 - 3) \cdot 1 = -2$

$(1 - S_y) \cdot y_f = (1 - 2) \cdot 1 = -1$

**Step 2:** Construct the transformation matrix.

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply to each vertex.

For $A(0, 0)$:

$$
A' = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow A' = (-2, -1)
$$

For $B(2, 0)$:

$$
B' = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 - 2 \\ 0 - 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow B' = (4, -1)
$$

For $C(2, 2)$:

$$
C' = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 - 2 \\ 4 - 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow C' = (4, 3)
$$

For $D(0, 2)$:

$$
D' = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 - 2 \\ 4 - 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow D' = (-2, 3)
$$

**Step 4:** Verification — Check fixed point invariance.

$$
F' = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 - 2 \\ 2 - 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow F' = (1, 1) = F \;\checkmark
$$

**Final Answer:** The scaled square has vertices $A'(-2, -1)$, $B'(4, -1)$, $C'(4, 3)$, $D'(-2, 3)$.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Model Q-B]`

**Explain uniform and non-uniform scaling in 2D and 3D. A triangle with vertices P(1, 1), Q(3, 1), R(1, 4) is subjected to a uniform scaling with factor 2 about the origin. Find the new coordinates and comment on the area change.**

#### Part (a) — Theoretical Explanation (7 Marks)

**Uniform Scaling:**
- In uniform (isotropic) scaling, the scaling factors along all axes are **equal**: $S_x = S_y = S_z = k$.
- The shape, proportions, and aspect ratio of the object are **preserved**.
- The object is enlarged or reduced symmetrically about the fixed point.
- **2D matrix:** $\begin{bmatrix} k & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & k & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- **3D matrix:** $\begin{bmatrix} k & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & k & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & k & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- **Area/Volume Effect:** In 2D, area scales by $k^2$. In 3D, volume scales by $k^3$.

**Non-Uniform Scaling:**
- In non-uniform (anisotropic / differential) scaling, the scaling factors along different axes are **unequal**: $S_x \neq S_y$ (2D) or $S_x \neq S_y \neq S_z$ (3D).
- The shape and proportions of the object are **altered** — it appears stretched or compressed along specific directions.
- **2D matrix:** $\begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- **3D matrix:** $\begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & S_z & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- **Area/Volume Effect:** In 2D, area scales by $S_x \cdot S_y$. In 3D, volume scales by $S_x \cdot S_y \cdot S_z$.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Uniform Scaling | Non-Uniform Scaling |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Scaling factors | $S_x = S_y$ | $S_x \neq S_y$ |
| Shape preserved | Yes | No |
| Area scaling factor | $S_x^2$ | $S_x \cdot S_y$ |
| Engineering use | Isotropic magnification | Aspect-ratio correction |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** Triangle vertices $P(1, 1)$, $Q(3, 1)$, $R(1, 4)$; uniform scaling factor $k = 2$ about the origin.

**Step 1:** Construct the uniform scaling matrix.

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply to each vertex.

For $P(1, 1)$:

$$
P' = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow P' = (2, 2)
$$

For $Q(3, 1)$:

$$
Q' = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow Q' = (6, 2)
$$

For $R(1, 4)$:

$$
R' = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 8 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow R' = (2, 8)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the area of the original triangle.

Using the shoelace formula for $P(1, 1)$, $Q(3, 1)$, $R(1, 4)$:

$$
A_{\text{original}} = \frac{1}{2} \vert x_P(y_Q - y_R) + x_Q(y_R - y_P) + x_R(y_P - y_Q) \vert
$$

$$
A_{\text{original}} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 1(1 - 4) + 3(4 - 1) + 1(1 - 1) \vert
$$

$$
A_{\text{original}} = \frac{1}{2} \vert -3 + 9 + 0 \vert = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 6 = 3 \text{ square units}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the area of the scaled triangle.

For $P'(2, 2)$, $Q'(6, 2)$, $R'(2, 8)$:

$$
A_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 2(2 - 8) + 6(8 - 2) + 2(2 - 2) \vert
$$

$$
A_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{1}{2} \vert -12 + 36 + 0 \vert = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 24 = 12 \text{ square units}
$$

**Step 5:** Comment on the area change.

Area ratio = $\frac{A_{\text{scaled}}}{A_{\text{original}}} = \frac{12}{3} = 4 = 2^2 = k^2$

**Comment:** Under uniform scaling with factor $k = 2$, the area is multiplied by $k^2 = 4$. This is a fundamental property: for any 2D object, area scales by the square of the linear scaling factor. Similarly, in 3D, volume scales by $k^3$.

**Final Answer:** The new vertices are $P'(2, 2)$, $Q'(6, 2)$, $R'(2, 8)$, and the area increases from $3$ to $12$ square units (a factor of $4 = k^2$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam**

- **Scaling** is a linear transformation that resizes an object by multiplying coordinates by scaling factors $S_x$, $S_y$ (and $S_z$ in 3D).
- **2D Origin Scaling Matrix** (homogeneous): $\begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- **3D Origin Scaling Matrix** (homogeneous): A $4 \times 4$ diagonal matrix with $S_x$, $S_y$, $S_z$ on the main diagonal and $1$ in the bottom-right corner.
- **Fixed-Point Scaling Matrix** (2D): $\begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & (1 - S_x) \cdot x_f \\ 0 & S_y & (1 - S_y) \cdot y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- **Composite Order for Fixed-Point Scaling**: $T_2 \cdot S \cdot T_1$ (Translate back × Scale × Translate to origin). This is applied as $P' = T_2 \cdot S \cdot T_1 \cdot P$.
- **Uniform scaling** ($S_x = S_y$) preserves shape; **non-uniform scaling** ($S_x \neq S_y$) alters shape.
- **Reflection is a special case of scaling** with negative scaling factors: $S_x = -1$ for Y-axis reflection, $S_y = -1$ for X-axis reflection.
- **Area scales by $S_x \cdot S_y$** in 2D; **Volume scales by $S_x \cdot S_y \cdot S_z$** in 3D.
- **Inverse scaling matrix**: Replace $S_i$ with $\frac{1}{S_i}$ (provided $S_i \neq 0$).
- **Homogeneous coordinates** are **mandatory** in KTU exam answers — never write a $2 \times 2$ matrix for a transformation that needs translation.
- **Always verify the fixed point** at the end of a numerical problem — this is a 1-mark insurance in the KTU marking scheme.
- **Singular scaling** (any $S_i = 0$) is a **degenerate transformation** — the object collapses to a lower dimension and becomes invalid for rendering.
- **Real-world applications**: CAD drafting, FEA meshing, 3D printing scale adjustment, image processing, animation rigging, CNC coordinate mapping.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric modelling: mathematical representation of curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Geometric modelling: mathematical representation of curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Mathematical Representation of Curves?

In the context of **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)**, a curve is a continuous, one-dimensional geometric entity defined in a 2D or 3D space. The **mathematical representation of curves** is the formal algebraic framework used to describe, store, manipulate, and visualize these geometric entities inside a computer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard):**
> A *curve* in CAD/CAM/CAE is defined as a locus of points satisfying a mathematical equation of the form:
> $$\vec{P}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) & y(t) & z(t) \end{bmatrix}^T, \quad t \in [t_0, t_n]$$
> where **t** is the parameter and **P(t)** is the position vector of a point on the curve.

### 1.2 Classification of Curves (KTU Syllabus Standard)

KTU recognizes two primary classes of curves for the B.Tech Board Examination:

| **Class** | **Type** | **Sub-Types** |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| **Class A** | Analytic / Synthetic | Lines, Circles, Conics, Super-ellipses |
| **Class B** | Parametric (Spline) | Hermite, Bezier, B-Spline, NURBS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why parametrics dominate CAD:**
> Analytic curves like $y = f(x)$ fail when the curve is **vertical** (since slope becomes infinite). Parametric curves $x = x(t), y = y(t)$ eliminate this singularity completely, which is why modern CAD systems (CATIA, NX, SolidWorks, Fusion 360) rely almost exclusively on parametric representations.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Skein of Yarn"

Imagine you are designing a **sweater pattern**:
- The **skein of yarn** is the curve.
- The **pattern instruction** ("knit 2, purl 3, repeat every row") is the **mathematical rule** that generates the curve.
- The **needle position** at any instant of knitting corresponds to the **parameter value t**.
- The **finished fabric outline** is the geometric curve.

Different "pattern instructions" (Hermite, Bezier, B-Spline) give you different **degrees of control**: some let you stretch the wool with your hands (Bezier control points), some have preset, fixed rule-books (Cubic Splines).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Exam Point:** The transition from analytic ($y = f(x)$) to parametric ($x(t), y(t), z(t)$) representation is the single most important paradigm shift in geometric modeling since 1970. The most common Bezier cubic curve degree is **n = 3**, the most common knot vector type is **Non-Uniform**, and the most common continuity between adjacent segments is **C² (parametric second derivative continuity)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Parametric vs Non-Parametric Curves — Operational Logic

A non-parametric (analytic) curve is expressed as:
$$y = f(x) \quad \text{or} \quad F(x, y) = 0$$

A parametric curve uses an auxiliary variable **t** (parameter, typically $0 \le t \le 1$):
$$x = x(t), \quad y = y(t), \quad z = z(t)$$

**Why is this preferred in CAD?**

- Analytic forms become **multi-valued** (a circle $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$ has two y-values per x).
- Vertical tangents cause **division by zero** in slope calculations.
- Parametric forms handle **3D space curves** naturally (e.g., a helix on a screw thread).
- **Derivatives are independent** in x and y, so curvature and torsion can be computed cleanly.

### 2.2 Continuity Conditions (Mandatory for KTU Board Exam)

Continuity describes how smoothly two curve segments join. KTU expects you to distinguish between **Parametric (C) and Geometric (G) continuity**.

| **Continuity** | **Conditions** | **Physical Meaning** | **Common Use** |
|----------------|----------------|----------------------|----------------|
| $C^0$ / $G^0$ | $\vec{P}_1(t_{end}) = \vec{P}_2(t_{start})$ | Curves touch (position match) | Wireframe |
| $C^1$ / $G^1$ | Derivatives match direction & magnitude | Tangent vectors aligned | Car body styling |
| $C^2$ / $G^2$ | + 2nd derivative match | Curvature is smooth | Aircraft fuselage, propeller blades |
| $G^1$ only | Tangent direction matches, magnitude may differ | Visually smooth, mathematically kinked | Trim surfaces |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cubic Spline, Hermite, Bezier, B-Spline

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are high-yield — they appear in nearly every KTU December/May Board Examination under PBPET504 Module 1.**

#### 2.3.1 General Cubic Polynomial (Cubic Spline Foundation)

A general cubic in 1D is:
$$P(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

In vector form for 3D:
$$\vec{P}(t) = \vec{a_0} + \vec{a_1}\, t + \vec{a_2}\, t^2 + \vec{a_3}\, t^3$$

#### 2.3.2 Hermite Form (Boundary Condition Specification)

Specified by **two endpoints and two tangent vectors** $\vec{P}_0, \vec{P}_1, \vec{R}_0, \vec{R}_1$:
$$\vec{P}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} t^3 & t^2 & t & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 & 1 & 1 \\ -3 & 3 & -2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \vec{P_0} \\ \vec{P_1} \\ \vec{R_0} \\ \vec{R_1} \end{bmatrix}$$

#### 2.3.3 Bezier Blending Functions (Bernstein Polynomials of degree n)

For a Bezier curve of degree **n** with **n+1 control points**:
$$B_i^n(t) = \binom{n}{i} t^i (1-t)^{n-i}, \quad i = 0, 1, \dots, n$$

The curve equation is:
$$\vec{P}(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \vec{b_i}\, B_i^n(t)$$

For the **Cubic Bezier (n = 3)**, the blending functions are:
$$B_0^3(t) = (1-t)^3$$
$$B_1^3(t) = 3t(1-t)^2$$
$$B_2^3(t) = 3t^2(1-t)$$
$$B_3^3(t) = t^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sum check (always asked in KTU exams):** $B_0^3(t) + B_1^3(t) + B_2^3(t) + B_3^3(t) = 1$ (partition of unity / convex hull property).

#### 2.3.4 Cubic B-Spline Basis Functions (Uniform, Open Knot Vector)

For a uniform cubic B-spline with open knot vector, the four basis functions on $t \in [t_i, t_{i+1}]$ are:

$$N_{0,3}(t) = \frac{1}{6} (1-t)^3$$

$$N_{1,3}(t) = \frac{1}{6} (3t^3 - 6t^2 + 4)$$

$$N_{2,3}(t) = \frac{1}{6} (-3t^3 + 3t^2 + 3t + 1)$$

$$N_{3,3}(t) = \frac{1}{6} t^3$$

The curve equation:
$$\vec{P}(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \vec{d_i}\, N_{i,3}(t)$$

#### 2.3.5 Rational Curves (Bezier Rational, NURBS)

$$\vec{P}(t) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{n} w_i \vec{b_i}\, B_i^n(t)}{\sum_{i=0}^{n} w_i B_i^n(t)}$$

where $w_i$ are **weights**. A weight $w_i \to \infty$ pulls the curve toward control point $\vec{b_i}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Industry** | **Curve Type Used** | **Why** |
|--------------|---------------------|---------|
| **Automotive body design** | B-Spline / NURBS | Local control — modifying a panel does not distort adjacent panels |
| **Aircraft wing (Boeing/Airbus)** | NURBS | Exact representation of conic sections (parabola, ellipse) with finite math |
| **Ship hull (Naval architecture)** | Cubic Bezier + Spline | Smoothness required for hydrodynamics |
| **Animation / VFX (Pixar)** | Subdivision curves | Limit surface from coarse control mesh |
| **CNC tool path** | Parametric spline | Tangent continuity prevents jerky motion |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Converting Analytic Line to Parametric Form (Worked)

**Problem:** Convert the line passing through $(2, 3)$ and $(7, 11)$ into parametric form.

**Step 1 — Direction vector calculation:**
$$\vec{d} = (7 - 2, 11 - 3) = (5, 8)$$

**Step 2 — Parametric equation form (anchor at point $P_0$):**
$$\vec{P}(t) = \vec{P_0} + t \cdot \vec{d}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $P_0 = (2, 3)$ and $\vec{d} = (5, 8)$:**
$$x(t) = 2 + 5t$$
$$y(t) = 3 + 8t$$
$$\vec{P}(t) = (2 + 5t, \, 3 + 8t), \quad t \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 4 — Verification at boundaries:**
- At $t = 0$: $\vec{P}(0) = (2, 3)$ ✓
- At $t = 1$: $\vec{P}(1) = (7, 11)$ ✓
- At $t = 0.5$: $\vec{P}(0.5) = (4.5, 7)$ → midpoint of the line ✓

**Step 5 — Convert to vector form:**
$$\vec{P}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} + t \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cubic Bezier Bernstein Polynomial Expansion (Sum = 1 Proof)

**Problem:** Show that the four cubic Bernstein polynomials sum to 1.

**Step 1 — Start with the binomial expansion of $[(1-t) + t]^3$:**
$$[(1-t) + t]^3 = 1^3 = 1$$

**Step 2 — Expand using binomial theorem** $\sum_{i=0}^{3} \binom{3}{i} t^i (1-t)^{3-i}$:
$$\binom{3}{0}t^0(1-t)^3 + \binom{3}{1}t^1(1-t)^2 + \binom{3}{2}t^2(1-t)^1 + \binom{3}{3}t^3(1-t)^0 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the binomial coefficient values** $\binom{3}{0}=1, \binom{3}{1}=3, \binom{3}{2}=3, \binom{3}{3}=1$:
$$1 \cdot (1-t)^3 + 3 \cdot t(1-t)^2 + 3 \cdot t^2(1-t) + 1 \cdot t^3 = 1$$

**Step 4 — Equate to Bezier form:**
$$B_0^3(t) + B_1^3(t) + B_2^3(t) + B_3^3(t) = 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Convex Hull Property** of Bezier curves — the curve always lies inside the polygon formed by the control points, and is the reason why CAD systems use Bezier for safe, predictable shape editing.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hermite to Bezier Conversion (Knot Equivalence)

**Problem:** Express the Hermite cubic matrix in terms of Bezier control points.

**Step 1 — Recall Hermite form:**
$$\vec{P}(t) = T \cdot M_H \cdot G_H$$
where $T = [t^3 \; t^2 \; t \; 1]$, $G_H = [\vec{P_0} \; \vec{P_1} \; \vec{R_0} \; \vec{R_1}]^T$.

**Step 2 — Hermite matrix:**
$$M_H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 & 1 & 1 \\ -3 & 3 & -2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to Bezier form via the transformation matrix $M_{HB}$:**
$$M_{HB} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1/3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -1/3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Apply:** $G_B = M_{HB} \cdot G_H$

This means the **four Bezier control points** in terms of Hermite data are:
$$\vec{b_0} = \vec{P_0}$$
$$\vec{b_1} = \vec{P_0} + \frac{1}{3} \vec{R_0}$$
$$\vec{b_2} = \vec{P_1} - \frac{1}{3} \vec{R_1}$$
$$\vec{b_3} = \vec{P_1}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The first and last Bezier control points **coincide** with the Hermite endpoints, while the two interior points lie **one-third of the tangent vector** away — this is a frequently tested result in KTU board examinations.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Evaluating a Cubic Bezier Point at t = 0.4

**Problem:** Find the point on a cubic Bezier curve at $t = 0.4$ given control points $\vec{b_0} = (0,0)$, $\vec{b_1} = (1, 3)$, $\vec{b_2} = (4, 3)$, $\vec{b_3} = (5, 0)$.

**Step 1 — Compute blending function values at $t = 0.4$:**

$$
B_0^3(0.4) = (1 - 0.4)^3 = (0.6)^3 = 0.216
$$

$$
B_1^3(0.4) = 3(0.4)(0.6)^2 = 3 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.36 = 0.432
$$

$$
B_2^3(0.4) = 3(0.4)^2(0.6) = 3 \cdot 0.16 \cdot 0.6 = 0.288
$$

$$
B_3^3(0.4) = (0.4)^3 = 0.064
$$

**Step 2 — Sum check:** $0.216 + 0.432 + 0.288 + 0.064 = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 3 — Compute the position vector:**

$$
x(0.4) = 0(0.216) + 1(0.432) + 4(0.288) + 5(0.064)
$$

$$
x(0.4) = 0 + 0.432 + 1.152 + 0.320 = 1.904
$$

$$
y(0.4) = 0(0.216) + 3(0.432) + 3(0.288) + 0(0.064)
$$

$$
y(0.4) = 0 + 1.296 + 0.864 + 0 = 2.160
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:** $\vec{P}(0.4) = (1.904, \, 2.160)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** The above blending function sum = 1 verification is **mandatory** to score full marks in a KTU Part A (3-mark) question. Students often skip it and lose 0.5 to 1 mark.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cubic Spline vs Bezier vs B-Spline

| **Property** | **Cubic Spline (Interpolation)** | **Bezier Curve** | **B-Spline** |
|--------------|--------------------------------|------------------|--------------|
| **Passes through control points** | Yes (all interior points) | Only at $t = 0$ and $t = 1$ (endpoints) | No (interior points) |
| **Local control** | No (global) | No (global — moving one point disturbs entire curve) | **Yes** (each segment influenced by ~4 control points) |
| **Continuity achievable** | $C^2$ | $C^0$ at segment joins, $C^\infty$ within segment | $C^2$ within, $C^{p-1}$ at knots |
| **Mathematical complexity** | High (tridiagonal system) | Low | Moderate |
| **Best for** | Interpolation from measured data | Single-segment design with intuitive control polygon | Complex multi-segment industrial design |
| **Standard in industry** | Legacy CNC systems | Fonts (TrueType uses Quadratic Bezier) | **NURBS** in all major CAD systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Curve Representation Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Geometric Curves in CAD] --> B[Analytic Curves]
    A --> C[Parametric Curves]
    A --> D[Synthetic Curves]

    B --> B1[Lines]
    B --> B2[Conics: Circle, Ellipse, Parabola, Hyperbola]
    B --> B3[Superquadrics]

    C --> C1[Interpolation Splines]
    C --> C2[Approximation Splines]

    C1 --> C1a[Natural Cubic Spline]
    C1 --> C1b[Clamped Cubic Spline]
    C1 --> C1c[Hermite Cubic]

    C2 --> C2a[Bezier Curves]
    C2 --> C2b[B-Spline Curves]
    C2 --> C2c[NURBS]

    D --> D1[Procedural Curves]
    D --> D2[Fractal Curves]

    C2a --> A1[Degree n, n+1 control points]
    C2b --> A2[Local control via knot vector]
    C2c --> A3[Industry standard, exact conics]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Parametric Curve Evaluation Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Define control points b0 to bn] --> S1[Choose parameter range t0 to tn]
    S1 --> S2[Choose evaluation point t]
    S2 --> S3[Compute blending functions B sub i of n at t]
    S3 --> S4[Compute weighted sum Pi times B sub i]
    S4 --> S5[Output point P of t]
    S5 --> S6{Need more points?}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Increment t by delta t] --> S3
    S6 -->|No| S8[End: Curve traced]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CAD Curve Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        I1[Control Points b0 to bn]
        I2[Knot Vector U]
        I3[Degree p]
        I4[Weights w sub i]
    end

    subgraph MathEngine[Mathematical Engine]
        M1[Blending Function Calculator]
        M2[Continuity Checker]
        M3[Curvature Analyzer]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        O1[Geometric Curve Display]
        O2[IGES STEP File Export]
        O3[Toolpath Generator for CNC]
        O4[FE Meshing for CAE]
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> M1
    I3 --> M1
    I4 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> O1
    M3 --> O2
    M3 --> O3
    M3 --> O4
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Diagram — How Bezier Control Points Shape the Curve

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0[Control Point b0] -- Tangent Direction --> P1[Control Point b1]
    P1 --> P2[Control Point b2]
    P2 -- Tangent Direction --> P3[Control Point b3]
    P3 --> Curve[Cubic Bezier Curve Inside Polygon]

    P0 --- P1
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3

    Curve -. Tangent at t=0 along b0 b1 .-> P0
    Curve -. Tangent at t=1 along b2 b3 .-> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The curve starts at $b_0$ heading toward $b_1$, and ends at $b_3$ approaching from $b_2$. This tangent property is a **favorite KTU question** — "Prove the end-tangent direction of a Bezier curve is along the line joining the first two control points."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between analytic and synthetic curves with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Analytic curves** are represented by an explicit mathematical equation of the form $y = f(x)$ or $F(x, y) = 0$. They are precise and continuous, but have limitations: vertical tangents cause undefined slopes, and 3D extensions are awkward. *Examples:* line $y = mx + c$, circle $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$.
>
> **Synthetic curves** (also called *procedural* or *free-form curves*) are generated by mathematical functions that do not have a simple closed form. They are typically defined by a set of control points and blending functions, giving the designer intuitive control over shape. *Examples:* Bezier curve, B-Spline curve.
>
> Analytic curves are used in standard geometric primitives, while synthetic curves dominate free-form surfaces in automotive and aerospace design.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the convex hull property of Bezier curves.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Convex Hull Property** states that a Bezier curve of degree $n$ with control points $\vec{b_0}, \vec{b_1}, \dots, \vec{b_n}$ always lies entirely within the **convex hull** of its control polygon.
>
> **Mathematical basis:** Since blending functions satisfy $B_i^n(t) \ge 0$ and $\sum_{i=0}^{n} B_i^n(t) = 1$ for $t \in [0,1]$, the curve $\vec{P}(t) = \sum B_i^n(t) \vec{b_i}$ is a **convex combination** of the control points. Any convex combination lies inside the convex hull.
>
> **Engineering significance:** The convex hull property guarantees that the curve will never exhibit unexpected loops or sharp reversals outside the control polygon. This is critical in CAD, as designers can predict the curve's shape by simply inspecting the control polygon.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Matrix form must be written explicitly** — writing only the answer $P(t) = $ without showing the matrix multiplication costs 3 marks.
> 2. **Always show the "sum = 1" verification** of Bernstein polynomials — losing this costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Do not confuse $C^1$ and $G^1$ continuity** — $C^1$ requires *equal magnitude* of tangent vectors; $G^1$ allows *different magnitudes* but matching directions.
> 4. **Bezier curves DO NOT pass through interior control points** — a common misconception that costs 2 marks when describing the curve's properties.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**(a) Derive the cubic Bezier blending functions (Bernstein polynomials) and prove that they form a partition of unity. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start with the binomial theorem:**
$$[(1-t) + t]^n = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \binom{n}{i} t^i (1-t)^{n-i}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Bernstein basis polynomial of degree $n$, index $i$:**
$$B_i^n(t) = \binom{n}{i} t^i (1-t)^{n-i}, \quad 0 \le i \le n$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $n = 3$ and expand each term:**

For $i = 0$:
$$B_0^3(t) = \binom{3}{0} t^0 (1-t)^3 = (1-t)^3$$

For $i = 1$:
$$B_1^3(t) = \binom{3}{1} t^1 (1-t)^2 = 3t(1-t)^2$$

For $i = 2$:
$$B_2^3(t) = \binom{3}{2} t^2 (1-t)^1 = 3t^2(1-t)$$

For $i = 3$:
$$B_3^3(t) = \binom{3}{3} t^3 (1-t)^0 = t^3$$

**Step 4 — Prove partition of unity:**
Add the four polynomials:
$$\sum_{i=0}^{3} B_i^3(t) = (1-t)^3 + 3t(1-t)^2 + 3t^2(1-t) + t^3$$

**Step 5 — Factor** $(1-t)^3 + t^3 = [(1-t) + t][(1-t)^2 - (1-t)t + t^2] = (1)[(1-t)^2 - t + t^2] = 1 - 2t + t^2 - t + t^2 = 1 - 3t + 2t^2$.

**Step 6 — Combine the middle terms:**
$$3t(1-t)^2 + 3t^2(1-t) = 3t(1-t)[(1-t) + t] = 3t(1-t)(1) = 3t - 3t^2$$

**Step 7 — Sum all four:**
$$(1 - 3t + 2t^2) + (3t - 3t^2) = 1 - 3t + 3t - 3t^2 + 2t^2 = 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 8 — Non-negativity check (essential for convex hull):**
For $t \in [0, 1]$, every term $t^i (1-t)^{3-i} \ge 0$, hence $B_i^3(t) \ge 0$.

---

**(b) Given four control points $\vec{b_0} = (1, 1)$, $\vec{b_1} = (2, 4)$, $\vec{b_2} = (5, 4)$, $\vec{b_3} = (6, 1)$, evaluate the cubic Bezier curve at $t = 0.5$ and find the tangent vector at the starting point. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute blending function values at $t = 0.5$:**

$$B_0^3(0.5) = (0.5)^3 = 0.125$$

$$B_1^3(0.5) = 3(0.5)(0.5)^2 = 3 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.25 = 0.375$$

$$B_2^3(0.5) = 3(0.5)^2(0.5) = 0.375$$

$$B_3^3(0.5) = (0.5)^3 = 0.125$$

**Step 2 — Verify sum:** $0.125 + 0.375 + 0.375 + 0.125 = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 3 — Compute point at $t = 0.5$:**

$$x(0.5) = 1(0.125) + 2(0.375) + 5(0.375) + 6(0.125)$$
$$x(0.5) = 0.125 + 0.750 + 1.875 + 0.750 = 3.500$$

$$y(0.5) = 1(0.125) + 4(0.375) + 4(0.375) + 1(0.125)$$
$$y(0.5) = 0.125 + 1.500 + 1.500 + 0.125 = 3.250$$

**Answer:** $\vec{P}(0.5) = (3.5, \, 3.25)$.

**Step 4 — Tangent vector at starting point** ($t = 0$):
The first derivative of a cubic Bezier is:
$$\vec{P'}(t) = 3 \left[ (1-t)^2 (\vec{b_1} - \vec{b_0}) + 2t(1-t)(\vec{b_2} - \vec{b_1}) + t^2(\vec{b_3} - \vec{b_2}) \right]$$

**Step 5 — At $t = 0$:**
$$\vec{P'}(0) = 3(\vec{b_1} - \vec{b_0}) = 3[(2,4) - (1,1)] = 3(1, 3) = (3, 9)$$

**Answer:** Tangent at start $= (3, 9)$, direction along $(1, 3)$, i.e., the line $\vec{b_0} \to \vec{b_1}$ as expected.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) Explain with proper matrix representation the cubic Hermite spline. Convert it into equivalent Bezier form and list the four Bezier control points in terms of Hermite data. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hermite cubic definition:**
A cubic Hermite segment is specified by **two endpoints** $\vec{P_0}, \vec{P_1}$ and **two tangent vectors** $\vec{R_0}, \vec{R_1}$ at those endpoints.

**Step 2 — Matrix form:**

$$
\vec{P}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} t^3 & t^2 & t & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 & 1 & 1 \\ -3 & 3 & -2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \vec{P_0} \\ \vec{P_1} \\ \vec{R_0} \\ \vec{R_1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand** (with $0 \le t \le 1$):
$$\vec{P}(t) = (2t^3 - 3t^2 + 1)\vec{P_0} + (-2t^3 + 3t^2)\vec{P_1} + (t^3 - 2t^2 + t)\vec{R_0} + (t^3 - t^2)\vec{R_1}$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to Bezier form:** The transformation matrix is:
$$M_{HB} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1/3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -1/3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Equate Hermite and Bezier coefficients** to obtain:
$$\vec{b_0} = \vec{P_0}$$
$$\vec{b_1} = \vec{P_0} + \tfrac{1}{3}\vec{R_0}$$
$$\vec{b_2} = \vec{P_1} - \tfrac{1}{3}\vec{R_1}$$
$$\vec{b_3} = \vec{P_1}$$

**Step 6 — Verification (partial):** At $t = 0$, Bezier gives $\vec{P}(0) = \vec{b_0} = \vec{P_0}$ ✓. At $t = 1$, Bezier gives $\vec{P}(1) = \vec{b_3} = \vec{P_1}$ ✓.

---

**(b) Explain the difference between C¹, C², G¹, and G² continuity between two curve segments. Give one industrial application where each is the minimum acceptable standard. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Parametric continuity C^n:**
A join between two segments $\vec{P_1}(t)$ and $\vec{P_2}(t)$ has $C^k$ continuity if the **position and first k derivatives match in both magnitude and direction** at the joining parameter.

**Step 2 — Geometric continuity G^n:**
A join has $G^k$ continuity if the **derivatives match in direction only**, allowing the magnitudes to differ. $G^k$ is therefore a **weaker** condition than $C^k$.

**Step 3 — Tabulate conditions:**

| **Type** | **Conditions** | **Industrial Application** |
|----------|----------------|----------------------------|
| $C^0 / G^0$ | Position match only | Wireframe CAD, dimensioning lines |
| $C^1 / G^1$ | First derivative continuity | Car body panels, consumer product casings |
| $C^2$ | Second derivative continuity | Aircraft wing surfaces, propeller blade sections |
| $G^2$ | Curvature direction continuous (curvature magnitude may differ) | Trimmed surfaces, fashion design (draping cloth) |

**Step 4 — Practical note:**
G² is sufficient for *visual* smoothness (e.g., animation, product design) but **NOT** sufficient for surfaces under *load* (e.g., pressure vessel, fuselage skin), which require $C^2$ to avoid stress concentration at the seam. KTU expects this distinction in the application.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam (PBPET504 Module 1)**

- [x] **Curve = locus of points parameterized by a single variable t.** Analytic form $F(x, y) = 0$ is replaced by parametric form $x = x(t), y = y(t), z = z(t)$ to avoid vertical-tangent singularities and to handle 3D space curves.

- [x] **Bernstein polynomial of degree n, index i:** $B_i^n(t) = \binom{n}{i} t^i (1-t)^{n-i}$. Sum is identically **1** for all $t \in [0, 1]$ (partition of unity).

- [x] **Cubic Bezier curve (n = 3):** $\vec{P}(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{3} \vec{b_i} B_i^3(t)$. Endpoints coincide with $\vec{b_0}$ and $\vec{b_3}$; curve lies in the convex hull of control polygon.

- [x] **Hermite cubic** uses 4 boundary data: $\vec{P_0}, \vec{P_1}, \vec{R_0}, \vec{R_1}$. Bezier equivalents: $\vec{b_1} = \vec{P_0} + \frac{1}{3}\vec{R_0}$ and $\vec{b_2} = \vec{P_1} - \frac{1}{3}\vec{R_1}$.

- [x] **Cubic B-Spline (uniform, open):** Each segment is influenced by **4 consecutive control points**; provides *local control* — moving $\vec{d_i}$ affects only segments around $i$.

- [x] **NURBS = Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline.** Rational = weights $w_i$ allow exact representation of conics. Non-uniform knot vector allows variable parameter spacing. **Industry standard** (IGES, STEP file format).

- [x] **Continuity hierarchy:** $C^2 \Rightarrow C^1 \Rightarrow C^0$ (and similarly for G). For engineering load-bearing surfaces, **$C^2$ is the minimum acceptable**; for cosmetic design, **$G^1$ is often sufficient**.

- [x] **KTU 2024 standard for 14-mark problems:** Always present Bezier/Hermite answers in **matrix form**, show the **derivative calculation** explicitly, and verify the **sum = 1 condition** of Bernstein polynomials.

- [x] **Common confusion cleared:** B-Spline is **not** Bezier. A single-segment cubic B-spline with open knot vector **is** a Bezier curve, but a multi-segment B-spline with interior knots is **not** (and gains local control as a benefit).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parametric representation of analytical and synthetic curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parametric representation of analytical and synthetic curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Parametric Representation of Curves: The Language of Modern CAD

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Computer-Aided Design (CAD), a **curve** is defined as the locus of points that satisfy a specific mathematical relationship. The **parametric representation** of a curve expresses the coordinates of a point on the curve as explicit functions of a single independent variable called a **parameter** (usually denoted by $u$, $t$, or $\theta$).

The general form of a parametric curve in 3D space is:

$$P(u) = \begin{bmatrix} x(u) \\ y(u) \\ z(u) \end{bmatrix}, \quad u \in [u_0, u_1]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A curve is considered *parametric* if every coordinate $(x, y, z)$ is expressed as a function of a common parameter $u$. The parameter has no physical meaning by itself — it is purely a *mathematical index* that traces the curve from its start point to its end point.

### Classification of Curves in CAD

Under the KTU 2024 scheme, curves are broadly classified into two fundamental families:

| Curve Type | Defining Feature | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Analytical Curves** | Defined by closed-form mathematical equations | Line, Circle, Ellipse, Parabola, Hyperbola, Conic sections |
| **Synthetic Curves** | Defined by a set of data points or control points; no single closed-form equation exists for the whole curve | Cubic Spline, Bezier, B-Spline, NURBS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two categories?** Early CAD systems could only handle *analytical* primitives (lines, arcs). The demand for aerodynamic car bodies, ship hulls, and aircraft wings (free-form surfaces) led to *synthetic* curves that are stitched together from polynomial segments and controlled by data points.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sat-Nav Trace" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car from **Kochi to Trivandrum** along the NH-66 highway.

- **Non-parametric (Explicit) form:** $y = f(x)$ — This is like saying *"the height of the road is a fixed function of how far east I am."* But the NH-66 winds back and forth in the $x$-direction, so a single $y = f(x)$ formula **cannot** describe it. The function fails or becomes multi-valued.

- **Parametric form:** $P(u) = (x(u), y(u))$ — This is like a **GPS black-box** that logs: *"At time $u = 0$ I am at $(x_0, y_0)$. At time $u = 0.5$ I am at $(x_1, y_1)$..."* The parameter $u$ acts like a **time-stamp** or **odometer reading**, and the car's $x$ and $y$ positions are *independently* tracked. This works for **any** shape, no matter how curvy, and even handles vertical sections.

**The profound insight:** A parametric representation decouples the $x$ and $y$ coordinates. They are no longer forced into a slave-master relationship $y = f(x)$. Each coordinate is free to follow its own polynomial, opening the door to modelling arbitrary 3D free-form shapes — the very backbone of modern CAD/CAM/CAE.

> [!TIP]
> **The parameter $u$ is normalized** in most CAD systems to the interval $[0, 1]$. At $u = 0$ you are at the curve's starting point; at $u = 1$ you are at the end point. This uniformity simplifies software algorithms enormously.

---

## 1.3 Comparison: Parametric vs. Non-Parametric Representation

| Feature | Non-Parametric (Explicit/Implicit) | Parametric |
|---|---|---|
| **Form** | $y = f(x)$ or $F(x,y) = 0$ | $x = x(u), \; y = y(u)$ |
| **Vertical Tangents** | Cannot represent (slope $\to \infty$) | Can represent without restriction |
| **Multi-valued curves** | Cannot represent | Can represent easily |
| **3D Extension** | Requires two equations (intersection) | Single unified formulation $P(u)$ |
| **Offset Curves** | Mathematically intractable | Computationally straightforward |
| **Continuity Control** | Implicit and complex | Explicit via $C^k$ and $G^k$ conditions |
| **Computational Cost** | Low for simple shapes | Slightly higher (two/three polynomials) |
| **KTU Exam Verdict** | Obsolete for production CAD | **Industry and KTU standard** |

---

## 1.4 Why Parametric Representation Dominates Modern CAD

1. **Independence of coordinates** — $x(u)$ and $y(u)$ are independent polynomials.
2. **Bounded parameter domain** — typically $u \in [0, 1]$ simplifies boundary conditions.
3. **Easy derivative evaluation** — $P'(u) = (x'(u), y'(u), z'(u))$ gives the tangent vector for trimming, offsetting, and machining tool-path generation.
4. **Affine invariance** — affine transformations (rotation, translation, scaling) apply directly to control points.
5. **Continuity control** — geometric continuity ($G^k$) and parametric continuity ($C^k$) are easy to enforce at segment junctions.
6. **NC Machining compatibility** — CNC controllers natively accept parametric tool paths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Whenever a question asks *"why parametric representation is preferred in CAD?"*, the answer must mention **at least three of the six reasons above**, with a special emphasis on *offset curves*, *derivative evaluation*, and *vertical tangents*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parametric Representation of Common Analytical Curves

### 2.1.1 Straight Line Segment

A line segment joining two points $P_0 = (x_0, y_0)$ and $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ is given by **linear interpolation**:

$$P(u) = (1 - u) P_0 + u P_1, \quad u \in [0, 1]$$

**Component-wise expansion:**

$$x(u) = x_0 + u(x_1 - x_0)$$
$$y(u) = y_0 + u(y_1 - y_0)$$

This is a **first-degree polynomial** (a linear blending of two end-points). It is the foundational building block — *all* higher-order curves in CAD are ultimately built by blending a larger set of control points with blending functions of increasing degree.

---

### 2.1.2 Circle

A circle of radius $r$ centred at $(x_c, y_c)$ is represented parametrically as:

$$x(u) = x_c + r \cos(2\pi u)$$
$$y(u) = y_c + r \sin(2\pi u)$$
$$u \in [0, 1]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The parameter $u$ is the **normalized angle**: at $u = 0$ the point is at $(x_c + r, y_c)$ (the "3 o'clock" position), and at $u = 1$ it completes one full revolution. The non-parametric form $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$ *cannot* give an orientation or a starting point — the parametric form does both effortlessly.

**Derivative (Tangent Vector):**

$$P'(u) = \begin{bmatrix} -2\pi r \sin(2\pi u) \\ \phantom{-}2\pi r \cos(2\pi u) \end{bmatrix}$$

The magnitude of the tangent vector is constant: $\vert P'(u) \vert = 2\pi r$. This is the **arc-length parameterization constant** — important for uniform tool-path feeds in CNC.

---

### 2.1.3 Ellipse

An ellipse with semi-major axis $a$ and semi-minor axis $b$:

$$x(u) = x_c + a \cos(2\pi u)$$
$$y(u) = y_c + b \sin(2\pi u)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** The same trigonometric parameter $u$ does **not** give uniform arc-length speed. To move at constant speed along the ellipse, one must use the *arc-length parameterization*, which has no closed form and is computed numerically.

---

### 2.1.4 Parabola

For a parabola $y^2 = 4ax$:

$$x(u) = au^2$$
$$y(u) = 2au$$

This is a **rational quadratic** in $u$. The same curve is **not representable** as a single-valued $y = f(x)$ if we flip its orientation. Parametric form handles both orientations naturally.

---

### 2.1.5 3D Helix (Cylindrical)

A helix of radius $r$ climbing with pitch $p$ per revolution:

$$x(u) = r \cos(2\pi u)$$
$$y(u) = r \sin(2\pi u)$$
$$z(u) = pu$$

**The helix is the canonical example** of a curve that *cannot exist* in a non-parametric form: for every $(x, y)$ there are infinitely many valid $z$ values.

---

## 2.2 Continuity Conditions at Curve Junctions

When two curve segments are joined (e.g., a cubic Bezier patch meeting another), the junction can have different levels of smoothness. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires mastery of two parallel hierarchies:

### 2.2.1 Parametric Continuity ($C^k$)

| Order | Condition at Junction $u^*$ | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $C^0$ | $P_1(u^*) = P_2(u^*)$ | Curves **meet** (positional continuity) |
| $C^1$ | $P'_1(u^*) = P'_2(u^*)$ | **Tangents match** (curves are smooth, not kinked) |
| $C^2$ | $P''_1(u^*) = P''_2(u^*)$ | **Curvatures match** (no abrupt change in bending) |
| $C^k$ | First $k$ derivatives match | Higher-order smoothness |

### 2.2.2 Geometric Continuity ($G^k$)

Geometric continuity is **weaker** than parametric continuity but visually equivalent in most cases. It allows the tangent vectors to be scaled (not just equal).

| Order | Condition | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $G^0$ | Curves share an endpoint | Positional continuity |
| $G^1$ | Tangent **directions** match (magnitudes may differ) | Visually smooth — no kink |
| $G^2$ | Curvature vectors match in direction & magnitude | Curvature continuity |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distinction (favourite KTU question):** $C^1 \Rightarrow G^1$, but $G^1 \not\Rightarrow C^1$. Geometric continuity is widely used in CAD because it is more *flexible* — designers can preserve smoothness while changing parameterization speed.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are universally required for KTU 2024 Module-1 ESE questions. Memorize the structure, not just the equation.**

| # | Curve / Concept | Parametric Formula | Parameter Range | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Line segment | $P(u) = (1-u)P_0 + uP_1$ | $u \in [0,1]$ | Linear blend |
| 2 | Circle | $x = x_c + r\cos\theta, \; y = y_c + r\sin\theta$ | $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$ | Periodicity |
| 3 | Ellipse | $x = x_c + a\cos\theta, \; y = y_c + b\sin\theta$ | $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$ | Anisotropic |
| 4 | Parabola ($y^2 = 4ax$) | $x = au^2, \; y = 2au$ | $u \in \mathbb{R}$ | Rational |
| 5 | Helix | $x = r\cos\theta, \; y = r\sin\theta, \; z = c\theta$ | $\theta \in \mathbb{R}$ | 3D only |
| 6 | Hermite Cubic | $P(u) = h_0 P_0 + h_1 P_1 + h_2 T_0 + h_3 T_1$ | $u \in [0,1]$ | Endpoint-based |
| 7 | Bezier (degree $n$) | $P(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} B_{i,n}(u) P_i$ | $u \in [0,1]$ | Convex hull |
| 8 | B-Spline (order $k$) | $P(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,k}(u) P_i$ | $u \in [u_0, u_m]$ | Local control |
| 9 | Parametric Derivative | $P'(u) = \left( \dfrac{dx}{du}, \dfrac{dy}{du} \right)$ | — | Tangent vector |
| 10 | Arc Length | $s = \int_{u_0}^{u_1} \vert P'(u) \vert \, du$ | — | Tool-path length |

> **Hermite blending functions:**
> $h_0 = 2u^3 - 3u^2 + 1$
> $h_1 = -2u^3 + 3u^2$
> $h_2 = u^3 - 2u^2 + u$
> $h_3 = u^3 - u^2$

> **Bezier blending function (Bernstein polynomial):**
> $B_{i,n}(u) = \binom{n}{i} u^i (1-u)^{n-i}$

> **B-Spline basis (Cox–de Boor recursion):**
> $N_{i,1}(u) = 1$ if $u_i \le u < u_{i+1}$, else $0$
> $N_{i,k}(u) = \dfrac{u - u_i}{u_{i+k-1} - u_i} N_{i,k-1}(u) + \dfrac{u_{i+k} - u}{u_{i+k} - u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,k-1}(u)$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Airfoil Design):** Synthetic curves like cubic B-splines define the cross-section of wings, where no analytical equation suffices. The local control property of B-splines allows engineers to tweak a single control point without distorting the rest of the airfoil.
- **Automotive Body Design:** Class-A surfaces (visible outer panels of cars) are designed using NURBS — a generalization of B-splines that handles conic sections and free-form shapes in a unified mathematical framework.
- **Ship Hulls:** Traditional hull lines are lofted using cubic splines through offsets taken from a half-model. The parametric continuity ($C^2$) ensures the hull surface is "fair" — no unfair undulations.
- **CNC Tool Path Generation:** The derivative $P'(u)$ of the parametric curve directly yields the **tangent direction**, which is the feed direction of the cutting tool. Arc length $s(u)$ is integrated to compute the total tool travel distance.
- **Reverse Engineering:** A point cloud scanned from a physical object (e.g., a clay model of a car dashboard) is fitted with a B-spline surface — only a synthetic curve representation can absorb hundreds of points without oscillation.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Parametric Form of a Line Segment

**Problem Statement:** Given two points $P_0 = (1, 2)$ and $P_1 = (4, 6)$, derive the parametric equation of the line joining them, and find the point at $u = 0.4$.

**Step 1 — Recall the general linear blending form:**

$$P(u) = (1 - u) P_0 + u P_1$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known coordinate vectors:**

$$P_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad P_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Write the component-wise equations:**

$$\begin{aligned} x(u) &= (1-u) \cdot 1 + u \cdot 4 \\ &= 1 - u + 4u \\ &= 1 + 3u \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} y(u) &= (1-u) \cdot 2 + u \cdot 6 \\ &= 2 - 2u + 6u \\ &= 2 + 4u \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Express in vector form:**

$$P(u) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + 3u \\ 2 + 4u \end{bmatrix}, \quad u \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at $u = 0.4$:**

$$\begin{aligned} x(0.4) &= 1 + 3(0.4) = 1 + 1.2 = 2.2 \\ y(0.4) &= 2 + 4(0.4) = 2 + 1.6 = 3.6 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore P(0.4) = (2.2, \; 3.6)$$

> **Verification:** The point $(2.2, 3.6)$ lies on the line $y = \frac{4}{3}x + \frac{2}{3}$: $\frac{4}{3}(2.2) + \frac{2}{3} = 2.933 + 0.667 = 3.6$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tangent Vector and Arc Length of a Circle

**Problem Statement:** For a unit circle ($r = 1$) centred at the origin, derive the tangent vector $P'(u)$ and compute the arc length for one complete revolution ($u \in [0, 1]$).

**Step 1 — Write the parametric form:**

$$x(u) = \cos(2\pi u), \quad y(u) = \sin(2\pi u)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $u$:**

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dx}{du} &= -2\pi \sin(2\pi u) \\ \frac{dy}{du} &= \phantom{-}2\pi \cos(2\pi u) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the tangent vector:**

$$P'(u) = \begin{bmatrix} -2\pi \sin(2\pi u) \\ \phantom{-}2\pi \cos(2\pi u) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the magnitude of the tangent vector:**

$$\begin{aligned} \vert P'(u) \vert &= \sqrt{ \left(\dfrac{dx}{du}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{du}\right)^2 } \\ &= \sqrt{ 4\pi^2 \sin^2(2\pi u) + 4\pi^2 \cos^2(2\pi u) } \\ &= \sqrt{4\pi^2 \left[ \sin^2(2\pi u) + \cos^2(2\pi u) \right]} \\ &= \sqrt{4\pi^2 \cdot 1} \\ &= 2\pi \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the arc length using the integral formula:**

$$s = \int_0^1 \vert P'(u) \vert \, du = \int_0^1 2\pi \, du = 2\pi [u]_0^1 = 2\pi$$

$$\boxed{s = 2\pi r \text{ (for } r = 1, \; s = 2\pi)}$$

This matches the classical formula $C = 2\pi r$, confirming the parametric representation is **arc-length parameterized** in this case (the speed is constant). This is the ideal scenario for CNC milling — the tool moves at a constant feed rate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hermite Cubic Curve from End Conditions

**Problem Statement:** Derive the Hermite cubic polynomial $P(u)$ for $u \in [0,1]$ given:
- Endpoints: $P(0) = P_0 = (0, 0)$, $P(1) = P_1 = (4, 4)$
- Tangents: $P'(0) = T_0 = (3, 0)$, $P'(1) = T_1 = (3, 4)$

**Step 1 — Assume the general cubic form:**

$$P(u) = a_0 + a_1 u + a_2 u^2 + a_3 u^3$$

**Step 2 — Enforce the four boundary conditions:**

$$\begin{aligned} P(0) &= a_0 = P_0 \\ P(1) &= a_0 + a_1 + a_2 + a_3 = P_1 \\ P'(0) &= a_1 = T_0 \\ P'(1) &= a_1 + 2a_2 + 3a_3 = T_1 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear system:**

From $P(0)$: $a_0 = P_0$
From $P'(0)$: $a_1 = T_0$
From $P(1) - P(0)$: $a_2 + a_3 = P_1 - P_0 - T_0$
From $P'(1) - P'(0)$: $2a_2 + 3a_3 = T_1 - T_0$

Solving simultaneously:

$$\begin{aligned} a_2 &= 3(P_1 - P_0) - 2T_0 - T_1 \\ a_3 &= -2(P_1 - P_0) + T_0 + T_1 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values (using $x$-components):**

- $P_{0x} = 0, \; P_{1x} = 4, \; T_{0x} = 3, \; T_{1x} = 3$

$$\begin{aligned} a_{0x} &= 0 \\ a_{1x} &= 3 \\ a_{2x} &= 3(4 - 0) - 2(3) - 3 = 12 - 6 - 3 = 3 \\ a_{3x} &= -2(4 - 0) + 3 + 3 = -8 + 6 = -2 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — The $x$-coordinate polynomial is:**

$$x(u) = 3u + 3u^2 - 2u^3$$

**Step 6 — Verify the boundary conditions:**

$$\begin{aligned} x(0) &= 0 = P_{0x} \;\checkmark \\ x(1) &= 3 + 3 - 2 = 4 = P_{1x} \;\checkmark \\ x'(u) &= 3 + 6u - 6u^2 \\ x'(0) &= 3 = T_{0x} \;\checkmark \\ x'(1) &= 3 + 6 - 6 = 3 = T_{1x} \;\checkmark \end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — The Hermite blending-function form:**

This entire process can be compactly written as:

$$P(u) = h_0(u) P_0 + h_1(u) P_1 + h_2(u) T_0 + h_3(u) T_1$$

with the four **Hermite basis functions**:

$$\begin{aligned} h_0(u) &= 2u^3 - 3u^2 + 1 \\ h_1(u) &= -2u^3 + 3u^2 \\ h_2(u) &= u^3 - 2u^2 + u \\ h_3(u) &= u^3 - u^2 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Verification of the partition-of-unity property:**

$$h_0 + h_1 + h_2 + h_3 = (2u^3 - 3u^2 + 1) + (-2u^3 + 3u^2) + (u^3 - 2u^2 + u) + (u^3 - u^2)$$

Collecting terms:

$$= (2 - 2 + 1 + 1)u^3 + (-3 + 3 - 2 - 1)u^2 + (0 + 0 + 1 + 0)u + (1 + 0 + 0 + 0)$$

$$= 2u^3 - 3u^2 + u + 1 \quad \text{Wait, let us recount carefully:}$$

$$h_0 + h_1 = (2u^3 - 3u^2 + 1) + (-2u^3 + 3u^2) = 1$$

$$h_2 + h_3 = (u^3 - 2u^2 + u) + (u^3 - u^2) = 2u^3 - 3u^2 + u$$

Adding: $1 + 2u^3 - 3u^2 + u = 2u^3 - 3u^2 + u + 1$

**This is *not* identically 1.** The Hermite functions as written have a partition-of-unity *deficiency* of $2u^3 - 3u^2 + u$. To achieve partition-of-unity, the **tangent vectors must be scaled by the segment length**. The corrected form is:

$$P(u) = h_0(u) P_0 + h_1(u) P_1 + h_2^*(u) T_0 + h_3^*(u) T_1$$

where $h_2^* = (u^3 - 2u^2 + u) \cdot L$ and $h_3^* = (u^3 - u^2) \cdot L$ with $L$ = arc length scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap:** Many textbooks state $h_0 + h_1 + h_2 + h_3 = 1$. This is **only** true when the tangent vectors are scaled by the chord length $(P_1 - P_0)$. The unscaled form **does not** satisfy partition of unity. Examiners test this exact subtlety.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cubic Bezier Curve and its Derivatives

**Problem Statement:** For a cubic Bezier curve with four control points $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3$, derive the polynomial form and the first two derivatives.

**Step 1 — Recall the Bernstein basis for $n = 3$:**

$$P(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{3} B_{i,3}(u) P_i$$

**Step 2 — Expand the four Bernstein polynomials using $\binom{3}{i} u^i (1-u)^{3-i}$:**

$$\begin{aligned} B_{0,3}(u) &= (1-u)^3 \\ B_{1,3}(u) &= 3u(1-u)^2 \\ B_{2,3}(u) &= 3u^2(1-u) \\ B_{3,3}(u) &= u^3 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Expand each polynomial to standard form:**

$$\begin{aligned} (1-u)^3 &= 1 - 3u + 3u^2 - u^3 \\ 3u(1-u)^2 &= 3u(1 - 2u + u^2) = 3u - 6u^2 + 3u^3 \\ 3u^2(1-u) &= 3u^2 - 3u^3 \\ u^3 &= u^3 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the cubic Bezier polynomial:**

$$P(u) = (1 - 3u + 3u^2 - u^3) P_0 + (3u - 6u^2 + 3u^3) P_1 + (3u^2 - 3u^3) P_2 + u^3 P_3$$

**Step 5 — Collect like powers of $u$:**

$$\begin{aligned} P(u) &= P_0 + (-3P_0 + 3P_1)u + (3P_0 - 6P_1 + 3P_2)u^2 + (-P_0 + 3P_1 - 3P_2 + P_3)u^3 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate once for the tangent vector:**

$$\begin{aligned} P'(u) &= (-3P_0 + 3P_1) + 2(3P_0 - 6P_1 + 3P_2)u + 3(-P_0 + 3P_1 - 3P_2 + P_3)u^2 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the tangent at the endpoints:**

$$\begin{aligned} P'(0) &= 3(P_1 - P_0) \\ P'(1) &= 3(P_3 - P_2) \end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The tangent at the start of a cubic Bezier curve is **three times** the vector from $P_0$ to $P_1$. The tangent at the end is three times the vector from $P_2$ to $P_3$. The control polygon edges $P_0P_1$ and $P_2P_3$ are therefore the **directors** of the curve's tangents at the endpoints.

**Step 8 — Differentiate twice for the curvature vector:**

$$P''(u) = 2(3P_0 - 6P_1 + 3P_2) + 6(-P_0 + 3P_1 - 3P_2 + P_3)u$$

$$P''(u) = 6\left[ (P_0 - 2P_1 + P_2) + (-P_0 + 3P_1 - 3P_2 + P_3)u \right]$$

**Step 9 — Endpoint curvatures:**

$$\begin{aligned} P''(0) &= 6(P_0 - 2P_1 + P_2) \\ P''(1) &= 6(P_1 - 2P_2 + P_3) \end{aligned}$$

These expressions will be critical for **curvature continuity ($C^2$ or $G^2$)** at the junction of two Bezier segments — a common KTU 14-mark question.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: B-Spline Local Support Property

**Problem Statement:** Show that a quadratic ($k = 3$) B-spline basis function $N_{i,3}(u)$ is non-zero over exactly $k = 3$ knot spans, and is therefore a *local* function.

**Step 1 — Use the Cox–de Boor recursion for $k = 1$ (order-1 basis):**

$$N_{i,1}(u) = \begin{cases} 1, & u_i \le u < u_{i+1} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is a rectangular pulse — non-zero on **one** knot span.

**Step 2 — Recursion for $k = 2$ (linear basis):**

$$N_{i,2}(u) = \dfrac{u - u_i}{u_{i+1} - u_i} N_{i,1}(u) + \dfrac{u_{i+2} - u}{u_{i+2} - u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,1}(u)$$

Since $N_{i,1}$ is non-zero on $[u_i, u_{i+1})$ and $N_{i+1,1}$ is non-zero on $[u_{i+1}, u_{i+2})$, $N_{i,2}$ is non-zero on $[u_i, u_{i+2})$ — **two** knot spans.

**Step 3 — Recursion for $k = 3$ (quadratic basis):**

$$N_{i,3}(u) = \dfrac{u - u_i}{u_{i+2} - u_i} N_{i,2}(u) + \dfrac{u_{i+3} - u}{u_{i+3} - u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,2}(u)$$

$N_{i,2}$ is non-zero on $[u_i, u_{i+2})$ and $N_{i+1,2}$ is non-zero on $[u_{i+1}, u_{i+3})$, so $N_{i,3}$ is non-zero on $[u_i, u_{i+3})$ — **three** knot spans. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalization:** For a B-spline basis function of order $k$, the support is exactly $k$ consecutive knot spans. This is the mathematical root of the famous **local control property**: moving control point $P_i$ only affects the curve over $k$ knot spans — the rest of the curve is untouched. This is the single biggest advantage of B-splines over Bezier curves.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Synthetic Curve Families

| Property | Hermite Cubic | Cubic Bezier | Cubic B-Spline | NURBS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Input Data** | 2 endpoints + 2 tangent vectors | 4 control points | $n+1$ control points + knot vector | Control points + weights + knots |
| **Number of Control Points** | 4 (positions only) | 4 | Any $n+1$ | Any $n+1$ |
| **Local Control** | Yes (endpoint-based) | No (global influence) | Yes (order $k$ knot spans) | Yes |
| **Convex Hull Property** | No | Yes | Yes | Yes (with positive weights) |
| **Affine Invariance** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (weights are invariant) |
| **Can exactly represent a circle?** | No | No (approximation only) | No (approximation only) | **Yes** (exact conic representation) |
| **Continuity Across Segments** | $C^1$ if tangents matched | $C^0$ typically | $C^{k-1}$ automatically | $C^{k-1}$ automatically |
| **Computational Cost** | Lowest | Low | Moderate | Highest |
| **Standard Use Case** | Interpolation problems | Font design, logo design | Surface modelling | Industry standard for all CAD/CAM |

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow: How a CAD Kernel Evaluates a Parametric Curve

This block diagram traces the journey of a parametric curve evaluation request inside a typical CAD kernel (e.g., OpenCASCADE, Parasolid, ACIS).

```mermaid
    A3 -->|No| A5[Load control points and blending functions]:::path
    A4 --> A6[Evaluate Pu at requested u]:::compute
    A5 --> A7[Compute Cox-de Boor basis OR Bernstein basis]:::compute
    A7 --> A6
    A6 --> A8[Compute Pu using sum of weighted control points]:::compute
    A8 --> A9[Compute Pprime u for tangent]:::output
    A9 --> A10[Output: Point plus Tangent Vector]:::output
```

## 4.3 Continuity Hierarchy — Geometric vs. Parametric

```mermaid
graph TD
    C0[C0: Position matches]:::c0 --> C1[C1: First derivative matches]:::c1
    C1 --> C2[C2: Second derivative matches]:::c2
    C2 --> C3[Ck: k-th derivative matches]:::c3

    G0[G0: Endpoints coincide]:::g0 --> G1[G1: Tangent directions match]:::g1
    G1 --> G2[G2: Curvature vectors match]:::g2
    G2 --> G3[Gk: k-th order geometric continuity]:::g3

    C1 -.->|implies| G1
    C2 -.->|implies| G2
    G1 -.->|does NOT imply| C1
    G2 -.->|does NOT imply| C2

```

## 4.4 B-Spline Local Support Illustration

