This is the *true power* of using a well-defined program origin. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Program Origin Selection Criteria | Criterion | Ideal Scenario | Avoidance Scenario | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Datum Alignment** | Coincides with a primary datum on the drawing | Off-center, asymmetric placement | | **Operator Accessibility** | Easy to touch off with edge finder | Hidden inside a deep pocket or boss | | **Coordinate Sign** | All features in **positive** quadrant | Mixed positive/negative coordinates | | **Repeatability** | Same offset for batch production | Requires re-measurement every setup | | **Inspection Compatibility** | Matches CMM or gauge datums | Mismatched with quality control plan | | **Tool Clearance** | Tool can approach without collision | Origin forces tool to traverse the fixture | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchy of Coordinate Origins in CNC ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Machine Origin M
Fixed by machine builder
e.g., Home Position] --> B[Workpiece Origin O
Program Origin
Set by programmer] A --> C[Tool Offset Memory
H1, H2, H3 ...] B --> D[Workpiece Coordinate System WCS
Xw Yw Zw] C --> E[Controller Computation
XM = X0 + XP] D --> E E --> F[Servo Motor Commands
Machine Coordinates XM YM ZM] F --> G[Actual Tool Position
relative to Machine Bed] H[Engineering Drawing Dimensions] --> I[Programmer writes NC code
using Program Origin coordinates] I --> D ``` **Interpretation of the Diagram:** - The **Machine Origin (M)** is the absolute reference for the controller. - The **Workpiece Origin (O)** is the local reference chosen by the programmer. - The **Tool Offset Memory** stores tool length and diameter compensations. - The **Controller** combines the workpiece coordinates, the program-origin offset, and the tool offsets to compute the final machine commands. - The **Programmer** writes the NC code entirely in the WCS, using program-origin coordinates that mirror the engineering drawing dimensions. ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Program Origin Placement on a Rectangular Workpiece ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Workpiece
Rectangular Block] --> B[Program Origin
at Lower-Left-Top Corner
X0 Y0 Z0] B --> C[Feature 1
Hole at XP1 YP1 ZP1] B --> D[Feature 2
Pocket at XP2 YP2 ZP2] B --> E[Feature 3
Slot at XP3 YP3 ZP3] C --> F[All coordinates positive
NC code is clean] D --> F E --> F ``` **Interpretation:** - Placing the program origin at the lower-left-top (LLT) corner ensures that **every feature on the part has positive X, Y, Z coordinates**. - This eliminates negative values, reduces the chance of sign errors, and matches the typical "first quadrant" convention used in most textbooks and KTU exam questions. ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Drawing to Machined Part ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Engineering Drawing
with Datums] --> B[Process Planning
Select Program Origin] B --> C[Part Programming
Write NC Code in WCS] C --> D[Post Processing
Generate Machine Code] D --> E[CNC Controller
Stores G54 G55 ... Offsets] E --> F[Operator Sets
Program Origin on Machine] F --> G[Machine Runs NC Code
Applies Offsets Internally] G --> H[Machined Part
Matches Drawing Dimensions] ``` **Interpretation:** This diagram captures the **complete information flow** — from the engineering drawing to the finished workpiece — highlighting that the program origin is the critical bridge between the **abstract drawing dimensions** and the **physical machine coordinates**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Program Origin. How is it different from Machine Origin?* **Model Answer:** The **Program Origin** is a predefined point on the workpiece (or fixture) chosen by the part programmer, which serves as the reference (0, 0, 0) for all coordinates in the NC part program. It is set by the programmer based on the part geometry and fixturing requirements. The **Machine Origin**, in contrast, is a permanent, fixed reference point on the CNC machine tool itself — typically set by the machine builder at the limit-switch or home position of each axis. It cannot be changed by the programmer or operator. > [!NOTE] > **Word Count Target:** ~60–80 words for a 3-mark answer. Including a small sketch of the two origins on a machine can fetch an extra mark. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any **four** factors to be considered while selecting the location of the program origin for a CNC milling job.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Accessibility** — The point should be easy to locate using an edge finder, dial indicator, or touch probe. 2. **Datum Alignment** — It should coincide with a primary datum feature on the engineering drawing. 3. **Positive Coordinates** — Placement should ensure that all feature coordinates are positive (no negative values). 4. **Repeatability** — The same offset should work for all workpieces in a production batch. 5. **Inspection Compatibility** — The point should align with the CMM or gauge datum for easy quality checks. > [!NOTE] > Any four of the above are sufficient. Bullet-point format is acceptable and recommended for 3-mark questions. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **concept of program origin** in CNC part programming. Discuss the **step-by-step procedure** to set the program origin on a CNC milling machine, and state **six criteria** for its proper selection. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** For a CNC turning operation, the program origin is set at the **right-hand face of the workpiece along the spindle axis centerline**. A tool is commanded to move from point $P_1 = (30, 2, 0)$ to point $P_2 = (50, -1, 0)$ in the workpiece coordinate system. If the program origin offset is $(X_0, Y_0, Z_0) = (200, 150, 300)$ mm from the machine origin, compute: - (i) The **machine coordinates** of $P_1$ and $P_2$. - (ii) The **length of the tool path** from $P_1$ to $P_2$. - (iii) The **midpoint coordinate** of the path. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Concept, Procedure, and Criteria [7 Marks]:** **Concept (2 marks):** The program origin is the reference point on the workpiece from which all tool positions in the NC program are measured. It is **not fixed by the machine** but is **chosen by the programmer** to simplify coding, ensure positive coordinates, and align with part datums. **Procedure (3 marks):** 1. Mount the workpiece on the machine table (or in the chuck for turning). 2. Bring each axis to a known reference (e.g., machine zero or a tool-presetter). 3. Using an **edge finder** or **touch probe**, locate the workpiece surfaces corresponding to the X, Y, and Z datums. 4. Move the tool (or spindle) to the exact chosen program-origin point. 5. Record the displayed machine coordinates $(X_0, Y_0, Z_0)$ into the appropriate **work offset register** (G54, G55, etc.). 6. Issue the corresponding G-code (e.g., `G54`) at the start of the program to activate the offset. **Selection Criteria (2 marks — any 4 of the 6 below):** 1. Coincidence with a primary drawing datum. 2. Easy operator accessibility. 3. All feature coordinates should be positive. 4. Repeatable across multiple workpieces. 5. Compatible with inspection (CMM) datums. 6. No tool-collisions when approaching the origin. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]:** **(i) Machine Coordinates (3 marks):** Using the derived relation $X_M = X_0 + X_P$ (and similarly for Y, Z): For $P_1 = (30, 2, 0)$: $$ X_{M1} = 200 + 30 = 230 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Y_{M1} = 150 + 2 = 152 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Z_{M1} = 300 + 0 = 300 \text{ mm} $$ For $P_2 = (50, -1, 0)$: $$ X_{M2} = 200 + 50 = 250 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Y_{M2} = 150 + (-1) = 149 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Z_{M2} = 300 + 0 = 300 \text{ mm} $$ **(ii) Tool Path Length (2 marks):** $$ \Delta X = 250 - 230 = 20 \text{ mm} $$ $$ \Delta Y = 149 - 152 = -3 \text{ mm} $$ $$ \Delta Z = 300 - 300 = 0 \text{ mm} $$ $$ L = \sqrt{20^{2} + (-3)^{2} + 0^{2}} = \sqrt{400 + 9} = \sqrt{409} \approx 20.224 \text{ mm} $$ **(iii) Midpoint (2 marks):** $$ X_{mid} = \frac{230 + 250}{2} = 240 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Y_{mid} = \frac{152 + 149}{2} = 150.5 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Z_{mid} = \frac{300 + 300}{2} = 300 \text{ mm} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the **sign** of $Y_{P2} = -1$ mm when adding the offset. Always substitute carefully and re-check. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **relationship between Machine Origin, Program Origin, and Tool Offset** in a CNC controller. Describe how a modern CNC controller uses the G54–G59 work offset registers to handle program origins. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A CNC milling machine has its machine origin at the back-left corner of the table (at the top of the Z travel). A rectangular workpiece (200 mm × 150 mm × 50 mm) is clamped on the table such that its lower-left-front corner is at machine coordinates (300, 400, 50) mm. The program origin is chosen at the **center of the top face** of the workpiece. A hole is to be drilled at the **center of the top face** of the workpiece. - (i) Determine the **program coordinates** of the hole position. - (ii) If a second hole is to be drilled at a point 40 mm to the right and 25 mm toward the front (along the +X and -Y directions respectively) from the first hole, determine its **program coordinates**. - (iii) Compute the **machine coordinates** of both holes. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Block Diagram and G54–G59 Explanation [7 Marks]:** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** (Refer to SECTION_4 Diagram 4.1 for the equivalent block flow.) The controller receives: - The **NC program** (written in WCS using program-origin coordinates). - The **work offset register** contents $(X_0, Y_0, Z_0)$ (set by the operator using G54–G59). - The **tool offset table** (containing tool length and diameter offsets $H_1, H_2, \dots$). The controller's internal computation: $$ X_M = X_{offset} + X_P + X_{tool} $$ $$ Y_M = Y_{offset} + Y_P + Y_{tool} $$ $$ Z_M = Z_{offset} + Z_P + Z_{tool} $$ **G54–G59 Explanation (4 marks):** - Modern CNC controllers (Fanuc, Siemens, Haas) provide **six (or more) persistent work offset registers**: G54, G55, G56, G57, G58, G59. - Each register stores a unique $(X_0, Y_0, Z_0)$ offset from the machine origin to a different program origin. - The programmer/operator can: - Set up **multiple workpieces** on a tombstone fixture (each with its own program origin). - Set up **multiple fixtures** on a pallet (each with its own G54–G59). - Switch between origins mid-program using a simple `G55` or `G56` command. - Unlike the older `G92` command (which is modal and can drift), G54–G59 offsets are **non-modal and absolute** — they remain valid until explicitly changed. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]:** **Given:** - Workpiece dimensions: $L = 200$ mm (X), $W = 150$ mm (Y), $H = 50$ mm (Z). - Lower-left-front corner of workpiece in machine coordinates: $(300, 400, 50)$ mm. - Program origin $O$ at the **center of the top face**. **(i) Program coordinates of the first hole [2 marks]:** The first hole is at the **center of the top face** — which **is** the program origin. $$ X_{P1} = 0 \text{ mm}, \quad Y_{P1} = 0 \text{ mm}, \quad Z_{P1} = 0 \text{ mm} $$ **(ii) Program coordinates of the second hole [2 marks]:** 40 mm to the right (+X) and 25 mm toward the front (-Y) from the first hole: $$ X_{P2} = 0 + 40 = 40 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Y_{P2} = 0 + (-25) = -25 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Z_{P2} = 0 \text{ mm} $$ **(iii) Machine coordinates of both holes [3 marks]:** First, find the program origin offset $(X_0, Y_0, Z_0)$ in machine coordinates. The center of the top face is at: $$ X_0 = 300 + \frac{200}{2} = 300 + 100 = 400 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Y_0 = 400 + \frac{150}{2} = 400 + 75 = 475 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Z_0 = 50 + 50 = 100 \text{ mm} $$ (Here, "+50" is the top face, since the lower-front-bottom corner is at Z = 50 mm and the workpiece is 50 mm tall.) Using $X_M = X_0 + X_P$: For Hole 1: $$ X_{M1} = 400 + 0 = 400 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Y_{M1} = 475 + 0 = 475 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Z_{M1} = 100 + 0 = 100 \text{ mm} $$ For Hole 2: $$ X_{M2} = 400 + 40 = 440 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Y_{M2} = 475 + (-25) = 450 \text{ mm} $$ $$ Z_{M2} = 100 + 0 = 100 \text{ mm} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The negative $Y_{P2}$ value of $-25$ mm would be eliminated in practice by choosing the program origin at a *different* corner — this is a classic KTU exam trap that tests whether students understand the importance of program-origin placement. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > 1. **Confusing Program Origin with Machine Origin** — This is a *fatal* conceptual error worth 2–3 marks in a 7-mark question. > 2. **Forgetting the Sign of Negative Program Coordinates** — When adding offsets like $X_M = X_0 + X_P$, students often miss the negative sign in $X_P$, leading to a completely wrong answer. > 3. **Skipping Units in Numerical Answers** — KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5 marks for missing or incorrect units (mm). > 4. **Writing the Tool Path Length Without Showing the Formula** — Always write $L = \sqrt{\Delta X^2 + \Delta Y^2 + \Delta Z^2}$ explicitly before substituting. > 5. **Not Drawing a Diagram for "Explain" Questions** — A 7-mark conceptual question *without* a diagram typically receives at most 4–5 marks. Always include a labeled sketch of the workpiece, the program origin, the machine origin, and the WCS axes. > 6. **Mixing G54 and G92 Conceptions** — G54–G59 are *work offset registers* (persistent); G92 is a *coordinate system setting* (modal). Examiners love to test this distinction. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - **Definition:** Program Origin = the (0, 0, 0) reference point on the workpiece chosen by the programmer. - **Not the same as Machine Origin** (which is fixed by the machine builder). - **Selection criteria:** accessibility, datum alignment, positive coordinates, repeatability, inspection compatibility, tool clearance. - **Typical location:** center of top face (for symmetric parts) or lower-left-front corner (for prismatic parts). - **Setting methods:** G54–G59 (modern, persistent) or G92 (older, modal). - **Key formula:** $X_M = X_0 + X_P$ (and similarly for Y, Z). - **Tool path length:** $L = \sqrt{\Delta X_P^2 + \Delta Y_P^2 + \Delta Z_P^2}$. - **WCS axes:** Z points away from workpiece toward spindle; X is primary cutting direction; Y follows right-hand rule. - **Negative coordinates = bad practice** — choose origin to avoid them. - **Multiple origins (G54–G59)** enable tombstone fixtures and palletized cells. - **Exam tip:** Always draw a labeled diagram for any "explain" question worth ≥ 5 marks. - **Common trap:** A negative program coordinate (e.g., $Y_P = -25$ mm) is a *signal* to reconsider the origin placement, not to accept it. - **KTU 2024 focus:** Questions typically combine a *conceptual* part (definition, types, selection criteria) with a *numerical* part (coordinate transformation, path length) within a single 14-mark question — practice both.

topics.title: Part origin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Part origin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Part Origin** (also called *Part Zero*, *Program Zero*, or *Work Zero*) is the arbitrarily fixed reference point on the physical workpiece from which all the dimensional coordinates of the part geometry are measured and to which every movement of the cutting tool is referenced during CNC part programming and machining.

In the ISO 6983 (DIN 66025) standard and modern CNC controllers (Fanuc, Siemens, Haas, Heidenhain), the part origin is designated as the origin of the **Work Coordinate System (WCS)**, conventionally written as `X0 Y0 Z0`. All tool path commands in the part program are interpreted relative to this point until a coordinate system change command (e.g., `G54`, `G55`) is issued.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Part origin selection is treated as a sub-topic under *CNC Part Programming Fundamentals* in Module 3. Students are expected to know its definition, selection rules, and its distinction from **Machine Origin**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are reading a treasure map. Before the map can tell you *where the treasure is*, you must first agree on a **starting landmark** (e.g., the old oak tree). Every instruction like *"walk 10 steps North"* is meaningless unless you know exactly where you began.

In CNC machining, the **workpiece itself** is the "map," and the **Part Origin** is the "old oak tree." Before writing a single line of G-code, the programmer must decide:
- *"From which corner of the raw stock will I measure all my coordinates?"*

That chosen corner — typically located at the intersection of two datum faces and the top surface — becomes the **Part Origin**, and the entire program flows from it.

---

### 1.3 Quick Reference — Key Reference Points on a CNC Machine

| Symbol | Name | Location | Who Sets It? |
|:------:|:-----|:---------|:-------------|
| **M** | Machine Origin / Machine Zero | Fixed at the machine's limit switches (home position) | Machine builder (permanent) |
| **P** | Part Origin / Part Zero | A point on the workpiece chosen by the programmer | CNC Programmer |
| **T** | Tool Reference Point | The gauge point on the tool holder / spindle nose | Setter / Operator |
| **W** | Work Offset (G54–G59) | The numerical distance between M and P, stored in the controller memory | CNC Operator |

> [!NOTE]
> In every KTU exam answer related to this topic, **always draw all four points** (M, P, T, W) in a single sketch — this is a guaranteed 2-mark differentiator in the board valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Part Origin Works

A CNC machine executes a program in **three distinct frames of reference**:

1. **Machine Frame (Absolute):** The controller internally counts pulses relative to the Machine Origin `M`. This frame is invisible to the programmer.
2. **Work Frame (User-Defined):** The controller applies a stored numerical shift — the *Work Offset* — that mathematically translates every command from Part-Origin coordinates into Machine-Origin coordinates.
3. **Tool Frame (Dynamic):** Tool length and diameter compensations (G43, G41/G42) are layered on top of the work frame to define the *Cutter Compensation Point*.

The mathematical relationship governing these frames is:

$$P_{machine} \;=\; P_{program} \;+\; W_{offset} \;+\; T_{compensation}$$

Where:
- $P_{machine}$ = Position commanded to the servo motors
- $P_{program}$ = Coordinate value written by the programmer (relative to Part Origin)
- $W_{offset}$ = Distance from Machine Origin to Part Origin (stored under G54–G59)
- $T_{compensation}$ = Tool length / radius offset

---

### 2.2 Selection Criteria for Part Origin (KTU Frequently Asked)

The part origin must be chosen such that:

- **Step 1 — Accessibility:** It lies on a finished machined surface or a clearly datable feature (avoid cast/rough surfaces).
- **Step 2 — Repeatability:** The setter/operator can locate it consistently on every new workpiece loaded onto the fixture.
- **Step 3 — Numerical Convenience:** It should maximize the use of positive coordinates and minimize negative values, reducing programming errors.
- **Step 4 — Symmetry Alignment:** For symmetrical parts, place it on the axis of symmetry to exploit left/right mirror commands (e.g., G51.1).
- **Step 5 — Datum Compliance:** It should align with the **GD&T datum reference frame** defined on the engineering drawing.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Purpose |
|:--------|:-------------------|:------|:--------|
| Work Offset (single axis) | $W_{x} = X_{machine} - X_{program}$ | mm | Determines the G54 X-shift value |
| Work Offset (3-axis) | $\vec{W} = \vec{M} - \vec{P}$ | mm | Vector form of the offset |
| Tool Length Compensation | $T_{length} = Z_{machine} - Z_{program} - Z_{offset}$ | mm | Applied via G43 H01 |
| Cutter Compensation Activation | $\Delta R = R_{tool}$ | mm | Applied via G41/G42 D## |
| Coordinate Transformation (Rotation) | $X' = X\cos\theta - Y\sin\theta$ | mm | Used when part origin is rotated |
| Coordinate Transformation (Rotation) | $Y' = X\sin\theta + Y\cos\theta$ | mm | Companion Y-axis rotation |
| Floating Zero Offset | $\Delta W_{x} = W_{x,\,new} - W_{x,\,old}$ | mm | Incremental shift between operations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule Applied:** All absolute-value notations and pipes have been replaced with `\vert` semantics where required. **Never** use a literal `\vert x \vert` inside a markdown table row — it breaks the parser.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of part origin is the silent backbone of:

- **Aerospace structural machining** — Where datum reference frames (DRF) must align with aircraft coordinate systems.
- **Automotive powertrain manufacturing** — Engine blocks and cylinder heads are programmed with the part origin at the cylinder bore axis-line intersection.
- **Multi-axis simultaneous machining (5-axis)** — Where the part origin dynamically shifts as the table rotates (A/B axes), requiring advanced **TCP** (Tool Center Point) programming.
- **CMM Inspection** — The same part origin used during machining is reused during quality inspection, ensuring full digital thread continuity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Work Offset Calculation from Known Coordinates

**Problem Statement:**  
A CNC milling machine has its Machine Origin at the rear-left-bottom corner of the table. The operator mounts a workpiece such that the **Part Origin** (chosen as the front-right-top corner of the stock) is located at:
- $X_{M \rightarrow P} = +350.000$ mm
- $Y_{M \rightarrow P} = +220.000$ mm
- $Z_{M \rightarrow P} = +75.000$ mm

Find the numerical values that must be entered in the controller's **G54 Work Offset Register**.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The controller's G54 register stores the *machine coordinates* of the part origin. Hence the work offset equals the absolute vector from the Machine Origin `M` to the Part Origin `P`.

$$
W_{x} \;=\; X_{P} \;-\; X_{M}
$$

Substituting the datum values:

$$
W_{x} \;=\; +350.000 \;-\; 0 \;=\; +350.000 \text{ mm}
$$

For the Y-axis:

$$
W_{y} \;=\; Y_{P} \;-\; Y_{M}
$$

$$
W_{y} \;=\; +220.000 \;-\; 0 \;=\; +220.000 \text{ mm}
$$

For the Z-axis:

$$
W_{z} \;=\; Z_{P} \;-\; Z_{M}
$$

$$
W_{z} \;=\; +75.000 \;-\; 0 \;=\; +75.000 \text{ mm}
$$

**Resulting G54 Register Values (to be keyed in by the operator):**

$$
\boxed{\;G54\,X = +350.000 \quad G54\,Y = +220.000 \quad G54\,Z = +75.000\;}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coordinate Transformation When Part Origin is Rotated

**Problem Statement:**  
A rectangular workpiece is mounted on a rotary table rotated by $\theta = 30^\circ$ about the Z-axis. The part origin is offset from the rotation center by:
- $X_{local} = +50.000$ mm
- $Y_{local} = +40.000$ mm

Compute the equivalent coordinates in the machine frame.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The transformation matrix for rotation in the XY-plane about Z-axis is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} X_{machine} \\ Y_{machine} \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_{local} \\ Y_{local} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing each trigonometric value:

$$
\cos 30^\circ \;=\; 0.8660254 \qquad \sin 30^\circ \;=\; 0.5000000
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
X_{machine} \;=\; (0.8660254)(+50.000) \;-\; (0.5000000)(+40.000)
$$

$$
X_{machine} \;=\; +43.3013 \;-\; 20.0000 \;=\; +23.3013 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
Y_{machine} \;=\; (0.5000000)(+50.000) \;+\; (0.8660254)(+40.000)
$$

$$
Y_{machine} \;=\; +25.0000 \;+\; +34.6410 \;=\; +59.6410 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;X_{machine} = +23.3013 \text{ mm} \qquad Y_{machine} = +59.6410 \text{ mm}\;}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Floating Zero Offset Between Two Setups

**Problem Statement:**  
Operation 10 (Facing) uses G54 with $W_{x,1} = +300.000$ mm. Operation 20 (Drilling) requires the part origin to be shifted by $\Delta X = -45.000$ mm in X. Compute the new G55 register value.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The floating zero offset is the algebraic difference between the desired and existing offsets:

$$
\Delta W_{x} \;=\; W_{x,\,new} \;-\; W_{x,\,old}
$$

Substituting:

$$
-45.000 \;=\; W_{x,\,new} \;-\; (+300.000)
$$

Solving for $W_{x,\,new}$:

$$
W_{x,\,new} \;=\; +300.000 \;+\; (-45.000) \;=\; +255.000 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;G55\,X = +255.000 \text{ mm (if Y and Z remain unchanged)}\;}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Fixed Part Origin (G54) | Floating Part Origin (G54 + G55) |
|:----------|:------------------------|:---------------------------------|
| Number of origins | One per setup | Multiple within a single setup |
| Code complexity | Lower | Higher (needs M-codes to switch) |
| Datum reference | Single DRF | Multiple datum transitions |
| Best suited for | Single-setup prismatic parts | Multi-operation jobs with repositioning |
| KTU exam weightage | High (Definitions & Sketches) | Medium (Calculations & Code snippets) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy of CNC Reference Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Machine Origin M] --> node2[Work Offset G54 to G59]
    node2 --> node3[Part Origin P on Workpiece]
    node3 --> node4[Programmed Coordinates X Y Z]
    node4 --> node5[Tool Length Compensation G43]
    node5 --> node6[Cutter Radius Compensation G41 G42]
    node6 --> node7[Final Servo Command to Motors]

    node8[CNC Programmer] --> node3
    node9[CNC Setter Operator] --> node1
    node9 --> node2

    classDef originBox fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef workBox fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef commandBox fill:#bfb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class node1,node3 originBox
    class node2,node4,node5,node6 workBox
    class node7 commandBox
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Part Origin Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engineering Drawing] --> B[Identify Datums DRF]
    B --> C{Candidate Locations}
    C -->|Symmetry Axis| D[Center of Symmetry]
    C -->|Edge Intersection| E[Corner of Datum Faces]
    C -->|Functional Feature| F[Bore Axis or Hole Center]
    D --> G[Evaluate Selection Criteria]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Finalized Part Origin P]
    H --> I[Enter G54 Offset]
    I --> J[Validate with Dry Run]
    J --> K[Production Machining]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Coordinate Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Program Coordinates in X Y Z] --> P2[Add Work Offset G54]
    P2 --> P3[Add Tool Length G43]
    P3 --> P4[Add Cutter Compensation G41 G42]
    P4 --> P5[Apply Rotation if Rotary Axis A B]
    P5 --> P6[Machine Coordinates Sent to Servo Drives]
    P6 --> P7[Physical Axis Movement]

    classDef progBox fill:#ffe,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef finalBox fill:#cfc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 progBox
    class P6,P7 finalBox
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Define **Part Origin** in CNC part programming. How is it different from the **Machine Origin**?

**Model Answer:**

**Part Origin** is the reference point on the workpiece itself, designated as `(X0, Y0, Z0)`, from which all coordinates in the CNC part program are measured.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Machine Origin | Part Origin |
|:-------|:---------------|:------------|
| Location | Fixed at machine limit switches | On the workpiece |
| Set by | Machine builder | Programmer / Setter |
| Visibility | Internal reference | Visible datum in the program |
| Code used | Implicit (controller internal) | G54 to G59 |

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q:** List any **four rules** that govern the selection of the Part Origin on a CNC workpiece.

**Model Answer:**

1. The Part Origin should lie on a **finished or pre-machined surface**, not on a rough cast face.
2. It should be **repeatable** so that every new workpiece in a batch can be located identically.
3. It should be chosen so that **most coordinates remain positive**, simplifying program writing.
4. For **symmetrical parts**, it must be placed on the **axis of symmetry** to facilitate mirror commands.
5. It must coincide with the **primary datum reference frame** given on the engineering drawing.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Part Origin**, **Machine Origin**, and **Work Offset** in a CNC machine with the help of a neat sketch. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Machine Origin (M):** It is the fixed, permanent reference point at the extreme limit of each axis travel, defined by limit switches at the time of machine commissioning. The controller counts all pulse commands from this origin. It cannot be changed by the user.

**Part Origin (P):** It is the programmer-defined reference point on the workpiece geometry from which all coordinates of the feature locations are measured. It is the origin of the **Work Coordinate System (WCS)**.

**Work Offset (W):** It is the numerical distance — stored in registers G54 to G59 — that represents the vector from the Machine Origin to the Part Origin. During program execution, the controller automatically adds this offset to every programmed coordinate.

**Sketch (to be drawn by student):**
- Draw the machine table in plan view
- Mark Machine Origin `M` at the rear-left corner
- Mark Part Origin `P` on the workpiece
- Draw arrow vectors $W_x$, $W_y$, $W_z$ from M to P
- Label the tool reference point `T` at the spindle nose

**(b)** A milling workpiece is mounted such that the chosen Part Origin is at machine coordinates `X = +250.000 mm, Y = +180.000 mm, Z = +85.000 mm`. If a programmed move is `G01 X40.000 Y30.000 Z-5.000`, determine the **actual machine coordinates** of the tool tip. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Using the transformation relation:

$$
P_{machine} \;=\; P_{program} \;+\; W_{offset}
$$

For X-axis:

$$
X_{machine} \;=\; +40.000 \;+\; +250.000 \;=\; +290.000 \text{ mm}
$$

For Y-axis:

$$
Y_{machine} \;=\; +30.000 \;+\; +180.000 \;=\; +210.000 \text{ mm}
$$

For Z-axis:

$$
Z_{machine} \;=\; -5.000 \;+\; +85.000 \;=\; +80.000 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;X = +290.000 \text{ mm} \quad Y = +210.000 \text{ mm} \quad Z = +80.000 \text{ mm}\;}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** What is a **Floating Part Origin**? When is it preferred over a fixed Part Origin? Provide an example with a G-code snippet using **G54 and G55**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Floating Part Origin** is a technique in which the programmer shifts the origin mid-program using multiple work-offset registers (G54 to G59) without physically re-clamping the workpiece.

**When Preferred:**
- Multi-operation jobs (facing → drilling → tapping) where each operation references a different datum.
- When the workpiece is large and tool travel would exceed limits from a single origin.
- When symmetric machining is required (mirror commands).

**Example G-code Snippet:**

```text
O1001 (FLOATING PART ORIGIN DEMO)
G21 G90 G94 (MM, ABSOLUTE, FEED/MIN)
G54 (SELECT FIRST PART ORIGIN FOR FACING)
T01 M06 (FACE MILL)
G00 X0 Y0 Z50.0
M03 S1200
G01 Z-1.0 F200
X100.0 Y0
G00 Z50.0
G55 (SWITCH TO SECOND PART ORIGIN FOR DRILLING)
T02 M06 (DRILL)
G00 X20.0 Y20.0 Z50.0
G01 Z-15.0 F100
G00 Z50.0
M05 M30
```

**(b)** With reference to the **Work Offset formula**, derive the G55 offset for a part where the X-distance between two consecutive datums is `85.000 mm` to the right of the G54 origin. The original G54 X-register holds `+300.000 mm`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The floating zero offset relationship is:

$$
W_{x,\,G55} \;=\; W_{x,\,G54} \;+\; \Delta X
$$

Given:
- $W_{x,\,G54} = +300.000$ mm
- $\Delta X = +85.000$ mm (to the right = positive direction)

Substituting:

$$
W_{x,\,G55} \;=\; +300.000 \;+\; (+85.000)
$$

$$
W_{x,\,G55} \;=\; +385.000 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;G55\,X = +385.000 \text{ mm}\;}
$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Students often forget that the Work Offset is *added* to programmed coordinates, not subtracted. Always remember: `Machine = Program + Offset`.
> 2. **Skipping the Sketch:** In 7-mark questions asking to "explain with a sketch," not drawing the diagram costs a flat **2–3 marks** even if the written answer is perfect.
> 3. **Mixing Machine Zero with Part Zero:** Writing "G54 sets the Machine Origin" will fetch **zero marks** — these are fundamentally different concepts.
> 4. **Forgetting the Z-offset:** Many students only calculate X and Y offsets, leaving Z blank. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and inches (`G20` vs `G21`) within the same answer script — always declare `G21` at the start.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Part Origin = `(X0, Y0, Z0)` on the workpiece, the reference for all CNC program coordinates.
- **Distinction:** Machine Origin (fixed, by builder) ≠ Part Origin (chosen by programmer).
- **Storage:** Stored in **G54–G59** work-offset registers.
- **Formula (HIGH-YIELD):** $P_{machine} = P_{program} + W_{offset}$.
- **Selection Rules:** Datum-aligned, repeatable, positive-biased, on finished surface, symmetry-respecting.
- **Floating Origin:** Multiple G-codes (G54–G59) used within one program for multi-operation parts.
- **Tool Compensation Layer:** Length (`G43 H##`) and Radius (`G41/G42 D##`) are applied *on top of* the work offset.
- **Always sketch** M, P, T, W in every exam answer.
- **Sign Convention:** +X = Right, +Y = Away, +Z = Up (standard milling convention per ISO 841).
- **Standard Reference:** ISO 6983 (G-code) and ISO 841 (axis naming) define the part-origin framework.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Setting up of origin - absolute and incremental programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Setting up of origin - absolute and incremental programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Setting Up of Origin" in CNC?

In CNC (Computer Numerical Control) part programming, **Setting up of Origin** refers to the procedure of defining a fixed reference point on the workpiece, tool, or machine from which all tool path coordinates are measured. Since a CNC controller cannot interpret a part drawing in graphical form, it requires an unambiguous numerical reference — this reference is called the **Origin**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBPET504 – Module 3):**
> Origin selection is the *first* and *most critical* step in any CNC part program. A wrongly declared origin invalidates every subsequent coordinate in the program.

### 1.2 Types of Origin in CNC Systems

There are fundamentally **four** types of origins used in CNC machining:

| # | Origin Type | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Machine Zero / Machine Reference Point** | M | Fixed point defined by the machine builder, used for machine calibration. Cannot be altered by the user. |
| 2 | **Workpiece Zero / Program Zero** | W | Selected by the part programmer on the workpiece itself. All program coordinates are referenced from this point. |
| 3 | **Fixture Zero** | F | Common offset origin when multiple identical parts are clamped in a fixture. |
| 4 | **Tool Zero / Tool Reference Point** | T | The reference point on the cutting tool (usually the gauge line or tool tip). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Workpiece Zero, W):** The point on the workpiece from which all program coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ are measured. It is conventionally located at the intersection of the workpiece top surface and the front-left corner for milling, or at the spindle centerline for turning.

### 1.3 The Two Modes of Dimensioning: Absolute vs. Incremental

The moment the origin is fixed, the programmer must choose how every subsequent coordinate will be expressed. This choice defines the entire "dimensioning philosophy" of the program and is governed by two G-codes:

- **G90 — Absolute Programming**
- **G91 — Incremental Programming**

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a classroom attendance register:**
> - **Absolute mode** is like writing each student's roll number from **1** onwards — every student has a unique fixed number. If student #5 is absent, you still say "Roll #5 is absent."
> - **Incremental mode** is like a teacher calling out names starting from where the last attendance left off — "5 students after Ram… that is Suresh." Here the number is *relative* to the previous person.
>
> In CNC, **Absolute = fixed reference from origin**, while **Incremental = reference from the previous tool position**.

---

## 1.5 Formal KTU Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Programming (G90):** A coordinate system in which every tool position is specified with respect to a fixed, pre-established origin (workpiece zero). The coordinates represent the *absolute distance* of the point from the origin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incremental Programming (G91):** A coordinate system in which every tool position is specified with respect to the *immediately preceding* tool position. The coordinates represent the *distance to be moved* (a delta) from the current location.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation

Let us formalize the relationship between the two modes. Consider a tool that traverses a sequence of points $P_1, P_2, P_3, \dots, P_n$ in the XY-plane, with the origin $W$ at $(0,0)$.

### 2.1.1 Absolute Coordinates

In absolute mode, each point $P_i$ is given directly by:

$$P_i = (X_i^{abs}, Y_i^{abs})$$

where $X_i^{abs}$ and $Y_i^{abs}$ are the perpendicular distances of point $P_i$ from the workpiece origin $W$.

### 2.1.2 Incremental Coordinates

In incremental mode, the coordinates of point $P_i$ are the *differences*:

$$\Delta X_i = X_i^{abs} - X_{i-1}^{abs}$$
$$\Delta Y_i = Y_i^{abs} - Y_{i-1}^{abs}$$

so that the *incremental coordinate block* for $P_i$ becomes $(U_i, V_i) = (\Delta X_i, \Delta Y_i)$.

### 2.1.3 Conversion Between the Two Modes

The conversion from absolute to incremental is a **difference operation**:

$$U_i = X_i^{abs} - X_{i-1}^{abs}, \quad V_i = Y_i^{abs} - Y_{i-1}^{abs}$$

Conversely, incremental to absolute is a **cumulative sum**:

$$X_i^{abs} = X_1^{abs} + \sum_{k=2}^{i} U_k$$
$$Y_i^{abs} = Y_1^{abs} + \sum_{k=2}^{i} V_k$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This dual-mode capability is highly useful in **mirror machining**, **sub-routine generation**, and **parametric part families**. Incremental programming excels when the part geometry is composed of repeated identical cuts (e.g., circular patterns, pocket milling), while absolute programming is preferred for complex profiles where any error in incremental distance compounds progressively.

---

## 2.2 Rules for Selecting the Origin Point

The choice of workpiece origin $W$ is governed by standard engineering conventions to ensure **reproducibility** and **ease of inspection**:

1. **Designer's Datum:** The origin should ideally coincide with the **datums** shown on the engineering drawing (e.g., the lower-left corner of the front view, the centerline of a turned part).
2. **Accessibility:** It should be easy to locate on the raw stock for the setup operator.
3. **Symmetry:** For symmetric parts, choose the center of symmetry — this allows negative coordinates to represent symmetric features.
4. **Inspection-Friendly:** Placing the origin at a feature that can be easily probed or measured (e.g., the center of a drilled hole) simplifies quality control.

---

## 2.3 Methods of Setting the Origin in CNC

### 2.3.1 Method 1: Using G92 (Older Convention — Still Tested in KTU)

In many controller manuals (FANUC, Siemens), the command:

```
G92 X__ Y__ Z__
```

tells the controller: *"From this moment onward, treat the current tool position as having coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ in the program."* The controller internally computes the offset from machine zero.

### 2.3.2 Method 2: Using Workpiece Coordinate System Offsets (G54–G59)

The modern and preferred method uses **six preset memory locations** (G54 through G59) plus extended ones (G54.1 P1…P48 on FANUC). Each holds the offset distance from machine zero to a workpiece zero.

```
G54          (selects the 1st workpiece coordinate system)
G90          (absolute mode)
G00 X10 Y10  (rapid move to absolute point X=10, Y=10)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Tip:** G54–G59 are *modal* and *persistent*. Once activated, they remain in effect until another G-code (G55, G56…) overrides them. This makes multi-fixture setups trivial.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Absolute (G90) | Incremental (G91) |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference point** | Workpiece zero (W) | Previous tool position |
| **Coordinate meaning** | True position from origin | Distance to move (delta) |
| **Mathematical form** | $P_i = (X_i^{abs}, Y_i^{abs})$ | $U_i = X_i^{abs} - X_{i-1}^{abs}$ |
| **Cumulative sum (i to absolute)** | $X_i^{abs} = X_1 + \sum_{k=2}^{i} U_k$ | $X_i^{abs}$ — direct |
| **Initial block** | Coordinates from $W$ | Coordinates from $W$ (first block uses absolute as start) |
| **Error propagation** | **No accumulation** (each block independent) | **Accumulates** (error in block *n* affects all subsequent) |
| **Best suited for** | Complex 2D/3D contours | Repetitive, equal-step patterns |
| **Cumulative distance** | $D_{tot} = \sum \sqrt{(X_i-X_{i-1})^2 + (Y_i-Y_{i-1})^2}$ | Same — depends on geometry |
| **Length of program** | Generally shorter | May be longer for irregular profiles |
| **Ease of editing/debug** | High — block can be moved freely | Low — sequence order is critical |
| **Standard ISO code** | **G90** | **G91** |
| **Modal status** | Modal | Modal |
| **Default on most CNC** | **Default (active at startup)** | Inactive (must be programmed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The codes G90 and G91 are **mutually exclusive modal codes** — only one can be active at a time. Programming both in the same block will trigger an alarm on most controllers.

---

## 2.5 Why the Choice of Origin Mode Matters in Real Engineering

| Industry Scenario | Recommended Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Milling an aerospace contour | **Absolute (G90)** | Errors do not compound; blocks can be re-ordered. |
| Drilling a bolt-circle pattern | **Incremental (G91)** | Equal angular steps are easy to express as deltas. |
| Engraving repeated letters | **Incremental with sub-routine** | Each letter is identical and offset incrementally. |
| Lathe turning of a stepped shaft | **Absolute (G90)** | Diameter values are given directly from spindle centerline. |
| Mirror-image part machining | **Mixed** | Absolute for base, then mirror via G51.1. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Converting Between Modes

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Model Style):**
> A CNC milling tool is programmed to move through the following sequence of points in the XY-plane. The workpiece zero is located at the lower-left corner of the stock.
>
> | Block | Point | Coordinates (mm) |
> |---|---|---|
> | N10 | $P_1$ | $(10, 10)$ |
> | N20 | $P_2$ | $(40, 10)$ |
> | N30 | $P_3$ | $(40, 30)$ |
> | N40 | $P_4$ | $(10, 30)$ |
>
> **Task:** Write the program using (a) **Absolute (G90)** mode and (b) **Incremental (G91)** mode. Then verify mathematically by converting one to the other.

---

### 3.1.1 Part (a) — Absolute Program (G90)

In absolute mode, each block lists the **true position** of the point from the origin $W = (0, 0)$.

| Block | Command | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| N05 | `G90 G54 G00 X10 Y10` | Select absolute mode, select workpiece coord. system 1, rapid to $P_1(10,10)$. |
| N10 | `G01 X40 Y10 F100` | Linear move to $P_2(40,10)$ at feed $100 \text{ mm/min}$. |
| N20 | `G01 X40 Y30` | Linear move to $P_3(40,30)$. |
| N30 | `G01 X10 Y30` | Linear move to $P_4(10,30)$. |
| N40 | `G01 X10 Y10` | Return to $P_1(10,10)$ — close the loop. |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** Notice that block N30 contains the value `X10` — even though the previous X was 40, we write **10**, not −30. This is the essence of absolute programming: coordinates are *always measured from the origin*, never from the previous position.

---

### 3.1.2 Part (b) — Incremental Program (G91)

We must now compute the **deltas** between successive points.

#### Step 1 — Compute $\Delta X$ and $\Delta Y$ for each transition

**Transition $P_1 \to P_2$:**

$$\Delta X_{1\to2} = X_2^{abs} - X_1^{abs} = 40 - 10 = +30 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{1\to2} = Y_2^{abs} - Y_1^{abs} = 10 - 10 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Transition $P_2 \to P_3$:**

$$\Delta X_{2\to3} = X_3^{abs} - X_2^{abs} = 40 - 40 = 0 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{2\to3} = Y_3^{abs} - Y_2^{abs} = 30 - 10 = +20 \text{ mm}$$

**Transition $P_3 \to P_4$:**

$$\Delta X_{3\to4} = X_4^{abs} - X_3^{abs} = 10 - 40 = -30 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{3\to4} = Y_4^{abs} - Y_3^{abs} = 30 - 30 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Transition $P_4 \to P_1$ (closing the loop):**

$$\Delta X_{4\to1} = X_1^{abs} - X_4^{abs} = 10 - 10 = 0 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{4\to1} = Y_1^{abs} - Y_4^{abs} = 10 - 30 = -20 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 2 — Write the Incremental Program

| Block | Command | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| N05 | `G91 G54 G00 X10 Y10` | Incremental mode ON; first move is *technically* absolute (from origin to $P_1$). |
| N10 | `G01 X30 F100` | Move $\Delta X = +30$, $\Delta Y = 0$ to reach $P_2$. |
| N20 | `G01 Y20` | Move $\Delta X = 0$, $\Delta Y = +20$ to reach $P_3$. |
| N30 | `G01 X-30` | Move $\Delta X = -30$, $\Delta Y = 0$ to reach $P_4$. |
| N40 | `G01 Y-20` | Move $\Delta X = 0$, $\Delta Y = -20$ back to $P_1$. |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** In incremental mode, **signs matter**. A negative sign (e.g., `X-30`) tells the tool to move *backwards* along the X-axis.

---

### 3.1.3 Verification via Reverse Conversion (Incremental → Absolute)

Let us verify the incremental program by summing the deltas, starting from the initial position $(10, 10)$:

**After N10:**
$$X = 10 + 30 = 40, \quad Y = 10 + 0 = 10 \quad \checkmark \text{ matches } P_2$$

**After N20:**
$$X = 40 + 0 = 40, \quad Y = 10 + 20 = 30 \quad \checkmark \text{ matches } P_3$$

**After N30:**
$$X = 40 + (-30) = 10, \quad Y = 30 + 0 = 30 \quad \checkmark \text{ matches } P_4$$

**After N40:**
$$X = 10 + 0 = 10, \quad Y = 30 + (-20) = 10 \quad \checkmark \text{ matches } P_1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Both programs produce the **identical tool path**, a square of side $\vert 30 \vert$ mm. This proves that the two modes are mathematically equivalent representations of the same geometry.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Error Propagation Analysis

A critical engineering distinction between the two modes lies in **how errors propagate**. Let us analyze this rigorously.

### 3.2.1 Setup

Suppose each block of the CNC program has a stochastic positioning error $\epsilon_i$ drawn from a distribution with mean zero and standard deviation $\sigma$. We wish to compute the cumulative positional error at the $n$-th point.

### 3.2.2 Absolute Mode Error Analysis

In absolute mode, each block independently targets an absolute coordinate. The positioning error in each block is **independent** of all others. Therefore, the variance of the error at point $n$ is:

$$\sigma_{abs,n}^2 = \sigma^2 \quad (\text{constant for all } n)$$

This is because the controller re-calculates the position from the fixed origin at every block. **Errors do not accumulate.**

### 3.2.3 Incremental Mode Error Analysis

In incremental mode, each block adds a delta to the *previous* (already-erroneous) position. The position at step $n$ is:

$$X_n^{abs} = X_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{n} U_i$$

with measured value:

$$\hat{X}_n^{abs} = X_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{n} (U_i + \epsilon_i)$$

The cumulative error is:

$$e_n = \hat{X}_n^{abs} - X_n^{abs} = \sum_{i=2}^{n} \epsilon_i$$

The variance of the cumulative sum of $n-1$ independent errors is:

$$\sigma_{inc,n}^2 = (n-1) \cdot \sigma^2$$

Taking the square root (standard deviation):

$$\sigma_{inc,n} = \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n-1}$$

### 3.2.4 Comparison Table

| Number of blocks, $n$ | $\sigma_{abs,n}$ | $\sigma_{inc,n}$ | Ratio $\sigma_{inc} / \sigma_{abs}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma$ | $0$ (no prior step) | 0 |
| 5 | $\sigma$ | $2.00\, \sigma$ | 2.00 |
| 10 | $\sigma$ | $3.00\, \sigma$ | 3.00 |
| 50 | $\sigma$ | $7.00\, \sigma$ | 7.00 |
| 100 | $\sigma$ | $9.95\, \sigma$ | 9.95 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write $\sigma_{inc,n} = n \cdot \sigma$ (linear growth). The correct growth is $\sigma \sqrt{n}$ (square-root / random-walk growth). This is a classic **1-mark loss** in valuation.

---

## 3.3 Tool-Length Offset and Origin Setup — Combined Derivation

When setting up origin on a CNC milling machine with multiple tools, the *machine zero* $M$ is fixed, the *workpiece zero* $W$ is at the workpiece surface, and the *tool zero* $T$ is at the spindle nose. The standard offsets are:

$$Z_{M \to W} = \text{distance from machine zero to workpiece top surface}$$
$$Z_{M \to T_i} = \text{distance from machine zero to tip of tool } i$$

The tool-length offset for tool $i$ is then:

$$H_i = Z_{M \to T_i} - Z_{M \to W}$$

When the controller executes a Z-move in the program, it interprets the coordinate relative to $W$, but the servo drives the spindle so that the tool tip reaches:

$$Z_{tip} = Z_M - (Z_{M \to W} + \Delta Z_{program})$$

For tool $i$, this becomes:

$$Z_{tip}^{(i)} = Z_M - (Z_{M \to T_i}) = \text{spindle position compensated by } H_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The origin $W$ is *defined once*, but the controller compensates for each tool's length automatically. This is why **G43 H__** (tool length compensation) is mandatory in any multi-tool program.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Origin Setup — Reference Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["MACHINE ZERO M"]
    nodeB["FIXTURE ZERO F"]
    nodeC["WORKPIECE ZERO W"]
    nodeD["TOOL ZERO T"]
    nodeE["G54 G55 G56 G57 G58 G59 OFFSET REGISTERS"]
    nodeF["G90 ABSOLUTE PROGRAM"]
    nodeG["G91 INCREMENTAL PROGRAM"]
    nodeH["CNC CONTROLLER SERVO DRIVE"]
    nodeI["SPINDLE AND TOOL MOTION"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The machine zero $M$ is the *root reference* of the entire coordinate hierarchy. From $M$, the fixture offset establishes the workpiece zero $W$, and from $W$, both absolute and incremental programs derive their coordinate values. All paths eventually converge at the controller's servo drive, which physically moves the spindle.

---

## 4.2 Coordinate Reference Comparison — Absolute vs. Incremental

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ABS["ABSOLUTE MODE G90 REFERENCE"]
        nodeW1["WORKPIECE ZERO W 0,0"]
        nodeP1A["P1 10,10"]
        nodeP2A["P2 40,10"]
        nodeP3A["P3 40,30"]
        nodeP4A["P4 10,30"]
        nodeW1 --> nodeP1A
        nodeW1 --> nodeP2A
        nodeW1 --> nodeP3A
        nodeW1 --> nodeP4A
    end

    subgraph INC["INCREMENTAL MODE G91 REFERENCE"]
        nodeP1I["START P1 10,10"]
        nodeP2I["P2 PLUS 30, 0"]
        nodeP3I["P3 PLUS 0, PLUS 20"]
        nodeP4I["P4 MINUS 30, 0"]
        nodeP1I --> nodeP2I
        nodeP2I --> nodeP3I
        nodeP3I --> nodeP4I
    end
```

---

## 4.3 G-Code Decision Flowchart — Which Mode to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["START: PART PROGRAMMING"]
    nodeQ1{"IS THE PART GEOMETRY A REPEATED EQUAL STEP PATTERN?"}
    nodeQ2{"ARE BLOCKS LIKELY TO BE RE-ORDERED OR EDITED OFTEN?"}
    nodeABS["USE G90 ABSOLUTE PROGRAMMING"]
    nodeINC["USE G91 INCREMENTAL PROGRAMMING"]
    nodeMIX["USE MIXED MODE WITH CARE"]

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -->|"YES e.g. BOLT CIRCLE"| nodeINC
    nodeQ1 -->|"NO"| nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -->|"YES e.g. PROTOTYPE"| nodeABS
    nodeQ2 -->|"NO e.g. FAMILY OF PARTS"| nodeMIX
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Use the mnemonic **A**bsolute for **A**rbitrary edits, **I**ncremental for **I**dentical step patterns.

---

## 4.4 Multi-Tool Origin Offset Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeMZ["MACHINE ZERO M"]
    nodeWZ["WORKPIECE ZERO W ON STOCK TOP"]
    nodeT1["TOOL 1 TIP T1"]
    nodeT2["TOOL 2 TIP T2"]
    nodeT3["TOOL 3 TIP T3"]
    nodeH1["OFFSET H1 IN G54 REGISTER"]
    nodeH2["OFFSET H2 IN G54 REGISTER"]
    nodeH3["OFFSET H3 IN G54 REGISTER"]
    nodeCTL["CNC CONTROLLER APPLIES G43 H COMPENSATION"]

    nodeMZ --> nodeWZ
    nodeWZ --> nodeT1
    nodeWZ --> nodeT2
    nodeWZ --> nodeT3
    nodeT1 --> nodeH1
    nodeT2 --> nodeH2
    nodeT3 --> nodeH3
    nodeH1 --> nodeCTL
    nodeH2 --> nodeCTL
    nodeH3 --> nodeCTL
```
