<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Differentiate between absolute and incremental programming in CNC. State the standard G-codes used.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Absolute Programming (G90):** Every coordinate in the program represents the *true position* of the tool measured from the **workpiece zero (W)**. Each block is independent.
>
> **Incremental Programming (G91):** Every coordinate represents the *distance to be moved* from the **immediately preceding tool position** (a delta value).
>
> The standard ISO G-codes are **G90** for absolute and **G91** for incremental. They are mutually exclusive modal codes.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is a workpiece zero? Why is its selection important?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Workpiece Zero (W)** is the origin point on the workpiece from which all program coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ are measured.
>
> **Importance of selection:**
> 1. It serves as the **common reference** for every coordinate in the program.
> 2. It should coincide with the **designer's datum** to ensure drawing-to-part correspondence.
> 3. It must be **easy to locate** on the raw stock by the setup operator.
> 4. A wrongly chosen or declared origin **invalidates every subsequent coordinate**, leading to scrapped parts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with *internal choice*. You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the various types of origins used in CNC machining. How is the workpiece zero set using the **G92** command?

**(b) [7 Marks)** A CNC milling cutter is to move through the following sequence of points. The workpiece zero is at the lower-left corner. Write the **complete NC program** using both **G90 (absolute)** and **G91 (incremental)** modes, and verify by computing the tool-path length.

| Block | Point | $X$ (mm) | $Y$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_1$ | 20 | 20 |
| 2 | $P_2$ | 60 | 20 |
| 3 | $P_3$ | 60 | 50 |
| 4 | $P_4$ | 20 | 50 |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Origin Types and G92 Setup

**Origin Types:**

1. **Machine Zero (M):** Fixed by the machine builder at a known position (e.g., the limit-switch end of the X-axis travel). Used for machine calibration and home positioning. **Cannot** be altered by the user.

2. **Workpiece Zero (W):** Selected by the programmer on the workpiece. For milling, it is conventionally at the **top-front-left** corner. For turning, it is at the **spindle centerline** on the finished face.

3. **Fixture Zero (F):** When multiple identical parts are mounted in a single fixture, this common origin simplifies programming for batch production.

4. **Tool Zero (T):** The reference point on the cutting tool — typically the **gauge line** of a milling tool or the **tool tip** of a turning tool.

**Setting W using G92:**

The command `G92 X__ Y__ Z__` is issued when the tool is *physically positioned* at the desired workpiece zero. The controller then calculates the offset $M \to W$ as:

$$\text{Offset}_{M \to W} = \text{Current machine coordinate} - (X_{prog}, Y_{prog}, Z_{prog})$$

For example, if the tool is physically at machine coordinate $(300.00, 250.00, -50.00)$ and the programmer wants $W$ to be declared at $(20, 20, 0)$:

$$\text{Offset}_{M \to W} = (300-20,\; 250-20,\; -50-0) = (280,\; 230,\; -50)$$

The controller stores this offset and uses it for all subsequent G90 blocks until G92 is re-issued.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Dual-Mode Program

**Step 1: Compute Incremental Deltas (Absolute → Incremental)**

**Transition $P_1 \to P_2$:**

$$\Delta X_{12} = 60 - 20 = +40 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{12} = 20 - 20 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Transition $P_2 \to P_3$:**

$$\Delta X_{23} = 60 - 60 = 0 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{23} = 50 - 20 = +30 \text{ mm}$$

**Transition $P_3 \to P_4$:**

$$\Delta X_{34} = 20 - 60 = -40 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{34} = 50 - 50 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Write the G90 Program**

| Block | Code | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| N05 | `G90 G54 G00 X20 Y20` | Absolute mode, rapid to $P_1$ |
| N10 | `G01 X60 F80` | Linear to $P_2$, feed $80$ mm/min |
| N20 | `G01 Y50` | Linear to $P_3$ |
| N30 | `G01 X20` | Linear to $P_4$ |
| N40 | `M30` | End of program |

**Step 3: Write the G91 Program**

| Block | Code | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| N05 | `G91 G54 G00 X20 Y20` | Incremental mode, first move from origin to $P_1$ |
| N10 | `G01 X40 F80` | Delta $(+40, 0)$ → $P_2$ |
| N20 | `G01 Y30` | Delta $(0, +30)$ → $P_3$ |
| N30 | `G01 X-40` | Delta $(-40, 0)$ → $P_4$ |
| N40 | `M30` | End of program |

**Step 4: Verify by Computing Total Tool-Path Length**

The geometry is a closed rectangle. Length of each side:

$$L_1 = \sqrt{(60-20)^2 + (20-20)^2} = \sqrt{40^2 + 0^2} = 40 \text{ mm}$$
$$L_2 = \sqrt{(60-60)^2 + (50-20)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 30^2} = 30 \text{ mm}$$
$$L_3 = \sqrt{(20-60)^2 + (50-50)^2} = 40 \text{ mm}$$
$$L_4 = \sqrt{(20-20)^2 + (20-50)^2} = 30 \text{ mm}$$

Total perimeter:

$$L_{tot} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + L_4 = 40 + 30 + 40 + 30 = 140 \text{ mm}$$

Both programs produce the **identical tool path of $140$ mm perimeter**, confirming equivalence.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **G54–G59** workpiece coordinate system offsets in CNC. How do they differ from the older **G92** method? List **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of incremental programming.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A drilling operation requires **6 holes** equally spaced on a **bolt circle** of diameter $80$ mm. The center of the circle is at workpiece coordinates $(100, 100)$. Using **incremental programming (G91)**, write the drilling program, assuming the drill is initially positioned at the center.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — G54–G59 vs. G92

**G54–G59 (Workpiece Coordinate System Offsets):**

These are six (plus extended G54.1 P1…P48) **persistent memory locations** in the CNC controller. Each holds the offset distance from the *machine zero* $M$ to a *workpiece zero* $W_k$ ($k = 1 \dots 6$). Once programmed into the register (typically via the controller's offset page), they remain valid across multiple part setups and program runs.

**Key Differences from G92:**

| Aspect | G92 | G54–G59 |
|---|---|---|
| Persistence | Volatile (lost on reset/power-off) | Persistent (saved in non-volatile memory) |
| Number of origins | One at a time | Up to **6 (or 48 extended)** simultaneously |
| Setting method | Programmed in NC code via tool touch-off | Set on the controller's offset page |
| Best use | Single-part, one-off jobs | Multi-fixture, batch, pallet-changing setups |

**Advantages of Incremental Programming:**

1. **Compact code for repeated patterns:** Equal steps (e.g., a bolt circle) require only one delta block, often reused via sub-routines.
2. **Easy relative modification:** If a step size changes, only the delta block needs editing — the origin is unaffected.

**Disadvantages of Incremental Programming:**

1. **Cumulative error:** Errors propagate as $\sigma \sqrt{n}$, making long programs less accurate.
2. **Cannot reorder blocks:** Block insertion or deletion requires re-computation of all subsequent deltas — tedious for complex edits.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Bolt Circle Drilling Program

**Given:**
- Bolt circle diameter $D = 80$ mm
- Center: $(100, 100)$ in workpiece coordinates
- 6 holes equally spaced → angular step $\theta = 360° / 6 = 60°$
- Drill starts at the center $(100, 100)$

**Step 1: Compute Hole Coordinates (Absolute, for reference)**

The $k$-th hole is located at:

$$X_k = 100 + \frac{D}{2} \cos(k\theta) = 100 + 40 \cos(k \cdot 60°)$$
$$Y_k = 100 + \frac{D}{2} \sin(k\theta) = 100 + 40 \sin(k \cdot 60°)$$

| Hole $k$ | $X_k$ (mm) | $Y_k$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $100 + 40\cos(60°) = 120.00$ | $100 + 40\sin(60°) = 134.64$ |
| 2 | $100 + 40\cos(120°) = 80.00$ | $100 + 40\sin(120°) = 134.64$ |
| 3 | $100 + 40\cos(180°) = 60.00$ | $100 + 40\sin(180°) = 100.00$ |
| 4 | $100 + 40\cos(240°) = 80.00$ | $100 + 40\sin(240°) = 65.36$ |
| 5 | $100 + 40\cos(300°) = 120.00$ | $100 + 40\sin(300°) = 65.36$ |
| 6 | $100 + 40\cos(360°) = 140.00$ | $100 + 40\sin(360°) = 100.00$ |

**Step 2: Compute Incremental Deltas from Center**

Starting at the center $(100, 100)$, the first move to hole 1 has:

$$\Delta X_1 = 120 - 100 = +20.00 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_1 = 134.64 - 100 = +34.64 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Compute Inter-Hole Deltas**

For $k = 1 \to 2$:

$$\Delta X_{1\to2} = 80 - 120 = -40.00 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{1\to2} = 134.64 - 134.64 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

For $k = 2 \to 3$:

$$\Delta X_{2\to3} = 60 - 80 = -20.00 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{2\to3} = 100 - 134.64 = -34.64 \text{ mm}$$

For $k = 3 \to 4$:

$$\Delta X_{3\to4} = 80 - 60 = +20.00 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{3\to4} = 65.36 - 100 = -34.64 \text{ mm}$$

For $k = 4 \to 5$:

$$\Delta X_{4\to5} = 120 - 80 = +40.00 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{4\to5} = 65.36 - 65.36 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

For $k = 5 \to 6$:

$$\Delta X_{5\to6} = 140 - 120 = +20.00 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Y_{5\to6} = 100 - 65.36 = +34.64 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Write the Incremental Drilling Program**

| Block | Code | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| N05 | `G91 G54 G00 X20 Y34.64` | Incremental mode, rapid from center to hole 1 |
| N10 | `G81 R2 Z-10 F60` | Drilling cycle, retract plane R=2, depth Z=-10, feed 60 mm/min |
| N15 | `G80` | Cancel drilling cycle |
| N20 | `G00 X-40` | Delta to hole 2 |
| N25 | `G81 R2 Z-10 F60` | Drill hole 2 |
| N30 | `G80` | Cancel |
| N35 | `G00 X-20 Y-34.64` | Delta to hole 3 |
| N40 | `G81 R2 Z-10 F60` | Drill hole 3 |
| N45 | `G80` | Cancel |
| N50 | `G00 X20 Y-34.64` | Delta to hole 4 |
| N55 | `G81 R2 Z-10 F60` | Drill hole 4 |
| N60 | `G80` | Cancel |
| N65 | `G00 X40` | Delta to hole 5 |
| N70 | `G81 R2 Z-10 F60` | Drill hole 5 |
| N75 | `G80` | Cancel |
| N80 | `G00 X20 Y34.64` | Delta to hole 6 |
| N85 | `G81 R2 Z-10 F60` | Drill hole 6 |
| N90 | `G80 G00 X-140 Y-100` | Return to center $(0, 0)$ from hole 6 |
| N95 | `M30` | End of program |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Optimization:** In a real shop-floor program, a **sub-routine (M98)** would be called for each hole-drilling block to drastically shorten the program. The above expanded form is preferred for clarity in KTU theory exams.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing absolute and incremental in the same block** — A single block cannot be partially absolute and partially incremental. Mixing is only allowed between blocks, not within one.
> 2. **Forgetting the first block's rule** — In G91 mode, the *very first move is still measured from the origin* (since there is no "previous position" to reference). Students often incorrectly write `G91 X0 Y0` for the first move.
> 3. **Sign errors in G91** — A negative delta (e.g., `X-30`) is a legitimate move. Students sometimes omit the minus sign, which moves the tool in the wrong direction by $30$ mm — enough to scrap the workpiece.
> 4. **Origin declaration missing** — A program without explicit `G54` (or `G92`) declaration is incomplete. Examiners deduct marks for this.
> 5. **Wrong error-propagation formula** — Use $\sigma \sqrt{n}$, not $n \cdot \sigma$. (See Section 3.2.4.)
> 6. **Unit omission** — Always state units (mm) when writing coordinates, especially when trig functions are involved.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Setting Up of Origin: Absolute vs. Incremental Programming**

- **Origin Types (4):** Machine Zero ($M$), Workpiece Zero ($W$), Fixture Zero ($F$), Tool Zero ($T$).
- **Workpiece Zero (W):** The fixed reference point on the workpiece from which **all program coordinates** are measured.
- **G90 = Absolute Mode:** Coordinates are measured from $W$ (the fixed origin).
- **G91 = Incremental Mode:** Coordinates are deltas measured from the *previous tool position*.
- **Modal & Mutually Exclusive:** G90 and G91 are modal codes and **cannot coexist** in the same block.
- **Conversion Formula (Abs → Inc):**
  $$U_i = X_i^{abs} - X_{i-1}^{abs}, \quad V_i = Y_i^{abs} - Y_{i-1}^{abs}$$
- **Conversion Formula (Inc → Abs):**
  $$X_i^{abs} = X_1^{abs} + \sum_{k=2}^{i} U_k$$
- **Error Propagation:** Absolute error stays constant ($\sigma$); incremental error grows as $\sigma\sqrt{n}$.
- **Origin Selection Rules:** Coincide with designer's datum, easy to locate, symmetric if possible, inspection-friendly.
- **G54–G59:** Persistent multi-origin offsets (preferred for batch production).
- **G92:** Volatile single-origin declaration (older method, still tested in KTU).
- **First Block Rule in G91:** Always has a "previous position" implicitly at $W = (0, 0)$ — write the first move as if from the origin.
- **Best Practice Use Cases:**
  - **G90:** Complex contours, prototypes, frequently edited programs.
  - **G91:** Bolt circles, arrays, sub-routines, family-of-parts machining.
- **Standard Reference:** ISO 6983 (G-code language) and ISO 14649 (STEP-NC, the modern alternative).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparatory codes and miscellaneous codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Preparatory codes and miscellaneous codes # 📘 MODULE 3 — PREPARATORY CODES & MISCELLANEOUS CODES ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > In Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM) and CNC part programming, **Preparatory Codes (commonly called G-codes or G-functions)** are standardized alphanumeric instructions that *prepare* the CNC machine tool for a specific type of motion, positioning mode, or machining operation. **Miscellaneous Codes (commonly called M-codes or M-functions)** are auxiliary instructions that control *non-motion* machine functions such as spindle ON/OFF, coolant flow, tool changes, and program end operations. ### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" Approach Imagine you are writing a recipe to bake a cake: - **G-codes are like the preparation steps** — "preheat the oven to 180°C," "grease the pan," "set to convection mode." They don't *do* the baking themselves, but they set up *how* the next steps will be performed. - **M-codes are like the utility switches** — "turn ON the oven light," "start the timer," "stop the mixer." They control *supporting machinery*, not the actual mixing motion. In CNC programming: - **G-codes** → Prepare the controller for a specific motion type (linear, circular, rapid) or coordinate mode (absolute/incremental). - **M-codes** → Activate/deactivate physical machine accessories (spindle, coolant, chuck, tool changer). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Note:** > Almost every KTU 2024 Part A question from CNC Part Programming module includes a one-line question: *"What are G-codes and M-codes?"* or *"List any five preparatory functions with their functions."* Memorizing the standard G and M codes is **non-negotiable** for scoring marks. ### 1.2 Classification of Preparatory (G) Codes G-codes are classified into two major categories based on their behavior after execution: | Class | Name | Behavior | Examples | |-------|------|----------|----------| | **Modal** | Persistent / Sticky Codes | Once activated, remain in effect until *canceled* by another code in the same group | G00, G01, G02, G03, G90, G91 | | **Non-Modal** | One-shot Codes | Active *only for the block in which they are written* | G04 (Dwell), G28 (Return to Reference), G92 | > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick:** *Modal = Persistent Mode* — think of it as a **"sticky note"** on the controller that stays until peeled off. ### 1.3 Standard G-Code Reference Table (As per ISO 6983 / EIA RS-267) | G-Code | Group | Function Description | |--------|-------|----------------------| | **G00** | 01 | Rapid positioning (no cutting, max traverse) | | **G01** | 01 | Linear interpolation at programmed feed rate | | **G02** | 01 | Circular interpolation — Clockwise (CW) | | **G03** | 01 | Circular interpolation — Counter-Clockwise (CCW) | | **G04** | 00 | Dwell (programmable pause) | | **G17 / G18 / G19** | 02 | Plane selection (XY / XZ / YZ) | | **G20 / G70** | 06 | Inch unit programming | | **G21 / G71** | 06 | Metric (mm) unit programming | | **G28** | 00 | Return to machine zero through intermediate point | | **G40** | 07 | Cutter compensation — CANCEL | | **G41** | 07 | Cutter compensation — LEFT of tool path | | **G42** | 07 | Cutter compensation — RIGHT of tool path | | **G43** | 08 | Tool length compensation — positive direction | | **G80** | 09 | Cancel canned cycle | | **G81 to G89** | 09 | Fixed / Canned drilling cycles | | **G90** | 03 | Absolute coordinate programming | | **G91** | 03 | Incremental coordinate programming | | **G92** | 00 | Pre-setting of work coordinate system / Spindle speed clamp | | **G94** | 05 | Feed rate in mm/min (or inch/min) | | **G95** | 05 | Feed rate in mm/rev (or inch/rev) | | **G96** | 05 | Constant surface speed (CSS) | | **G97** | 05 | Spindle speed in RPM (cancel CSS) | ### 1.4 Standard M-Code Reference Table | M-Code | Function Description | |--------|----------------------| | **M00** | Program stop (unconditional) | | **M01** | Optional stop (only if operator enables switch) | | **M02** | End of program | | **M03** | Spindle ON — Clockwise (CW) rotation | | **M04** | Spindle ON — Counter-Clockwise (CCW) rotation | | **M05** | Spindle STOP | | **M06** | Automatic Tool Change (ATC) | | **M07** | Coolant ON — Mist coolant | | **M08** | Coolant ON — Flood coolant | | **M09** | Coolant OFF | | **M10** | Chuck / Work-clamp ON (lathe) | | **M11** | Chuck / Work-clamp OFF (lathe) | | **M19** | Spindle orientation (tool change position) | | **M30** | End of program + rewind (most common end code) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb (KTU Valuation):** > A single NC block can typically contain **only one G-code from the same group**, but **multiple M-codes** can appear together (e.g., `N50 M08 M03 S1500;`). --- # 🔬 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Operational Logic & Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of Major Preparatory Codes ### 📐 A. G00 — Rapid Positioning - **Purpose:** Move the tool from current position to a new point at the **maximum traverse rate** of the machine. - **Feed Rate:** Controller-defined rapid rate (typically **5000–30000 mm/min** depending on machine). - **Motion Type:** Point-to-point (each axis moves independently at its own max rate; the path is *NOT* necessarily a straight line). - **Use Case:** Tool positioning between cuts, rapid retracts, non-cutting motion. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction:** G00 moves as fast as possible but is **NOT a cutting move**. Using G00 for actual cutting is a programming error. ### 📐 B. G01 — Linear Interpolation - **Purpose:** Move the tool in a **straight line** at a **programmed feed rate (F)**. - **Syntax:** `N__ G01 X__ Y__ Z__ F__ ;` - **Feed Rate:** Must be specified using F-word (e.g., `F150` → 150 mm/min if G94 is active). - **Engineering Utility:** The most common code used for straight-line milling, turning, and drilling operations. ### 📐 C. G02 & G03 — Circular Interpolation These are the most *mathematically intensive* codes and frequently appear in KTU university exam problems. - **G02 → Clockwise (CW) circular arc** - **G03 → Counter-Clockwise (CCW) circular arc** **Direction is determined by viewing along the *negative* axis direction** (Right-Hand Rule for arcs). **Syntax for Circular Interpolation:** - **End-point + Radius method:** `N__ G02/G03 X__ Y__ R__ F__ ;` - **End-point + Center method (preferred for KTU problems):** `N__ G02/G03 X__ Y__ I__ J__ K__ F__ ;` Where: - `X, Y, Z` → End-point coordinates of the arc - `I, J, K` → Vector from arc start to arc center (in X, Y, Z respectively) - `R` → Arc radius (signed: positive for angles ≤ 180°, negative for > 180°) ### 📐 D. G90 vs G91 — Coordinate Programming Modes | Parameter | G90 (Absolute) | G91 (Incremental) | |-----------|---------------|-------------------| | **Coordinates** | Referenced from **work origin (zero point)** | Referenced from **current tool position** | | **Best For** | Most engineering drawings (dimensioned from one origin) | Simple repetitive patterns, drilling patterns | | **Example** | `G90 G01 X50 Y30;` → Move to point (50,30) from origin | `G91 G01 X50 Y30;` → Move 50 in X and 30 in Y from current pos | ### 📐 E. G94 vs G95 — Feed Rate Specification | Mode | Units | Typical Application | |------|-------|---------------------| | **G94** | mm/min (or inch/min) | Milling — independent of spindle speed | | **G95** | mm/rev (or inch/rev) | Turning — feed per spindle revolution | ### 📐 F. G04 — Dwell (Programmable Pause) - **Purpose:** Pause the machine for a specified time (used for chip breaking, hole bottom cleanup, indexing). - **Syntax:** `N__ G04 X__ ;` or `N__ G04 P__ ;` - **Units:** X → seconds, P → milliseconds (in some controllers). ### 📐 G. G41/G42 — Cutter Compensation - **G41** → Tool offsets to the **LEFT** of programmed path (looking in direction of tool motion). - **G42** → Tool offsets to the **RIGHT** of programmed path. - **G40** → Cancels cutter compensation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Allows the *same program* to run with different tool diameters without re-writing — the tool radius (D-offset) is set in the controller register. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application | |---|-------------------|------------|-------------| | 1 | Arc End-Point Calculation (Center Method) | $X_e = X_c + I, \quad Y_e = Y_c + J$ | Finding arc end coordinates | | 2 | Vector to Center | $I = X_c - X_s, \quad J = Y_c - Y_s$ | From start point (s) to center (c) | | 3 | Arc Radius from I, J | $R = \sqrt{I^2 + J^2}$ | Verifying arc geometry | | 4 | Spindle Surface Speed | $V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | $V$ in m/min, $D$ in m, $N$ in rpm | | 5 | Spindle RPM | $N = \dfrac{V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | For G96 CSS programming | | 6 | Feed per Minute | $f_{min} = f_{rev} \times N$ | Conversion G95 ↔ G94 | | 7 | Dwell Time (Chip Break) | $t_d = \dfrac{\text{revolutions to dwell}}{N}$ | For G04 dwell in turning | | 8 | Tool Length Compensation | $Z_{actual} = Z_{programmed} + H_{offset}$ | G43/H_ usage | | 9 | Total Programming Time | $T_{total} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{f_i} + \sum t_{dwell}$ | Time estimation in CAM | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where pipes are used (like $\vert I \vert$, $\vert J \vert$), they have been replaced with `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve the markdown table rendering — please ignore the visual difference; the mathematical meaning is identical.** ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Utility of G/M Codes | |--------|---------------------| | **Aerospace** | 5-axis contour milling of turbine blades — uses G02/G03 extensively | | **Automotive** | Cylinder block machining — heavy use of canned cycles (G81–G89) | | **Die & Mold** | 3D surface milling — uses G41/G42 cutter compensation and G95 feed-per-tooth | | **Medical Implants** | Micro-machining of titanium implants — G04 dwell for chip evacuation | --- # 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Arc End-Point from Center Method This is the most commonly tested derivation in KTU Module-3 problems. ### Problem Statement: A CNC milling program must machine a circular arc whose: - Start point: $S = (X_s, Y_s)$ - Center: $C = (X_c, Y_c)$ - Required: End-point coordinates $E = (X_e, Y_e)$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Define the I and J vectors** The vector from start point to center is the difference of their coordinates: $$ \begin{aligned} I &= X_c - X_s \\ J &= Y_c - Y_s \end{aligned} $$ This is by ISO 6983 convention: $I, J, K$ represent the *incremental* distances from the arc start point to the arc center along $X, Y, Z$ axes respectively. **Step 2 — Express End-Point Coordinates** Since the center $C$ lies at distance $R$ from both start $S$ and end $E$: $$ \begin{aligned} X_e &= X_c + I_{end} \\ Y_e &= Y_c + J_{end} \end{aligned} $$ where $I_{end}$ and $J_{end}$ are the projections of the radius vector at the endpoint. **Step 3 — Solve Numerically (Example)** Given: Start point $S = (20, 30)$, Center $C = (50, 30)$, Arc is a **quarter circle in G02 (CW)**. $$ \begin{aligned} I &= X_c - X_s = 50 - 20 = 30 \\ J &= Y_c - Y_s = 30 - 30 = 0 \\ R &= \sqrt{I^2 + J^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + 0^2} = 30 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ For a quarter CW arc ending at $E = (50, 0)$: $$ \begin{aligned} X_e &= X_c + I_{final} = 50 + 0 = 50 \\ Y_e &= Y_c + J_{final} = 30 + (-30) = 0 \end{aligned} $$ Final NC Block: ``` N50 G02 X50.0 Y0.0 I30.0 J0.0 F100 ; ``` **Step 4 — Verification using radius equation** Distance from center $(50, 30)$ to end-point $(50, 0)$: $$ R_{check} = \sqrt{(50-50)^2 + (0-30)^2} = 30 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Spindle Speed from Surface Speed (G96 Programming) When a CNC lathe is programmed with **G96 (Constant Surface Speed)**, the controller continuously adjusts spindle RPM based on the *current* diameter being cut. This is critical for turning operations to maintain consistent tool life. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Define Surface Speed (Cutting Speed)** Surface speed $V$ is the linear velocity of the workpiece surface at the cutting point: $$ V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N $$ where: - $V$ = cutting speed (m/min) - $D$ = instantaneous diameter of cut (m) - $N$ = spindle rotational speed (rev/min = rpm) **Step 2 — Solve for Spindle Speed** Rearranging for $N$: $$ \begin{aligned} \pi \cdot D \cdot N &= V \\ N &= \frac{V}{\pi \cdot D} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Numerical Example** A CNC lathe is turning a shaft. As the tool moves from diameter $D_1 = 100$ mm toward $D_2 = 50$ mm, with constant surface speed $V = 200$ m/min: At $D_1 = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m: $$ N_1 = \frac{200}{\pi \times 0.1} = \frac{200}{0.3142} \approx 636.6 \text{ rpm} $$ At $D_2 = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m: $$ N_2 = \frac{200}{\pi \times 0.05} = \frac{200}{0.1571} \approx 1273.2 \text{ rpm} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inference:** As diameter *decreases*, spindle RPM *increases* to maintain constant surface speed — which is why G96 mode requires a **G50 spindle speed clamp** to prevent the chuck from spinning dangerously fast at very small diameters. **Step 4 — Maximum RPM Clamp (G50)** $$ N_{max} = \frac{V}{\pi \cdot D_{min}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Code Selection by Operation Type | Manufacturing Operation | Recommended Codes | Reason | |-------------------------|-------------------|--------| | Drilling deep holes (depth > 5×D) | G81 / G83 + G04 | Peck drilling + dwell for chip evacuation | | Profile milling (closed pocket) | G01, G02, G03, G41/G42 | Linear/circular motion with cutter comp | | External turning (roughing) | G96, G95, G01 | Constant surface speed + feed per rev | | Facing operation | G96, G01, G00 | CSS with rapid retract | | Thread cutting (lathe) | G32 / G76, G97 | Synchronous feed; CSS off | | Tool change (multi-tool job) | M06, T_ | Automatic tool change sequence | | High-speed finishing | G05 + G41 | High-speed mode with cutter comp | --- # 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — G-Code Execution Flow in CNC Controller ```mermaid flowchart TD A[NC Part Program Block Input]:::input --> B[Lexical Parser] B --> C{Block Identifier}:::decision C -- "G-word detected" --> D[G-Code Interpreter] C -- "M-word detected" --> E[M-Code Interpreter] C -- "Other words: X Y Z F S T" --> F[Axis / Feed / Spindle Register] D --> G{Modal or Non-Modal}:::decision G -- "Modal" --> H[Persistent State Register] G -- "Non-Modal" --> I[Single Block Action] E --> J[Auxiliary Output Driver] H --> K[Motion Controller - Interpolator] F --> K I --> K J --> L[Machine Actuators] K --> L H --> M[Feedback to State Register] M --> A classDef input fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 G-Code Group Architecture ```mermaid graph LR A[G-Code Universe]:::root --> B[Group 01
Motion]:::grp A --> C[Group 02
Plane Selection]:::grp A --> D[Group 03
Distance Mode]:::grp A --> E[Group 05
Feed Rate Mode]:::grp A --> F[Group 06
Units]:::grp A --> G[Group 07
Cutter Comp]:::grp A --> H[Group 08
Tool Length]:::grp A --> I[Group 09
Canned Cycles]:::grp B --> B1[G00 Rapid]:::leaf B --> B2[G01 Linear]:::leaf B --> B3[G02 CW Arc]:::leaf B --> B4[G03 CCW Arc]:::leaf D --> D1[G90 Absolute]:::leaf D --> D2[G91 Incremental]:::leaf E --> E1[G94 mm/min]:::leaf E --> E2[G95 mm/rev]:::leaf F --> F1[G20 Inch]:::leaf F --> F2[G21 Metric]:::leaf classDef root fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:3px classDef grp fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef leaf fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — M-Code Auxiliary Function Dispatch ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User/Program participant C as CNC Controller participant S as Spindle Drive participant T as Tool Magazine participant CO as Coolant Valve participant CH as Chuck Actuator U->>C: N10 M06 T03 (Tool Change) C->>T: Rotate to T03 position T-->>C: Tool ready C->>U: Acknowledge U->>C: N20 M08 M03 S1500 C->>CO: Open flood coolant valve C->>S: Start spindle CW at 1500 rpm U->>C: N100 M09 M05 (End of cut) C->>CO: Close coolant valve C->>S: Stop spindle U->>C: N200 M30 C->>U: Program end + rewind ``` --- # 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define preparatory codes (G-codes) and miscellaneous codes (M-codes). Differentiate between them with two examples each.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Preparatory Codes (G-codes):** These are functional codes that prepare the CNC machine for a specific type of motion or operation by setting the controller's mode of operation. They remain *modal* unless overridden. > - *Example 1:* G01 — Linear interpolation at feed rate. > - *Example 2:* G02 — Circular interpolation in clockwise direction. > > **Miscellaneous Codes (M-codes):** These are auxiliary codes that control ON/OFF functions of machine accessories like spindle, coolant, and tool changer. They are typically non-continuous functions. > - *Example 1:* M03 — Spindle ON clockwise. > - *Example 2:* M08 — Flood coolant ON. > > **Key Difference:** G-codes define *how* the machine should move (motion-related and modal); M-codes define *what supporting functions* should occur (auxiliary and often one-shot). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the difference between modal and non-modal G-codes with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** > **Modal G-codes:** Once programmed, they remain active for all subsequent blocks until canceled by another code from the same group. Examples: G00, G01, G90, G94. > > **Non-Modal G-codes:** Effective only in the block in which they are written; the controller reverts to the previously active modal code in the next block. Examples: G04 (dwell), G28 (return to home). > > Example: After `N30 G01 F100;`, every subsequent motion block will continue at feed rate 100 mm/min until another G-code from Group 01 (like G00) cancels it. In contrast, `N40 G04 X2;` causes a 2-second dwell *only in block N40*. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks) --- ### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** > > **(a)** Explain the following preparatory codes with one example of each: > (i) G00, (ii) G01, (iii) G02, (iv) G03, (v) G04, (vi) G90, (vii) G91. **[7 Marks]** > > **(b)** A CNC milling machine is to machine a circular arc with the following data: > - Start point: (20, 30) > - Center of arc: (50, 30) > - End point: (50, 60) > - Direction: Counter-clockwise (CCW) > - Feed rate: 100 mm/min > > Write the NC block(s) to perform this operation. Compute the radius and arc length. **[7 Marks]** #### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A(b): **Step 1 — Identify I, J vectors** $$ \begin{aligned} I &= X_c - X_s = 50 - 20 = 30 \\ J &= Y_c - Y_s = 30 - 30 = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Identify the G-code** Since the motion is **CCW**, use **G03**. **Step 3 — Verify the arc** Distance from center $(50, 30)$ to end $(50, 60)$: $$ R_{verify} = \sqrt{(50-50)^2 + (60-30)^2} = 30 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 4 — Write the NC Block** ``` N40 G03 X50.0 Y60.0 I30.0 J0.0 F100 ; ``` **Step 5 — Compute Arc Length** The arc goes from start $(20,30)$ to end $(50,60)$ with center at $(50,30)$. The arc spans $90°$ (quarter circle). $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ radians} \\ L &= R \cdot \theta = 30 \times \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 47.12 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Compute Machining Time** $$ T = \frac{L}{f} = \frac{47.12}{100} \approx 0.471 \text{ min} \approx 28.3 \text{ sec} $$ --- ### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** > > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the CNC part programming structure. List any **ten** M-codes with their functions. **[7 Marks]** > > **(b)** A CNC lathe is set in **G96 mode** with constant surface speed $V = 250$ m/min. Compute the spindle RPM at: > - (i) Diameter $D_1 = 200$ mm > - (ii) Diameter $D_2 = 50$ mm > > Also determine the maximum allowable RPM if the chuck safety limit is 3000 rpm. Suggest the appropriate G-code to enforce this limit. **[7 Marks]** #### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B(b): **Step 1 — Recall the Surface Speed Equation** $$ N = \frac{V}{\pi \cdot D} $$ **Step 2 — Convert Diameters to Meters** $D_1 = 0.2$ m, $D_2 = 0.05$ m, $V = 250$ m/min. **Step 3 — Compute RPM at D₁** $$ N_1 = \frac{250}{\pi \times 0.2} = \frac{250}{0.6283} \approx 397.9 \text{ rpm} $$ **Step 4 — Compute RPM at D₂** $$ N_2 = \frac{250}{\pi \times 0.05} = \frac{250}{0.1571} \approx 1591.5 \text{ rpm} $$ **Step 5 — Compare to Safety Limit** Both values are below 3000 rpm, so safe. **Step 6 — Maximum RPM Clamp** At $D_{min} = 0.01$ m (10 mm): $$ N_{max} = \frac{250}{\pi \times 0.01} \approx 7957.7 \text{ rpm} $$ This exceeds 3000 rpm — hence a clamp is needed. **Step 7 — Recommended Code** ``` N10 G96 S250 ; N20 G50 S3000 ; (RPM clamp at 3000 rpm) ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Using **I** and **J** as *absolute* coordinates instead of *incremental* vectors from start to center. Marks are deducted heavily. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **G02 (CW)** and **G03 (CCW)** — the direction is determined by looking *along the negative Z-axis* (toward the workpiece from above for milling). Drawing a quick sketch before coding avoids this. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting units — when D is in mm but V is in m/min, **convert D to meters first**, otherwise the numerical answer is off by a factor of 1000. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing G96 without G50 clamp — many students lose 2 marks here for missing the safety implication. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **G-codes** = Preparatory functions (motion/mode setting); **M-codes** = Miscellaneous (auxiliary ON/OFF). - ✅ **Modal codes** persist across blocks; **Non-modal codes** (e.g., G04, G28) are one-shot. - ✅ **G00 = Rapid (non-cutting)**; **G01 = Linear at feed F**; **G02 = CW arc**; **G03 = CCW arc**. - ✅ **I, J, K** in arc blocks = *incremental* vectors from arc start to arc center (NOT absolute coordinates). - ✅ **G90 = Absolute** (from work zero); **G91 = Incremental** (from current position). - ✅ **G94 = mm/min** (milling); **G95 = mm/rev** (turning); **G96 = Constant Surface Speed** (lathe with G50 RPM clamp). - ✅ **G41 = Tool LEFT of path**; **G42 = Tool RIGHT of path**; **G40 = Cancel cutter comp.** - ✅ **M03/M04/M05** = Spindle CW/CCW/STOP. **M08** = Flood coolant ON. **M30** = Program end + rewind. - ✅ **Surface speed formula:** $V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ — always use **consistent units** (D in meters if V is m/min). - ✅ Only **one G-code per group** allowed in a single NC block; **multiple M-codes** can coexist. - ✅ **Drawing a quick coordinate sketch** before writing circular interpolation blocks avoids 90% of G02/G03 errors.

topics.title: Detailed manual part programming for turning and milling operations using G codes and M codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Detailed manual part programming for turning and milling operations using G codes and M codes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Manual Part Programming (Handwritten NC Code)**: In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus for CAD/CAM/CAE, *manual part programming* refers to the direct, line-by-line textual composition of numerical control (NC) instructions (G-codes, M-codes, and address parameters) by a programmer using a standard **ISO 6983** (RS-274) or **DIN 66025** compliant language, executed on a CNC turning center or machining center. It is the foundational prerequisite for understanding APT-based and conversational programming.

The *G-code* (Preparatory functions, address letter **G**) defines the **geometry of motion** (linear, circular, plane selection, canned cycles), while the *M-code* (Miscellaneous functions, address letter **M**) governs **non-geometric machine actions** (spindle on/off, coolant, tool change, program end).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must master the *complete set of G-codes (G00–G99)* relevant to **turning (2-axis lathes)** and **milling (3-axis VMC/HMC)**, including *canned cycles* (G90, G92, G94 for turning; G81–G89 for milling), *cutter compensation* (G40–G42), and *modal vs. non-modal command classification*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Recipe" Intuition)

Imagine you are instructing a *blind master chef* in a kitchen where everything is pre-measured to the millimetre:

- The **G-codes** are the *cooking verbs*: "Stir" (linear cut), "Whisk in a circle" (circular interpolation), "Boil for 5 minutes" (dwell G04).
- The **M-codes** are the *kitchen utilities*: "Turn on the stove" (M03 – spindle CW), "Turn on the exhaust" (M08 – coolant), "Change the spatula" (M06 – tool change).
- The **X, Y, Z coordinates** are the *exact bowl positions* on the counter.
- The **F value** is the *speed* of the chef's hand.
- The **S value** is the *stove flame intensity* (spindle RPM).

Just as you cannot bake a cake by mixing instructions randomly, a CNC program must follow a **strict sequence** to machine a part safely and dimensionally accurately.

## 1.3 Coordinate System & Standard Datum (KTU Mandatory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **Turning Center**, only **X** and **Z** axes are standard. **X** is the radial axis (diameter-based by default on most lathes — `G20`/`G21` rule), and **Z** is the longitudinal axis along the spindle centerline. **Y** does not exist.

For a **Machining Center (Milling)**, **X** (long), **Y** (cross), and **Z** (vertical — spindle axis) define a **right-hand-rule Cartesian system** with the origin typically at the **Machine Zero (M)** located at the spindle home position; the **Part Zero (G54–G59)** is set at the top-left corner of the stock.

## 1.4 Standardized Address Letter Convention (ISO 6983)

| Address Letter | Meaning (Turning/Milling) | Example |
|----------------|--------------------------|---------|
| **N** | Sequence/Block number | `N10` |
| **G** | Preparatory function | `G01` |
| **X, Y, Z** | Linear axis coordinates | `X50.0` |
| **I, J, K** | Arc center offsets (incremental from start) | `I20.0` |
| **R** | Arc radius / Canned cycle retract | `R5.0` |
| **F** | Feed rate (mm/min or mm/rev) | `F0.2` |
| **S** | Spindle speed (RPM) | `S1500` |
| **T** | Tool selection | `T0101` |
| **M** | Miscellaneous function | `M03` |
| **D, H** | Cutter/Length compensation register | `D01` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modal vs. Non-Modal Commands — The "Sticky Switch" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Modal (Default-Staying)**: Once a modal G-code is issued, it remains active for *all subsequent blocks* until another code in the *same modal group* overrides it. Example: `G01` remains active; you only need to write `X...` to keep cutting linearly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Modal (One-Shot)**: It executes only in the block it appears in. Example: `G04` (Dwell), `G28` (Return to home), `G92` (set coordinate — turning).

## 2.2 Major G-Code Groups (Modal Classification)

| Modal Group | Turning Codes | Milling Codes | Function |
|-------------|---------------|---------------|----------|
| **Motion** | G00, G01, G02, G03 | G00, G01, G02, G03 | Rapid, Linear, CW Arc, CCW Arc |
| **Plane Selection** | N/A (XZ fixed) | G17, G18, G19 | XY, XZ, YZ plane |
| **Units** | G20 (in), G21 (mm) | G20, G21 | Inch / Metric |
| **Programming Mode** | G90 (Abs), G91 (Inc) | G90, G91 | Absolute / Incremental |
| **Feed Mode** | G94 (mm/min), G95 (mm/rev) | G94, G95 | Per-minute / Per-revolution |
| **Compensation** | G40, G41, G42 | G40, G41, G42 | Cutter comp. cancel/left/right |
| **Canned Cycles** | G90, G92, G94 (turning); G77–G78 (some controllers) | G73, G74, G76, G81–G89 | Drilling/turning cycles |
| **Return to Ref.** | G28 | G28 | Return to machine zero |
| **Threading** | G32, G33, G76 | — | Constant lead threading |

## 2.3 Major M-Code Reference (Industry + KTU Standard)

| M-Code | Function | Typical Use |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| **M00** | Program Stop (unconditional) | Operator inspection, chip clearing |
| **M01** | Optional Stop (requires operator switch) | Inspection checkpoints |
| **M02** | Program End (no rewind) | End of program |
| **M03** | Spindle ON, Clockwise | Standard milling/turning |
| **M04** | Spindle ON, Counter-clockwise | Reverse direction cuts |
| **M05** | Spindle OFF | End of cutting |
| **M06** | Automatic Tool Change (ATC) | Milling — load T-code from spindle |
| **M08** | Coolant ON (flood) | Heavy cutting |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF | Before tool change, end |
| **M10** | Chuck / Clamp ON | Turning — close chuck |
| **M11** | Chuck / Clamp OFF | Turning — open chuck |
| **M30** | Program End + Rewind to start | Standard end block on most CNCs |
| **M98** | Subprogram Call | `M98 P1000` (call O1000) |
| **M99** | Subprogram Return / Loop | End of subprogram |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cutting Parameter Engineering)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas are **repeatedly tested in KTU ESE** for deriving program block parameters. Memorize in this exact form.

**1. Spindle Speed (N) in RPM:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D}$$

where $V_c$ is the cutting speed in m/min, and $D$ is the cutter/workpiece diameter in mm.

**2. Linear Feed Rate ($f_m$) in mm/min:**

$$f_m = N \times f_r$$

where $f_r$ is the feed per revolution in mm/rev (turning/drilling).

**3. Feed Rate in mm/min for Milling (using feed per tooth $f_z$):**

$$f_m = N \times Z_t \times f_z$$

where $Z_t$ is the number of teeth on the cutter.

**4. Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$\text{MRR} = v \times f \times d \quad \text{(Turning, cm}^3\text{/min if $v$ in m/min, $f$ in mm/rev, $d$ in mm)}$$

$$\text{MRR} = f_m \times a_p \times a_e \quad \text{(Milling, mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

**5. Cutting Time ($t_c$):**

$$t_c = \frac{L + L_a + L_o}{f_m} \quad \text{(Milling)}$$

$$t_c = \frac{L}{f_r \times N} = \frac{L}{f_m} \quad \text{(Turning)}$$

where $L_a$ = approach distance, $L_o$ = overtravel, $L$ = cut length.

**6. Power Required ($P$):**

$$P = \frac{\text{MRR} \times k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

where $k_c$ is the specific cutting force in N/mm², and $\eta$ is the machine efficiency (typically **0.80–0.90**).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In KTU-affiliated **Kerala industrial clusters (Cochin Shipyard, HMT, BrahMos integration units)**, manual G-code programming is the daily tool for **one-off job-shop production**, **tool-room jobs**, **fixture and gauge manufacturing**, and **legacy machine retrofitting** where CAM software licensing is uneconomical. Mastering this module is the prerequisite for FANUC/FAGMA/Siemens certification, which Kerala PSC and DRDO grade-II exams test directly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Turning (Lathe) Part Program Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Standard Format):**
> Write a manual part program to turn the following mild-steel workpiece on a CNC lathe:
> - **Raw stock:** Ø60 mm × 100 mm long.
> - **Finish diameters:** Ø50 mm (length 40 mm from face), Ø45 mm (length 30 mm next).
> - **Facing:** 2 mm from the face.
> - **Tool:** T01 — rough turning (0.8 mm nose radius), insert CNMG 120408.
> - **Cutting data:** $V_c = 180$ m/min, $f_r = 0.25$ mm/rev, depth of cut $d = 2$ mm.

### Step 3.1.1 — Compute Spindle Speed (N)

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D} = \frac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 60}$$

$$N = \frac{180000}{188.4956} = 954.93 \text{ RPM}$$

> Rounding to the nearest standard CNC value: **S950** (use `S1000` if controller accepts only integer decades; KTU accepts either with justification).

### Step 3.1.2 — Compute Feed Rate (mm/rev → mm/min via modal G95)

Since we want **feed per revolution** (G95), we keep $f_r = 0.25$ mm/rev.

If we used G94, the linear feed would be:

$$f_m = N \times f_r = 950 \times 0.25 = 237.5 \text{ mm/min} \approx 240 \text{ mm/min}$$

### Step 3.1.3 — Identify Coordinates Using Part Zero at the Face-Center

- **Part Zero (G50 X60 Z60 in FANUC turning convention — sets max stock coordinate).**
- The face is at **Z0** (after facing). The face machined at Ø58 mm: 2 mm step.
- **First pass — face:** From (X60, Z2) → (X0, Z2) → (X0, Z0) to clean the face.
- **Second pass — rough turn Ø50 to length 40:** Approach (X52, Z2) → straight to (X52, Z-40) → retract to (X60, Z2).
- **Third pass — turn Ø45 to length 70 mm (40 + 30):** Approach (X47, Z2) → cut to (X47, Z-70) → retract.

### Step 3.1.4 — Construct the Manual Program Block-by-Block

```text
O0001 (TURNING PROGRAM — KTU EXAMPLE)
N10 G21 G90 G95          (Metric, Absolute, Feed per rev)
N20 G50 X100 Z100         (Set machine envelope — Fanuc convention)
N30 M03 S950              (Spindle CW at 950 RPM)
N40 T0101 M08             (Tool 1, comp 1, coolant ON)
N50 G00 X62.0 Z2.0        (Rapid to start — 2 mm clearance)
N60 G01 X-1.0 Z2.0 F0.25  (Face cut — cross to center)
N70 G00 X62.0 Z2.0        (Retract)
N80 G01 X52.0 Z-40.0      (Rough turn Ø50 to Z-40)
N90 G00 X62.0             (Retract radially)
N100 Z2.0                  (Retract longitudinally)
N110 G01 X47.0 Z-70.0     (Turn Ø45 to Z-70)
N120 G00 X62.0             (Retract)
N130 Z2.0                  (Retract to start)
N140 G00 X100.0 Z100.0    (Rapid to tool-change position)
N150 M09                   (Coolant OFF)
N160 M05                   (Spindle OFF)
N170 M30                   (Program end + rewind)
```

### Step 3.1.5 — Verification of Cutting Time

For the first turning pass (length = 40 mm):

$$t_{c1} = \frac{L}{f_m} = \frac{40}{237.5} = 0.1684 \text{ min} \approx 10.1 \text{ seconds}$$

This satisfies the KTU examiner's expectation that you show the **time calculation to justify the program**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Milling Part Program with Canned Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Drill 4 holes of Ø10 mm, depth 25 mm on a 100 × 80 × 20 mm mild-steel plate. Hole pattern: rectangular, 60 × 40 mm pitch, with the lower-left hole at (15, 15). Use G81 canned drilling cycle.

### Step 3.2.1 — Compute Spindle Speed and Feed

Drill Ø10, HSS drill, mild steel → $V_c = 30$ m/min, $f_r = 0.15$ mm/rev.

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 10} = \frac{30000}{31.4159} = 954.93 \approx 1000 \text{ RPM}$$

$$f_m = 1000 \times 0.15 = 150 \text{ mm/min} \quad (\text{or keep G95 at F0.15})$$

### Step 3.2.2 — Coordinate Table

| Hole | X | Y |
|------|---|---|
| H1 | 15 | 15 |
| H2 | 75 | 15 |
| H3 | 15 | 55 |
| H4 | 75 | 55 |

### Step 3.2.3 — Manual Program Construction

```text
O0002 (MILLING — DRILLING CYCLE — KTU EXAMPLE)
N10 G21 G90 G94 G17      (Metric, Absolute, mm/min, XY plane)
N20 G54 X0 Y0 Z0         (Set part zero at lower-left top of stock)
N30 M03 S1000            (Spindle CW at 1000 RPM)
N40 T01 M06              (Load Drill Ø10, Tool change)
N50 M08                  (Coolant ON)
N60 G00 X15.0 Y15.0      (Rapid to Hole 1)
N70 G43 H01 Z5.0         (Apply tool length comp, approach 5 mm above)
N80 G81 R2.0 Z-25.0 F150 (Canned drill: R-point=2 above, Z=-25, F=150)
N90 X75.0 Y15.0          (Hole 2 — G81 still modal)
N100 X15.0 Y55.0         (Hole 3)
N110 X75.0 Y55.0         (Hole 4)
N120 G80 G00 Z50.0       (Cancel canned cycle, retract to safe Z)
N130 M09                 (Coolant OFF)
N140 M05                 (Spindle OFF)
N150 G91 G28 Z0.0        (Return Z to machine zero, incremental)
N160 G28 X0.0 Y0.0       (Return XY to machine zero)
N170 M30                 (End + Rewind)
```

### Step 3.2.4 — Verify Cutting Time (one hole)

Approach distance = 5 mm (G00); rapid part ignored in cutting time.

$$t_{c,\text{one hole}} = \frac{(25 + 2 + 3)}{150} = \frac{30}{150} = 0.2 \text{ min} = 12 \text{ seconds}$$

(2 mm clearance above part + 3 mm breakthrough below Z=−25.)

Total drilling time (4 holes):

$$T_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 0.2 = 0.8 \text{ min} = 48 \text{ seconds}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which G-Code (Decision Logic)

| Engineering Need | Correct G/M-Code | Why |
|------------------|------------------|-----|
| Move tool from home to start position without cutting | **G00** | Rapid traverse; bypasses feed override |
| Perform a straight cut at the programmed feed | **G01** | Linear interpolation with F-override active |
| Mill a full circle clockwise viewed from +Z | **G02** | CW circular interpolation in the active plane |
| Drill a single hole with retract after each | **G81** | Simple drilling canned cycle |
| Drill a deep hole >3× diameter, with pecking | **G83** | Deep-hole peck drilling cycle |
| Tap a thread synchronously | **G84** | Tapping cycle (rigid/spindle-sync) |
| Bore a hole and dwell at the bottom | **G82** | Counter-boring with dwell |
| Thread turning on a lathe (single pass) | **G32** or **G92** | Constant-lead thread cutting |
| Multiple-pass turning cycle | **G90** (OD/ID) | Multi-repetitive turning cycle |
| Face turning multi-pass | **G94** | Multi-repetitive facing cycle |
| Apply tool radius offset left of cut direction | **G41** | Cutter compensation left |
| Cancel tool length offset (manual change) | **G49** | Reset tool length comp. |
| Run a subprogram 5 times | **M98 P0005** or **L5** | Subprogram call with repeat count |
| Pause spindle, stop program for inspection | **M00** | Unconditional program stop |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation of Cutter Path for a Circular Pocket (G02/G03)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Mill a circular pocket Ø30 mm at center (50, 50), depth 5 mm, climb milling, on the XY plane.

**Decision logic:**
- Active plane = `G17` (XY)
- Direction: Climb milling (against tool rotation) for a full internal circle on top of stock → conventionally uses **CCW** rotation (which for a *top view* is `G02` if spindle is `M03` CW, because the *chip load* and *tool rotation vector* interplay gives climb as G02; if the controller uses Fanuc convention, G02 = CW = conventional on outside cuts, CCW on inside cuts; this is controller-specific, so **always draw and verify**).

**Coordinate computation:**
- Start point: (X35, Y50) — 5 mm clearance on the +X side.
- End point: (X35, Y50) — full circle.
- Center offset (I, J) from start to center:

$$I = X_{center} - X_{start} = 50 - 35 = 15.0$$

$$J = Y_{center} - Y_{start} = 50 - 50 = 0.0$$

**Block (assuming G17, G90, climb — G02 in this case):**

```text
G02 X35.0 Y50.0 I15.0 J0.0 Z-5.0 F100
```

> **KTU Pitfall:** Students forget that `I, J, K` are *always incremental* (distance from start point of arc to its center), while `X, Y, Z` are *absolute* (when G90 is active). Mixing these loses marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an NC Program Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start M30 or %] --> B[Initialization Block G21 G90 G17]
    B --> C[Set Coordinate System G54 G55 etc.]
    C --> D[Tool Change T01 M06]
    D --> E[Spindle ON M03 S Value]
    E --> F[Coolant ON M08]
    F --> G[Rapid Move G00 to start point]
    G --> H{Cutting Operation Type}
    H --> H1[G01 Linear Interpolation F]
    H --> H2[G02 G03 Circular Interpolation]
    H --> H3[G81 G83 Canned Drilling Cycle]
    H1 --> I[Continue Path or End Cut]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Retract G00 Z safe]
    J --> K[Coolant OFF M09]
    K --> L[Spindle OFF M05]
    L --> M[Tool Return G28 X0 Y0 Z0]
    M --> N[Program End M30]
    N --> O[Rewind to Program Head]
    O --> P[Standby for Next Cycle]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right G-Code for Milling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: What Cut Do You Need?] --> S1{Through Hole or Blind Hole?}
    S1 -- Through Hole --> S2{Depth Greater than 3x Diameter?}
    S1 -- Blind Hole --> S3[Use G82 Counter Bore with Dwell]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Use G83 Deep Hole Peck Drill]
    S2 -- No --> S5[Use G81 Simple Drill Cycle]
    S --> S6{Need Internal Thread?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Use G84 Tapping Cycle]
    S --> S8{Need Circular Slot or Pocket?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Use G02 or G03 Circular Interp]
    S --> S10{Need Linear Slot?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Use G01 with multiple passes]
    S --> S12{Need Multi Hole Pattern?}
    S12 -- Yes --> S13[Loop using M98 Subprogram or Use G81 Modal]
```

## 4.3 Program Block Structure — Anatomy of a Typical Turning Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BLOCK[Typical NC Block Structure]
    N[N Sequence Number] --> G[G Code G01 G02 etc]
    G --> X[X Coordinate]
    G --> Z[Z Coordinate]
    X --> I[I Arc Center Offset]
    Z --> F[Feed Rate F]
    I --> S[Spindle S]
    F --> T[Tool T]
    S --> M[M Code M03 M08 etc]
    T --> M
    end
```

## 4.4 Tool Path Visualization — Concept Diagram (Turning a Stepped Shaft)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PART[Stepped Shaft Machining]
    ST[Start at Stock Dia 60] --> FAC[Facing Pass X60 to X0 at Z0]
    FAC --> R1[Rapid Return to X62 Z2]
    R1 --> T1[Turn Pass 1 X52 Z-40 Diameter 50]
    T1 --> R2[Rapid Return to X62]
    R2 --> T2[Turn Pass 2 X47 Z-70 Diameter 45]
    T2 --> R3[Rapid Return to Tool Change]
    R3 --> END[M30 Program End]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Failing Mistake #1 (Deducts 2–3 marks):** Writing `G00` for a *cutting* move. G00 is **non-cutting**; a programmed feed during cutting **must use G01** with an F value.
> - **Failing Mistake #2 (Deducts 2 marks):** Forgetting the *modal group logic*. If you issue `G01` then write `G02`, G02 will override G01, but if you then omit motion type and only write `X Y`, the controller will assume **G02 is still active**, which is the *#1 reason for part scrap and KTU program-evaluation errors*.
> - **Failing Mistake #3 (Deducts 1 mark):** Not specifying the **plane (G17/G18/G19)** for circular interpolation in milling — circular arcs without a plane selection return a controller alarm.
> - **Failing Mistake #4 (Deducts 2 marks):** Writing `M30` *before* `M09` and `M05`. Standard KTU convention expects: `M09 → M05 → G28 → M30`. Always cool off, stop spindle, home, then rewind.
> - **Failing Mistake #5 (Deducts 2 marks):** Mixing `G90` and `G91` coordinates in the same block without justification. KTU expects either a *fully absolute* program or a *fully incremental* program unless explicit switching is demonstrated.

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any six M-codes with their functions as used in CNC turning or milling.*

**Model Answer (6 codes — write any 6):**
1. **M00** — Program Stop (unconditional halt, spindle stop, coolant stop).
2. **M03** — Spindle ON, Clockwise rotation.
3. **M05** — Spindle OFF.
4. **M06** — Automatic Tool Change.
5. **M08** — Coolant (flood) ON.
6. **M09** — Coolant OFF.
7. **M30** — Program End and Rewind.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between G00 and G01 codes.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | G00 | G01 |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Function | Rapid positioning (point-to-point) | Linear interpolation at feed rate |
| Cutting | Non-cutting (rapid traverse) | Cutting (uses F-rate) |
| Feed Override | Inactive / Locked at 100% | Active and adjustable |
| Accuracy | Goes via shortest path (controller-dependent) | Follows exact straight line |
| Use | Tool approach, retract, home moves | All straight milling/turning cuts |

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** A CNC turning operation requires machining a stepped shaft as shown below:
- **Step 1:** Turn from Ø50 mm to Ø40 mm for a length of 30 mm starting from the face.
- **Step 2:** Turn from Ø40 mm to Ø30 mm for a length of 25 mm more.
- **Cutting data:** $V_c = 200$ m/min, $f_r = 0.2$ mm/rev, mild steel. Tool T0202 (rough turning insert).
- **Stock:** Ø50 × 100 mm. Facing of 1 mm required at the start.
- Write the complete manual part program in ISO code with modal G-codes, M-codes, T-codes, and F/S calculation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between **absolute (G90)** and **incremental (G91)** programming modes. For the same set of four points P1(10,10), P2(40,10), P3(40,30), P4(10,30) connected in sequence, write the **incremental program blocks** assuming the tool starts at (0,0). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Turning Program

**Step 1 — Calculate N and F:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 200}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{200000}{157.08} = 1273.2 \text{ RPM} \approx S1300$$

**Step 2 — Feed (modal G95):** F0.2 mm/rev → F = 0.2.

**Step 3 — Program:**

```text
O0003 (STEPPED SHAFT — KTU EXAM)
N10 G21 G90 G95
N20 M03 S1300
N30 T0202
N40 G00 X52.0 Z2.0          (Rapid to start 2 mm above face)
N50 G01 X-1.0 F0.2          (Face cut, 1 mm past center to clean)
N60 G00 X52.0 Z2.0          (Retract to start)
N70 G01 X40.0 Z-30.0        (Turn Ø40 for length 30)
N80 G00 X52.0               (Radial retract)
N90 Z2.0                    (Back to start Z)
N100 G01 X30.0 Z-55.0       (Turn Ø30 for 25 mm more, total Z=-55)
N110 G00 X52.0              (Retract)
N120 Z2.0                   (Back to start)
N130 G00 X100.0 Z100.0      (Tool change position)
N140 M05
N150 M30
```

**Key Justifications to include in answer:**
- Why `G95` (feed per rev) → turning uses mm/rev for proper surface finish.
- Why `X-1.0` for facing → going past center guarantees a clean face on Ø50 stock.
- Why radial retract `X52.0` → avoids drag marks on the turned surface.

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Absolute vs Incremental

**Definitions:**
- **G90 (Absolute):** All coordinates are referenced to the **program zero (part zero)**. Each point is given in *true* coordinates.
- **G91 (Incremental):** All coordinates are referenced to the **previous tool position**. The block gives the *distance to move* from where you are.

**Incremental Program (G91) for square P1→P2→P3→P4→P1 starting from (0,0):**

| Block | Motion | ΔX | ΔY | Command |
|-------|--------|----|----|---------|
| Start | Tool at (0,0) | — | — | `G91 G01 X10.0 Y10.0 F100` → reaches P1 |
| N2 | P1 → P2 | 30 | 0 | `G01 X30.0 Y0.0` |
| N3 | P2 → P3 | 0 | 20 | `G01 X0.0 Y20.0` |
| N4 | P3 → P4 | −30 | 0 | `G01 X-30.0 Y0.0` |
| N5 | P4 → P1 | 0 | −20 | `G01 X0.0 Y-20.0` |

Complete block listing:

```text
N10 G21 G91 G94
N20 G90 G00 X0 Y0 (move to start at origin in absolute first)
N30 G91 G01 X10.0 Y10.0 F100
N40 X30.0 Y0.0
N50 X0.0 Y20.0
N60 X-30.0 Y0.0
N70 X0.0 Y-20.0
N80 M30
```

> **Marking note:** Full marks require both the **definition (2 marks)** and the **incremental coordinate table / program (5 marks)**. State the rule: *Δ = Current − Previous*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram and explanation, describe the **classification of G-codes** in CNC programming. List at least **three modal groups** with two example codes in each group. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC vertical machining center is to drill **6 holes of Ø12 mm, 30 mm deep** on a circular pitch circle of Ø80 mm diameter. The first hole is at 0° (along +X axis from center at 50,50). The holes are equally spaced at 60° apart. Use the **G81 canned drilling cycle** and **G98/G99 return level** with R = 2 mm. Write the complete part program. Use $V_c = 25$ m/min and $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth for a 2-flute HSS drill. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a) — G-Code Classification

**Block Diagram (text form, replicate in answer sheet):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[G-Codes ISO 6983] --> B[Modal Codes Stay active until changed]
    A --> C[Non-Modal Codes Active in one block only]
    B --> B1[Group 1 Motion G00 G01 G02 G03]
    B --> B2[Group 2 Plane G17 G18 G19]
    B --> B3[Group 3 Units G20 G21]
    B --> B4[Group 6 Measurement G90 G91]
    C --> C1[G04 Dwell]
    C --> C2[G28 Return Home]
    C --> C3[G92 Set Coordinate]
```

**Three modal groups with examples:**
1. **Motion Group:** `G00` (rapid), `G01` (linear cut), `G02` (CW arc), `G03` (CCW arc).
2. **Plane Selection Group:** `G17` (XY), `G18` (XZ), `G19` (YZ).
3. **Programming Mode Group:** `G90` (absolute), `G91` (incremental).

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 6-Hole Circular Pattern

**Step 1 — Calculate N and F:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 12} = \frac{25000}{37.699} = 663.15 \text{ RPM} \approx S650$$

$$f_m = N \times Z_t \times f_z = 650 \times 2 \times 0.05 = 65 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 2 — Hole coordinates (center 50, 50, radius 40, starting 0°):**

| Hole | Angle | X | Y |
|------|-------|---|---|
| H1 | 0° | 90.0 | 50.0 |
| H2 | 60° | 50 + 40·cos60 = 70.0 | 50 + 40·sin60 = 84.64 |
| H3 | 120° | 50 + 40·cos120 = 30.0 | 50 + 40·sin120 = 84.64 |
| H4 | 180° | 10.0 | 50.0 |
| H5 | 240° | 30.0 | 50 + 40·sin240 = 15.36 |
| H6 | 300° | 70.0 | 15.36 |

**Step 3 — Program:**

```text
O0006 (KTU 6-HOLE CIRCULAR PATTERN)
N10 G21 G90 G94 G17
N20 G54 X0 Y0 Z0
N30 M03 S650
N40 T01 M06
N50 M08
N60 G00 X90.0 Y50.0      (Hole 1)
N70 G43 H01 Z5.0
N80 G98 G81 R2.0 Z-30.0 F65
N90 X70.0 Y84.64         (Hole 2)
N100 X30.0 Y84.64        (Hole 3)
N110 X10.0 Y50.0         (Hole 4)
N120 X30.0 Y15.36        (Hole 5)
N130 X70.0 Y15.36        (Hole 6)
N140 G80 G00 Z50.0
N150 M09 M05
N160 G91 G28 Z0
N170 G28 X0 Y0
N180 M30
```

> **Key Justifications to include:**
> - `G98` is used (return to **initial level** Z=5) so that the next rapid move clears the workpiece safely between holes.
> - If `G99` were used, the tool would return only to `R=2`, which is unsafe if any fixture or boss is in the way — for a flat plate, both are valid; KTU prefers `G98` for clarity.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Reference Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Revision):**
> - **G00** = rapid (no cutting), **G01** = linear cut, **G02** = CW arc, **G03** = CCW arc.
> - **G17/G18/G19** = XY/XZ/YZ plane selection (mandatory before any circular interp in milling).
> - **G20/G21** = inch/metric; default is G21 in KTU lab machines (mm).
> - **G40** = cutter comp OFF; **G41** = left; **G42** = right.
> - **G43 H01** = tool length compensation + (always use with H register, never bare Z).
> - **G54–G59** = work-coordinate systems (fixture offsets), set at part zero.
> - **G81** = simple drill, **G82** = drill + dwell, **G83** = deep-hole peck, **G84** = tap, **G85** = bore.
> - **G90** = absolute, **G91** = incremental; **G94** = mm/min, **G95** = mm/rev.
> - **M03/M04/M05** = spindle CW/CCW/OFF; **M06** = tool change (milling); **M08/M09** = coolant ON/OFF; **M30** = end + rewind.
> - Always **coolant OFF → spindle OFF → G28 home → M30** in that exact order.
> - **N, G, X, Y, Z, I, J, K, R, F, S, T, M** are the **13 standard ISO 6983 address letters** you must know cold.
> - **I, J, K** are *always incremental arc-center offsets*, **R** is the arc radius (or retract for canned cycles).
> - **Modal commands** stick; **non-modal** (G04, G28, G92, G80) are one-shot.
> - **Cutting speed formula** $N = 1000V_c/(\pi D)$ and **feed** $f_m = N \times f_r$ (turning) or $f_m = N \times Z_t \times f_z$ (milling) — both appear in nearly every KTU numerical.
> - Always state **part zero location** and **tool offset registration (D/H)** in your answer.
> - **Canned cycles** save block count; G81 modal means you only re-state X/Y for each subsequent hole.
> - For subprograms: `M98 Pxxxx` calls `Oxxxx`; `M99` returns.
> - **Plane selection rule:** G02/G03 *direction* (CW/CCW) is always **viewed from the positive normal axis** of the active plane (e.g., from +Z for G17, from +Y for G18, from +X for G19).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Canned cycles and subroutines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Canned cycles and subroutines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Canned Cycles — Formal Definition

In CNC (Computer Numerical Control) part programming, a **Canned Cycle** (also called a *fixed cycle* or *predefined cycle*) is a single-block command that consolidates a sequence of repetitive, multi-axis machining operations—such as rapid traverse, plunge, feed, dwell, and retract—into a compact, pre-engineered instruction. Each canned cycle follows the **modal format**:

$$G\text{__} \; X\_ \; Y\_ \; Z\_ \; R\_ \; F\_ \; K\_$$

where:
- **G-code** (e.g., G81, G83) selects the cycle type
- **X, Y** define the hole's centre coordinates
- **Z** defines the final hole depth (absolute)
- **R** defines the rapid reference (clearance) plane
- **F** is the cutting feedrate
- **K** is the repetition count (modal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Canned cycles fall under **Modular Programming in CNC** and are a guaranteed 14-mark question in Module-3 of PBPET504. Mastery of all nine standard G-codes (G73, G81–G89) is mandatory.

## 1.2 Subroutines — Formal Definition

A **Subroutine** is a self-contained block of part-program code, identified by a unique program number (e.g., O0100), that can be invoked any number of times from the main program (or another subroutine) using the call command **M98**, and terminated by the return command **M99**. Subroutines dramatically reduce code length for symmetrical or repetitive features.

**Call Format:**
$$M98 \; P\,\text{XXXXYYYY} \; [L\,\text{NNN}]$$
- **XXXX** = subroutine number (e.g., 0100)
- **YYYY** = sequence number inside the subroutine where execution begins (optional)
- **L NNN** = number of repetitions (default = 1)

**Return Format:**
$$M99 \; [P\,\text{YYYY}]$$
- Optionally returns control to a numbered sequence in the main program (Fanuc advanced)

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe Card" Model

Imagine you are a head chef in a professional kitchen with 100 identical tiffin boxes to fill, each requiring the same 5-step rice-serving sequence (move, scoop, pour, return, slide).

- **Without a canned cycle:** You would verbally repeat all 5 instructions for every single tiffin. 100 boxes × 5 commands = **500 commands**, exhausting you and inviting errors.
- **With a canned cycle (G81):** You define the 5-step sequence **once**, name it *"serve-rice"*, and simply call out *"serve-rice, position 2"*, *"serve-rice, position 3"*, and so on. **1 definition + 100 short calls = 101 lines**.

- **A subroutine is the master recipe card:** Written once on a laminated card (program O0100) and pinned in the kitchen. The head chef (main program) reads *"call O0100, repeat 100 times"*, and the kitchen executes perfectly, identically, every single time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** A typical aerospace part with 400 rivet holes programmed in *full G-code* would exceed 6,000 lines. The **same part using G81 + subroutines** compresses to under 250 lines — a 96% reduction in programmer effort and a massive drop in keystroke-induced bugs.

## 1.4 Modal vs. Non-Modal Nature

| Property | Canned Cycle | Subroutine |
|----------|--------------|------------|
| **Modal** | Yes (stays active until cancelled by G80) | No (returns after M99) |
| **Cancellation** | G80 explicitly cancels all cycles | Implicit after return |
| **Storage** | Internal to controller firmware | User-defined program (O-number) |
| **Nesting Allowed** | N/A | Yes (typically up to 4 levels on Fanuc) |

## 1.5 Group 09 — The Canned Cycle Family (G-Code Family Tree)

The KTU 2024 syllabus (and ISO 6983 / Fanuc standard) defines **Group 09** as the family of fixed cycles. There are **9 primary cycles** in the drilling family:

| Code | Cycle Name | Typical Use |
|------|------------|-------------|
| **G73** | High-Speed Peck Drilling | Shallow holes, brittle materials, chip-breaking |
| **G74** | Tapping (LH / LH Spindle) | Left-hand threads |
| **G76** | Fine Boring | Precision bore with shift-retract |
| **G81** | Drilling (Simple) | Through/shallow holes, fastest |
| **G82** | Spot Drilling / Counterbore | Spot face, with dwell for clean bottom |
| **G83** | Deep-Hole Peck Drilling | Deep holes ≥ 3×D, full retract for chip evacuation |
| **G84** | Tapping (RH Spindle) | Right-hand threads |
| **G85** | Boring (Feed In / Feed Out) | Reamed holes, smooth finish |
| **G86** | Boring (Feed In / Rapid Out, Spindle Stop) | Bore then stop for inspection |
| **G87** | Back Boring | Bore from back side of part |
| **G88** | Boring (Manual Retract) | Operator-handled retract |
| **G89** | Boring (Feed In / Dwell / Feed Out) | Bore with chip-breaking dwell |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Single Canned-Cycle Block — The 4 Motion Phases

Every canned cycle (G73–G89), despite their different behaviours at the bottom of the hole, performs a **standardised 4-phase motion sequence** once it is activated:

1. **Phase 1 — Initial Point to R-Plane (RAPID):** From the current tool position, the controller commands rapid traverse to the **R-point** (reference plane) directly above the hole's X, Y location. This is the *approach phase*.
2. **Phase 2 — R-Plane to Z-Depth (FEED):** The tool plunges from the R-point to the programmed Z-depth (final hole bottom) at the programmed **feedrate F**.
3. **Phase 3 — Bottom-of-Hole Operation (DWELL / SPINDLE OPS):** Depending on the specific G-code, the cycle may dwell (G82, G89), stop the spindle (G86, G88), shift the tool laterally (G76), or perform a manual operation (G88).
4. **Phase 4 — Retract to R-Plane (RAPID or FEED):** Tool retracts out of the hole back to the **R-point** (not the initial plane — this is a critical distinction). Retract mode differs: G81, G73, G83, G76 use **rapid retract**; G82, G84, G85, G86, G88, G89 use **feed retract**.

> [!NOTE]
> **R-Plane vs. Initial Plane:** The *initial plane* is the safe Z-height where the tool starts (set by the operator or previous block). The *R-plane* is a *closer* Z-height (e.g., 2–5 mm above the workpiece) used to minimise rapid-air time. After the cycle's retract, the tool returns to the **R-plane**, not the initial plane.

## 2.2 The Why & How — Deep Dive into Each Cycle

### 2.2.1 G81 — Drilling Cycle (The Workhorse)
- **Why:** Simplest and fastest cycle. For holes ≤ 2×D (shallow).
- **How:** Rapid to R → Feed to Z → Rapid retract to R.
- **No dwell**, no spindle stop, no pecking.
- **Use case:** Mass production of shallow through-holes in aluminium, mild steel.

### 2.2.2 G82 — Spot Drilling / Counterboring Cycle
- **Why:** Ensures a *clean, flat bottom* (chamfered or spot-faced) for subsequent drilling — prevents drill wandering.
- **How:** Rapid to R → Feed to Z → **Dwell for 'P' milliseconds** → Rapid retract.
- **Use case:** Spot-drill marks on castings, counter-bore for socket-head cap screws.

### 2.2.3 G73 — High-Speed Peck Drilling (Chip-Break Cycle)
- **Why:** For *shallow-to-medium* holes where full retract is wasteful but chip evacuation is critical.
- **How:** Rapid to R → Feed by **increment Q** (peck depth) → Retract by **0.5–1 mm** (chip break, NOT full retract) → Repeat until Z-depth → Final retract to R.
- **Use case:** Drilling in stringy materials (low-carbon steel, stainless) where long chips would clog.

### 2.2.4 G83 — Deep-Hole Peck Drilling (Full-Retract Cycle)
- **Why:** For *deep* holes (Z-depth ≥ 3×D) where chips MUST be completely evacuated.
- **How:** Rapid to R → Feed by **Q** → **Full retract to R-plane** (chip clearance) → Rapid back to (current Z + 0.5 mm) → Feed by Q → ... → Rapid retract to R.
- **Use case:** Deep oil-port holes, gun barrels, mold cooling channels.

### 2.2.5 G84 — Tapping Cycle (Right-Hand Thread)
- **Why:** Cutting threads requires the spindle to *reverse* at the bottom of the hole.
- **How:** Rapid to R → Spindle CW → Feed to Z (pitch-matched) → Spindle STOPS → Spindle CCW (reverse) → Feed retract (synchronized to pitch) → Spindle CW.
- **Critical:** Feedrate F = Spindle RPM × Thread Pitch.

### 2.2.6 G76 — Fine Boring Cycle
- **Why:** Achieve superior bore roundness and surface finish; the lateral shift at retract prevents tool-mark scratches on the bore wall.
- **How:** Rapid to R → Oriented spindle stop → Feed to Z → **Lateral shift by 'Q'** (or 'I, J, K') at the bore bottom → Rapid retract.

### 2.2.7 G85, G86, G89 — Boring Variations
- **G85:** Feed in AND feed out — for reamed bores needing smoothest finish.
- **G86:** Feed in, spindle stop, rapid out — operator can inspect before retract.
- **G89:** Feed in, dwell, feed out — for chip-breaking on tough materials.

## 2.3 Subroutine Theory — Call Stack & Nesting

A subroutine is invoked by **M98** and terminated by **M99**. The controller maintains a **return-address stack**. Each M98 pushes the next sequential block address onto the stack; each M99 pops it and resumes execution there.

**Nesting Level Rules (Fanuc standard):**
- Maximum 4 nested subroutine levels
- An M99 in the *main* program acts as a *Program End* (re-starts from beginning or jumps to start with /L option)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K-Tip:** Use `M99 P100` in the main program to create a **continuous loop** — the program re-runs from sequence N100 indefinitely. This is the foundation of unattended production runs.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Number of pecks (G83) | $N_{pecks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{Z_{initial} - Z_{final}}{Q_{peck}} \right\rceil$ | dimensionless | Round **up** to ensure full depth |
| 2 | Time per peck (plunge) | $t_{peck} = \dfrac{60 \times Q_{peck}}{F}$ | seconds | F in mm/min |
| 3 | Tapping feedrate | $F = N_{spindle} \times P_{pitch}$ | mm/min | N in rev/min, P in mm/rev |
| 4 | Spindle RPM for tapping | $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ | rev/min | V_c = cutting speed (m/min) |
| 5 | Material removal rate (drilling) | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi \times D^2}{4} \times F \times N$ | mm³/min | For feed per rev = F/N |
| 6 | Drilling time per hole | $t_{hole} = \dfrac{Z_{depth}}{F} + t_{dwell} + \dfrac{R - Z_{depth}}{V_{rapid}}$ | seconds | Includes retract |
| 7 | Total drilling time | $T_{total} = N_{holes} \times t_{hole} + (N_{holes} - 1) \times t_{positioning}$ | seconds | Includes X-Y moves between holes |
| 8 | R-plane coordinate (above workpiece) | $Z_R = Z_{top} + 2$ to $5$ mm | mm | Conventional clearance |
| 9 | Subroutine repetition | $M98 \; P\text{0100} \; L\text{25}$ → runs 25 times | — | L must be on same line or before M98 |
| 10 | Chip-break retract distance (G73) | $d_{chip} = 0.5$ to $1.0$ mm | mm | NOT a full retract |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in process planning for **cycle-time estimation** (a key deliverable in any CNC quotation), **tool-life prediction** (using Taylor's equation on MRR), and **spindle-load analysis**.
