# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Number of Pecks for a Deep-Hole Drilling Cycle (G83)

**Problem Statement:** A steel block requires 6 through-holes, each of diameter $D = 12$ mm and depth $Z_{depth} = 60$ mm. The drill point angle is $118°$ (standard). The R-plane is set $3$ mm above the workpiece top surface. The recommended peck depth is $Q = 4$ mm (i.e., 3×D, conservative for steel). The cutting feedrate is $F = 180$ mm/min. The controller uses Fanuc-style absolute programming.

**Step 1 — Establish the absolute Z coordinates.**

$$
Z_{top} = 0 \text{ mm} \quad ; \quad Z_R = +3 \text{ mm} \quad ; \quad Z_{final} = -60 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Total drilling travel distance.**

$$
\Delta Z = Z_R - Z_{final} = 3 - (-60) = 63 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of pecks required.**

$$
N_{pecks} = \left\lceil \frac{\Delta Z}{Q} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{63}{4} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 15.75 \right\rceil = 16 \text{ pecks}
$$

> **Logic explanation:** The ceiling function is used because a *partial* final peck is still a *full* peck motion; we cannot leave a 0.75 mm sliver unfed.

**Step 4 — Verify the last peck depth (16th peck).**

$$
Q_{16} = \Delta Z - (N_{pecks} - 1) \times Q = 63 - 15 \times 4 = 3 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Logic explanation:** The last peck is 3 mm (less than the standard 4 mm), which is acceptable because the drill is already engaged in continuous cutting.

**Step 5 — Total plunge time (all pecks).**

$$
t_{plunge} = \frac{60 \times \Delta Z}{F} = \frac{60 \times 63}{180} = 21 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 6 — Add retract time for the 15 full retracts (the 16th peck retracts to R at the end of the cycle, so only 15 intermediate full retracts).**

Assuming rapid traverse $V_{rapid} = 4800$ mm/min and intermediate retract distance = (current Z to R):

> [!NOTE]
> Intermediate retracts are from successive Z positions up to R. Total retract path length:
> $\sum d_{retract} = \sum_{i=1}^{15} (Z_R - Z_i)$ where $Z_i = 3 - i \times 4$ for $i = 1$ to $15$.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{15} (3 - (3 - 4i)) = \sum_{i=1}^{15} 4i = 4 \times \frac{15 \times 16}{2} = 480 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t_{retract} = \frac{60 \times 480}{4800} = 6 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 7 — Add the final retract to R (from $Z = -60$ to $Z_R = +3$).**

$$
t_{final\_retract} = \frac{60 \times 63}{4800} = 0.7875 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 8 — Time per hole.**

$$
t_{hole} = t_{plunge} + t_{retract} + t_{final\_retract} = 21 + 6 + 0.7875 = 27.79 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 9 — Total time for 6 holes (assume 1.2 s positioning between holes, $V_{xy} = 4800$ mm/min over 30 mm pitch).**

$$
t_{positioning} = \frac{60 \times 30}{4800} = 0.375 \text{ s/position}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 6 \times 27.79 + 5 \times 0.375 = 166.74 + 1.875 = 168.6 \text{ seconds} \approx 2.81 \text{ minutes}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cycle Selection Logic Tree

| Hole Type | Depth Ratio (L/D) | Recommended Cycle | Reason |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|--------|
| Spot / Centre mark | L/D < 0.5 | **G82** with dwell | Clean flat bottom |
| Shallow through-hole | 0.5 ≤ L/D ≤ 2 | **G81** | Fastest, no chip issue |
| Medium-depth, stringy chip | 2 < L/D ≤ 3 | **G73** (high-speed peck) | Chip-break, no full retract |
| Deep hole, brittle chip | L/D > 3 | **G83** (full-retract peck) | Complete chip evacuation |
| Tapped hole | Any | **G84** | Synchronised spindle/feed |
| Precision bore | L/D ≤ 4 | **G85 or G76** | Fine finish, no marking |
| Bore + inspect | Any | **G86** | Spindle stop before retract |

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Subroutine Repetition Logic

**Scenario:** A pattern of 8 holes arranged in a 2×4 grid (2 rows, 4 columns), 25 mm pitch. The part is 200 mm × 80 mm.

**Step 1 — Define the subroutine (incremental mode G91):**

```
O0200 (SUB: DRILL_ONE_HOLE)
G91 G81 Z-15 R-97 F120
G80 G90
M99
```

> **Logic explanation:** The subroutine drills ONE hole 15 mm deep using a relative Z motion of $-15$ from the R-plane. The tool returns to R and waits for the next call.

**Step 2 — Main program positions to first hole and calls subroutine 8 times:**

```
O0100 (MAIN PROGRAM)
G90 G54 G00 X0 Y0          (absolute mode, rapid to first hole)
M98 P0200 L8                (call subroutine 8 times — same hole!)

G91 G00 X25 Y0              (move to second column in row 1)
M98 P0200 L4                (drill 4 holes in row 1 — manual loop)

G90 G00 X0 Y20              (move to row 2)
M98 P0200 L4                (drill 4 holes in row 2)
M30
```

**Step 3 — Optimisation using L-count within the call (for row pattern):**

A *single subroutine call* with **L4** invokes the subroutine 4 times consecutively *at the same X, Y*. To drill along X axis, you must use **incremental moves between the calls**:

```
G91
M98 P0200                    (drill hole 1)
G00 X25                      (shift to hole 2)
M98 P0200
G00 X25                      (shift to hole 3)
M98 P0200
G00 X25                      (shift to hole 4)
M98 P0200
```

> **Elegance upgrade:** Use a **second subroutine** that contains the X-increment + drill call — then call that 4 times.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Canned-Cycle Motion Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Tool at Initial Point] --> B[Phase 1: G00 Rapid to XY Hole Position]
    B --> C[Phase 2: G00 Rapid Down to R-Plane]
    C --> D[Phase 3: G01 Feed Plunge to Z-Depth]
    D --> E{Cycle Specific Bottom Operation}
    E -->|G81| F[No dwell, Immediate Retract]
    E -->|G82| G[Dwell for P ms, Then Retract]
    E -->|G83| H[Retract Fully to R, Rapid back, Repeat]
    E -->|G84| I[Spindle Stop, Reverse, Synchronized Retract]
    E -->|G76| J[Orient Spindle, Shift by Q, Retract]
    F --> K[Phase 4: Rapid or Feed Retract to R-Plane]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{More holes K > 0?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Increment XY to next hole position]
    M --> C
    L -->|No| N[Cycle Cancelled by G80]
    N --> O[End: Tool at last R-Plane, ready for next tool]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The diamond nodes (`C{}`) are *decision points* where the cycle branches. The rectangle `H` (G83) is recursive — it loops back to `C` for each peck. After all K repetitions are done, `G80` cancels the modal cycle and the tool awaits the next instruction.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Subroutine Call-Return Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Main Program O0100] --> A1[Block N10: Positioning Block]
    A1 --> A2[Block N20: M98 P0200 L1 Call Subroutine]
    A2 --> A3[Block N30: Positioning Move]
    A3 --> A4[Block N40: M98 P0200 L1 Call Subroutine]
    A4 --> A5[Block N50: M30 End of Main Program]
    A2 --> B1[Subroutine O0200 Start]
    B1 --> B2[Block N100: G81 Drilling Cycle Block]
    B2 --> B3[Block N110: M99 Return to Main]
    B3 --> A3
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> P1[Program Stop and Rewind]
    B3 --> P1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The flow shows the *call-return handshake*. When the main program reaches `M98 P0200`, control transfers to the subroutine `O0200` block N100. The subroutine executes the drilling cycle and hits `M99`, which transfers control back to the **next sequential block in the main program** (N30). This is precisely how nesting and looping work.

## 4.3 Architecture Flow — Canned Cycle Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Part Drawing Received] --> S1{Hole Feature Type?}
    S1 -->|Through Hole Shallow| S2[Select G81]
    S1 -->|Spot Face| S3[Select G82 with Dwell]
    S1 -->|Deep Hole D more than 3x| S4[Select G83 Full Peck]
    S1 -->|Medium Depth Stringy Chip| S5[Select G73 Chip Break]
    S1 -->|Threaded Hole| S6[Select G84 RH Tap]
    S1 -->|Precision Bore| S7[Select G76 Fine Bore]
    S1 -->|Reamed Finish| S8[Select G85]
    S1 -->|Inspect Mid Cycle| S9[Select G86]
    S2 --> S10[Define R, Z, F, K in program]
    S3 --> S10
    S4 --> S10
    S5 --> S10
    S6 --> S10
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11{Number of identical holes > 1?}
    S11 -->|Yes| S12[Wrap code in Subroutine O0200]
    S11 -->|No| S13[Inline single cycle call]
    S12 --> S14[Main: M98 P0200 Ln]
    S13 --> S15[Main: Direct cycle block]
    S14 --> S16[Simulate and Verify]
    S15 --> S16
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a *decision-support flowchart* a CNC programmer follows before writing a single line of code. It enforces the **cycle-first, subroutine-if-repeated** best practice.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any six standard canned cycles used in CNC part programming and state the specific application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| G-Code | Cycle Name | Application |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **G73** | High-speed peck drilling | Shallow holes in chip-forming materials |
| **G81** | Drilling cycle | Simple through-holes up to 2×D depth |
| **G82** | Spot drilling | Spot-facing with dwell for clean bottom |
| **G83** | Deep-hole peck drilling | Deep holes > 3×D with full chip evacuation |
| **G84** | Tapping cycle (RH) | Right-hand internal threads |
| **G85** | Boring cycle | Reamed or fine-bored holes |
| **G86** | Boring with spindle stop | Boring followed by operator inspection |

> *(Any six of the above carry full 3 marks. Naming without application = 1.5 marks only.)*

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the function of M98 and M99 with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

- **M98** is a *Subroutine Call* command. Syntax: `M98 PXXXXYYYY Lnnnn` — calls program OXXXX starting from sequence NYYYY, repeated `nnnn` times.
- **M99** is a *Subroutine Return* command. When executed inside a subroutine, it transfers control back to the block following the M98 call in the calling program.

**Example:**

```
O0100 (MAIN)
G90 G00 X0 Y0
M98 P0200 L4       (calls subroutine 4 times)
G00 X100 Y100
M30
O0200 (SUBROUTINE)
G91 G81 Z-10 R-95 F100
G80 G90
M99                 (returns to main program)
```

(3 marks: 1 mark M98 syntax, 1 mark M99 function, 1 mark example)

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of the **G83 deep-hole peck drilling cycle**. Discuss the difference between G73 and G83 in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC milling machine is to drill **6 holes** of diameter **Ø15 mm** in a **3×2 rectangular pattern** (3 columns, 2 rows), pitch **30 mm × 40 mm**. The part top is at Z=0, R-plane at Z=+3 mm, hole depth = 25 mm, peck depth Q = 5 mm, feedrate = 150 mm/min. Calculate: (i) number of pecks per hole, (ii) total plunge time, and (iii) total cycle time (neglect X-Y moves). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Sketch (text representation):**

```
Z-axis
 ↑
 |        ____
 |       |    |  ← Drill (Ø15)
 |   R---+----+   Z_R = +3
 |       |////|   <- feed plunge (peck 1: Z=-5)
 |       |////|
 |       +----+   Z = -5
 |       |////|   <- FULL RETRACT to R (chip clear)
 |       |////|
 |       +----+   Z = -10 (peck 2 begins)
 |       ....
 |       |////|
 |       +----+   Z = -25 (final Z)
 |       |////|
 +-------+----+--------
        X, Y = hole centre
```

**Working Principle of G83:**
1. Tool rapids to hole centre (X, Y).
2. Tool rapids to **R-plane** (Z = +3).
3. Tool **feeds** down by peck increment Q = 5 mm (to Z = -5).
4. Tool performs **full rapid retract** to R-plane (chip evacuation).
5. Tool rapids back to (current Z + 0.5 mm approach).
6. Steps 3–5 repeat until Z = -25 is reached.
7. Final rapid retract to R-plane.

**Difference between G73 and G83:**

| Parameter | G73 (Chip-Break) | G83 (Deep-Hole) |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Retract type** | Partial retract (0.5–1 mm) | Full retract to R-plane |
| **Cycle time** | Faster | Slower (more retract motion) |
| **Chip evacuation** | Limited (chip-break only) | Excellent (full clearance) |
| **Use** | Shallow-to-medium, stringy chip | Deep holes ≥ 3×D |
| **Coolant requirement** | Flood or through-tool | Through-tool coolant MANDATORY |

(Marks distribution: sketch 2 marks, working 2 marks, comparison table 3 marks = 7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Hole depth = 25 mm
- R-plane offset above Z = 3 mm
- Q = 5 mm
- F = 150 mm/min
- Number of holes = 6 (3×2 pattern, position moves neglected)

**Step 1 — Total Z travel:**
$$
\Delta Z = Z_R - Z_{final} = 3 - (-25) = 28 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — (i) Number of pecks:**
$$
N_{pecks} = \left\lceil \frac{\Delta Z}{Q} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{28}{5} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.6 \rceil = 6 \text{ pecks}
$$

**Verification of last peck depth:**
$$
Q_6 = 28 - 5 \times 5 = 3 \text{ mm (acceptable)}
$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Total plunge time:**

The total plunge distance is still 28 mm regardless of pecking:
$$
t_{plunge} = \frac{60 \times \Delta Z}{F} = \frac{60 \times 28}{150} = 11.2 \text{ seconds per hole}
$$

**Step 4 — Total retract time (assuming $V_{rapid} = 6000$ mm/min):**

Number of intermediate full retracts = 5 (the 6th peck's retract happens at cycle end).

Intermediate retract distances (Z to R for each):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} d_i = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (3 - (-5i)) = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (3 + 5i) = 15 + 5 \times \frac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15 + 75 = 90 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t_{intermediate\_retract} = \frac{60 \times 90}{6000} = 0.9 \text{ s}
$$

**Final retract (from Z = -25 to R = +3):**
$$
t_{final\_retract} = \frac{60 \times 28}{6000} = 0.28 \text{ s}
$$

**Time per hole:**
$$
t_{hole} = 11.2 + 0.9 + 0.28 = 12.38 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Total cycle time for 6 holes:**

$$
T_{total} = 6 \times 12.38 = 74.28 \text{ seconds} \approx 1 \text{ min } 14 \text{ s}
$$

(Marks: setup 1, peck count 2, plunge time 1.5, retract time 1.5, total 1 = 7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **subroutines** in CNC part programming. Discuss the L-count option, subroutine nesting, and the use of M99 with P-address. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a **complete CNC part program** using Fanuc G-code to drill 4 holes of Ø10 mm at coordinates (X20, Y20), (X80, Y20), (X80, Y60), and (X20, Y60) on a 100 mm × 80 mm plate. Use canned cycle G81 and a subroutine. Assume: tool change at (X0, Y0, Z+50), R-plane at Z+3, hole depth Z = -15, feedrate = 100 mm/min, spindle speed = 1200 RPM. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Concept of Subroutines:**
A subroutine is a standalone part program (identified by an O-number) that can be invoked from the main program (or another subroutine) using the **M98** call command and terminated by **M99** return. The primary purpose is **code modularity** — to avoid repetition when the same machining sequence is required at multiple locations.

**L-count Option:**
The **L** parameter in the M98 call specifies the number of consecutive repetitions.
Example: `M98 P0200 L4` → calls subroutine O0200 four times in succession at the *current* tool position. (Important: L repeats the subroutine, NOT the X-Y positioning — programmer must position separately for each unique location.)

**Subroutine Nesting:**
A subroutine can call another subroutine, forming a stack. Fanuc controllers support up to **4 levels** of nested subroutine calls. Each `M98` pushes the return address; each `M99` pops it.

**Use of M99 with P-address:**
The `P` parameter in `M99` specifies the *sequence number* in the main program to which control returns. Example: `M99 P0500` returns execution to block N0500 in the calling program. If used in the *main* program alone (e.g., `M99` with no P), the program **loops back to the start** — a continuous production mode.

**Example of nesting:**

```
O0100 (MAIN)
M98 P0200
M30
O0200 (SUB-1)
M98 P0300
M99
O0300 (SUB-2)
G81 ...
M99
```

(7 marks: concept 2, L-count 2, nesting 1.5, M99 with P 1.5)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Complete Part Program:**

```
%
O0100 (MAIN PROGRAM - DRILL 4 HOLES)
(TOOL: T01 - DRILL DIA 10 MM)
N05 G90 G54 G17 G21 G94          (Absolute mode, work coord XY plane, metric, feed/min)
N10 G00 X0 Y0 Z50                (Rapid to tool-change position)
N15 T01 M06                      (Tool change to T01)
N20 M03 S1200                     (Spindle CW at 1200 RPM)
N25 G00 X20 Y20 Z5                (Position above first hole)
N30 M98 P0200 L1                  (Call subroutine once at first location)
N35 G00 X80 Y20                   (Position to second hole)
N40 M98 P0200 L1                  (Call subroutine)
N45 G00 X80 Y60                   (Position to third hole)
N50 M98 P0200 L1                  (Call subroutine)
N55 G00 X20 Y60                   (Position to fourth hole)
N60 M98 P0200 L1                  (Call subroutine)
N65 G80                           (Cancel canned cycle)
N70 G00 X0 Y0 Z50                 (Return to home)
N75 M05                           (Spindle stop)
N80 M30                           (End of program)

O0200 (SUBROUTINE - DRILL ONE HOLE)
N105 G90 G99 G81 Z-15 R3 F100    (Drill cycle, return to R after each hole)
N110 G80                          (Cancel within sub is optional)
N115 M99                          (Return to main program)
%
```

**Verification Checklist (Examiner's Expectation):**

| Element | Present | Marks |
|---------|---------|-------|
| G90, G54, G21 setup | ✓ | 1 |
| Tool change + spindle start | ✓ | 1 |
| 4 position blocks (X20 Y20 → X80 Y20 → X80 Y60 → X20 Y60) | ✓ | 1.5 |
| Four M98 calls (L1) | ✓ | 1 |
| G81 format with R3, Z-15, F100 | ✓ | 1 |
| Subroutine O0200 with G81 block | ✓ | 1 |
| M99 return + M30 end | ✓ | 0.5 |
| **Total** | | **7** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing 2–3 Marks Per Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting G80** before the next motion block → the canned cycle remains modal and the controller drills an *unintended* hole at the next X, Y. This is the #1 reason for part rejection in industry AND for marks deduction in KTU valuations.
> 2. **Using G91 inside subroutine without resetting to G90 in main** → incremental X-Y positions are interpreted additively, causing a "drift" of hole locations.
> 3. **Confusing R-plane with Initial Plane** — many students retract to the *initial plane* in their explanation, which is wrong; the cycle retracts to the **R-plane** only.
> 4. **Calculating peck count using `floor` instead of `ceil`** → a partial final peck means the hole is NOT drilled to full depth.
> 5. **M98 P0200 vs M98 P200** — omitting the leading zero (e.g., `P200` instead of `P0200`) may cause the controller to interpret the address as **P200YY** where 200 is a sequence number, NOT a program number. Always use **4-digit program numbers** in Fanuc.
> 6. **Not cancelling M03 with M05 before M30** — controller may throw alarm on next program load.
> 7. **Writing the subroutine AFTER M30 in the program listing** — KTU expects the subroutine block to be physically present in the same file, but **conceptually** it is a separate entity. Examiners may dock 0.5 marks for procedural order.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Recall Checklist (Print This Before Exam!)**
>
> - [ ] **Canned cycle syntax:** `G_ X_ Y_ Z_ R_ F_ K_` — Z is the *final* depth (absolute), R is the *clearance* above the part, F is feedrate, K is repetition count.
> - [ ] **G81** is for *simple* shallow holes; **G83** is for *deep* holes with full retract pecking; **G73** is for *chip-breaking* in medium holes.
> - [ ] **G82** adds a *dwell* at the bottom — use for spot-facing; **G76** adds a *lateral shift* at retract — use for fine boring.
> - [ ] **G84 (tapping)** requires the feedrate to be **synchronised** to the spindle RPM via the thread pitch: $F = N \times P$.
> - [ ] **All canned cycles are CANCELLED by G80.** Always issue G80 before a non-cycle motion.
> - [ ] **M98 PXXXX LNN** calls subroutine OXXXX, NN times. **M99** returns to the calling block.
> - [ ] **M99 Pnnnn** in the main program causes an *unconditional jump* to sequence Nnnnn — used for **continuous production loops**.
> - [ ] **Nesting depth** on Fanuc = **4 levels maximum**; deeper nesting triggers an alarm.
> - [ ] **Peck count formula:** $N_{pecks} = \lceil \Delta Z / Q \rceil$. Always **ceiling**, never floor.
> - [ ] **Time per hole** = plunge time + retract time + (dwell if any); plunge time = $60 \times \Delta Z / F$.
> - [ ] **R-plane is ABOVE the workpiece** (typically +2 to +5 mm); Initial Plane is the safe Z where the controller starts.
> - [ ] **Use subroutines** whenever the same machining pattern repeats **more than 2 times**.
> - [ ] **G91 inside subroutine, G90 in main program** is the standard Fanuc convention.
> - [ ] **Group 09** is the G-code family for all canned cycles.
> - [ ] **Incremental mode (G91)** within a subroutine allows *parametric repetition* using L-count without re-positioning in the main.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked *"Explain the difference between G73 and G83"*, ALWAYS start with the **retract type** (partial vs. full) — that single distinction accounts for 2 of the 3 marks allocated. Then add the *use-case* (chip-break vs. chip-evacuation) and the *typical depth ratio* (≤3×D vs. >3×D).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cutting cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cutting cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cutting Cycles (Canned Cycles / Fixed Cycles):** A *canned cycle* is a **pre-programmed, single-block subroutine** stored permanently in the CNC machine's firmware that consolidates a sequence of repetitive tool movements (rapid positioning, cutting feed, retract, return-to-start) into **one standardized G-code instruction**. They are invoked to perform recurring operations such as **turning, facing, thread cutting, drilling, boring, and tapping** with a single line of code, drastically reducing program length, minimizing operator error, and ensuring cycle-time optimization.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, cutting cycles are categorized as:

| Category | Operations Covered | Typical G-Codes |
|---|---|---|
| **Lathe Turning Cycles** | Straight turning, facing, thread cutting | G90, G92, G94 |
| **Lathe Multi-Repeat (Contour) Cycles** | Roughing, finishing of complex profiles | G70, G71, G72, G73 |
| **Milling / Drilling Canned Cycles** | Drilling, boring, tapping, counterboring | G81 – G89 |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Instantly Clear

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Microwave vs. The Stove"**
> Imagine you want to boil water. On a **stove**, you must manually perform each step: place the pot, light the burner, set the flame, monitor, turn it off, and remove the pot. That is like writing **every single motion block by block** in a CNC program.
> A **microwave** does all those steps automatically with a single button — you just say "boil for 2 minutes." That is a **canned cycle** in CNC: you specify the *start point*, the *target point*, and the *feed/plunge depth*, and the controller automatically executes rapid → feed → retract → return, all in **one G-code line**.

This automation is precisely why canned cycles are mandatory for any production-grade CNC part program examined in the KTU ESE.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Feed unit:** `mm/rev` (turning) or `mm/min` (milling)
> - **Standard Spindle speed unit:** `rev/min` (RPM)
> - **Cutting Speed (V):** measured in **m/min**
> - **Depth of Cut (d):** measured in **mm/pass**
> - **Thread Lead (F) in G92:** measured in **mm/rev** (must equal the thread pitch)
> - **Coordinate convention:** X = diameter, Z = length (lathe); X, Y = planar, Z = spindle axis (mill)

---

## 1.4 Core Construct — The Generic 4-Block Architecture of a Canned Cycle

Every cutting cycle, regardless of operation, follows the **universal 4-block logic**:

1. **Block 1 — Rapid to Start Position (Approach)**
2. **Block 2 — Plunge / Engage at Cutting Feed**
3. **Block 3 — Cutting Feed Along the Working Path**
4. **Block 4 — Retract (Rapid) → Return to Start**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The student's job in a KTU exam is to **identify which block does which motion** and to **correctly size the parameters** (depth, feed, finish allowance).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lathe Simple Cutting Cycles

### 2.1.1 G90 — Straight Turning Cycle (Cylindrical Turning)

**Purpose:** Removes material along the **Z-axis** with multiple passes along the **X-axis** to reduce diameter.

**Format (Single Block Form):**
```
G90 X__ Z__ F__ ;
```

**Operational Logic (4 Steps):**
1. Tool starts at the cycle's reference point.
2. **Rapid** to the X-coordinate (radial approach).
3. **Feed (linear interpolation)** to the Z-coordinate (longitudinal cut).
4. **Rapid** retract to the start point — ready for the next pass.

> Each subsequent block with a smaller **X** value performs the **next roughing pass**. The cycle is **modal** and repeats until a non-G90 code (e.g., G00, G01) is issued.

**Worked Concept (Why it works):**
- The cycle uses **G00 internally for the radial approach** and the **final retract**.
- It uses **G01 (linear feed)** only for the actual material removal.
- This eliminates 3 manual blocks per pass → saves **~60% program length** for stepped shafts.

---

### 2.1.2 G92 — Thread Cutting Cycle

**Purpose:** Cuts **straight, tapered, or face threads** on a lathe.

**Format:**
```
G92 X__ Z__ F__ ;
```
- `F` = **Thread Lead** in `mm/rev` (NOT the feed rate in the usual sense — it represents axial advance per spindle revolution).

**Operational Logic:**
1. Rapid to start point.
2. **G32 (thread cutting feed)** along the thread length to `X, Z`.
3. **Rapid retract** off the thread flanks.
4. **Rapid return** to the start.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The **spindle must be running at a constant RPM** with **spindle encoder synchronization**. Without encoder feedback, thread lead errors occur. The same **F value** is kept in every block; **X is reduced** in successive blocks to cut deeper passes.

**Thread Depth Calculation (Metric ISO):**
$$H = 0.6134 \times P$$
where `H` = thread depth (mm) and `P` = pitch (mm).

**Number of Passes (Empirical):**
$$N_{passes} \approx 5 \text{ to } 12 \quad (\text{depending on material & pitch})$$

---

### 2.1.3 G94 — Facing Cycle (End Facing)

**Purpose:** Machines a **flat face** perpendicular to the spindle axis.

**Format:**
```
G94 X__ Z__ F__ ;
```

**Operational Logic:**
1. Tool at reference point.
2. **Rapid** to X-coordinate.
3. **Feed** in the Z-direction (longitudinal facing).
4. **Rapid retract** in Z, then **rapid return** to start.

> Each successive block reduces **Z** to remove the next layer — tool sweeps across the **face** of the workpiece.

---

## 2.2 Lathe Multi-Repeat (Contour) Cycles

### 2.2.1 G71 — Rough Turning Cycle (OD/ID)

**Two-block format:**
```
G71 U(d) R(e) ;
G71 P(ns) Q(nf) U(Δu) W(Δw) F__ ;
```
- `U(d)` = **depth of cut per pass** (radius value, mm)
- `R(e)` = **retract amount** (mm)
- `P(ns)` = **first sequence number** of the finished profile description
- `Q(nf)` = **last sequence number** of the profile
- `U(Δu)` = **X-axis finishing allowance** (diameter value)
- `W(Δw)` = **Z-axis finishing allowance**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** The profile described between `P` and `Q` must be a **monotonically changing contour** (X must consistently increase OR decrease per block in the roughing direction). The controller uses this profile to auto-generate the roughing passes.

---

### 2.2.2 G72 — Rough Facing Cycle

Identical to G71 **but the cuts are taken radially toward the centerline (in Z direction cuts)**. The tool approach direction is reversed.

```
G72 W(d) R(e) ;
G72 P(ns) Q(nf) U(Δu) W(Δw) F__ ;
```

---

### 2.2.3 G73 — Pattern Repeating (Closed-Loop) Cycle

For **forged / cast / pre-formed** parts that already resemble the final shape.

```
G73 U(Δi) W(Δk) R(d) ;
G73 P(ns) Q(nf) U(Δu) W(Δw) F__ ;
```
- `U(Δi)` = X-axis distance from current tool position to the first profile point
- `W(Δk)` = Z-axis distance to first profile point
- `R(d)` = **number of divisions** of the total stock removal

---

### 2.2.4 G70 — Finishing Cycle

```
G70 P(ns) Q(nf) ;
```
Reads the profile stored between `ns` and `nf` and executes it as a **single continuous finishing pass**, using the finishing allowances `U(Δu)` and `W(Δw)` to leave exact dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Pairing:** `G71/G72/G73` (rough) + `G70` (finish) = the canonical two-step turning strategy.

---

## 2.3 Milling Canned Cycles (G81 – G89)

All milling canned cycles follow the **initial motion sequence**:
1. **Initial plane (Z)** — set by G98 (return to initial) or G99 (return to R-point).
2. **R-plane** — the rapid approach plane just above the workpiece.
3. **Z-target** — final cutting depth.
4. **Retract** — back to R-plane (G99) or Initial plane (G98).

### 2.3.1 G81 — Simple Drilling Cycle
```
G81 X__ Y__ Z__ R__ F__ ;
```
No dwell at the bottom; tool rapid-retracts after reaching Z.

### 2.3.2 G82 — Drilling + Dwell (Counterbore)
```
G82 X__ Y__ Z__ R__ P__ F__ ;
```
Adds a **dwell (P milliseconds)** at the bottom — produces a clean, flat-bottomed hole.

### 2.3.3 G83 — Deep Hole Peck Drilling Cycle
```
G83 X__ Y__ Z__ R__ Q__ F__ ;
```
- `Q` = **depth of each peck** (incremental).
- After each peck, the tool **rapid-retracts to the R-plane** to clear chips, then re-plunges for the next `Q` increment. This prevents **chip jamming and tool breakage** in deep holes (>3× drill diameter).

### 2.3.4 G84 — Tapping (Right-Hand Thread)
```
G84 X__ Y__ Z__ R__ F__ ;
```
- Spindle **reverses** at the bottom of the hole.
- Feed rate `F` = **thread pitch × spindle RPM** (must be exact).
- Requires **spindle encoder with rigid tapping** capability.

### 2.3.5 G85 – G89 — Boring Variations
| Code | Operation | Retract |
|---|---|---|
| G85 | Boring (feed in, feed out) | Feed |
| G86 | Boring + Spindle stop | Rapid |
| G87 | Back boring | Manual/Rapid |
| G88 | Boring + Dwell + Spindle stop | Manual |
| G89 | Boring + Dwell | Feed |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All parameters must be used with the **absolute** value separator `\vert` or `\mid` to comply with markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | Lathe & Mill |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rev/min | All cycles |
| 3 | Feed Rate (Turning) | $f_r = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | G90, G94 |
| 4 | Material Removal Rate (Turning) | $MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f_r$ | mm³/min | Productivity |
| 5 | Thread Depth (Metric) | $H = 0.6134 \cdot P$ | mm | G92 |
| 6 | Thread Depth (Whitworth) | $H = 0.6403 \cdot P$ | mm | G92 |
| 7 | Tap Feed | $F_{tap} = P \cdot N$ | mm/min | G84 |
| 8 | Number of G71 Passes | $n = \left\lceil \dfrac{D_{blank} - D_{final} - \Delta u}{2 \cdot d} \right\rceil$ | passes | Roughing |
| 9 | G83 Peck Increment | $Q \le 3 \cdot D_{drill}$ | mm | Deep holes |
| 10 | G98 / G99 Plane | $Z_{R} \ge Z_{top} + 2 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Safety |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aerospace Industry:** G73 is critical for **pre-formed blisks and turbine disks** — the forging already resembles the airfoil profile.
> - **Automotive (Mass Production):** G71 + G70 is the **workhorse for crankshaft, camshaft, and piston turning** — used in millions of parts annually.
> - **Mold & Die:** G83 with reduced peck `Q` values prevents **chip evacuation failure** in ejector pin holes.
> - **Oil & Gas:** G84 with rigid tapping produces **API-spec threaded couplings** for downhole tools.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thread Cutting Parameter Calculation (G92)

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A M20 × 2.0 metric external thread must be cut on a mild steel workpiece (initial diameter = 20.0 mm blank). Spindle speed = 500 RPM. Compute: (a) Thread depth `H`, (b) Feed rate `F` parameter, (c) Number of roughing passes (assume 0.4 mm cut per pass), (d) Final X-coordinate for the last pass.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**(a) Thread Depth:**
$$H = 0.6134 \times P = 0.6134 \times 2.0 = 1.2268 \text{ mm}$$

This is the **total radial depth** that must be removed.

**(b) Feed Rate (F parameter for G92):**
For thread cutting, the F parameter equals the **thread lead** (pitch for single-start):
$$F = P = 2.0 \text{ mm/rev}$$

**(c) Number of Roughing Passes:**
With 0.4 mm cut per pass, the number of passes for the full depth:
$$N = \left\lceil \frac{H}{\text{cut/pass}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1.2268}{0.4} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.067 \rceil = 4 \text{ passes}$$

**(d) Final X-coordinate (target diameter):**
The thread depth is **radial**, so the diameter reduction is `2 × H`:
$$D_{final} = D_{blank} - 2 \times H = 20.0 - 2 \times 1.2268 = 17.5464 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Program Skeleton:**
```
G92 X19.20 Z-30.0 F2.0 ;   (Pass 1: depth 0.4 mm radial)
G92 X18.40 Z-30.0 F2.0 ;   (Pass 2)
G92 X17.95 Z-30.0 F2.0 ;   (Pass 3)
G92 X17.55 Z-30.0 F2.0 ;   (Pass 4 — final)
```

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — G71 Roughing Cycle Pass Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A shaft blank of Ø80 mm is to be rough turned to Ø60 mm using G71 with `U(d) = 2.0 mm` and finish allowance `U(Δu) = 1.0 mm`. Compute: (a) Total material to be removed (radial), (b) Number of G71 roughing passes.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**(a) Total Radial Material to be Removed:**
$$\Delta D = D_{blank} - D_{final} = 80 - 60 = 20 \text{ mm (diameter)}$$

But finish allowance `Δu = 1.0 mm` is left for the G70 pass, so the G71 must leave:
$$D_{G71\,exit} = D_{final} + \Delta u = 60 + 1.0 = 61 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore, the material removed by **G71 itself**:
$$\Delta D_{G71} = 80 - 61 = 19 \text{ mm (diameter)} = 9.5 \text{ mm (radial)}$$

**(b) Number of Passes:**
`d = 2.0 mm` is the **radial depth of cut per pass**. Therefore:
$$n = \left\lceil \frac{\Delta D_{G71}}{2 \cdot d} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{19}{2 \times 2.0} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.75 \rceil = 5 \text{ passes}$$

**Logical Check:** 5 passes × 2.0 mm/pass = 10 mm radial = 20 mm diameter — but we need 19 mm, so the **last pass is reduced to 1.5 mm radial (3.0 mm diameter)** to honor the finish allowance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — G83 Peck Drilling Strategy

**Problem Statement:**
Drill a **120 mm deep hole** in mild steel using a Ø10 mm drill with peck increment `Q = 15 mm`. Initial plane is 5 mm above the workpiece top; R-plane is 2 mm above. Compute: (a) Number of pecks, (b) Total Z-axis travel, (c) Time per peck (assume cutting feed = 200 mm/min, rapid = 2000 mm/min).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**(a) Number of Pecks:**
$$n = \left\lceil \frac{Z_{total}}{Q} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{120}{15} \right\rceil = 8 \text{ pecks}$$

**Logical check:** 8 × 15 = 120 mm ✓

**(b) Total Z-axis Travel:**
For each peck except the last:
- Plunge (cutting): 15 mm
- Retract (rapid) to R-plane: 15 + 2 = 17 mm
- Re-plunge (rapid) to current depth: 17 mm

For the last peck: plunge 15 mm (no retract).

Total = (7 × 49) + 15 = **358 mm** of combined motion.

**(c) Time Calculation:**
Cutting time per peck (15 mm at 200 mm/min):
$$t_{cut} = \frac{15}{200} = 0.075 \text{ min}$$

Rapid time per peck (17 mm up + 17 mm down = 34 mm at 2000 mm/min):
$$t_{rapid} = \frac{34}{2000} = 0.017 \text{ min}$$

Time per peck = 0.075 + 0.017 = **0.092 min** (for 7 pecks); last peck = 0.075 min.

Total time = (7 × 0.092) + 0.075 = **0.719 min ≈ 43.1 seconds**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cycle Selection Decision Tree (Textual)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **KTU 14-mark long-answer template**. When asked *"Which cycle would you use and why?"*, traverse this matrix:

| Workpiece Feature | Operation Type | Recommended Cycle | Justification (Why?) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plain cylindrical step | External roughing | **G90** | Simple straight cuts, low complexity |
| Cylindrical step near chuck | Facing the end | **G94** | Radial sweeps for face material removal |
| Straight or tapered thread | Threading | **G92** | Built-in lead synchronization |
| Complex monotonic profile, billet | Shaft roughing | **G71** | Auto-generates passes from final contour |
| Complex monotonic profile, facing surface | Hub/disc roughing | **G72** | Toolpath oriented radially |
| Forged/cast near-net shape | Roughing of preformed stock | **G73** | Closed-loop toolpath saves time |
| Any roughed profile | Final precision pass | **G70** | Uses stored profile with finishing feed |
| Through hole, shallow | Drilling | **G81** | Minimal logic, fast |
| Hole requiring flat bottom | Counterbore | **G82** | Dwell cleans the bottom |
| Hole > 3×D deep | Deep hole | **G83** | Pecking breaks chips, prevents jamming |
| Threaded hole | Tapping | **G84** | Synchronized spindle reversal |
| Precision bore | Boring | **G85–G89** | Fine surface finish and size control |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Universal Canned Cycle Flow Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start0[Start: Cycle Invoked G8X X_ Y_ Z_ R_ F_] --> move1
    move1[Rapid to R-plane above target] --> plunge2
    plunge2[Plunge at Cutting Feed to Z-depth] --> operation3
    operation3{Operation Type?}
    operation3 --> type1[Dwell at Bottom G82 G89]
    operation3 --> type2[Spindle Reverse G84 Tapping]
    operation3 --> type3[Continue Cutting G81 G85]
    type1 --> retract4
    type2 --> retract4
    type3 --> retract4
    retract4{Return Plane G98 or G99?}
    retract4 --> plane1[G99: Rapid to R-plane]
    retract4 --> plane2[G98: Rapid to Initial Plane]
    plane1 --> nextblock
    plane2 --> nextblock
    nextblock{Next Block with same cycle code?}
    nextblock --> same[YES: Repeat for new X Y position]
    nextblock --> stop[NO: Cycle Cancelled]
    same --> move1
    stop --> endCycle[End: Resume Modal State]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Lathe Roughing + Finishing Strategy (G71 + G70)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    part[Workpiece: 80 mm diameter blank] --> setup[Tool Setup at Reference Point]
    setup --> g71start[Issue G71 Block 1: U-d R-e]
    g71start --> g71cont[Issue G71 Block 2: P-Q U-Du W-Dw F]
    g71cont --> profile[Profile Described N1 to Nf with G00 G01]
    profile --> roughing[Controller Auto-Generates Roughing Passes]
    roughing --> decision{Roughing Complete?}
    decision --> no1[Continue passes at U-d depth]
    no1 --> roughing
    decision --> yes1[Stop: Leave Finish Allowance Du Dw]
    yes1 --> g70[G70 P__ Q__: Finishing Cycle]
    g70 --> finish[Tool follows exact profile at finishing feed]
    finish --> partout[Final Part at 60 mm with surface finish]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — G83 Peck Drilling Cycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    zinit[Initial Plane: 5 mm above workpiece] --> zr[R-plane: 2 mm above workpiece]
    zr --> peck1[Peck 1: Plunge Q mm at feed]
    peck1 --> retract1[Rapid Retract to R-plane: Chip Evacuation]
    retract1 --> rapidback1[Rapid Re-plunge to previous depth - 0.5 mm]
    rapidback1 --> depthcheck{Depth = Z target?}
    depthcheck --> no2[Peck N+1: Plunge next Q mm]
    no2 --> peck1
    depthcheck --> yes2[Final Peck: Reach Z-target]
    yes2 --> finalretract[Rapid Retract to Initial Plane G98]
    finalretract --> cycleend[Cycle complete, next hole position]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CNC Controller + Canned Cycle Firmware

```mermaid
block-beta
    blockBeta
        columns 3
        A["Part Program G-code File"]:::input
        B["G-code Parser and Interpreter"]:::process
        C["Modal State Register M S T F"]:::process
        D["Canned Cycle Subroutine Library G71 G72 G73 G70 G81 to G89"]:::core
        E["Motion Planner Linear Circular"]:::process
        F["Axis Servo Drivers X Y Z Spindle"]:::output
        G["Feedback Encoders Position RPM"]:::feedback
        A --> B
        B --> C
        B --> D
        C --> D
        D --> E
        E --> F
        F --> G
        G --> E
    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    classDef process fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    classDef core fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    classDef output fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
    classDef feedback fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#6600cc
    class A input
    class B,C,E process
    class D core
    class F output
    class G feedback
```

**Interpretation of the Architecture:**
- The **G-code Parser** identifies a cycle code (e.g., G71) and extracts its arguments.
- The **Modal State Register** holds persistent values (current tool offset, active plane, feed mode).
- The **Canned Cycle Subroutine Library** in firmware expands the single line into a sequence of motion blocks.
- The **Motion Planner** interpolates trajectories and dispatches pulses to the **servo drivers**.
- **Encoders** close the loop for position and spindle RPM — critical for **G84 (tapping)** and **G92 (threading)** synchronization.

---

## 4.5 Schematic — Thread Geometry and Depth Relationship (G92)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Major Diameter D] --> B[Pitch Diameter Dp]
    B --> C[Minor Diameter Dminor]
    A -- "H above" --> C
    A -- "H below" --> C
    P[Pitch P] --> B
    H[Thread Depth H equals 0.6134 P] --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> For an M20 × 2.0 thread: Major Ø = 20.0 mm, Pitch = 2.0 mm, Depth H = 1.2268 mm, Minor Ø = 17.5464 mm, Pitch Ø ≈ 18.376 mm.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a canned cycle. List any four canned cycles used in CNC milling machines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A canned cycle is a single G-code instruction that internally executes a predefined sequence of operations (rapid approach, cutting feed, retract) for repetitive machining tasks, simplifying programming and reducing code length.
>
> Four milling canned cycles: **G81** (drilling), **G82** (drilling with dwell), **G83** (peck drilling), **G84** (tapping).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between G90 and G94 turning cycles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | G90 (Turning) | G94 (Facing) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Feed Direction** | Along Z-axis (longitudinal) | Along X-axis (radial) |
| **Cuts a** | Cylinder / diameter reduction | Flat end face |
| **Typical Use** | Stepped shafts | Shoulders, face plates |
| **Direction of material removal** | Outer diameter stock | End face stock |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Pattern:** The question paper offers two 14-mark questions, and the student answers ONE. Each 14-mark question is split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

> A mild steel shaft of initial diameter Ø60 mm is to be rough turned to Ø40 mm using the **G71 roughing cycle**. Depth of cut per pass `d = 2 mm` (radial), retract `e = 1 mm`, finish allowance `Δu = 0.5 mm` (diameter), `Δw = 0.2 mm`, feed = 0.25 mm/rev.
>
> **(i)** Write the complete G71 program blocks (assume profile from N10 to N40).
> **(ii)** Calculate the number of roughing passes.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Program Blocks:**

```
O0001 ;
N05 G21 G97 G98 ;
N10 T0101 ;
N15 G00 X62.0 Z2.0 M03 S1200 ;
N20 G71 U2.0 R1.0 ;
N25 G71 P30 Q35 U0.5 W0.2 F0.25 ;
N30 G00 X40.0 ;             (first profile block — X)
N35 G01 Z-50.0 ;            (last profile block — Z)
N40 G70 P30 Q35 F0.1 ;      (finishing pass)
N45 G00 X100.0 Z100.0 M05 ;
N50 M30 ;
```

**(ii) Number of Passes:**

Material to be removed radially by G71:
$$\Delta r = \frac{60 - (40 + 0.5)}{2} = \frac{19.5}{2} = 9.75 \text{ mm}$$

Number of passes:
$$n = \left\lceil \frac{9.75}{2.0} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.875 \rceil = 5 \text{ passes}$$

(Last pass will be 1.75 mm radial to honor the finish allowance.)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> A **M24 × 3.0** external thread is to be cut on a Ø24 mm blank using **G92** thread cutting cycle. Spindle speed = 400 RPM. Cut 0.5 mm per pass (radial). Write the program and compute the total number of passes.

**Model Solution:**

**Calculations:**
$$H = 0.6134 \times 3.0 = 1.8402 \text{ mm (total radial depth)}$$
$$F = P = 3.0 \text{ mm/rev}$$
$$n = \left\lceil \frac{1.8402}{0.5} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.68 \rceil = 4 \text{ passes}$$
$$D_{final} = 24.0 - 2(1.8402) = 20.3196 \text{ mm}$$

**Program:**
```
O0002 ;
N05 G21 G97 G99 M03 S400 ;
N10 T0303 ;
N15 G00 X24.0 Z5.0 ;
N20 G92 X23.0 Z-30.0 F3.0 ;   (Pass 1: 0.5 mm radial)
N25 G92 X22.0 Z-30.0 F3.0 ;   (Pass 2)
N30 G92 X21.0 Z-30.0 F3.0 ;   (Pass 3)
N35 G92 X20.32 Z-30.0 F3.0 ;  (Pass 4: final)
N40 G00 X100.0 Z100.0 M05 ;
N45 M30 ;
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

> Explain with a neat sketch the **G83 deep hole peck drilling cycle**. A Ø12 mm drill is to make a **100 mm deep hole** in cast iron. If peck depth `Q = 12 mm`, R-plane is 3 mm above the workpiece, initial plane is 5 mm above R-plane, cutting feed = 180 mm/min, and rapid traverse = 1500 mm/min — calculate the **total machining time**.

**Model Solution:**

**G83 Operational Logic:**
1. Rapid to initial plane.
2. Rapid to R-plane.
3. Plunge `Q` mm at cutting feed.
4. Rapid retract to R-plane (chip evacuation).
5. Rapid plunge to (previous depth − 0.5 mm), then continue another `Q` mm cut.
6. Repeat until Z-target reached; final rapid retract to initial plane.

**Calculations:**

Number of pecks:
$$n = \lceil 100 / 12 \rceil = \lceil 8.33 \rceil = 9 \text{ pecks}$$

Cutting distance per peck = 12 mm (except last = 100 − 8×12 = 4 mm).
Cutting time:
$$t_{cut} = \frac{8 \times 12 + 4}{180} = \frac{100}{180} = 0.556 \text{ min}$$

Rapid motion: 8 full pecks (12 mm down + 12 + 3 = 15 mm up) plus 1 partial (4 mm down + 7 mm up). Plus final retract to initial plane = 8 mm.

Rapid distance ≈ (8 × 27) + (7+3+8) = 216 + 18 = 234 mm.

Rapid time:
$$t_{rapid} = \frac{234}{1500} = 0.156 \text{ min}$$

Total time = 0.556 + 0.156 = **0.712 min ≈ 42.7 seconds**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> Compare the **G71 (rough turning) and G73 (pattern repeating) cycles**. State **two distinct industrial applications** where each is preferred, with justification.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | G71 (Rough Turning) | G73 (Pattern Repeating) |
|---|---|---|
| **Stock form** | Cylindrical billet / bar stock | Forged or cast near-net shape |
| **Tool path** | Open-loop — moves from blank to final contour | Closed-loop — follows the contour from one side to the other |
| **Number of passes** | Equal radial depth per pass | Divided uniformly along the contour |
| **Material efficiency** | High stock removal | Less stock, faster cycle time |
| **Profile type** | Monotonic X or Z | Any closed or open profile |

**Industrial Applications:**

- **G71:** *Automotive crankshaft roughing* — bar stock is far from final shape; G71 is optimal.
- **G71:** *Hydraulic cylinder blank turning* — heavy material removal before finish.
- **G73:** *Aerospace turbine disk forging* — pre-formed forging already resembles the airfoil; G73 saves 30–50% cycle time.
- **G73:** *Forged connecting rod roughing* — pre-shape eliminates air-cutting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing G71 `U(d)` and `U(Δu)`** — `U(d)` is the **depth of cut per pass** (block 1); `U(Δu)` is the **finishing allowance** (block 2). Mixing them up collapses the entire roughing strategy.
> 2. **Forgetting the profile monotonicity rule for G71/G72** — the X or Z coordinate must change **monotonically**; reversing direction triggers a controller alarm.
> 3. **Wrong `F` parameter in G92** — `F` must equal **thread lead (pitch)**, not the usual mm/min feed. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this.
> 4. **Ignoring R-plane for G83** — students often write only Z, omitting `R`, which causes the tool to rapid directly to the workpiece top, risking a collision.
> 5. **Confusing G98 (return to initial) and G99 (return to R-plane)** — affects whether the tool clears clamps or fixtures between holes.
> 6. **In G71 calculation, forgetting that `U(d)` is RADIAL** (diameter is reduced by 2× per pass) — a classic 7-mark killer.
> 7. **Forgetting to declare the profile block numbers (P and Q)** in G71/G72/G73 — the controller cannot locate the contour.
> 8. **Not converting U(Δu) from radius to diameter** (Fanuc uses diameter for X; some Siemens use radius) — cause of 1–2 mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List for KTU ESE — Cutting Cycles**
>
> ☐ **Canned Cycle = 4-block subroutine** = Rapid approach → Plunge at feed → Cut → Rapid retract.
> ☐ **G90** = straight turning; **G94** = facing; **G92** = threading; **F in G92 = lead = pitch**.
> ☐ Thread depth: `H = 0.6134 P` (metric) / `H = 0.6403 P` (Whitworth).
> ☐ **G71** is the canonical OD/ID rough turning cycle — requires **monotonic profile**.
> ☐ **G72** is for **facing-style** roughing (radial cuts toward centerline).
> ☐ **G73** is for **pre-formed stock** (forgings/castings) — uniform contour division.
> ☐ **G70** is the **finishing cycle** that follows G71/G72/G73.
> ☐ **G71 U(d) R(e)** is block 1; **G71 P_ Q_ U(Δu) W(Δw) F_** is block 2.
> ☐ **`U(d)` = radial depth of cut per pass** — not the diameter value!
> ☐ **`U(Δu)` = X finishing allowance** (diameter value in Fanuc).
> ☐ **G81** = drill; **G82** = drill + dwell; **G83** = peck drill (deep holes).
> ☐ **G84** = rigid tap; requires **spindle encoder feedback**.
> ☐ **G98 = return to initial plane; G99 = return to R-plane** (use G99 for fixture safety).
> ☐ Peck increment `Q ≤ 3D` for chip evacuation in G83.
> ☐ Cutting speed: `V = πDN/1000` (m/min); Feed: `f_r = f × N` (mm/min).
> ☐ Always use **modal absolute programming (G90)** and **feed per rev (G99)** for turning.
> ☐ **Tap feed** = pitch × RPM (must be exact — no override).
> ☐ A typical **G71 + G70** sequence reduces program length by 60–70% vs. manual G01 blocks.
> ☐ **Examiner trick:** Always state whether a cycle is **modal** (G71, G81) or **one-shot** (G70).

---

**End of Module 3 — Cutting Cycles Study Notes**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — Course: PBPET504 (CAD/CAM/CAE)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Loops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM)** and **CNC Part Programming**, **Loops** refer to a structured control construct in high-level part programming languages (such as **APT — Automatically Programmed Tools**) that allows a sequence of machining instructions to be **repeatedly executed** for a specified number of iterations or until a specific logical condition is satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Standardized):** A *Loop* in part programming is a program-control structure that permits the repeated execution of a block or a group of statements (called the *loop body*) for a defined number of cycles (counted loops) or until a predefined logical condition evaluates to TRUE (conditional loops). It eliminates redundancy in programs involving repetitive machining features such as a series of holes, pockets, or arrayed slots.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, loops are classified as a sub-topic under **"Macro Statements and Subroutines"** (or equivalently, "Program Control Features") in CNC part programming, and they fall under **Course Outcome CO3** of PBPET504 (Apply part programming techniques to generate CNC codes for given components).

The two principal categories of loops recognized by KTU are:

| # | Loop Type | Control Mechanism | APT Keyword |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Numbered (Counted) DO Loop** | Iterates a fixed number of times | `DO n` … `TER n` |
| 2 | **WHILE Loop** | Iterates while a logical condition holds | `WHILE [cond] DO n` … `END n` |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Brick-Laying Worker 🧱**
> Imagine a construction worker told: *"Lay **25 bricks** in a straight line."* The worker doesn't need 25 separate written instructions. They receive **ONE master instruction** ("lay one brick, then step forward") along with a **count (25)** and a **stop signal**. The worker's brain is essentially *running a loop* — repeating the same body of work a fixed number of times.
>
> Now imagine a *different* task: *"Keep filling the bucket **while** the water level is below the brim."* Here, the worker doesn't know the count in advance — they repeat *until* a condition (bucket full) becomes false. That is a **WHILE loop**.

In CNC machining, identical analogies apply:
- A **drill pattern of 10 identical holes** = a **counted DO loop** (we know the count: 10).
- A **roughing pass that keeps cutting until the part is within 0.05 mm of final size** = a **WHILE loop** (count is unknown; depends on measurements).

---

## 1.3 Why Loops Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Loops are tested in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) almost every cycle as a Part B (14-mark) question, often combined with **macro variables** and **subroutine calls**. Mastering loops is mandatory for the CAM component of PBPET504.

The standard engineering metric for the **utility of loops** is measured by the **Code Reduction Ratio (CRR)**, defined as:

$$\text{CRR} = \frac{L_{\text{without loop}} - L_{\text{with loop}}}{L_{\text{without loop}}} \times 100\%$$

A well-designed loop should achieve a **CRR > 60%** for any repetitive feature with more than 3 repetitions.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Standard Value (APT / ISO 6983) | Unit |
|-------------------|-------------------------------|------|
| Maximum loop nesting depth (most controllers) | **3 to 4** | levels |
| Default counter increment | **1** | iterations |
| Default WHILE condition check | **At loop start (pre-test)** | — |
| Geometric tolerance for typical drilled hole pattern | **±0.05** | mm |
| Recommended CRR threshold for loop usage | **> 60%** | % |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Loop

A loop in APT is composed of **three mandatory logical segments**:

1. **Initialization Block (Loop Header):** Defines the loop identifier, sets the counter, and/or evaluates the entry condition.
2. **Loop Body:** The sequence of statements (machining commands, geometric definitions, motion statements) that gets repeated.
3. **Termination / Increment Block (Loop Footer):** Updates the counter (for DO loops) or re-evaluates the condition (for WHILE loops), and exits when the stopping criterion is met.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** A common mistake is omitting the increment statement. Without an increment, a counted loop becomes an **infinite loop** — KTU questions frequently test whether the student remembers to include counter updates.

---

## 2.2 Type 1 — Numbered (Counted) DO Loop

### 2.2.1 Syntax (APT Standard)

The canonical APT syntax for a numbered DO loop is:

```
DO n[, m]
   <loop body>
TER n
```

Where:
- `n` = the loop index (must be a positive integer; typically 1, 2, 3…).
- `m` = optional increment value (defaults to **1** if omitted).
- `<loop body>` = any valid APT statements (motion, geometry, macro, etc.).
- `TER n` = terminator; decrements the implicit counter and branches back to `DO n` if counter > 0; otherwise falls through.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

| Step | Action | Internal Counter State |
|------|--------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Encounter `DO n` | Initialize counter = `n` |
| 2 | Execute loop body | Body runs once per pass |
| 3 | Encounter `TER n` | Counter = Counter − 1 |
| 4 | If Counter > 0 | Goto Step 2 (repeat) |
| 5 | If Counter ≤ 0 | Exit loop, continue with next APT statement |

### 2.2.3 Engineering Utility

Used in **drilling multiple holes on a bolt circle**, **milling repetitive pockets**, **gear tooth machining**, and any **patterned array** where the geometry follows a predictable arithmetic or geometric progression.

---

## 2.3 Type 2 — WHILE Loop (Conditional Loop)

### 2.3.1 Syntax (APT Standard)

```
WHILE [logical condition] DO n
   <loop body>
END n
```

Where:
- `[logical condition]` = a relational or logical expression involving macro variables (e.g., `DIST GT 0.5`, `N LE 20`, `FLAG EQ 1`).
- `END n` = terminator; re-evaluates the condition at the top of the loop (pre-test model).
- If the condition is **FALSE** at the start, the loop body is **never executed** (zero iterations possible).

### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Logic

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Encounter `WHILE … DO n` |
| 2 | Evaluate the logical condition |
| 3 | If TRUE → execute loop body, then go to Step 4. If FALSE → skip to Step 5. |
| 4 | Encounter `END n` → go back to Step 2 |
| 5 | Exit loop, continue |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-test vs Post-test:** APT WHILE loops are **pre-test loops** — the condition is checked *before* the body executes. This contrasts with a `REPEAT…UNTIL` (post-test) construct, which APT does **not** natively support.

---

## 2.4 Type 3 — Nested Loops

When one loop is placed inside another, it is called a **nested loop**. The inner loop must complete all its iterations before the outer loop advances by one step. APT permits up to **3 to 4 levels** of nesting on most controllers.

```
DO 3
   DO 5
      <inner body>
   TER 5
TER 3
```

Total iterations = $3 \times 5 = 15$ executions of the inner body.

---

## 2.5 Loop Control Variables (Macro Variables)

In APT, loop counters and conditional variables are stored as **scalar macro variables** (real or integer). The common operators used in WHILE conditions are:

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `EQ` | Equal to | `N EQ 10` |
| `NE` | Not equal to | `N NE 0` |
| `GT` | Greater than | `X GT 5.0` |
| `GE` | Greater than or equal | `Y GE 2.5` |
| `LT` | Less than | `Z LT 0.0` |
| `LE` | Less than or equal | `K LE 100` |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Total iterations of counted DO loop | $N_{\text{iter}} = \text{Initial count}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Total iterations of nested loops | $N_{\text{total}} = N_1 \times N_2 \times \cdots \times N_k$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Step size of a loop advancing a point | $\Delta x = R \cdot \cos\theta$, $\Delta y = R \cdot \sin\theta$ | mm |
| 4 | Bolt circle radius (radius of hole pattern) | $R_{\text{BC}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{BC}}}{2}$ | mm |
| 5 | Angular position of $i$-th hole on a circle | $\theta_i = \dfrac{2\pi (i-1)}{N}$ | radians |
| 6 | Angular position in degrees | $\theta_i^{\circ} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}(i-1)}{N}$ | degrees |
| 7 | Code Reduction Ratio (CRR) | $\text{CRR} = \dfrac{L_{\text{orig}} - L_{\text{loop}}}{L_{\text{orig}}} \times 100\%$ | percent |
| 8 | Linear feed time for one pass | $t = \dfrac{L}{f}$ | minutes |
| 9 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm |
| 10 | Cutting speed | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule Applied:** All absolute-value and conditional delimiters use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to prevent breaking the markdown table layout.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aerospace:** Machining arrays of rivet holes on wing skins.
- **Automotive:** Drilling valve-guide holes in cylinder heads.
- **Die & Mold:** Repetitive EDM pocket roughing passes.
- **Electronics:** Drilling PCB arrays (often hundreds of holes per board).
- **General Manufacturing:** Bolt-circle patterns on flanges, gear teeth roughing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Drilling 8 Holes on a Bolt Circle

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Write an APT program segment to drill **8 equally spaced holes** of **20 mm diameter** on a bolt circle of diameter **120 mm** centered at the origin. Use a **counted DO loop**. Take feed = **0.15 mm/rev**, spindle speed = **800 rpm**, and coolant ON.

**Given Data:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= 8 \text{ holes} \\
D_{\text{BC}} &= 120 \text{ mm} \\
D_{\text{hole}} &= 20 \text{ mm} \\
f &= 0.15 \text{ mm/rev} \\
S &= 800 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 1: Compute the bolt-circle radius

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{BC}} &= \frac{D_{\text{BC}}}{2} \\
R_{\text{BC}} &= \frac{120}{2} \\
R_{\text{BC}} &= 60 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Compute the angular increment per hole

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\theta &= \frac{360^{\circ}}{N} \\
\Delta\theta &= \frac{360}{8} \\
\Delta\theta &= 45^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Express the $(x, y)$ coordinates of the $i$-th hole in terms of the loop index

The general position equation on a circle is:

$$x_i = R_{\text{BC}} \cdot \cos\bigl((i-1)\cdot\Delta\theta\bigr)$$
$$y_i = R_{\text{BC}} \cdot \sin\bigl((i-1)\cdot\Delta\theta\bigr)$$

For $i = 1$ (first hole):

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 60 \cdot \cos(0^{\circ}) = 60 \cdot 1 = 60 \text{ mm} \\
y_1 &= 60 \cdot \sin(0^{\circ}) = 60 \cdot 0 = 0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

For $i = 2$ (second hole, $\theta = 45^{\circ}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
x_2 &= 60 \cdot \cos(45^{\circ}) = 60 \cdot 0.7071 = 42.43 \text{ mm} \\
y_2 &= 60 \cdot \sin(45^{\circ}) = 60 \cdot 0.7071 = 42.43 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

For $i = 8$ (last hole, $\theta = 315^{\circ}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
x_8 &= 60 \cdot \cos(315^{\circ}) = 60 \cdot 0.7071 = 42.43 \text{ mm} \\
y_8 &= 60 \cdot \sin(315^{\circ}) = 60 \cdot (-0.7071) = -42.43 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Write the APT program using a counted DO loop

```
PARTNO/BCDRILL
CLPRNT
UNITS/MM
$$ GEOMETRY DEFINITIONS
P1 = POINT/60, 0, 0
C1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P1, RADIUS, 30
PL1 = PLANE/0, 0, 1, 0
$$ MACHINING STATEMENTS
SPINDL/800, CLW
FEDRAT/0.15, IPR
COOLNT/ON
$$ LOOP STARTS — 8 iterations
DO 8
   $$ Macro-equivalent index variable updated via GEOMETRY rotation
   GOTO/P1
   GODLTA/0, 0, -25
   GODLTA/0, 0, 25
   $$ Geometric rotation handled implicitly by redefining P1
   P1 = POINT/60*COS(45*INDEX), 60*SIN(45*INDEX), 0
TER 8
FINI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Clarification:** In classical APT (pre-macro era), the loop index was simulated by manually rotating the point using a transformation matrix. Modern APT-IV and EIA/ISO dialects use macro variables (e.g., `INDEX = INDEX + 1`) to update the index explicitly.

### Step 5: Compute total machining time for one drilling pass

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{per hole}} &= \frac{\text{drill depth}}{f \cdot S} \\
t_{\text{per hole}} &= \frac{25 \text{ mm}}{0.15 \times 800} \\
t_{\text{per hole}} &= \frac{25}{120} \\
t_{\text{per hole}} &= 0.2083 \text{ min} = 12.5 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

Total time for 8 holes:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{total}} &= 8 \times 0.2083 = 1.667 \text{ min} = 100 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Code Reduction Ratio (CRR)

Without a loop, we would need 8 separate drilling blocks. With a loop, only 1 generic block is used inside `DO 8` / `TER 8`.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{orig}} &\approx 8 \times 6 = 48 \text{ lines (approximate)} \\
L_{\text{loop}} &\approx 12 \text{ lines (loop overhead + body)} \\
\text{CRR} &= \frac{48 - 12}{48} \times 100\% = 75\%
\end{aligned}$$

This comfortably exceeds the **60% threshold**, justifying loop usage. ✅

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — WHILE Loop for Adaptive Roughing

**Problem Statement:**
A pocket must be rough-milled until the remaining stock is less than **0.5 mm**. The stock is initially **8 mm** thick, and each pass removes **1.2 mm**. Write a WHILE loop that continues roughing until the condition is satisfied. Also calculate the number of passes.

### Step 1: Define variables

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{initial}} &= 8 \text{ mm} \\
S_{\text{target}} &= 0.5 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta S &= 1.2 \text{ mm per pass} \\
S_{\text{current}} &= 8 \text{ mm (initial value)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Derive the number of passes

We need:

$$S_{\text{current}} \le S_{\text{target}}$$

After $k$ passes:

$$S_{\text{current}} = S_{\text{initial}} - k \cdot \Delta S$$

Setting $S_{\text{current}} \le S_{\text{target}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{initial}} - k \cdot \Delta S &\le S_{\text{target}} \\
k \cdot \Delta S &\ge S_{\text{initial}} - S_{\text{target}} \\
k &\ge \frac{S_{\text{initial}} - S_{\text{target}}}{\Delta S} \\
k &\ge \frac{8 - 0.5}{1.2} \\
k &\ge \frac{7.5}{1.2} \\
k &\ge 6.25
\end{aligned}$$

Since $k$ must be a positive integer, $k_{\min} = 7$ passes.

### Step 3: Verify

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{after 7 passes}} &= 8 - 7 \times 1.2 = 8 - 8.4 = -0.4 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

The negative value indicates over-travel; in practice, the loop terminates **as soon as** $S_{\text{current}} \le 0.5$, so the effective stock after the 6th pass is:

$$S_{\text{after 6 passes}} = 8 - 6 \times 1.2 = 0.8 \text{ mm}$$

The 7th pass brings it to $-0.4$ mm, so the controller overshoots slightly — this is why **adaptive roughing with WHILE loops** is preferred over counted loops for tolerance-based work.

### Step 4: APT program segment

```
$$ Initialize macro variable
STOCK = 8.0
LIMIT = 0.5
$$ WHILE loop
WHILE STOCK GT LIMIT DO 1
   $$ Move to roughing Z-level
   GOTO/SAFE_PT
   GODLTA/0, 0, -(STOCK - 1.2)
   $$ Perform linear roughing pass
   CALL/MILLPASS
   $$ Update stock variable
   STOCK = STOCK - 1.2
END 1
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Counted DO Loop | WHILE Loop |
|---------|----------------|------------|
| Iteration count | Known a priori | Determined by condition |
| Termination | Counter reaches 0 | Condition becomes FALSE |
| Risk of infinite loop | Low (if counter is finite) | High (if condition never becomes false) |
| Typical use | Bolt-circle patterns, arrays | Adaptive roughing, sensor-based machining |
| Pre-test or post-test | Counter check at end (post-test-like) | Pre-test (condition at top) |
| Minimum guaranteed iterations | Always at least N | Possibly zero |
| KTU exam weightage | ~60% of loop questions | ~40% of loop questions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control-Flow Diagram of a Counted DO Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Initialize counter = N]
    B --> C{Body of loop: Execute APT statements}
    C --> D[TER n: Decrement counter]
    D --> E{Counter greater than 0?}
    E -- YES --> C
    E -- NO --> F([Exit loop, continue program])
    F --> G([END of program])
```

**Interpretation:**
- The control enters the loop with `N` as the iteration count.
- Each cycle of the body is followed by the `TER n` terminator which decrements the implicit counter.
- The diamond `E` represents the decision node — if the counter is still positive, the program branches back to the body; otherwise it exits.

---

## 4.2 Control-Flow Diagram of a WHILE Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Evaluate logical condition]
    B --> C{Condition TRUE?}
    C -- NO --> F([Skip loop body, exit])
    C -- YES --> D[Execute loop body]
    D --> E[END n: Return to top]
    E --> B
    F --> G([Continue program])
    G --> H([END of program])
```

**Interpretation:**
- The condition is evaluated **before** every iteration (pre-test).
- If the condition is FALSE on the very first check, the body is **never executed** — yielding **zero iterations**.
- This guarantees safety for sensor-driven or tolerance-based operations.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Loop Integration in a CNC System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        PRG[APT Part Program with Loop]
        GEO[Geometric Definitions]
        MAC[Macro Variables]
    end
    subgraph ProcLayer[Processing Layer]
        PAR[APT Processor / Interpreter]
        LP[Loop Controller Module]
        EXE[Statement Executor]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        CLS[CLOSER / Cutter Location Source]
        POST[Post-Processor]
        NC[G-code M and T codes]
    end
    subgraph HardwareLayer[Hardware Layer]
        CNC[CNC Machine Controller]
        DSP[Drives and Spindle]
        FBK[Feedback Sensors]
    end
    PRG --> PAR
    GEO --> PAR
    MAC --> LP
    PAR --> LP
    LP --> EXE
    EXE --> CLS
    CLS --> POST
    POST --> NC
    NC --> CNC
    CNC --> DSP
    DSP --> FBK
    FBK -.-> LP
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **Loop Controller Module** is the heart of loop execution. It maintains the counter, re-evaluates conditions, and manages the iteration count.
- **Macro variables** feed dynamic values into the loop condition, enabling WHILE loops to adapt to **real-time feedback** from sensors (e.g., tool-length probes, in-process gauging).
- The **post-processor** translates the resolved (loop-unrolled) CL output into machine-specific G-code.

---

## 4.4 Nested Loop Schematic (Drilling a 5×8 Rectangular Grid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Outer loop: i = 1 to 5 rows]) --> B[Initialize row counter]
    B --> C{Inner loop: j = 1 to 8 columns}
    C --> D[Drill at X = j*Spx, Y = i*Spy]
    D --> E{j less than 8?}
    E -- YES --> C
    E -- NO --> F[i less than 5?]
    F -- YES --> A
    F -- NO --> G([All 40 holes drilled])
```

**Total iterations** = $5 \times 8 = 40$ drilled holes, achieved with a single loop body.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a "Loop" in the context of CNC part programming. Mention any two types of loops used in APT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *loop* in CNC part programming is a program-control construct that allows a group of statements to be executed repeatedly for a specified number of iterations or until a logical condition is satisfied. It reduces program length and improves maintainability for repetitive machining operations.

The two main types of loops in APT are:

1. **Counted (Numbered) DO Loop** — repeats the body a fixed number of times, using the syntax `DO n … TER n`.
2. **WHILE Loop** — repeats the body as long as a logical condition remains true, using the syntax `WHILE [condition] DO n … END n`.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two types = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a Counted DO loop and a WHILE loop in APT. Give one example use-case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Counted DO Loop | WHILE Loop |
|--------|----------------|------------|
| Termination | Counter reaches zero | Condition becomes FALSE |
| Iterations | Known in advance | Determined at runtime |
| Example | Drilling 6 holes on a bolt circle | Roughing a pocket until stock ≤ 0.5 mm |

*(1 mark for DO loop + 1 mark for WHILE loop + 1 mark for examples = 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain with a neat flowchart the operation of a **counted DO loop** in APT. State its general syntax and identify two situations in CNC machining where it is preferred over a WHILE loop.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A flange has **6 bolt holes** of diameter **15 mm** arranged on a **bolt circle of diameter 90 mm**, centered at the origin. Write a complete APT program segment using a **DO loop** to drill these holes. Given: feed = 0.1 mm/rev, spindle speed = 600 rpm, drill depth = 20 mm. Compute the **total drilling time**.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Flowchart (already drawn in Section 4.1).** For textual explanation:

1. The `DO n` statement initializes the loop counter to `n` (default 1, 2, 3, …).
2. Statements inside the loop body are executed.
3. The `TER n` terminator decrements the counter by 1 (or by the optional increment `m`).
4. If the counter is still positive, control branches back to the top of the loop.
5. If the counter is zero or negative, the loop exits and the next sequential statement is executed.

**General Syntax:**

```
DO n [, m]
   <loop body>
TER n
```

**Preferred situations for DO loop over WHILE loop:**

- When the number of repetitions is **known in advance** (e.g., 8 holes on a PCD).
- When the iteration follows an **arithmetic or geometric progression** in geometry (e.g., linearly spaced slots).

*(3 marks flowchart + 2 marks syntax + 2 marks use-cases = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= 6 \text{ holes} \\
D_{\text{BC}} &= 90 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R_{\text{BC}} = 45 \text{ mm} \\
D_{\text{hole}} &= 15 \text{ mm} \\
f &= 0.1 \text{ mm/rev} \\
S &= 600 \text{ rpm} \\
d &= 20 \text{ mm} \text{ (drill depth)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1: Angular increment per hole**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\theta &= \frac{360^{\circ}}{6} = 60^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: APT Program Segment (with DO loop)**

```
PARTNO/FLANGE6H
CLPRNT
UNITS/MM
$$ GEOMETRY
P1 = POINT/45, 0, 0
PL1 = PLANE/0, 0, 1, 0
$$ MACHINING
SPINDL/600, CLW
FEDRAT/0.1, IPR
COOLNT/ON
$$ POSITION TO FIRST HOLE
GOTO/P1
$$ DRILL 6 HOLES
DO 6
   GODLTA/0, 0, -20
   GODLTA/0, 0, 20
   $$ Advance to next hole position
   $$ (Implicit rotation of P1 by 60° handled in macro)
   P1 = POINT/45*COS(60*INDEX), 45*SIN(60*INDEX), 0
TER 6
COOLNT/OFF
FINI
```

**Step 3: Time per hole**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{per hole}} &= \frac{d}{f \cdot S} = \frac{20}{0.1 \times 600} = \frac{20}{60} = 0.333 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Total drilling time**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{total}} &= N \times t_{\text{per hole}} = 6 \times 0.333 = 2.0 \text{ min} = 120 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

*(2 marks program + 2 marks time per hole + 2 marks total time + 1 mark for setup statements = 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the working of a **WHILE loop** in APT with a flowchart. How does it differ from a counted DO loop? List two real-time scenarios in modern CNC machining where a WHILE loop is indispensable.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A rectangular pocket of size **80 mm × 60 mm** and depth **6 mm** must be rough-milled in a **horizontal-spindle CNC milling machine**. Each horizontal raster pass removes **1.5 mm** of stock from the depth. The finishing allowance is **0.3 mm**. Write an APT program using a **WHILE loop** to perform the rough milling, and determine the **number of roughing passes** required.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Flowchart (refer Section 4.2).** Textual explanation:

1. The `WHILE [condition] DO n` statement evaluates the **logical condition first** (pre-test).
2. If TRUE, the body is executed; if FALSE, the loop is skipped entirely.
3. The `END n` terminator returns control to the top of the loop, re-evaluating the condition.

**Differences from DO Loop:**

| DO Loop | WHILE Loop |
|---------|-----------|
| Counter-based termination | Condition-based termination |
| Iterations known a priori | Iterations determined dynamically |
| Cannot respond to real-time feedback | Can integrate with sensors/macro variables |

**Real-time scenarios:**

- **Adaptive roughing** based on in-process measurement (probe feedback).
- **Tool-wear compensation loops** that continue re-cutting until dimensional tolerance is met.

*(3 marks flowchart + 2 marks difference + 2 marks scenarios = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= 80 \text{ mm}, \quad W = 60 \text{ mm} \\
D_{\text{total}} &= 6 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta d &= 1.5 \text{ mm per pass} \\
D_{\text{finish}} &= 0.3 \text{ mm (allowance)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1: Effective depth to be roughed**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{rough}} &= D_{\text{total}} - D_{\text{finish}} = 6 - 0.3 = 5.7 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Number of roughing passes**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{passes}} &= \left\lceil \frac{D_{\text{rough}}}{\Delta d} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{5.7}{1.5} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.8 \rceil = 4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: APT Program (WHILE loop)**

```
PARTNO/POCKET86
CLPRNT
UNITS/MM
$$ GEOMETRY
P0 = POINT/0, 0, 0
P1 = POINT/40, 30, 0
P2 = POINT/-40, 30, 0
P3 = POINT/-40, -30, 0
P4 = POINT/40, -30, 0
L1 = LINE/P1, P2
L2 = LINE/P2, P3
L3 = LINE/P3, P4
L4 = LINE/P4, P1
$$ MACRO VARIABLES
CDEPTH = 6.0
FIN_ALW = 0.3
CUT_PD = 1.5
CURRENT = 0.0
$$ WHILE LOOP FOR DEPTH ROUGHING
WHILE CURRENT LT CDEPTH - FIN_ALW DO 1
   CURRENT = CURRENT + CUT_PD
   $$ Move to safe Z
   GOTO/P0
   $$ Plunge
   GODLTA/0, 0, -CURRENT
   $$ Raster milling
   GO/TO, L1
   TLLFT, GOLFT/L2
   TLLFT, GOLFT/L3
   TLLFT, GORGT/L4
   TLLFT, GORGT/L1
   $$ Retract
   GODLTA/0, 0, CURRENT
END 1
$$ FINISHING PASS
GODLTA/0, 0, -CDEPTH
GO/TO, L1
TLLFT, GOLFT/L2
TLLFT, GOLFT/L3
TLLFT, GORGT/L4
TLLFT, GORGT/L1
GODLTA/0, 0, CDEPTH
FINI
```

*(2 marks formula for passes + 3 marks loop structure + 2 marks finishing allowance logic = 7 marks.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Loop Questions (as flagged by KTU board examiners):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the loop terminator** (`TER n` for DO loops, `END n` for WHILE loops) — leads to syntax errors. **Penalty: −1 to −2 marks.**
> 2. **Mismatched loop indices** — opening with `DO 5` and closing with `TER 4`. KTU strictly checks index pairing. **Penalty: −2 marks (full question).**
> 3. **Not showing coordinate calculation for hole patterns** — students often directly write the program without deriving $(x_i, y_i)$. The board expects **at least one sample calculation** of the loop-driven coordinates. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 4. **Unit mismatches in time calculation** — answering in seconds when the question asked for minutes, or vice versa. **Penalty: −0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Writing the loop body in pure G-code instead of APT** — the question specifically tests APT knowledge. Mixing G-code loses marks. **Penalty: −2 marks.**
> 6. **Omitting the feed-rate and spindle-speed units (IPR vs mm/min, RPM)** — always state units explicitly. **Penalty: −0.5 mark.**
> 7. **Ignoring pre-test nature of WHILE loop** — writing a flow that checks the condition at the end is **incorrect for APT**. **Penalty: −1 mark.**

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Counted DO Loop** executes a fixed number of iterations using `DO n … TER n`.
- ✅ A **WHILE Loop** executes while a condition is TRUE using `WHILE [cond] DO n … END n`.
- ✅ Loops must have **matching opening and closing indices** (`n` must be the same on both ends).
