- ✅ **Nested loops** multiply iteration counts: $N_{\text{total}} = N_1 \times N_2 \times \cdots$.
- ✅ The **WHILE loop is pre-test** — zero iterations are possible if the condition is initially FALSE.
- ✅ APT does **not** support a native `REPEAT…UNTIL` (post-test) construct.
- ✅ For **bolt-circle patterns**, the loop index $i$ gives $\theta_i = \dfrac{360^{\circ}(i-1)}{N}$ and $(x_i, y_i) = (R\cos\theta_i, R\sin\theta_i)$.
- ✅ The **Code Reduction Ratio (CRR)** quantifies loop benefit; aim for **CRR > 60%**.
- ✅ For **adaptive roughing**, prefer **WHILE loops**; for **fixed patterns**, prefer **counted DO loops**.
- ✅ Always specify **units explicitly** in feed (`IPR` or `MMPM`) and spindle speed (`RPM` or `SFM`).
- ✅ Maximum recommended **nesting depth** is **3–4 levels** to avoid controller memory issues.
- ✅ Loop indices in APT are conventionally integers **1, 2, 3, …** (some controllers also accept 0).
- ✅ For WHILE loops, **macro variables** (`STOCK`, `LIMIT`, etc.) drive the condition dynamically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sub program and macros
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sub program and macros

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Subprograms in CNC Machining — Formal Definition

A **subprogram** is a self-contained, separately written NC (Numerical Control) program that performs a frequently repeated machining sequence and is stored in the controller memory under a unique program number (address **O** or **Oxxxx**). It is invoked (called) from a main program (or from another subprogram) and, upon completion, returns control automatically to the calling program at the next sequential block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):**
> A subprogram is a separate, reusable block of NC code that allows a common machining pattern (e.g., a bolt-hole circle, a pocket, a drill pattern) to be written **once** and executed **many times**, reducing program length, eliminating redundancy, and simplifying editing.

The two M-codes that govern subprogram flow on FANUC-style controllers (and most controllers adopted by KTU-affiliated labs) are:

- **M98** — Subprogram Call
- **M99** — Subprogram End / Return

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe Card" View

Imagine you are a head chef writing a master recipe book for a banquet of **12 identical desserts**:

- **Main Program** = the master recipe book
- **Subprogram** = a single recipe card for "Chocolate Mousse" stored in a separate index
- **M98** = the instruction *"Pull the Chocolate Mousse card from the index and execute it"*
- **M99** = the implicit instruction *"After finishing, put the card back and return to the master book at the next line"*

Instead of writing the mousse recipe 12 times (one for each plate), you write it once and call it 12 times. That is exactly what a subprogram does for repetitive machining features like **bolt-hole circles, equally spaced slots, or stepped pockets**.

---

## 1.3 Macros in CNC — Formal Definition

A **macro** (also called a **custom macro**, **parametric program**, or **user macro**) is a sophisticated NC program that uses **variables, arithmetic expressions, logical operators, conditional branching, and looping structures** to produce flexible, parameter-driven machining cycles. Where a plain subprogram is a "static" reusable block, a macro is a "smart" reusable block that can compute geometry, make decisions, and adapt at run-time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Keyword:** Macros transform NC programming from a *fixed-coordinate* (G-code) paradigm into a *parametric* paradigm — the same program can machine **a 10 mm pocket or a 47.5 mm pocket** simply by changing input variables, with no edits to the geometry blocks.

Macro capability is enabled by a **Macro Option** in the controller. The standard FANUC Custom Macro B (and its modern equivalents) is the de facto reference for KTU examinations.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Spreadsheet Function" View

If a subprogram is a recipe card, then a **macro is a programmable spreadsheet formula**:

- A subprogram has fixed numbers (drill at X10 Y20, X10 Y40, X10 Y60 …).
- A macro has **variables** (#1 = starting angle, #2 = number of holes, #3 = radius). The controller computes each hole position at run-time using trigonometry.

So instead of typing 8 hole positions, you tell the controller: *"Place #2 holes evenly on a circle of radius #3, starting at angle #1."* The macro does the math. This is the bridge between **manual NC programming** and **parametric CAM-driven programming**.

> [!TIP]
> Quick differentiator for the exam:
> - **Subprogram** = reusable *fixed* sequence → reduces typing.
> - **Macro** = reusable *parametric* sequence → reduces typing **and** enables family-of-parts machining.

---

## 1.5 Standard Symbols & Identifiers at a Glance

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **O** or **Oxxxx** | Program number | `O0001`, `O0100` |
| **M98** | Call subprogram | `M98 P0100` |
| **M99** | Return / end subprogram | Last block of subprogram |
| **#** | Variable identifier | `#1`, `#100`, `#3006` |
| **[ ]** | Expression enclosure | `IF [#1 GT 10] GOTO 5` |
| **EQ, NE, GT, LT, GE, LE** | Comparison operators | `IF [#1 EQ 0] GOTO 2` |
| **=** | Assignment | `#3 = #1 + #2` |
| **DO / END** | Loop delimiters | `WHILE [#1 LT 100] DO 1` ... `END 1` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Subprogram Architecture — Operational Flow

A subprogram is structured in **three logical zones**:

1. **Header (optional but recommended)** — Safe start block, work-coordinate setup, spindle/Coolant on.
2. **Body** — The actual machining sequence (drilling, milling, tapping …).
3. **Return Block** — A final block containing **M99**, which transfers control back.

**Call Format (FANUC / KTU lab convention):**

$$\text{M98} \; \text{P}\!\left<\text{Oxxxx}\right> \; \text{Q}\!\left<k\right>$$

where **P** carries the program number of the subprogram to call (e.g., `P0100` → calls program `O0100`), and **Q** is the **number of repetitions** of that subprogram call. If Q is omitted, the subprogram executes exactly once.

> [!NOTE]
> On some controllers, the P value is split: `M98 P50010` could mean *repeat 5 times, program 0010*. The KTU-adopted FANUC convention used in this module is `M98 P0100 L5` or `M98 P0100 Q5` depending on the post-processor — read the question carefully.

**Return Format:**

$$\text{M99} \; \text{P}\!\left<\text{N}\right>$$

If `M99` is used alone, the controller returns to the block immediately after the calling M98 block. If `M99 P5` is used, the controller returns to sequence number N5 anywhere in the calling program — a useful trick for restarting at a specific step.

---

## 2.2 Nested Subprograms

A subprogram may itself call another subprogram, up to a controller-defined nesting limit (typically **4 to 8 levels** on FANUC 0i-M). Each call uses a separate program-number slot in the call stack.

```
Main (O0001)  → calls  O0100
O0100          → calls  O0200   (level 2)
O0200          → calls  O0300   (level 3)
```

When the innermost subprogram finishes (`M99`), control unwinds one level at a time, returning up the call stack.

---

## 2.3 Macro Variables — Three Tiers

| Tier | Range | Scope | Persistence | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Local** | `#1` – `#33` | Reset to *vacant* (≈ 0) on power-up; cleared between main and subprograms when argument-passing is used. | Lost at M30 / power-off | `#1`, `#10` |
| **Common** | `#100` – `#199` (and sometimes `#200` – `#999`) | Shared between main and all subprograms. | Retained even after M30 (if option enabled) | `#100 = 50.0` |
| **System** | `#1000` and above (e.g., `#3006`, `#4001`, `#5221`, `#5021` …) | Read-only or semi-fixed; provide controller-internal data (modal codes, tool offsets, current position, timer, etc.) | Read-only | `#5021` = X-axis machine coordinate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **vacant variable** in macro B evaluates to **0** in arithmetic context and to **"vacant"** (treated as *not equal* to 0) in conditional context. This subtlety is a common KTU exam trap.

---

## 2.4 Macro Operators — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.4.1 Arithmetic & Assignment

$$ \#i \;=\; \#j \; \bigl<op\bigr> \; \#k $$

where the operator $\bigl<op\bigr>$ may be **+** (add), **−** (subtract), **\*** (multiply), **/** (divide), **MOD** (remainder), or **OR/XOR/AND** (bitwise, on some controllers).

### 2.4.2 Trigonometric & Mathematical Functions

| Function | Meaning | Argument Units | Output Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SIN[#x]** | sine | **degrees** (on most FANUC) | dimensionless |
| **COS[#x]** | cosine | **degrees** | dimensionless |
| **TAN[#x]** | tangent | **degrees** | dimensionless |
| **ASIN[#x]** | arcsine | dimensionless | **degrees** |
| **ACOS[#x]** | arccosine | dimensionless | **degrees** |
| **ATAN[#x]** | arctangent (single arg) or ATAN[#,#] (two-arg form) | dimensionless | **degrees** |
| **SQRT[#x]** | square root | any | same as input |
| **ABS[#x]** | absolute value | any | same as input |
| **FIX[#x]** | truncate toward $-\infty$ (floor) | any | integer |
| **FUP[#x]** | round up toward $+\infty$ (ceiling) | any | integer |
| **ROUND[#x]** | round to nearest integer | any | integer |
| **LN[#x]** | natural logarithm | positive | dimensionless |
| **EXP[#x]** | $e^{\#x}$ | any | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **FANUC Convention:** Trig arguments are in **degrees**, not radians. `#30 = SIN[30]` gives **0.5**, not $\sin(30^\circ)$ confusion. Always state unit assumption in the exam.

### 2.4.3 Logical & Comparison Operators (used inside `[ ]`)

| Operator | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **EQ** | equal to |
| **NE** | not equal to |
| **GT** | greater than |
| **LT** | less than |
| **GE** | greater than or equal to |
| **LE** | less than or equal to |

### 2.4.4 Branching and Looping Constructs

| Construct | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Unconditional branch | `GOTO n;` | Jump to sequence number Nn |
| Conditional branch | `IF [condition] GOTO n;` | Conditional jump |
| Conditional loop | `WHILE [condition] DO m; … END m;` | Iterate while condition true |
| Three-way IF | `IF [condition] THEN … ELSE … ENDIF;` | Optional in Macro B+ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Nested loops must use **distinct identifiers** (1, 2, 3 …). DO 1 inside DO 1 is **illegal**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Compact Revision Table)

| # | Construct / Equation | Form |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Subprogram call | $\text{M98 P}\bigl<O_{xxxx}\bigr>\text{Q}\bigl<k\bigr>$ |
| 2 | Subprogram return | $\text{M99 P}\bigl<N_{n}\bigr>$ |
| 3 | Variable assignment | $\#i = \#j \pm \#k$ |
| 4 | Polar to Cartesian (hole-on-circle) | $X = R \cos\theta, \quad Y = R \sin\theta$ |
| 5 | Angle increment in loop | $\theta_{i+1} = \theta_i + \dfrac{360^\circ}{N}$ |
| 6 | Linear interpolation (parametric) | $X_t = X_0 + t\,(X_1 - X_0)$ |
| 7 | Number of loop iterations | $N = \bigl\lfloor (X_{\text{end}} - X_{\text{start}})/\Delta X \bigr\rfloor + 1$ |
| 8 | Trig argument | $\theta$ in **degrees** (FANUC) |
| 9 | System variable for current X | $\#5021$ |
| 10 | System variable for modal G-code | $\#4006$ |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Subprograms** are heavily used for **bolt-hole patterns, family-of-parts, mirrored machining (mirror-image subprograms), and indexing tables** (4th-axis subprograms).
- **Macros** are the foundation of **probing cycles, automatic tool-length measurement, family-of-parts machining, and canned-cycle customization**. Industry-grade probing macros in modern CNCs (Renishaw OMP60, Blum) are essentially custom-macro programs measuring dimensions and adjusting offsets in real time.
- In **KTU lab practice**, students use subprograms for **bolt-hole circle drilling** on the CNC trainer, and macros for **parametric pocket milling** (variable size, depth, and step-over).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Polar Hole-Position Equation (Bolt-Hole Circle)

**Problem statement:** A bolt-hole circle of radius $R$ contains $N$ equally spaced holes. The starting angle (measured CCW from the +X axis) is $\theta_0$. Derive the $(X, Y)$ coordinate of the $i$-th hole.

**Step 1 — Set up the polar coordinate.** In the $XY$-plane, a point on a circle of radius $R$ at angle $\theta$ is given by the standard polar-to-Cartesian conversion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= R \cos\theta \\
Y &= R \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the angle of the $i$-th hole.** Since the $N$ holes divide the full $360^\circ$ into equal sectors, the angular increment is

$$
\Delta\theta \;=\; \dfrac{360^\circ}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the starting angle.** The $i$-th hole (with $i$ indexed from $0$ to $N-1$) is at

$$
\theta_i \;=\; \theta_0 + i \cdot \Delta\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Cartesian pair.** The final per-hole coordinates are

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
X_i &= R \cos\!\bigl(\theta_0 + i \cdot 360^\circ / N\bigr) \\
Y_i &= R \sin\!\bigl(\theta_0 + i \cdot 360^\circ / N\bigr)
\end{aligned}
\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Macro realization.** Using FANUC Custom Macro B with $R$ stored in `#1`, $\theta_0$ in `#2`, and $N$ in `#3`, the iteration body becomes

```
#4 = #2 + #5 * 360.0 / #3      (θ_i)
#6 = #1 * COS[#4]              (X_i)
#7 = #1 * SIN[#4]              (Y_i)
```

where `#5` is the running index $i$ and `#6`, `#7` are written into the positioning block `X#6 Y#7`.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Linear Passes for a Pocket

**Problem statement:** A rectangular pocket of length $L$ must be milled with a tool of diameter $D$, leaving a finishing allowance. The center-to-center step-over is $0.8D$. Derive the integer number of passes.

**Step 1 — Effective step-over.** The lateral distance covered per pass is

$$
S \;=\; 0.8\,D
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric requirement.** The total lateral sweep must at least equal the pocket length $L$:

$$
n \cdot S \;\geq\; L
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the lower bound.**

$$
n \;\geq\; \dfrac{L}{0.8\,D}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the ceiling (use FUP).** Because $n$ must be a positive integer, we apply the ceiling function:

$$
\boxed{\; n \;=\; \text{FUP}\!\left[\dfrac{L}{0.8\,D}\right] \;}
$$

**Step 5 — Macro realization.**

```
#10 = 100.0                   (L = 100 mm)
#11 = 10.0                    (D = 10 mm)
#12 = 0.8 * #11               (S = 8 mm)
#13 = FUP[#10 / #12]          (n = 13 passes)
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Subprogram vs Macro (Side-by-Side Comparison)

| Dimension | **Subprogram** | **Macro** |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | Separate program (Oxxxx) | Either separate program or embedded block |
| **Parameter passing** | None (fixed values) | Via local variables `#1`–`#33` as arguments |
| **Call syntax** | `M98 Pxxxx Qk` | Same call, but with **argument list** (e.g., `M98 P0100 R50.0 A30.0 N8`) |
| **Arithmetic** | None | Full: +, −, ×, ÷, MOD, trig, exponential |
| **Decision-making** | None | `IF`, `WHILE`, `GOTO` |
| **Reusability across part families** | Limited (fixed geometry) | High (parameter-driven) |
| **Programming effort** | Low | Medium to high |
| **Best for** | Identical repeated features | Family-of-parts, probing, custom cycles |
| **Nesting limit** | 4–8 levels | Same (subprograms can be called from within macros) |
| **KTU exam weightage** | 4–6 marks | 8–10 marks |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Macro for Bolt-Hole Circle

**Specification:** Drill 6 equally spaced $\phi 8$ mm holes on a circle of radius **40 mm**, centred at the workpiece origin, starting angle $30^\circ$, depth 10 mm.

**Step 1 — Declare and assign variables.**

```
#1 = 40.0      (radius R)
#2 = 30.0      (starting angle θ0)
#3 = 6         (number of holes N)
#4 = 0         (running index i)
#5 = 10.0      (drill depth)
```

**Step 2 — Initial tool position.** Move to the first hole position using $i = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_0 &= 30^\circ + 0 \cdot (360/6) = 30^\circ \\
X_0 &= 40 \cos 30^\circ = 40 \cdot 0.86603 = 34.641 \\
Y_0 &= 40 \sin 30^\circ = 40 \cdot 0.5 = 20.000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Macro loop body.**

```
WHILE [#4 LT 6] DO 1
    #10 = #2 + #4 * 360.0 / #3                (θ_i)
    #11 = #1 * COS[#10]                      (X_i)
    #12 = #1 * SIN[#10]                      (Y_i)
    G81 X#11 Y#12 Z-[#5] R2.0 F80            (drill cycle)
    #4 = #4 + 1                              (i ← i + 1)
END 1
M99
```

**Step 4 — Verify hole 3 ($i = 3$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_3 &= 30^\circ + 3 \cdot 60^\circ = 210^\circ \\
X_3 &= 40 \cos 210^\circ = 40 \cdot (-\sqrt{3}/2) \approx -34.641 \\
Y_3 &= 40 \sin 210^\circ = 40 \cdot (-0.5) = -20.000
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the expected symmetry — the hole diametrically opposite the starting hole is correctly computed by the macro.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition check:** The polar-to-Cartesian math in this section is exactly the same as the conversion that the controller performs implicitly in a **G68 rotated coordinate system** macro. Knowing the formula means you can verify the controller's output in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Subprogram Call / Return Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start of MAIN program O0001]) --> initA[Work coord G54, Tool change T01 M06]
    initA --> posA[Rapid to safe Z, then to first hole XY]
    posA --> callA{M98 P0100 Q6}
    callA --> subStart([Enter SUBPROGRAM O0100])
    subStart --> machA[Machining sequence: drill / mill / tap]
    machA --> returnChkA{Repeat counter finished?}
    returnChkA -->|No| machA
    returnChkA -->|Yes| retA[M99 Return to MAIN]
    retA --> contA[Next sequential block in MAIN]
    contA --> endA[M30 End of program]
    endA --> stopA([Program halt])
```

## 4.2 Macro Program Flow (Decision + Loop Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start of MACRO program]) --> initB[Assign initial values to variables #1, #2, #3]
    initB --> idxInit[Initialize counter #4 = 0]
    idxInit --> condB{WHILE condition: #4 LT #3}
    condB -->|False| exitB[M99 Return]
    condB -->|True| compB[Compute angle #10 = #2 + #4 * 360 / #3]
    compB --> compX[Compute X = #1 * COS#10]
    compX --> compY[Compute Y = #1 * SIN#10]
    compY --> outputB[Write G-code block using computed X Y]
    outputB --> incB[Increment counter #4 = #4 + 1]
    incB --> condB
```

## 4.3 Comparison Tree — Subprogram vs Macro (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootA([NC Program Reusability]) --> branchA[Subprogram Approach]
    rootA --> branchB[Macro Approach]
    branchA --> noteA1[Static geometry, fixed values]
    branchA --> noteA2[Call: M98 Pxxxx Qk]
    branchA --> noteA3[Return: M99]
    branchB --> noteB1[Parametric, variable-driven]
    branchB --> noteB2[Arithmetic + logic + loops]
    branchB --> noteB3[Call: M98 Pxxxx with argument list]
    branchB --> noteB4[Built-in trig, exp, log functions]
```

## 4.4 Macro Variable Memory Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    memStart([Macro Variable Space]) --> tier1[Tier 1: Local #1 - #33]
    memStart --> tier2[Tier 2: Common #100 - #199]
    memStart --> tier3[Tier 3: System #1000+]
    tier1 --> t1a[Scope: within single macro call]
    tier1 --> t1b[Cleared on M30 / power-off]
    tier2 --> t2a[Scope: shared across main and subs]
    tier2 --> t2b[Persists after M30 if option enabled]
    tier3 --> t3a[Scope: controller-internal]
    tier3 --> t3b[Read-only or semi-fixed]
    tier3 --> t3c[Examples: #5021 X-axis, #4006 G-code modal, #3006 message]
```

## 4.5 Loop-and-Branch State Machine (Detailed)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> CheckCondition
    CheckCondition --> ExecuteBody: Condition True
    CheckCondition --> EndMacro: Condition False
    ExecuteBody --> ArithmeticCompute
    ArithmeticCompute --> OutputBlock
    OutputBlock --> IncrementCounter
    IncrementCounter --> CheckCondition
    EndMacro --> M99Return
    M99Return --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Differentiate between a subprogram and a macro program in CNC machining. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Subprogram | Macro |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A separately stored, reusable NC program with fixed geometry, invoked by `M98` and terminated by `M99`. | A reusable NC program that uses **variables, arithmetic, and conditional/looping logic** to produce parametric, decision-driven machining. |
| Geometry | Static — coordinates are hard-coded. | Dynamic — coordinates are computed at run-time from variables. |
| Industrial application | Bolt-hole circle drilling on a flange (identical holes, fixed pattern). | Probing cycle for in-process measurement of a milled pocket; family-of-parts machining. |

*(1 mark for correct definition of each, 1 mark for application)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the function of the codes `M98` and `M99` in a CNC subprogram. What does the address `Q` specify in an `M98` call?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`M98`** is the *subprogram call* code. It transfers control from the current program to a subprogram identified by the program number given in the address `P`. (1 mark)
- **`M99`** is the *subprogram return* code. It terminates the subprogram and returns control to the block immediately after the calling `M98` block. If `M99 Pnn` is used, control returns to sequence number `Nnn` in the calling program. (1 mark)
- The address **`Q`** in an `M98` call specifies the **number of repetitions** of the subprogram execution. If `Q` is omitted, the subprogram is executed exactly once. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **architecture and execution flow of a subprogram** in CNC. Discuss the role of `M98`, `M99`, nesting levels, and the `P`/`Q` arguments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a CNC subprogram to drill **8 equally spaced $\phi 6$ mm holes** on a **bolt-hole circle of radius 50 mm** centred at the workpiece origin, with a starting angle of $0^\circ$ and a drill depth of 12 mm. Show the calling main program. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Subprogram Architecture — 7 marks**

1. **Definition** (1 mark): A subprogram is a separate NC program stored in controller memory that performs a frequently repeated machining sequence, called by `M98` and terminated by `M99`.
2. **Call syntax** (2 marks): `M98 P<program number> Q<number of repetitions>`. Example: `M98 P0100 Q8` calls program `O0100` eight times.
3. **Return syntax** (1 mark): `M99` — returns to the block after the calling `M98`. `M99 P<n>` — returns to sequence number `Nn`.
4. **Nesting** (1 mark): A subprogram may call another subprogram up to the controller's nesting limit (typically 4–8 levels on FANUC 0i-M).
5. **Flow description** (2 marks): Main program → safe positioning → `M98` call → enter subprogram → machining sequence → check repeat count → either loop or `M99` → unwind call stack → resume main.

**Flowchart (as per SECTION 4.1):**
```
MAIN O0001  →  M98 P0100 Q8  →  SUB O0100
                                 ↓
                          Drill hole sequence
                                 ↓
                          M99 → returns to MAIN
```

**(b) Subprogram for 8-Hole Bolt-Hole Circle — 7 marks**

*Pre-computation check (for student verification):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\theta &= \dfrac{360^\circ}{8} = 45^\circ \\
\text{Hole 0:} \quad X_0 &= 50\cos 0^\circ = 50.000,\quad Y_0 = 50\sin 0^\circ = 0.000 \\
\text{Hole 1:} \quad X_1 &= 50\cos 45^\circ \approx 35.355,\quad Y_1 \approx 35.355 \\
\text{Hole 7:} \quad X_7 &= 50\cos 315^\circ \approx 35.355,\quad Y_7 \approx -35.355
\end{aligned}
$$

*Main Program (O0001):*
```
O0001 (MAIN - BOLT HOLE PATTERN)
G21 G90 G94 G54          (mm, absolute, feed/min, work coord)
G00 X0 Y0                (rapid to centre)
Z50.0                    (safe Z)
M98 P0100                (call subprogram O0100 once)
G00 Z100.0 M09           (retract, coolant off)
M30                      (end)
```

*Subprogram (O0100):*
```
O0100 (DRILL 8 HOLES ON 50 mm RADIUS)
G00 X50.0 Y0             (Hole 1: 0°)
G81 Z-12.0 R2.0 F60      (drill cycle, depth 12, retract 2)
G91 X35.355 Y35.355 L7   (relative moves to remaining 7 holes)
   ; relative because 50cos45 - 50cos0 = 35.355 - 50 = -14.645
   ; (but absolute is simpler — see alternative below)
G80 G90                 (cancel canned cycle, back to absolute)
M99                     (return to main)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cleaner alternative** (absolute mode, no incremental math): List all 8 hole positions explicitly in the subprogram. This is what most KTU lab students do because absolute mode is easier to verify by inspection.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define a **macro program** in CNC. List and explain the **three tiers of macro variables** (local, common, system) with at least one example of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a **FANUC Custom Macro B** program to machine **a rectangular pocket of variable size**. The macro should accept **length `L` (in `#1`)**, **width `W` (in `#2`)**, **depth `D` (in `#3`)**, and **step-over `S` (in `#4`)** as inputs. Use a `WHILE` loop to generate the passes. Show the calling main program. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Macro Program & Variable Tiers — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** A **macro** is a parametric NC program that uses variables (`#i`), arithmetic operators, and control structures (`IF`, `WHILE`, `GOTO`) to perform flexible, run-time-computed machining. The most common reference is **FANUC Custom Macro B**.

**Variable Tiers (5 marks):**

| Tier | Range | Example | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local** | `#1` – `#33` | `#1 = 50.0` (input length) | Scope: one macro call. Cleared at M30 / power-off. Used to receive arguments. |
| **Common** | `#100` – `#199` (or up to `#999` depending on option) | `#100 = 1` (parts counter) | Scope: shared between main and subprograms. Retained after M30 (with option). |
| **System** | `#1000` and above | `#5021` = current X-axis machine coord; `#4006` = current modal G-code; `#3006` = message to operator | Read-only or controller-defined. Used to read state, modal info, position, alarms, timers. |

**(b) Parametric Rectangular Pocket Macro — 7 marks**

*Pre-computation (for student understanding):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{passes}} &= \text{FUP}\!\left[\dfrac{L}{S}\right] \\
X_{i} &= -L/2 + i \cdot S, \quad i = 0, 1, \dots, n_{\text{passes}}-1
\end{aligned}
$$

*Main Program (O0001):*
```
O0001 (POCKET MAIN)
G21 G90 G94 G54
G00 X0 Y0 Z50.0
M98 P0200 L100.0 W60.0 D5.0 S10.0    (call macro with args)
G00 Z100.0 M09
M30
```

*Macro Subprogram (O0200):*
```
O0200 (RECTANGULAR POCKET MACRO)
#5  = 0                                (pass index i)
#6  = FUP[#1 / #4]                     (number of passes)
WHILE [#5 LT #6] DO 1
    #7  = -#1/2 + #5 * #4              (current X)
    G00 X#7 Y[-#2/2]                   (rapid to pass start)
    G01 Z-[#3] F40                     (plunge to depth)
    G01 Y[#2/2] F100                   (mill along +Y)
    G01 X[#7 + #4] Y[#2/2]            (step over to next pass)
    G01 Y[-#2/2]                       (mill back along −Y)
    #5  = #5 + 1                       (i ← i + 1)
END 1
G00 Z50.0
M99
```

*Argument-passing convention used:* Letter addresses `L, W, D, S` in the M98 call correspond to local variables `#1, #2, #3, #4` respectively (G-code ASCII mapping: L → #1, W → #2, D → #3, S → #4). This is controller-specific; FANUC standard uses fixed G-letter-to-#-slot mapping per the controller's "argument correspondence table".

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students forget that **FANUC trig functions use degrees**, not radians. Writing `SIN[30]` expecting $\sin(30\,\text{rad})$ will lose 1–2 marks.
> - Students often write `IF [#1 GT 10] GOTO 5` with the **sequence number missing or mistyped**. Always ensure `GOTO n` is followed by an actual `N` block in the program.
> - When using `WHILE … DO m`, the matching `END m` must use the **same identifier** `m`. Mismatched `END` numbers are a guaranteed zero for the loop body.
> - Variables must be **initialized before use**. A bare `#10 = #2 + #5 * 360 / #3` without first setting `#2, #3, #5` will produce a *vacant-variable* trap (vacant treated as 0 in arithmetic — silent wrong answer).
> - Trig argument in Macro B: angles are in **degrees**. `SIN[30]` → 0.5.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision (read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Subprogram** = reusable *fixed* NC block, called by **M98**, ended by **M99**. Use `Q` for repeat count, `P` for program number. (4–8 levels of nesting on FANUC 0i-M.)
- **Macro** = reusable *parametric* NC block with **variables (#), arithmetic, and logic**. Three tiers: **Local (#1–#33), Common (#100–#199), System (#1000+)**.
- **Trig functions in Macro B use DEGREES**, not radians.
- **Branching**: `GOTO n;` (unconditional), `IF [condition] GOTO n;` (conditional). **Looping**: `WHILE [cond] DO m; … END m;` — `m` must match.
- **Comparison operators inside `[ ]`**: `EQ, NE, GT, LT, GE, LE`.
- **Argument passing** in M98: letter addresses in the call (e.g., `L, W, D, S`) map to local variables `#1, #2, #3, #4` per the controller's correspondence table.
- **Vacant variables** evaluate to **0** in arithmetic but to *"not zero"* in conditional context — silent-pitfall warning.
- **System variable highlights**: `#5021` (X machine coord), `#4006` (modal G-code), `#3006` (operator message), `#5221` (X-axis G54 offset value).
- **Polar-to-Cartesian conversion** (the most-asked macro formula):

$$
X = R\cos\theta, \qquad Y = R\sin\theta
$$

- **Number of pocket passes formula**: $n = \text{FUP}[L / S]$ where $S$ is the step-over.
- **Angle increment in a bolt-hole loop**: $\Delta\theta = 360^\circ / N$.
- **Industrial uses to memorize**: subprograms → bolt-hole patterns, family parts; macros → probing, family-of-parts, custom canned cycles, automatic offset adjustment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tool radius compensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tool radius compensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Tool Radius Compensation (TRC)**, also called **Cutter Compensation (CC)** or **Cutter Diameter Compensation (CDC)**, is a CNC controller feature that automatically offsets the programmed tool path by the radius of the cutting tool, so that the *cutter center* traces a path that is parallel to, but displaced from, the *programmed contour*.

It allows the part programmer to write the code based on the **drawing dimensions** (i.e., the actual finished part geometry) rather than on the **tool center path**. The CNC control unit then dynamically shifts the tool by a distance equal to the cutter radius, either to the **Left (G41)** or to the **Right (G42)** of the programmed direction of motion, and cancels it with **G40**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – PBPET504 Module 3**
> Tool radius compensation is a mandatory topic under *CNC Part Programming*. Students must be able to distinguish between the **Programmed Path (CL — Cutter Location data)** and the **Actual Tool Path (Offset Path)**, and to compute intersection points between adjacent compensated blocks.

### 1.2 Types of Tool Compensation

There are two fundamentally different compensations in modern CNC controls:

| # | Compensation Type | Purpose | Typical G-Codes |
|---|-------------------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Tool Length Compensation (TLC)** | Compensates for the difference in length between the programmed tool and the actual loaded tool along the spindle axis (Z-axis). | **G43**, **G44**, **G49** |
| 2 | **Tool Radius Compensation (TRC)** | Compensates for the finite diameter of a milling cutter so that the *side* of the tool, not its center, follows the programmed contour. | **G40**, **G41**, **G42** |

> [!NOTE]
> This note exclusively covers **Tool Radius Compensation** (TRC), the syllabus-relevant portion of Module 3.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Map vs Road" Intuition

Imagine you are a tourist driving from City **A** to City **B**, and your car's GPS always displays the **centerline** of every road. But your car has a width of **2 m** and the road has a **low concrete curb** exactly on the right-hand edge. If you steer using the centerline, the right wheels will scrape the curb. A smarter GPS would automatically shift the displayed path to the **left** by exactly half your car width (1 m), so that the right edge of your car is safe.

In this analogy:

- The **centerline path** in the GPS = the *programmed CL path* (what the part programmer writes).
- The **curb (right edge of road)** = the *true part profile*.
- The **half-car-width offset** = the **tool radius**.
- The **GPS auto-shifting left** = the CNC control's **TRC feature (G41 — Left)**.
- If you turn around and drive home on the **left side** of the road, the GPS would shift the path to the **right** → that is **G42 (Right)**.

This single mental model removes all confusion about why TRC is needed, why direction matters, and why we write the code on the part drawing rather than on the tool centerline.

### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Standard convention:** Tool diameter is entered in the **Offset Register (D-register)** in mm (metric) or inches (imperial).
- **Sign convention:** The offset distance **R** is **always positive** in modern Fanuc / Siemens / Heidenhain controls; the **direction** is controlled by G41/G42.
- **Pre-condition for TRC activation:** A non-zero **D-code offset value** must be loaded before G41/G42 is issued; otherwise the controller throws an alarm (e.g., Fanuc **PS0020**).
- **Plane of compensation:** TRC is applied only in the active **plane** (default **XY → G17**). The spindle axis (Z) is unaffected by G41/G42.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Tool Radius Compensation is Needed

1. **Programmer Convenience:** The part programmer describes the contour using the dimensions shown on the engineering drawing. Without TRC, the programmer would have to compute the *offset path* manually for every tool change — a tedious, error-prone job.
2. **Tool Flexibility:** The same NC program can be run with tools of *different diameters* (e.g., a 10 mm end mill, then a 12 mm end mill) by simply changing the value in the **offset register**, without editing the program.
3. **Tool Wear Compensation:** As the cutter wears, its diameter shrinks. By updating the offset value, the dimensional accuracy of the part is restored without re-programming.
4. **Spring-back / Deflection:** For slender tools, an effective smaller radius can be entered to compensate for cutter deflection.
5. **Lead-in / Lead-out Generation:** Modern TRC algorithms automatically generate smooth approach and retract moves, improving surface finish at the start/end of a cut.

### 2.2 Programmed Path vs. Actual Tool Path

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Programmed Path** | CL | The trajectory of the *cutter center* that the programmer writes (or, equivalently, the contour if interpreted as a finished edge). |
| **Offset Path** | OC | The trajectory the cutter center *actually* follows once G41/G42 is active. It is the programmed contour shifted by distance **R** perpendicular to the direction of motion. |
| **Intersection Point** | IP | The point where two adjacent offset blocks meet. Computed by the controller using the programmed geometry and the tool radius **R**. |
| **Lead-in (Approach) Block** | — | The first block with G41/G42 active; provides the transition from the un-compensated approach to the compensated contour. |
| **Lead-out (Retract) Block** | — | The block where G40 is issued; smoothly returns the tool to its unclamped center path. |

### 2.3 G-Codes for Tool Radius Compensation

| G-Code | Function | Direction of Offset |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| **G40** | Cutter Compensation **CANCEL** | No offset — tool center = programmed path |
| **G41** | Cutter Compensation **LEFT** | Tool is shifted to the **left** of the programmed direction of motion (looking along +ve feed direction) |
| **G42** | Cutter Compensation **RIGHT** | Tool is shifted to the **right** of the programmed direction of motion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direction Rule (Right-Hand Rule):** Stand at the start of a block and look in the direction of feed (forward travel). Your **LEFT hand side** → G41; your **RIGHT hand side** → G42.

### 2.4 Activation, Execution, and Cancellation Sequence

The standard Fanuc/Siemens three-stage TRC sequence is:

1. **Lead-in (Approach):** Move to a position one tool-radius away from the contour, then issue **G41 D\_** (or G42 D\_).
2. **Machining:** Machine the full contour; the controller continuously offsets the path.
3. **Lead-out (Retract):** After the last machining block, issue **G40** to cancel compensation. The tool returns to the un-offset path.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Rule | Units |
|---|--------------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Offset distance | $R = \dfrac{D}{2}$ where $D$ = cutter diameter | mm |
| 2 | Number of offset registers used | 1 per tool (typically D01–D99) | — |
| 3 | Vector from programmed contour to cutter center | $\vec{n} = R \cdot \hat{u}_{\perp}$ where $\hat{u}_{\perp}$ is unit vector perpendicular to feed direction (rotated 90° CCW for G41, CW for G42) | mm |
| 4 | Intersection angle between adjacent blocks | $\theta$ = included angle between the two block vectors | rad or ° |
| 5 | Condition for valid (non-overshoot) compensation | $\theta < 180°$ for *inside corners* (concave for the cutter); $\theta < 90°$ for *outside corners* in some controllers | — |
| 6 | Effective radius after wear adjustment | $R_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{nominal}} - 2 \Delta w}{2}$ where $\Delta w$ = flank wear per side | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (not bare `|`) when writing absolute-value or norm symbols inside markdown tables to prevent breaking the table layout.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aero-structural milling:** TRC allows roughing with a large diameter and finishing with a smaller one using the **same NC code**, just by switching the D-register value.
- **Die and mold finishing:** As the cutter wears during a long mold job, the operator periodically reduces the offset value to maintain dimensional accuracy.
- **Multi-axis machining:** On 5-axis machines, TRC becomes *3D tool compensation*, projecting the tool radius onto the local surface normal — an extension of the same 2D principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Geometry of the Offset Path — Vector Derivation

Let a programmed block be a straight-line segment from point $\mathbf{P}_1$ to point $\mathbf{P}_2$.

$$
\mathbf{P}_1 = (x_1, y_1), \qquad \mathbf{P}_2 = (x_2, y_2)
$$

**Step 1 — Direction vector along the block:**

$$
\vec{v} = \mathbf{P}_2 - \mathbf{P}_1 = (x_2 - x_1,\; y_2 - y_1)
$$

**Step 2 — Unit vector along the block:**

$$
\hat{u} = \dfrac{\vec{v}}{\vert \vec{v} \vert} = \dfrac{(x_2 - x_1,\; y_2 - y_1)}{\sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Perpendicular unit vector (rotated +90° for G41 LEFT):**

A 2-D rotation by $+90°$ counter-clockwise maps $(a,b) \to (-b, a)$. Therefore:

$$
\hat{u}_{\perp,\text{G41}} = (-u_y,\; u_x)
$$

**Step 4 — Perpendicular unit vector (rotated –90° for G42 RIGHT):**

A rotation by $-90°$ (CW) maps $(a,b) \to (b, -a)$. Therefore:

$$
\hat{u}_{\perp,\text{G42}} = (u_y,\; -u_x)
$$

**Step 5 — Offset (cutter-center) points:**

$$
\mathbf{P}_1^{\text{off}} = \mathbf{P}_1 + R \cdot \hat{u}_{\perp,\text{comp}}
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_2^{\text{off}} = \mathbf{P}_2 + R \cdot \hat{u}_{\perp,\text{comp}}
$$

This gives the parallel offset segment on which the *cutter center* actually travels.

### 3.2 Intersection Point Between Two Adjacent Compensated Blocks

Consider two consecutive programmed blocks $AB$ and $BC$. After applying the offset $R$ to the **left** (G41), we obtain offset lines $A'B'$ and $B'C'$. The cutter must travel along $A'B'$ and then along $B'C'$, meeting at the intersection point $I$.

**Step 1 — Write the equations of the two offset lines in parametric form.**

For block $AB$, with unit perpendicular $\hat{n}_1$ (G41 direction):

$$
L_1: \; \mathbf{r}(s) = \mathbf{A} + R\,\hat{n}_1 + s \cdot \hat{u}_1, \quad s \in \mathbb{R}
$$

For block $BC$, with unit perpendicular $\hat{n}_2$:

$$
L_2: \; \mathbf{r}(t) = \mathbf{B} + R\,\hat{n}_2 + t \cdot \hat{u}_2, \quad t \in \mathbb{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions and solve for $s, t$.**

$$
\mathbf{A} + R\,\hat{n}_1 + s\,\hat{u}_1 = \mathbf{B} + R\,\hat{n}_2 + t\,\hat{u}_2
$$

**Step 3 — Take components along $\hat{u}_1$ and along $\hat{u}_2$.**

In the scalar form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A_x + R\,n_{1x}) + s\,u_{1x} &= (B_x + R\,n_{2x}) + t\,u_{2x} \\
(A_y + R\,n_{1y}) + s\,u_{1y} &= (B_y + R\,n_{2y}) + t\,u_{2y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the 2×2 linear system for $s$ and $t$.**

Using Cramer's rule with the determinant $\Delta$:

$$
\Delta = u_{1x}\,u_{2y} - u_{1y}\,u_{2x}
$$

$$
s = \dfrac{\det\!\begin{pmatrix} B_x - A_x + R\,n_{2x} - R\,n_{1x} & u_{2x} \\ B_y - A_y + R\,n_{2y} - R\,n_{1y} & u_{2y} \end{pmatrix}}{\Delta}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $s$ back to obtain the Intersection Point $I$.**

$$
\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{A} + R\,\hat{n}_1 + s\,\hat{u}_1
$$

This is precisely the value the CNC control computes in real time for every tool-motion block transition — typically across a **look-ahead window of 5–200 blocks** depending on the controller brand.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Compensation Behaviour at Corners

| Corner Type | Included Angle $\theta$ | Geometry | Behaviour with TRC |
|-------------|------------------------|----------|--------------------|
| **Sharp Outside Corner** (convex) | $\theta < 180°$ | Tool path bends *away* from the corner | Automatic **overshoot** by the cutter; controller truncates at the intersection point $I$. |
| **Sharp Inside Corner** (concave) | $\theta < 180°$ but viewed from the part side | Tool path bends *toward* the part | May **gouge** if $\theta$ is too acute. Fanuc allows compensation up to $\theta < 90°$ on inside corners before alarm. |
| **Straight Continuation** | $\theta = 180°$ | Blocks are collinear | No intersection; offset lines are simply concatenated. |
| **Reversal** | $\theta = 0°$ | Tool reverses direction | Illegal under G41/G42 — controller throws **INTERFERENCE ERROR**. |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem.** A milling cutter of diameter $D = 12$ mm machines a corner where block 1 travels from $A = (0, 0)$ to $B = (40, 0)$, and block 2 travels from $B = (40, 0)$ to $C = (40, 30)$. Compensation is **G41 (Left)** in the **G17** plane. Compute the offset intersection point $I$.

**Solution.**

**Step 1 — Tool radius.**

$$
R = \dfrac{D}{2} = \dfrac{12}{2} = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Unit vectors of the two programmed blocks.**

$$
\hat{u}_1 = (1, 0), \qquad \hat{u}_2 = (0, 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Perpendicular unit vectors (G41 → +90° CCW).**

$$
\hat{n}_1 = (-0, 1) = (0, 1), \qquad \hat{n}_2 = (-1, 0)
$$

**Step 4 — Offset endpoint of block 1 (after B).**

$$
B' = B + R\,\hat{n}_1 = (40, 0) + 6 \cdot (0, 1) = (40, 6)
$$

**Step 5 — Offset starting point of block 2 (before B).**

$$
B'' = B + R\,\hat{n}_2 = (40, 0) + 6 \cdot (-1, 0) = (34, 0)
$$

**Step 6 — Intersection of line through $(40,0)$ parallel to $\hat{u}_1$ offset by $R$ and line through $(40,0)$ parallel to $\hat{u}_2$ offset by $R$.**

Block 1 offset line: $y = 6$.
Block 2 offset line: $x = 34$.

$$
I = (34, 6)
$$

**Step 7 — Verify by the determinant formula.**

$$
\Delta = u_{1x}\,u_{2y} - u_{1y}\,u_{2x} = (1)(1) - (0)(0) = 1
$$

Substituting $A = (0,0)$, $B = (40,0)$, $R\,\hat{n}_1 = (0,6)$, $R\,\hat{n}_2 = (-6,0)$:

$$
s = \dfrac{(40 - 0 - 6 - 0)\cdot 1 - 0 \cdot 1}{1} = 34
$$

$$
I = (0,0) + (0,6) + 34 \cdot (1,0) = (34, 6)
$$

**Result:** The cutter-center passes through $I = (34, 6)$, exactly $R = 6$ mm away from the sharp outside corner of the part at $(40, 0)$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Tool Radius Compensation. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the difference between G41 and G42.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tool Radius Compensation (TRC) is a CNC controller feature that automatically offsets the programmed tool path by the cutter radius so that the side of the tool, not its center, follows the part contour.

- **G41 — Cutter Compensation LEFT:** When looking along the direction of programmed feed, the cutter is offset to the **left** of the contour.
- **G42 — Cutter Compensation RIGHT:** When looking along the direction of programmed feed, the cutter is offset to the **right** of the contour.
- **G40 — Cutter Compensation CANCEL:** Cancels any active offset and brings the tool center back onto the programmed path.

*Sketch: a rectangular contour with two arrows showing offset to the left (G41) and right (G42) — labelled axes X and Y, cutter diameter D.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any three situations in which Tool Radius Compensation is necessary in a CNC milling program.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Tool diameter variation:** The same NC program must be usable with cutters of different diameters by simply updating the offset register value.
2. **Tool wear compensation:** As the cutter wears during a long run, dimensional accuracy is restored by reducing the offset value without re-programming.
3. **Lead-in / lead-out moves:** Modern TRC automatically generates smooth approach and retract motions to improve surface finish at cut entry and exit.

*(Any three valid points = 3 marks; one mark each.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**  *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]*

**(a)** A CNC milling cutter of diameter **12 mm** machines a profile with two consecutive linear blocks:
- Block 1: from **A (0, 0)** to **B (50, 0)**
- Block 2: from **B (50, 0)** to **C (50, 40)**

Compensation is **G41 (Left)** in the **G17 (XY) plane**.
Using the vector method, compute the cutter-center offset points and the intersection point $I$.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the complete sequence of **lead-in, machining, and lead-out blocks** required to machine a closed rectangular pocket using G41/G42. State the role of the **D-register**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Given:**
$$
D = 12 \text{ mm}, \quad R = \dfrac{12}{2} = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 1 — Programmed block vectors.**
$$
\vec{v}_1 = (50, 0) - (0,0) = (50, 0), \quad \vec{v}_2 = (50, 40) - (50, 0) = (0, 40)
$$

**Step 2 — Unit vectors.**
$$
\hat{u}_1 = (1, 0), \quad \hat{u}_2 = (0, 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Perpendicular unit vectors for G41 (rotation +90° CCW).**

Rule: $(a,b) \to (-b, a)$.
$$
\hat{n}_1 = (-0, 1) = (0, 1), \quad \hat{n}_2 = (-1, 0)
$$

**Step 4 — Offset endpoints.**
$$
B_1^{\text{off}} = B + R \hat{n}_1 = (50, 0) + 6(0,1) = (50, 6)
$$
$$
B_2^{\text{off}} = B + R \hat{n}_2 = (50, 0) + 6(-1, 0) = (44, 0)
$$

**Step 5 — Intersection of the two offset lines.**
$$
\text{Line 1: } y = 6, \qquad \text{Line 2: } x = 44
$$
$$
\boxed{I = (44, 6)}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification using the determinant formula.**
$$
\Delta = u_{1x}\,u_{2y} - u_{1y}\,u_{2x} = (1)(1) - (0)(0) = 1
$$
$$
s = \dfrac{(B_x - A_x + R\,n_{2x} - R\,n_{1x})\,u_{2y} - (B_y - A_y + R\,n_{2y} - R\,n_{1y})\,u_{2x}}{\Delta}
$$
$$
s = \dfrac{(50 - 0 - 6 - 0)(1) - (0 - 0 + 0 - 6)(0)}{1} = 44
$$
$$
I = A + R\,\hat{n}_1 + s\,\hat{u}_1 = (0,0) + (0,6) + 44\,(1,0) = (44, 6) \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The cutter-center passes through $I = (44, 6)$, exactly **6 mm** away from the sharp corner at $(50, 0)$.

---

##### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Diagram (labelled rectangular pocket):**

```
        (X4,Ymax) -------------- (Xmax,Ymax)
              |                    |
              |   G42 (OUTSIDE)    |
              |--------------------|
        (X4,Y4) ------ I ------- (Xmax,Y4)
              |     G41 (INSIDE)   |
              |                    |
        (Xmin,Ymin) ----------- (Xmax,Ymin)
```

**Complete Block Sequence (Fanuc-style G-code):**

```
O0001;
G21 G90 G17 G94;        (Metric, Absolute, XY-plane, Feed/min)
G00 X-20 Y-20;          (SAFE APPROACH — uncompensated)
Z5 M03 S1200;           (Spindle ON, rapid to clearance)
G01 Z-5 F100;           (Plunge to depth)
G41 D01 X0 Y0 F200;     (LEAD-IN: G41 LEFT, D-register 01)
G01 X80 Y0;             (Machining block 1)
G01 X80 Y60;            (Machining block 2)
G01 X0 Y60;             (Machining block 3)
G01 X0 Y0;              (Machining block 4 — close pocket)
G40 G01 X-20 Y-20;      (LEAD-OUT: G40 cancels compensation)
G00 Z50 M05;            (Retract, spindle OFF)
M30;                    (Program end)
```

**Role of the D-register:**
The **D-register** (e.g., D01) is a memory location in the CNC's offset table that stores the **cutter radius compensation value R**. Activating `G41 D01` tells the controller: "From now on, offset every subsequent motion to the LEFT by the value stored in offset register D01." Operators update D01 (via the controller's offset page) to switch tools or compensate for wear **without modifying the NC program**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**  *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]*

**(a)** Explain with a labelled sketch the **three stages** of implementing cutter compensation: lead-in, machining, and lead-out. What happens if the G40 cancellation block is **omitted**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slot **20 mm wide** and **80 mm long** is to be milled along the X-axis using a **10 mm diameter** end mill. Compensation is **G42 RIGHT** in G17 plane. Determine:
(i) the cutter radius $R$,
(ii) the offset distance of the cutter center from each long edge of the slot,
(iii) whether the tool can finish the slot in a single pass or needs **multiple passes**.
**(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Three Stages of Cutter Compensation:**

1. **Lead-in (Approach) Stage:** The tool is positioned at a point that is **one tool-radius away** from the contour, but **outside** the part. The compensation command (`G41` or `G42`) is issued in the *first motion block toward the contour*. The controller reads the D-register, computes the perpendicular offset, and shifts the tool path accordingly. The cutter is now offset but has not yet touched the part edge.

2. **Machining Stage:** Successive blocks are executed with G41/G42 active. The controller continuously computes the offset path, handling every intersection point between adjacent blocks automatically (using the vector formula derived in §3.2). The side of the cutter, not its center, follows the part profile.

3. **Lead-out (Retract) Stage:** The last machining block ends, and the **G40** command is issued in the next motion block (typically moving away from the part). The controller cancels the offset, and the tool center returns to the uncompensated programmed path for safe retract.

**Consequence of omitting G40:**
If G40 is never issued, the offset remains active **indefinitely**. On the next tool motion (often a rapid `G00` to a tool-change position or to start a new feature), the controller will offset that motion too, causing an **unintended cutter path** — typically resulting in a tool crash, scrap part, or controller alarm. Modern controllers may also raise a **"COMPENSATION NOT CANCELLED"** alarm at M30 / program end.

---

##### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
$$
\text{Slot width} = 20 \text{ mm}, \quad D = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 80 \text{ mm}
$$

**(i) Cutter radius:**

$$
R = \dfrac{D}{2} = \dfrac{10}{2} = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Offset distance of cutter center from each long edge:**

Because the cutter is symmetric and the slot is straight, the cutter center lies exactly on the **centerline** of the slot. The perpendicular distance from the cutter center to either long edge is:

$$
d = \dfrac{\text{Slot width}}{2} = \dfrac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

Equivalently, the cutter body extends **5 mm** beyond the cutter center on each side, leaving a **clearance of 5 mm** between the cutter edge and each slot wall. The programmed CL path is therefore a straight line **X = centerline**, **Y = 0** to **Y = 80**.

**(iii) Single-pass or multi-pass?**

For a slot of width **20 mm** machined with a **10 mm** cutter, the diameter is exactly half the slot width. The cutter fits inside the slot with **5 mm clearance on each side**, so:

- ✅ A **single pass** along the slot length is sufficient to cut the full width to depth.
- If the slot had been narrower than the cutter diameter, multi-passes with step-over (using G41/G42 with progressive Y-shifts) would be required.
- A **spring pass** (light cut at the same path) is often recommended to remove cutter-deflection marks, but it is not geometrically necessary.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Direction confusion:** Students frequently write "G41 = Right" and "G42 = Left". The correct mnemonic is **G41-LEFT, G42-RIGHT** (the second letter matches: **L**eft → 41, **R**ight → 42).
> 2. **Forgetting the D-code:** Activating G41/G42 *without* a D-code (e.g., `G41` instead of `G41 D01`) produces an alarm in most controllers and **zero marks** for the program.
> 3. **Wrong perpendicular sign:** When rotating $\hat{u}$ by $+90°$ for G41, students often write $(u_y, -u_x)$ — that is the G42 formula. Mix-up leads to the offset being on the wrong side and the answer being marked wrong despite correct setup.
> 4. **Confusing TRC with TLC:** Tool *Length* Compensation (G43/G44) acts along the **Z-axis**; Tool *Radius* Compensation (G41/G42) acts in the active **XY plane**. Examiners award zero if these are swapped.
> 5. **Omitting G40 in part programs:** KTU evaluators specifically check for proper lead-out. Missing G40 = up to **−2 marks**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **TRC = Cutter Compensation = Cutter Diameter Compensation** — all three terms refer to the same feature.
- **G40** = cancel, **G41** = LEFT, **G42** = RIGHT (mnemonic: 4-1-L, 4-2-R).
- **Active plane:** Only G17 (XY) by default; G18 and G19 select other planes.
- **Two prerequisites:** (1) a non-zero D-register value, (2) the G41/G42 must be issued in a **linear motion block** (not in a rapid alone).
- **Three stages:** Lead-in → Machining → Lead-out (G40).
- **Vector formula:** $\hat{u}_{\perp,\text{G41}} = (-u_y, u_x)$ and $\hat{u}_{\perp,\text{G42}} = (u_y, -u_x)$.
- **Intersection Point (IP):** Computed by the controller; the cutter never physically visits the programmed corner of the part on outside corners.
- **Critical angle rule:** Inside corners with included angle **< 90°** can cause **gouging** and trigger controller alarms; avoid them by adding fillets to the part design.
- **Offset value R** is **positive** in modern controllers; sign of offset is governed by G41/G42.
- **Tool wear compensation:** Reduce $R$ in the D-register as the cutter wears — no program edit needed.
- **Look-ahead buffer:** Modern controllers read ahead 5–200 blocks to anticipate intersection points; never assume single-block execution.
- **TRC ≠ TLC:** Tool *Radius* Compensation uses G41/G42; Tool *Length* Compensation uses G43/G44/G49.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CNC programming with interactive graphics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** CNC programming with interactive graphics # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **CNC Programming with Interactive Graphics** refers to the modern, computer-integrated methodology of authoring, verifying, and optimizing instruction sets (G-codes/M-codes or APT statements) for Computer Numerically Controlled (CNC) machine tools, using graphical user interfaces (GUIs) that allow the programmer to define geometry, select tools, simulate tool paths, and visualize the machining process in real-time on a 2D/3D display. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Interactive graphics-based CNC programming is a subset of Computer-Aided Part Programming (CAPP) where the programmer interacts directly with the computer screen using a cursor, menu-driven commands, and graphical prompts, replacing the older text-only APT-style punch-card era. The two principal streams covered under this Module-3 topic are: 1. **Manual Part Programming** — Direct, line-by-line G-code/M-code entry by the programmer for simple components. 2. **Computer-Assisted Part Programming (APT-based & Graphical)** — High-level language programming (APT) followed by post-processing; later augmented with **interactive graphics** for geometric definition and **tool-path verification**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy (Pizza Shop):** Think of CNC programming like writing a recipe for a robotic chef (the CNC machine). In the **old days (Manual Programming)**, you wrote every instruction by hand — "move 50 mm right, lower the cutter, rotate at 1000 RPM…" — exactly like typing each recipe step on paper. In the **APT era**, you described the pizza shape mathematically — "a circle of radius 100 mm centered at origin" — and the computer filled in the steps. With **Interactive Graphics**, you simply **draw the pizza shape on a touchscreen**, point-and-click the cutting tool on the crust, and the computer instantly shows you a 3-D animated preview of the chef cutting it. You literally *see* the slice before any real flour is wasted. That visual confirmation is what "interactive graphics" brings to CNC programming. The key conceptual leap is the **shift of responsibility from the human brain (calculating every cutter offset) to the computer (calculating automatically and showing a simulation)**. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Key Metrics (Highlighted) | Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol | |---|---| | **APT language origin year** | **1958**, MIT (Automatically Programmed Tools) | | **Standard NC coordinate axes** | **X, Y, Z (linear); A, B, C (rotary)** per **ISO 841 / EIA RS-267** | | **Feed-rate units** | **mm/min** (metric) or **in/min** (imperial) | | **Spindle speed units** | **RPM (revolutions per minute)** | | **Tool-length compensation register** | **H-offset (typically H01–H99)** | | **Cutter-radius compensation register** | **D-offset (typically D01–D99)** | --- ## 1.4 Sub-Topic Inventory for KTU Module-3 Coverage The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the following items under this topic: 1. Manual part programming structure 2. APT language — geometry statements, motion statements, post-processor statements 3. Computer-assisted part programming workflow 4. Interactive graphics programming — geometric definition via cursor 5. Graphics display elements (lines, arcs, points, splines, surface patches) 6. Tool path simulation — **2-D wireframe** and **3-D solid (material removal) simulation** 7. Dynamic graphics — rotation, zoom, pan, sectioning 8. Macro programming (parametric family of parts) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Methods of CNC Programming (Hierarchical Overview) Modern KTU examinations expect the student to be able to **list, define, and compare** the following three methods. They are arranged from least to most sophisticated. ### Method 1 — Manual Part Programming (Low-level, G-code) - Performed directly at the machine console or via a text editor. - Suitable for **simple 2½-D geometries** (holes, slots, rectangular pockets, simple contours). - Every coordinate and every preparatory function (G-code) is written manually. - **Limitation:** extremely tedious for complex 3-D surfaces; no built-in geometry; no graphics verification. ### Method 2 — Computer-Assisted Part Programming (High-level Language: APT) - The programmer writes the part program in an **English-like symbolic language** (APT, ADAPT, EXAPT, COMPACT II). - The source code is processed by a **mainframe compiler** in three stages: 1. **Translation** — converts geometry & motion statements into intermediate "cutter location (CL)" data. 2. **CL-file generation** — numerical tool-tip coordinates. 3. **Post-processing** — converts CL data into machine-specific G/M-codes. - **Limitation:** still text-based; programmer cannot "see" the part. ### Method 3 — Interactive Graphics Programming (CAD/CAM with simulation) - Integrates a **CAD modeler + NC engine + post-processor + graphical simulator** into a single workstation. - The user **picks geometry directly from the on-screen drawing** (cursor pointing). - Tool paths are **computed automatically** (cutter compensation, lead-in/out, trochoidal milling all handled). - **Dynamic tool-path simulation** shows material removal in real time. - This is the **industry-standard method** in 2024-era CAM packages (Mastercam, CATIA, NX, Fusion 360, Delcam). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Distinguish between manual, APT, and interactive graphics part programming"* — this is a guaranteed **7-mark question** almost every year. --- ## 2.2 Manual Part Program Structure (Word-Address Format) A standard KTU-conformant manual part program follows the **ISO/EIA word-address format**: ``` % O1001 (PROGRAM NUMBER) N10 G21 G90 G94 (SAFETY BLOCK — metric, absolute, feed/min) N20 T01 M06 (TOOL CHANGE — tool 1) N30 S1500 M03 (SPINDLE CW at 1500 RPM) N40 G00 X0 Y0 Z50 (RAPID TO SAFE PLANE) N50 G01 Z-5 F100 (PLUNGE TO DEPTH) N60 ... (MACHINING BLOCKS) Nxx G00 Z100 M09 (RETRACT, COOLANT OFF) Nxx M05 (SPINDLE STOP) Nxx M30 (PROGRAM END) % ``` ### Major Categories of Codes | Category | Examples | Function | |---|---|---| | **Preparatory (G-codes)** | G00, G01, G02, G03, G90, G91 | Define motion type & coordinate mode | | **Miscellaneous (M-codes)** | M03, M05, M06, M08, M30 | Define auxiliary on/off functions | | **Tool / Offset** | T01, H01, D01 | Tool selection and compensation | | **Axis words** | X, Y, Z, A, B, C | Linear & rotary coordinates | | **Feed & Speed** | F100, S1500 | Feed-rate and spindle speed | --- ## 2.3 APT Language — The Historical High-Level Foundation **APT (Automatically Programmed Tools)** is the **mother of all CAM languages**. Even modern CAM software uses APT-derived constructs under the hood. The KTU syllabus specifically tests APT for 2½-D milling and lathe work. ### 2.3.1 Three Statement Families in APT | Family | Purpose | Sample | |---|---|---| | **Geometry Statements** | Define points, lines, circles, planes | `P1 = POINT/10, 20, 0`
`L1 = LINE/P1, P2`
`C1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, RADIUS` | | **Motion Statements** | Define cutter movement | `FROM/SETPT` (start)
`GOTO/P1` (point-to-point)
`GOLFT/L1, PAST, L2` (contour, left of part)
`GORGT/L1, PAST, L2` (right of part)
`TLLFT, TLRGT, ON` (tool-side indicators) | | **Auxiliary / Post-Processor Statements** | Spindle, coolant, feed, tool | `SPINDL/1500, CLW`
`COOLNT/ON`
`FEDRAT/100, IPM`
`CUTTER/10, 0`
`FINI` (end) | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Students must remember the **four tool-side indicators**: `TLLFT` (tool left of contour), `TLRGT` (tool right), `ON` (tool on contour), `TANTO` (tangent — used at corners). ### 2.3.2 APT Cutter-Compensation Convention If the tool is to the **left of the contour**, the inside of the contour is the part — used for **outside (male) features**. If the tool is to the **right of the contour**, the inside is still the part — used for **inside (female) pockets**. --- ## 2.4 Interactive Graphics Programming — The Modern Workflow The interactive graphics programming workflow contains the following **seven logical stages**: 1. **CAD Model Import / Creation** — load STEP, IGES, or native CAD file. 2. **Work-Coordinate System (WCS) Definition** — origin and axes set by clicking on stock or face. 3. **Stock / Fixture Definition** — bounding box for raw material, clamp avoidance zones. 4. **Tool Library Selection** — diameter, flute length, holder, material. 5. **Operation Strategy Selection** — facing, roughing, finishing, drilling, contouring. 6. **Parameter Input (graphics-driven)** — depths, stepovers, feeds via dialog boxes; **no manual G-code typing**. 7. **Tool-Path Generation + Verification** — 2-D / 3-D simulation, back-plot, gouge check. ### 2.4.1 Graphics Display Elements (What the screen can show) | Element | Representation in CAM | |---|---| | **Lines** | Wireframe edges of part / fixture | | **Arcs & Circles** | Bolt holes, fillets, bosses | | **Splines (NURBS)** | Free-form surfaces (airfoil, mould cavity) | | **Points** | Drill locations | | **Surface patches** | Sculpted surfaces (Bezier, Coons) | | **Solid models** | Voxel/voxel-like material removal simulation | | **Tool envelope** | Cylinder/cone swept by cutter | | **Holder envelope** | Collision-checked body of tool holder | ### 2.4.2 Dynamic Simulation Features - **Pan, zoom, rotate** — view tool path from any angle. - **Sectioning** — clip the model to inspect internal pockets. - **Material removal simulation** — voxel-based subtraction shows the part as it actually appears during cutting. - **Collision detection** — flags holder/workpiece and holder/fixture interference. - **Back-plot** — traces tool center-point path line-by-line. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Important:** "Interactive graphics programming eliminates the need for a separate dry-run on the actual machine, thereby saving costly set-up time and avoiding scrap." — This is the standard **2-mark definition** students must reproduce verbatim in the ESE. --- ## 2.5 Macro Programming (Parametric Family of Parts) Macro programming uses **variables, arithmetic, loops, and conditional branches** to generate a family of similar parts from a single template. KTU frequently asks for a **simple macro definition** (e.g., bolt-hole circle macro). A macro program uses **G65 call** and **local variables #1–#33** mapped to address letters A–Z (minus G, L, N, O). --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Master Table — Examiners will test conversions like mm ↔ inch and RPM ↔ cutting speed.** | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Spindle speed (RPM) | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rev/min (RPM) | $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm | | Feed-rate (linear) | $F = N \cdot Z \cdot f_z$ | mm/min | $Z$ = number of teeth, $f_z$ = feed/tooth | | Material removal rate | $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ depth, $a_e$ width, $v_f$ feed | | Inch to mm | $1\ \text{inch} = 25.4\ \text{mm}$ | — | Constant for KTU numerical | | Block format time | $T_{block} = \dfrac{L}{F}$ plus accel/decel | s | For process-time estimation | | APT cutter offset vector | $\vec{O} = R \cdot \hat{n}$ | mm | $R$ = cutter radius, $\hat{n}$ = outward normal | | Lead-in arc length | $L_{in} = R \cdot \theta_{in}$ | mm | Used in interactive graphics ramp-on | > **Critical LaTeX note for tables:** Vertical bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown parsing errors. --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Machining of turbine blades with 5-axis simultaneous APT/UG programs — interactive graphics detects gouges before the £100,000 blade is ruined. - **Automotive:** Engine block cylinder bores — macro programs produce variants of the same block for 1.6 L, 1.8 L, 2.0 L engines. - **Die & Mould:** Free-form surface milling — impossible to manually program; interactive graphics is the **only** viable path. - **Medical implants:** Patient-specific bone plates — fully parametric macro + interactive graphics. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Sample Manual Part Program — Full Step-by-Step Construction (KTU 14-mark Style) > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (Slot Milling):** Mill a rectangular slot 80 mm × 40 mm × 5 mm deep, centered at (50, 50), in a 120 mm × 120 mm × 10 mm mild-steel plate. Use a Ø10 mm 4-flute HSS end-mill at 1200 RPM, feed 80 mm/min, counterclockwise. ### Step 1 — Convert the geometric description to coordinates. Slot corners (lower-left, lower-right, upper-right, upper-left): $$P_1 = (10,\, 30,\, 0)$$ $$P_2 = (90,\, 30,\, 0)$$ $$P_3 = (90,\, 70,\, 0)$$ $$P_4 = (10,\, 70,\, 0)$$ > Logic: Slot spans X = 10→90 (80 mm) and Y = 30→70 (40 mm); Z = 0 is top of plate; depth = −5 mm. ### Step 2 — Choose the safe Z (rapid clearance above the workpiece). $$Z_{safe} = +5\ \text{mm above top surface}$$ ### Step 3 — Determine the spindle speed and feed (cross-check using formulas). $$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 25}{\pi \cdot 10} \approx 796\ \text{RPM}$$ For simplicity the KTU problem often pre-supplies values, so we adopt $N = 1200$ RPM as given. $$F = N \cdot Z \cdot f_z = 1200 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.0167 \approx 80\ \text{mm/min}\ \checkmark$$ ### Step 4 — Write the part program (block-by-block). ``` % O0201 (RECTANGULAR SLOT 80x40x5) N10 G21 G90 G94 (METRIC, ABSOLUTE, FEED/MIN) N20 G54 (WORK COORDINATE ZERO) N30 T01 M06 (TOOL 1: Ø10 END-MILL) N40 S1200 M03 (SPINDLE CW, 1200 RPM) N50 G00 X0 Y0 Z25 (RAPID TO SAFE Z ABOVE ORIGIN) N60 M08 (COOLANT ON) N70 G01 Z-5 F80 (PLUNGE TO DEPTH AT 80 mm/min) N80 G01 X10 Y30 (APPROACH SLOT CORNER P1) N90 G01 X90 (CUT ALONG X+ TO P2) N100 G01 Y70 (CUT ALONG Y+ TO P3) N110 G01 X10 (CUT ALONG X- TO P4) N120 G01 Y30 (CUT ALONG Y- BACK TO P1) N130 G00 Z25 (RETRACT) N140 M09 (COOLANT OFF) N150 M05 (SPINDLE STOP) N160 G91 G28 X0 Y0 Z0 (RETURN TO HOME) N170 M30 (PROGRAM END, REWIND) % ``` ### Step 5 — Verify by graphical back-plot. In interactive graphics, the **CL points** computed from the above are: $$CL_1 = (5,\, 35,\, -5)$$ $$CL_2 = (95,\, 35,\, -5)$$ $$CL_3 = (95,\, 65,\, -5)$$ $$CL_4 = (5,\, 65,\, -5)$$ > Logic: CL points are shifted outward by cutter radius $R = 5$ mm in the normal direction at each edge. --- ## 3.2 Sample APT Program — Worked Through (Line by Line) **Problem:** Mill the periphery of a triangular plate with vertices A(0,0), B(80,0), C(40,60) using a Ø10 mm cutter, counterclockwise, with the cutter to the LEFT of the contour (outside milling of the plate edges). ``` PARTNO TRIANG MACHINE MILL CLPRNT INTOL/0.02 OUTTOL/0.02 CUTTER/10.0 (CUTTER DIAMETER 10 mm) $ GEOMETRY P1 = POINT/0, 0, 0 P2 = POINT/80, 0, 0 P3 = POINT/40, 60, 0 L1 = LINE/P1, P2 L2 = LINE/P2, P3 L3 = LINE/P3, P1 $ MOTION FROM/SETPT (MACHINE ZERO) SPINDL/1200, CLW COOLNT/ON FEDRAT/80, IPM GO/TO, L1, TO, L2, TLLFT, GOLFT/L2, PAST, L3 GORGT/L3, PAST, L1 GOLFT/L1, PAST, L2 GOTO/SETPT COOLNT/OFF SPINDL/OFF FINI ``` ### Explanation of motion block (line by line): | APT Line | English Meaning | |---|---| | `GO/TO, L1, TO, L2, TLLFT` | Move **to** the intersection of L1 and L2, tool on the left of the contour, then continue cutting the first edge L1. | | `GOLFT/L2, PAST, L3` | Move along L2, going left of the part, **past** the intersection with L3. | | `GORGT/L3, PAST, L1` | Move along L3, going right of the part, **past** the intersection with L1. | | `GOLFT/L1, PAST, L2` | Continue along L1 until past L2 — completes the loop. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why `TLLFT` for counterclockwise milling of an outside contour?** > When the cutter is on the left of the contour and the part is on its right, the rotation of a standard right-hand end-mill (CCW from above) pulls the cutter INTO the workpiece — producing a **conventional (climb) cut** with cleaner finish. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Cutter-Compensation Offset (Analytical Geometry) For a planar contour described by an oriented curve $\vec{C}(t)$, the **offset curve** (tool-center path) at a distance $R$ outward is: $$\vec{C}_{offset}(t) = \vec{C}(t) + R \cdot \hat{n}(t)$$ where $\hat{n}(t)$ is the outward unit normal. For an explicit curve $y = f(x)$, the normal vector is: $$\hat{n}(t) = \dfrac{(-f'(x),\ 1)}{\sqrt{1 + (f'(x))^2}}$$ Substituting and simplifying: $$\vec{C}_{offset}(t) = \bigl(x - R \cdot f'(x)/k,\ \ f(x) + R/k\bigr), \quad k = \sqrt{1 + [f'(x)]^2}$$ > **Engineering Meaning:** This is exactly what the **interactive graphics engine computes automatically** when you click "Compute Tool Path with Cutter Compensation". The manual APT programmer had to write the offset equations implicitly via `TLLFT`/`TLRGT`/`ON` keywords. ### Worked Numerical Offset Example Given a straight edge from $P_1 = (10, 30)$ to $P_2 = (90, 30)$ and cutter radius $R = 5$ mm, with tool to the LEFT (i.e., part is to the right, Y+ side): $$P_{1,offset} = (10,\ 30 + 5) = (10, 35)$$ $$P_{2,offset} = (90,\ 30 + 5) = (90, 35)$$ > Logic: The normal to a horizontal line $y = 30$ pointing toward the part (upward, Y+) is $(0, +1)$. Adding $R \cdot \hat{n}$ shifts the tool path up by 5 mm. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Programming Methods | Attribute | Manual | APT | Interactive Graphics | |---|---|---|---| | **Programming time** | Highest for complex parts | Moderate | **Lowest** | | **Operator skill** | Highest | High | Moderate (CAM trained) | | **Geometry handling** | None (must compute manually) | Symbolic | **Direct from CAD model** | | **Cutter compensation** | G41/G42 + manual D-offset | `TLLFT/TLRGT` keyword | **Automatic, real-time** | | **Tool-path verification** | None | Textual dry-run only | **3-D solid simulation** | | **Suitable for free-form surfaces** | No | Limited | **Yes (NURBS, 5-axis)** | | **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate (mainframe) | Highest (software license) | | **Typical KTU application** | Holes, slots, simple pockets | 2½-D prismatic parts | Moulds, blades, sculptured parts | --- ## 3.5 Macro Programming — Worked Example (Bolt-Hole Circle) **Objective:** Drill 8 holes of Ø6 mm on a circle of radius 50 mm centered at (100, 100). ``` % O0301 (BOLT HOLE CIRCLE MACRO) #1 = 100 (CENTER X) #2 = 100 (CENTER Y) #3 = 50 (RADIUS) #4 = 8 (NUMBER OF HOLES) #5 = 0 (COUNTER) #6 = 0 (INITIAL ANGLE) WHILE [#5 LT #4] DO 1 #7 = #6 * 3.14159 / 180 (CONVERT TO RAD) #8 = #1 + #3 * COS[#7] (HOLE X) #9 = #2 + #3 * SIN[#7] (HOLE Y) G81 X#8 Y#9 Z-10 R2 F80 (DRILL CYCLE) #5 = #5 + 1 (INCREMENT COUNTER) #6 = #6 + 360 / #4 (STEP ANGLE) END 1 M30 % ``` ### Step-by-step Logic: | Line | Operation | |---|---| | `#7 = #6 * π/180` | Convert current angle from degrees to radians for trig functions. | | `#8 = #1 + R·cos θ` | X-coordinate of i-th hole on the bolt circle. | | `#9 = #2 + R·sin θ` | Y-coordinate of i-th hole on the bolt circle. | | `G81` | Standard drilling canned cycle (rapid, plunge, retract). | | `#5 = #5 + 1` and `#6 = #6 + 360/8 = 45°` | Advance to next hole position. | | `WHILE…END 1` | Loop until 8 holes have been drilled. | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** Macro programming demonstrates the power of **parametric part families** — change `#3 = 60` and `#4 = 12`, the same program drills a 60 mm bolt circle with 12 holes. This is the conceptual bridge from CNC programming to **generative manufacturing**. --- ## 3.6 Process-Time Calculation (Numerical Derivative) For the slot example (3.1), total path length $L$ is the perimeter: $$L = 80 + 40 + 80 + 40 = 240\ \text{mm}$$ Plus one rapid approach of 53 mm (from origin to P1) and one retract of 25 mm: $$L_{total} = 53 + 240 + 25 = 318\ \text{mm}$$ Cutting time at $F = 80$ mm/min (cutting portion only): $$T_{cut} = \dfrac{240}{80} = 3.0\ \text{min}$$ Plus rapid moves at typical $v_{rapid} = 5000$ mm/min: $$T_{rapid} = \dfrac{78}{5000} \approx 0.016\ \text{min}$$ $$T_{total} \approx 3.016\ \text{min}$$ > **Engineering Meaning:** This is what the **interactive graphics simulator** displays on-screen as "Estimated Cycle Time" — the same formula, executed thousands of times per program in the CAM engine. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Hierarchy of CNC Programming Methods ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CNC PART PROGRAMMING] --> B[METHOD 1: MANUAL G-CODE] A --> C[METHOD 2: COMPUTER-ASSISTED APT] A --> D[METHOD 3: INTERACTIVE GRAPHICS CAM] B --> B1[Word-Address Format] B --> B2[Direct at Machine Console] B --> B3[Simple 2.5D Geometry Only] C --> C1[APT Source Code] C --> C2[Translation Stage] C --> C3[CL File Generation] C --> C4[Post-Processor] C --> C5[Machine Specific G-Code] D --> D1[CAD Model Import] D --> D2[Feature Recognition] D --> D3[Operation Strategy Selection] D --> D4[Automatic Tool Path Generation] D --> D5[2D and 3D Simulation] D --> D6[Collision and Gouge Check] D --> D7[Post-Processed G-Code] B3 --> E[Limitation: No Graphical Verification] C5 --> E D7 --> F[Modern Industry Standard 2024] style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff style C fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff style D fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style F fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — APT Language Processing Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Programmer Writes APT Source] --> P2[Stage 1: Translation] P2 --> P3[Compute CL Cutter Location Points] P3 --> P4[Stage 2: CL File Numerical Data] P4 --> P5[Stage 3: Post Processor] P5 --> P6[Machine Specific G and M Code] P6 --> P7[CNC Machine Tool Execution] P7 --> P8[Finished Component] style P1 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000 style P2 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#000 style P3 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#000 style P4 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#000 style P5 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#000 style P6 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#000 style P7 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#000 style P8 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Interactive Graphics CAM Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Load CAD Model STEP or IGES] --> S2[Define Work Coordinate System] S2 --> S3[Define Stock and Fixture] S3 --> S4[Select Tool from Library] S4 --> S5[Choose Operation Strategy] S5 --> S6[Set Feeds Speeds and Depths] S6 --> S7[Generate Tool Path Automatically] S7 --> S8{Verification Pass} S8 -->|Collision Found| S9[Modify Tool or Strategy] S9 --> S5 S8 -->|Clean| S10[Back Plot 2D Simulation] S10 --> S11[3D Material Removal Simulation] S11 --> S12{User Approves} S12 -->|No| S6 S12 -->|Yes| S13[Run Post Processor] S13 --> S14[Download G Code to CNC] style S8 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style S11 fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000 style S14 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ##
