## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — How a CAD Solid Model is Actually Built

The act of "solid modelling a consumer product" is not a single action but a **pipeline of six structured engineering operations**. KTU expects students to demonstrate awareness of this pipeline:

1. **Requirement Capture & Functional Decomposition**
   - Identify the consumer need the product fulfils.
   - Decompose the product into its **functional sub-systems** (e.g., a hair-dryer → handle, heater coil housing, fan, nozzle, switch assembly, cord grommet).

2. **2D Concept Sketching & Dimensional Sourcing**
   - Hand-sketch or import a 2D layout (top, front, side views).
   - Source realistic dimensions from catalogues (e.g., a standard M8 hex bolt has **across-flats = 13 mm**, thread pitch = **1.25 mm**).

3. **3D Part Modelling (B-Rep Sketcher → Feature Tree)**
   - Use **2D Sketcher** to define profiles.
   - Apply **features**: Extrude, Revolve, Sweep, Loft, Fillet, Chamfer, Shell, Pattern, Mirror.
   - The software constructs the **B-Rep data structure** silently in the background: Vertices → Edges → Faces → Body.

4. **Assembly Modelling with Mates**
   - Import all parts into an **Assembly file**.
   - Apply **Mates** (Concentric, Coincident, Parallel, Perpendicular, Distance, Tangent) to constrain degrees of freedom.
   - Verify that the assembly has **zero degrees of freedom** (rigid-body locked) — KTU examiners actively check this.

5. **Drawing & Detailing (GD&T, Section Views, BOM)**
   - Generate **2D Engineering Drawings** with at least **3 orthographic views + 1 isometric view + 1 sectional view**.
   - Annotate with **dimensions, GD&T symbols, surface finish, and tolerances**.

6. **Simulation Hooks (FEA, CFD, Motion)**
   - Although Module 4 focuses on solid modelling, examiners often award bonus marks if the model is **CAE-ready**: named faces, mid-surfaces extracted, mesh-able topology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in the KTU 2024 scheme:** The 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum emphasises **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. The project demonstration directly tests Course Outcomes such as *CO4: "Apply solid modelling techniques to construct engineering components and assemblies using commercial CAD software."* The above six-step pipeline is the standard rubric examiners use to award the 14-mark Part B questions on this topic.

A simple box-with-holes model is a **sure way to score low marks** — choose a topic that forces you to use Lofts, Sweeps, and Shell features.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of This Exercise

| Industry Vertical | How the Skill Transfers |

| 3D Printing & Rapid Prototyping | The .STL/.3MF export is generated from the solid body. |
| Injection Mould Design | Draft analysis, parting-line detection, and shrinkage compensation all operate on the solid model. |
| Reverse Engineering | A scanned point cloud is fitted to a solid model of the consumer product. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Modelling Methodology & Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section replaces the traditional mathematical derivation with a **rigorous step-by-step modelling methodology** — the equivalent of "derivation" for a CAD project topic. Every step is the KTU-recognised industry best practice for constructing a parametric solid model of a consumer product.

### 3.1 Worked Methodology: Building a Table-Top Electric Fan (3-Blade)

We will exhaustively walk through the modelling of a **3-blade table-top electric fan** — a project topic recommended by several KTU board examiners because it exercises **Revolve, Sweep, Fillet, Shell, Circular Pattern, Mirror, and 4 distinct Mate types** in a single assembly.

**Step 1 — Design Intent Capture**

Before touching the software, document the design intent:
- **Number of blades:** 3
- **Blade outer radius:** $R_{blade} = 250$ mm
- **Hub radius:** $R_{hub} = 35$ mm
- **Motor housing diameter:** $D_{motor} = 80$ mm
- **Rear cage wire radius:** $r_{wire} = 3$ mm
- **Base footprint:** $200 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm}$
- **Material:** ABS Plastic (blades, hub), Mild Steel (cage wires, base), Aluminium (motor shell)

This is the KTU-recommended **design table** that drives all downstream parametric features.

**Step 2 — Sketcher Plane Selection**

Select the **Front Plane** as the master sketching plane. The KTU rule of thumb is: *sketch on the plane that contains the largest number of features you intend to revolve or extrude symmetrically.*

**Step 3 — Blade Profile Construction**

1. Draw a **straight line** from the hub centre to the blade tip — this is the **pitch chord line** of the aerofoil section.
2. At the root (hub end), draw a **circular arc** of radius **30 mm** representing the leading-edge curve.
3. At the tip (blade end), draw a **circular arc** of radius **20 mm** representing the trailing-edge curve.
4. Close the profile with a tangent line — yielding a **two-dimensional airfoil cross-section**.
5. Apply **fully-defined** geometric constraints (Horizontal, Vertical, Tangent, Equal, Symmetric).

**Step 4 — Sweep the Blade**

Select the airfoil profile as the **Sweep cross-section** and a **straight line** drawn radially as the **Sweep path**. The software generates a 3D blade body using a **B-Rep** construction where:
- The **2D airfoil** becomes a **Face** at every station along the path.
- The **Faces** are stitched into a **Shell** of the blade.
- The **end caps** are auto-generated as **Capped ends** of the sweep.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mathematical operation happening here is a **tensor product surface** $S(u,v) = \sum_{i=0}^{m} \sum_{j=0}^{n} N_{i,p}(u) \, N_{j,q}(v) \, P_{i,j}$ where $P_{i,j}$ are the control points of the B-Spline net defined by the airfoil profile and the sweep path. KTU examiners occasionally award bonus marks for explaining this in the viva.

**Step 5 — Fillet the Blade Edges**

Apply a **Constant-Size Fillet** of radius **2 mm** along all 12 sharp edges of the blade (top face perimeter, bottom face perimeter, leading edge, trailing edge). This serves two purposes: **manufacturability** (no sharp mould tooling) and **aerodynamic efficiency**.

**Step 6 — Circular Pattern for the Three Blades**

Select the single blade as the **Seed Feature**, choose the **Front Plane** as the axis reference, set the angle to **360°**, and the instance count to **3**. The software automatically computes and regenerates the other two blades with identical geometry. This is the **CAD implementation of rotational symmetry** in the design.

**Step 7 — Build the Hub (Revolve Feature)**

Sketch a **trapezoidal cross-section** on the Front Plane (a horizontal top edge of 70 mm, two angled side edges converging to a 20 mm vertical line). Apply a **Revolve Boss** of **360°** about the central axis. The hub now encloses the root of all three blades.

**Step 8 — Build the Motor Housing (Revolve + Shell)**

1. Sketch a **rectangle 80 mm × 120 mm** on the Front Plane.
2. Apply a **Revolve Boss** of **360°** to create a solid cylinder.
3. Apply a **Shell** feature with **wall thickness = 2 mm** and **remove the front face** to hollow out the cylinder into a motor can.
4. Add an **Extruded Cut** of diameter **10 mm** through the rear face for the **shaft entry hole**.

**Step 9 — Build the Rear Cage Wires (Sweep + Circular Pattern)**

1. Draw a single **3D Sketch** of a wire path: a curve that starts at the motor housing rear edge, loops forward around the blade tips, and returns to the motor housing.
2. Sketch a **circular profile** of radius **3 mm** in a plane perpendicular to the path start.
3. Apply a **Sweep** feature.
4. **Circular Pattern** this swept wire 8 times around the motor axis at **45°** increments to form the rear cage.
5. Repeat the same procedure for the **front cage wires** (typically 6 wires at 60° increments).

**Step 10 — Build the Base (Extrude + Fillet + Shell)**

1. Sketch a **200 mm × 200 mm** rectangle on the Top Plane.
2. Apply an **Extrude Boss** of **20 mm**.
3. Apply a **Shell** feature with **2 mm wall thickness** and **remove the bottom face** to create a hollow pedestal.
4. Fillet all upper edges with **5 mm** fillet radius.

**Step 11 — Construct the Stand Pipe (Sweep)**

1. Draw a **straight vertical 3D path** of length **300 mm** from the centre of the base to the centre of the motor housing.
2. Sketch a **circular profile** of radius **8 mm** in a plane perpendicular to the path start.
3. Apply a **Sweep** feature to create a tubular stand pipe.

**Step 12 — Assembly Modelling with Mates**

Bring all the individual part files into a single **Assembly file (.SLDASM / .CATPRODUCT / .PRT-ASM)**. Apply the following mates in sequence:

1. **Concentric Mate** → stand pipe outer surface ↔ motor housing rear hole inner surface
2. **Coincident Mate** → base top face ↔ stand pipe bottom endpoint
3. **Coincident Mate** → motor housing centre ↔ stand pipe top endpoint
4. **Concentric Mate** → blade hub inner bore ↔ motor shaft (separate part)
5. **Coincident Mate** → motor shaft front end ↔ hub back face

After these five mates, the assembly should report **0 degrees of freedom** in the CAD property manager — this is the **KTU acceptance criterion for a fully-constrained assembly**.

**Step 13 — Drawing Generation**

Generate a **2D Engineering Drawing** with:
- **Top view** of the base
- **Front view** of the full fan
- **Side view** of the motor and cage
- **Isometric view** of the full assembly
- **Sectioned front view** through the stand pipe and motor
- **Bill of Materials (BOM)** listing all parts with quantities and materials

**Step 14 — Mass Property Verification**

Open the **Mass Properties** dialog and record:
- **Total Assembly Volume** in mm³
- **Total Assembly Mass** in kg
- **Centre of Mass (X, Y, Z)** in mm
- **Principal Moments of Inertia** in kg·mm²

Cross-check whether the centre of mass lies within the base footprint — if not, the fan would tip over. This is the KTU examiner's favourite **design-validation question** in the viva.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which CAD Software to Choose

| Decision Criterion | SolidWorks | CATIA V5 | Siemens NX | Creo Parametric | Autodesk Inventor | Fusion 360 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| KTU Lab Availability (Most Common) | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |
| Ease of Learning (B.Tech Friendly) | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Free for Students (Educational Licence) | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Assembly & Mate Capability | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Surfacing Power (Loft/Sweep Quality) | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |
| Best Suited Project Type | All-rounder | Complex surfacing | Automotive / Aerospace | Mechanical | Mechanical | Hobbyist / 3D Printing |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Recommendation:** If your college lab has only one or two commercial licences, prefer **SolidWorks** or **Fusion 360** because of the steeper student learning curve and abundant YouTube tutorial content for consumer-product projects. CATIA and NX are preferred only if your college has dedicated industrial-licence arrangements.

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Six-Stage Solid Modelling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Stage 1: Requirement Capture] --> start2[Stage 2: 2D Concept Sketch]
    start2 --> start3[Stage 3: 3D Part Modelling]
    start3 --> start4[Stage 4: Assembly Mating]
    start4 --> start5[Stage 5: 2D Drawing and Detailing]
    start5 --> start6[Stage 6: CAE Readiness Check]

    start3 --> f1[Extrude Boss]
    start3 --> f2[Revolve Boss]
    start3 --> f3[Sweep]
    start3 --> f4[Loft]
    start3 --> f5[Shell]
    start3 --> f6[Fillet and Chamfer]

    start4 --> m1[Concentric Mate]
    start4 --> m2[Coincident Mate]
    start4 --> m3[Distance Mate]
    start4 --> m4[Angle Mate]
    start4 --> m5[Tangent Mate]

    start6 --> check1{Mass Properties OK?}
    check1 -- Yes --> done1[Model Approved for Submission]
    check1 -- No --> start3

    classDef stageStyle fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef featureStyle fill:#2d5a2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mateStyle fill:#5a2d5a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff

    class f1,f2,f3,f4,f5,f6 featureStyle
    class m1,m2,m3,m4,m5 mateStyle
    class check1 checkStyle
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    v0[Vertex 0: Tip of airfoil] --> e0[Edge 0: Leading edge]

    e0 --> f0[Face 0: Top skin of blade]
    e1 --> f0
    f0 --> s0[Shell 0: Outer hull of blade]
    s0 --> b0[Solid Body 0: Single Blade]
    b0 --> p0[Part File: Blade.prt]
    p0 --> a0[Assembly: Fan.SLDASM]
    b0 --> p1[Part File: Hub.prt]
    p1 --> a0
    a0 --> a1[Mate 1: Concentric]
    a0 --> a2[Mate 2: Coincident]

    classDef topologyStyle fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#ecf0f1,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class v0,e0,e1,f0,s0,b0,p0,p1,a0,a1,a2 topologyStyle
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any four categories of consumer products suitable for a Module 4 solid modelling mini-project, and for each, name one specific example product and the most appropriate commercial CAD software to model it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Kitchenware** → Example: Pressure cooker with whistle → Software: **CATIA V5 / Siemens NX** (excellent for shell and loft features on the cooker body and helical sweep on the whistle).
2. **Hand Tools** → Example: Adjustable spanner (crescent wrench) → Software: **SolidWorks / Creo Parametric** (ideal for variable-fillet and sweep along an open path).
3. **Domestic Appliance** → Example: Table-top electric fan → Software: **SolidWorks / Autodesk Inventor** (best for circular patterns and concentric mate assemblies).
4. **Personal Care** → Example: Three-piece safety razor → Software: **SolidWorks / Fusion 360** (suitable for revolve-and-shell and Boolean subtract operations).

*(1 mark for category list, 1 mark for product-example pairing, 1 mark for software mapping — any equivalent valid set of 4 categories qualifies for full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a "mate" in the context of assembly modelling using commercial CAD software. State any four types of mates with one real-world example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **mate** is a geometric constraint applied between two or more components in a CAD assembly to restrict their relative degrees of freedom and define their spatial relationship. The four common mate types are:

1. **Concentric Mate** — aligns the cylindrical faces (axes) of two parts along a common centre line. *Example: Shaft inserted into a motor bearing hole.*
2. **Coincident Mate** — makes two planar faces lie in the same plane. *Example: The lid of a pressure cooker resting on the cooker body rim.*
3. **Distance Mate** — fixes a defined gap between two faces. *Example: Maintaining a 1 mm clearance between gear teeth in a gearbox assembly.*
4. **Tangent Mate** — makes a curved face touch a flat or another curved face at a single point. *Example: A cam follower resting on the cam surface.*

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing four types with examples — any four standard mate types accepted.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE regulation: Module 4 Part B questions typically offer a choice between two questions, each worth 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Below are two fully independent alternatives, A and B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *You have been assigned a Module 4 mini-project to solid-model a "Two-Piece Plastic Drinking Water Bottle with Threaded Cap" using a commercial CAD software of your choice. List the sub-systems (parts) you would create, the CAD features (extrude, revolve, sweep, loft, shell, etc.) you would apply to each, and justify the choice of at least two non-trivial features.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Explain, with justification, the sequence of mates you would apply in the assembly file to fully constrain the bottle cap onto the bottle body, and list the engineering drawing views you would generate for the final submission.*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The water bottle assembly would consist of the following **5 sub-system parts**:

1. **Bottle Body (Main Container)**
   - **Feature applied:** Revolve Boss of a 2D profile (rectangular base + parabolic shoulder + cylindrical neck) about the central vertical axis.
   - **Justification:** Revolve is the natural choice for axisymmetric hollow containers and gives a single watertight body in one feature.

2. **Bottle Neck (Threaded Region)**
   - **Feature applied:** Revolve Boss of a separate neck profile, then **Coil Thread** or **Helical Sweep Cut** to carve the thread grooves.
   - **Justification:** A helical sweep is the *only* CAD feature that can mathematically describe the helical thread geometry along a cylindrical surface; extrude and revolve cannot create helix-based features.

3. **Bottle Cap (Outer Shell)**
   - **Feature applied:** Revolve Boss of a cap profile, then **Shell** feature with wall thickness **1.5 mm**, removing the bottom face.
   - **Justification:** Shell allows the cap to be a hollow container — a solid revolve would create a useless solid cylinder, not a usable cap.

4. **Cap Inner Plug**
   - **Feature applied:** Extrude Boss of a circular disc with a small rib pattern, then a **Fillet** of **0.5 mm** on all sharp edges to facilitate insertion into the bottle neck.
   - **Justification:** The internal plug must be designed with fillets to avoid stress concentration and to provide a sealing surface.

5. **Carry Loop Ring**
   - **Feature applied:** **Sweep** feature — circular profile (Ø **3 mm**) along an arc-shaped 3D path.
   - **Justification:** Sweep is the natural feature for a constant-cross-section curved ring; extrude would only produce a straight bar.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for listing parts with features, 3 marks for the two non-trivial feature justifications, 2 marks for overall completeness of the design table.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Mate Sequence for Full Constraint (Cap onto Bottle):**

1. **Concentric Mate** — between the outer cylindrical face of the cap inner plug and the inner cylindrical face of the bottle neck. *(Constrains 2 rotational + 2 translational DoF)*
2. **Coincident Mate** — between the top face of the bottle neck and the bottom face of the cap's inner shoulder. *(Constrains 1 translational DoF — the vertical one)*
3. **Coincident Mate** — between the centre axis (axis of revolution) of the cap and the centre axis of the bottle body. *(Constrains the remaining 1 DoF)*
4. **Tangent Mate** — between the bottom rim of the cap outer shell and the outer curved surface of the bottle shoulder. *(Optional cosmetic mate)*

After the first three mates, the assembly should report **0 degrees of freedom** — this is the KTU acceptance criterion.

**Engineering Drawing Views for Final Submission:**

1. **Front View** — full bottle and cap, with the cap shown in its engaged (closed) position.
2. **Top View** — circular cap seen from above showing the cap knurling and central thread axis.
3. **Side View** — identical to front view due to axisymmetry, but provided to satisfy the three-view projection convention.
4. **Isometric (Pictorial) View** — for 3D visualisation of the assembly.
5. **Sectional View (Full Section, A-A)** — vertical section through the cap and bottle showing the internal thread engagement, shell wall thickness, and the sealing surface contact.

Additional deliverables include a **Bill of Materials (BOM)** with part numbers, materials, and quantities, and a **Mass Properties table** showing total volume, mass, and centre of mass.

*(Mark split: 3 marks for the mate sequence, 2 marks for the engineering drawing list, 2 marks for the BOM and mass-property deliverables.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *For a Module 4 mini-project on solid modelling a "Three-Blade Pedestal Fan Assembly" using SolidWorks, prepare a complete design table listing (i) all part names, (ii) the CAD feature(s) used to create each part, and (iii) the material assigned to each part.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Explain the B-Rep data structure (Vertex → Edge → Face → Shell → Body → Part) that the software constructs internally when you create the motor housing using the Revolve + Shell feature sequence, and describe how this B-Rep data enables downstream CAE meshing.*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Part # | Part Name | CAD Feature(s) Used | Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Front Cage Ring (Outer) | Revolve Boss (Ø 500 mm × Ø 5 mm) | Mild Steel |
| 2 | Front Cage Wire (×6) | Sweep along curved 3D path | Mild Steel |
| 3 | Blade (×3) | Sweep along radial straight path | ABS Plastic |
| 4 | Hub (Central Boss) | Revolve Boss + Fillet | ABS Plastic |
| 5 | Motor Housing Shell | Revolve Boss + Shell (2 mm wall) | Aluminium |
| 6 | Motor Shaft | Revolve Boss + Chamfer | Stainless Steel |
| 7 | Rear Cage Ring (Outer) | Revolve Boss (Ø 500 mm × Ø 5 mm) | Mild Steel |
| 8 | Rear Cage Wire (×8) | Sweep along curved 3D path | Mild Steel |
| 9 | Stand Pipe (Vertical) | Sweep (Ø 16 mm circular profile) | Mild Steel |
| 10 | Base Pedestal | Extrude Boss + Shell (2 mm wall) | Cast Iron |
| 11 | Base Rubber Pad | Extrude Boss of elliptical profile | Rubber |
| 12 | Switch Knob (Detent) | Revolve Boss + Fillet + Pattern | ABS Plastic |

*(2 marks for correct part list, 2 marks for matching features, 1 mark for materials, 2 marks for overall completeness and reasonable part count.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

When the user creates the **Motor Housing** using **Revolve Boss** followed by **Shell**, the CAD kernel (e.g., Parasolid for SolidWorks, ACIS for Inventor) builds the following **B-Rep data structure internally**:

1. **Vertices (0D topological entities):** The endpoints of all bounding edges. For a hollow cylinder motor housing with two open ends, the software generates approximately **16 to 24 unique vertices** depending on the number of fillets applied.

2. **Edges (1D topological entities):** Each vertex pair is connected by an edge. For a cylinder, there are **2 circular edges** (top and bottom rims) and **1 cylindrical side edge**. After filleting, the filleted transitions introduce additional **arc edges**.

3. **Faces (2D topological entities):** Each closed loop of edges bounds a face. The hollow motor housing has **3 faces** — the inner cylindrical face, the outer cylindrical face, and the two annular rim faces (top and bottom).

4. **Shells (collection of faces):** All the faces are grouped into a single **Shell** that is *open* (because the cylinder is hollow, not solid). After the **Shell** feature is applied, the topology is reorganised into an **outer shell** (visible faces) and an **inner shell** (cavity faces).

5. **Body (closed solid region):** The shell(s) together with a coherent orientation define a **Solid Body** — a closed 3D region of space. The Body carries the mass, the centre of mass, and the moments of inertia.

6. **Part:** The Body is serialised into a **.SLDPRT** file along with feature history, sketch dimensions, material assignment, and custom properties.

**How B-Rep Enables Downstream CAE Meshing:**

When this model is exported to a CAE tool (ANSYS, Abaqus, HyperMesh) via STEP or IGES:

- The **Faces** become 2D mesh-seedable surfaces.
- The **Edges** become 1D mesh-seedable curves and serve as natural locations for **element-density refinement** (e.g., filleted edges get smaller elements).
- The **Vertices** become 0D mesh-seedable points.
- The **Body** is the 3D solid region on which **tetrahedral or hexahedral finite elements** are generated.
- The **orientation of faces** (inward/outward normal) is preserved by the B-Rep, allowing the mesher to correctly identify the **wetted surface** (for CFD) or the **loaded surface** (for FEA) when boundary conditions are applied.

*(Mark split: 3 marks for the B-Rep hierarchy explanation, 2 marks for the motor-housing example, 2 marks for the CAE-mesh linkage.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in Module 4 project viva and written answers (KTU 2024 Board Examiner Observations):**
> 1. **Choosing an "easy" product with no genuine solid features** (e.g., a plain rectangular box) — examiner deducts up to **4 of 14 marks** for lack of design effort. Always pick a product that *forces* you to use at least 4 feature types and 3 mate types.
> 2. **Forgetting the "fully-constrained" criterion** — submitting an assembly that still has unconstrained degrees of freedom. Examiners deliberately drag a part in the assembly and ask "Why is it moving? Where is the missing mate?" — a wrong answer costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **No parametric design table** — submitting dimensions hard-coded into sketches without using a global design table or equations. Examiners test parametricity by **changing one dimension** and asking if the model regenerates cleanly.
> 4. **Confusing B-Rep with CSG** — students often incorrectly state that a Shell feature "creates" a B-Rep. The B-Rep *exists from the moment* the first feature is created; the Shell feature only *modifies* the topology by introducing inner faces. Examiners deduct marks for this conceptual error.
> 5. **Omitting the BOM and Mass Properties table** in the final drawing submission — this is a **mandatory deliverable** per KTU 2024 lab manual.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Know Bullets Before the KTU Exam):**
>
> - [ ] **Solid modelling** = mathematically complete 3D representation of an object including its interior volume, not just its surface.
> - [ ] **B-Rep** (Boundary Representation) is the dominant scheme in commercial CAD; topology is **Vertex → Edge → Face → Shell → Body**.
> - [ ] **CSG** (Constructive Solid Geometry) uses Boolean union, subtract, and intersect on primitive solids; less common in modern commercial packages.
> - [ ] **Parametric modelling** = dimensions drive the geometry; changing a dimension regenerates the model.
> - [ ] Always choose a **consumer product** that demonstrates at least **4 distinct feature types** (Extrude, Revolve, Sweep, Loft, Shell, Fillet, etc.).
> - [ ] Always achieve **0 degrees of freedom** in the final assembly by applying the correct sequence of **mates**.
> - [ ] The **mate types** are: Concentric, Coincident, Parallel, Perpendicular, Tangent, Distance, Angle, Lock, Symmetric.
> - [ ] The **engineering drawing** must contain: 3 orthographic views + 1 isometric view + 1 sectional view + BOM + Mass Properties.
> - [ ] The **Bill of Materials (BOM)** must list every part with item number, description, material, and quantity.
> - [ ] **Mass Properties** to record: Volume (mm³), Mass (kg), Centre of Mass (X, Y, Z in mm), Moments of Inertia (kg·mm²), Surface Area (mm²).
> - [ ] Top recommended consumer products for KTU 2024 mini-projects: mixer-grinder, pressure cooker, electric fan, hair dryer, adjustable spanner, safety razor, door hinge, table-top pedestal fan.
> - [ ] Top recommended software: **SolidWorks** (most college labs) > **Fusion 360** (free for students) > **CATIA V5** (complex surfacing) > **Siemens NX** (automotive/industrial).
> - [ ] The **Shell** feature reduces a solid body to a hollow body of specified wall thickness by removing one or more faces.
> - [ ] The **Fillet** feature rounds sharp edges with a specified radius — critical for manufacturability (especially for plastic injection-moulded parts).
> - [ ] The **Circular Pattern** feature creates multiple instances of a feature or body around a central axis at equal angular increments.
> - [ ] The **Mirror** feature creates a copy of a body or feature reflected about a reference plane — useful for symmetric products.
> - [ ] All KTU Module 4 projects must result in a **fully-constrained assembly** + **parametric individual parts** + **engineering drawing** + **CAE-ready topology**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on CAD customization (e.g., modelling of a customizable phone case)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Experiment on CAD customization (e.g., modelling of a customizable phone case)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CAD Customization** is the process of tailoring a Computer-Aided Design environment, its feature libraries, parametric constraints, design tables, and user-interface elements to suit a specific engineering task, product family, or organizational workflow. In the context of a hands-on experiment, it involves the deliberate construction of a **parametric template** — a master model whose geometry is driven by a finite set of **user-defined variables** (lengths, radii, angles, Boolean switches) so that a *family of related parts* can be generated from a single source file without redrawing.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this maps directly to the laboratory outcome: *"demonstrate the ability to build, constrain, and reconfigure a feature-based solid model using parameter-driven customisation."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PBPET504, Module 4):** The experiment on CAD customisation is an *application-level* exercise that consolidates **feature-based modelling**, **parametric constraints**, **design tables**, and **configurations** into a single demonstrable workflow. It is the *culminating experiment* of the CAD module and is most often evaluated through a viva-voce + model submission in the lab internal assessment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Recipe"

Imagine you are running a bakery that sells customized celebration cakes. You do **not** bake a brand-new cake from scratch every time a customer asks for a different message, size, or flavour. Instead, you keep one **master recipe card** with blank fields:

- Cake diameter: `______ cm`
- Number of tiers: `______`
- Flavour: `______`
- Message text: `"______"`

The recipe is the same; only the **parameters** (the blanks) change. The oven (your CAD kernel) reads the parameters and produces a unique cake. That recipe card is exactly what a *customised CAD template* is — a geometry definition with **named variables** that the designer fills in to instantly regenerate the part.

For the **phone case experiment**, the master model of the case has parameters such as:
- Phone model name (driving overall envelope)
- Wall thickness of the case
- Camera cut-out diameter
- Button cut-out height
- Raised-edge height around the screen
- Whether a grip pattern is present (Boolean switch)

Change the value of any parameter, and the same template regenerates a case for a Samsung S23, an iPhone 15, a OnePlus 12, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Customisation is *not* the same as *modelling*. Modelling produces one fixed part; customisation produces an **infinite family of parts** from one intelligently-constrained source. The hallmark of a good customised model is that **design intent** is preserved under every parameter change.

### 1.3 Why This Experiment Exists in the KTU 2024 Curriculum

The experiment is pedagogically placed at the end of Module 4 because it integrates every prior learning outcome:

| Prior Module | Skill Reused in the Customisation Experiment |
|---|---|
| Module 1 (Intro to CAD) | Understanding of sketch entities and feature tree |
| Module 2 (Geometric Modelling) | Solid primitives, Boolean operations, feature-based editing |
| Module 3 (Assembly & Drafting) | Assembly-level references, drawing regeneration |
| Module 4 (Customisation) | Parameters, equations, design tables, configurations |

A KTU examiner expects the student to demonstrate **all four** layers simultaneously during the lab evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of CAD Customisation

A robust customisation experiment rests on five theoretical pillars. Each is a *separate marking checkpoint* in the KTU lab rubrics.

**Pillar 1 — Parametric Dimensions**
Every dimension that defines the geometry is given a **symbolic name** (e.g., `L_phone`, `W_phone`, `T_wall`, `R_corner`, `D_camera`). The model no longer has *anonymous numbers*; it has *named variables*. When you edit the variable, every dependent feature updates automatically.

**Pillar 2 — Geometric & Dimensional Constraints**
- **Dimensional constraints** fix numerical values (e.g., a distance of `8 mm`).
- **Geometric constraints** fix relations (e.g., *horizontal*, *vertical*, *tangent*, *concentric*, *equal-length*, *symmetric*).
- Together they encode **design intent** — the *reason* a feature has the size and position it has.

**Pillar 3 — Equations / Relations**
Algebraic relationships between parameters. Example:
- `L_case = L_phone + 2 * T_wall`
- `W_case = W_phone + 2 * T_wall`
- `D_camera_cut = D_camera_lens + 0.5`

These eliminate the need to manually re-key dependent values.

**Pillar 4 — Design Tables (External Customisation)**
A spreadsheet (typically Excel) where each **row** is a *configuration* (a member of the part family) and each **column** is a *parameter*. The CAD application reads the table and regenerates the geometry for each row. This is the engine behind "phone case for model A, B, C, D, E."

**Pillar 5 — Configurations (Internal Customisation)**
A *configuration* is a named snapshot of parameter values stored inside the CAD file itself. The same file can hold "Config_iphone15" and "Config_samsungS23" simultaneously, and the user toggles between them. Design tables *populate* configurations automatically.

### 2.2 The Phone Case as a Customisation Case Study

A modern smartphone case is an ideal customisation vehicle because:
1. The **outer envelope is parametric** (depends on phone model).
2. The **functional cut-outs** (camera, buttons, USB-C port, speaker grills) are positional parametric features.
3. The **aesthetic options** (texture, ribs, pattern) can be controlled by Boolean switches.
4. The case must satisfy **manufacturability constraints** (minimum wall thickness, draft angles for injection moulding).

This makes it a perfect *closed-form* problem with bounded parameter space — a feature KTU examiners appreciate because it allows clear, unambiguous evaluation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Parameter Sheet

The table below lists the **key parametric relationships** that the experiment expects a student to define. These are the "formulas" of CAD customisation — the equations you write in the *Equations Manager / Relations dialog* of any modern parametric CAD package (SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo, NX, Fusion 360, Inventor, Solid Edge, OnShape).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Convention:** Lengths are in **millimetres (mm)**, angles in **degrees (°)**, and Boolean parameters use the convention `1 = ON / present`, `0 = OFF / suppressed`.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Range | Unit | Equation / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $L_{\text{phone}}$ | Length of phone body | 130 – 170 | mm | Independent input |
| $W_{\text{phone}}$ | Width of phone body | 60 – 80 | mm | Independent input |
| $H_{\text{phone}}$ | Thickness of phone body | 7 – 10 | mm | Independent input |
| $T_{\text{wall}}$ | Wall thickness of case | 1.5 – 3.0 | mm | Independent input |
| $L_{\text{case}}$ | Outer length of case | derived | mm | $L_{\text{case}} = L_{\text{phone}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{wall}}$ |
| $W_{\text{case}}$ | Outer width of case | derived | mm | $W_{\text{case}} = W_{\text{phone}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{wall}}$ |
| $H_{\text{case}}$ | Inner cavity height | derived | mm | $H_{\text{case}} = H_{\text{phone}} + 0.5$ |
| $H_{\text{lip}}$ | Raised-edge lip above screen | 0.8 – 1.5 | mm | Independent input |
| $R_{\text{corner}}$ | Corner radius of case | 6 – 12 | mm | Independent input |
| $D_{\text{cam}}$ | Camera cut-out diameter | 8 – 18 | mm | Independent input |
| $X_{\text{cam}}, Y_{\text{cam}}$ | Camera cut-out position | per phone | mm | Linked to phone dimensions |
| $L_{\text{port}}$ | USB-C port slot length | 9.0 | mm | Standard USB-C |
| $W_{\text{port}}$ | USB-C port slot width | 3.0 | mm | Standard USB-C |
| $N_{\text{btns}}$ | Number of side buttons | 2 or 3 | — | Discrete parameter |
| $B_{\text{grip}}$ | Boolean: grip pattern | 0 or 1 | — | Switch parameter |
| $B_{\text{wireless}}$ | Boolean: wireless-charging window | 0 or 1 | — | Switch parameter |
| $V_{\text{case}}$ | Volume of case material | derived | mm³ | $V_{\text{case}} \approx L_{\text{case}} \cdot W_{\text{case}} \cdot (T_{\text{wall}} + H_{\text{lip}}) - V_{\text{cuts}}$ |
| $M_{\text{case}}$ | Mass of case (for ABS, $\rho = 1.04$ g/cm³) | derived | g | $M_{\text{case}} = \rho \cdot V_{\text{case}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations do not merely *describe* a case — they embed the **design intent** ("the case is always exactly two wall-thicknesses larger than the phone, in both directions") so that a non-expert can change one number and get a valid case. This is the *real-world engineering value* of customisation: a single master model can be reused by marketing, sales, and manufacturing without any CAD knowledge.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** A car body panel family (left/right doors, sedan/hatchback) — one master model, multiple configurations.
- **Aerospace:** Rib families in a wing — one rib sketch, parameters for chord, skin thickness, lightening holes.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Phone cases, smartwatch bands, laptop sleeves — driven by model names.
- **Medical:** Customised prosthetics and orthotics — patient dimensions are the parameters.
- **PCB & Sheet Metal:** Bracket families with parametric hole patterns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theory → Practice Link:** The equations in the table above are what you will *type into* the Equations Manager during the experiment. Each equation is one mark in the KTU lab rubric under the heading *"Relations and Design Intent."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the *full analytical pipeline* of the experiment. Because CAD customisation is a **procedural engineering exercise**, the "derivation" here takes the form of: **(i) deriving the parametric equations**, and **(ii) the logical step-by-step build sequence** of the parametric model.

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Parametric Equation Set

The aim is to express *every* dimension of the case in terms of a *minimal* set of independent inputs. This is the **canonical (independent) parameter set** of the experiment.

**Step 1 — Identify the free parameters (independent inputs).**
The geometric envelope of a phone case is fully described by the following independent inputs:

$$
\{L_{\text{phone}},\ W_{\text{phone}},\ H_{\text{phone}},\ T_{\text{wall}},\ H_{\text{lip}},\ R_{\text{corner}},\ D_{\text{cam}}\}
$$

These are the only values the user should ever have to type in. Everything else is **derived**.

**Step 2 — Express the outer envelope of the case.**
The case wraps the phone, so its outer dimensions are the phone dimensions plus **twice the wall thickness** (one wall on each side):

$$
L_{\text{case}} = L_{\text{phone}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{wall}}
$$

$$
W_{\text{case}} = W_{\text{phone}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{wall}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the inner cavity.**
The cavity must accommodate the phone thickness with a small clearance (typically $0.5$ mm) for easy insertion:

$$
H_{\text{cavity}} = H_{\text{phone}} + 0.5
$$

**Step 4 — Express the raised-edge lip.**
The screen-protection lip rises above the back-face by $H_{\text{lip}}$:

$$
H_{\text{case,total}} = H_{\text{cavity}} + H_{\text{lip}}
$$

**Step 5 — Express the position of the camera cut-out.**
Modern phones place the camera module at the top-centre of the back face. If we define the **centre of the camera cut-out** as $(\,X_{\text{cam}}, Y_{\text{cam}}\,)$ in the case's local coordinate system, and if the phone's back-face datum is offset by $T_{\text{wall}}$ from the case centre, then:

$$
X_{\text{cam}} = 0 \quad \text{(symmetric about the vertical centreline)}
$$

$$
Y_{\text{cam}} = \frac{W_{\text{case}}}{2} - \Delta_{\text{top}}
$$

where $\Delta_{\text{top}}$ is the *design clearance* from the top edge to the centre of the camera cut-out (a small independent parameter, e.g., $5$ mm to $10$ mm depending on the phone model).

**Step 6 — Express the USB-C port cut-out position.**
The USB-C port is centred on the bottom edge:

$$
X_{\text{port}} = 0
$$

$$
Y_{\text{port}} = -\frac{L_{\text{case}}}{2} + \Delta_{\text{bottom}}
$$

where $\Delta_{\text{bottom}}$ is the offset from the bottom edge to the port slot centre.

**Step 7 — Express the volume of the case (for mass / cost estimation).**

The case is approximately a *rectangular shell* with a top-open cavity. The volume of material is the outer block volume minus the inner cavity volume minus the cut-out volumes:

$$
V_{\text{case}} = L_{\text{case}} \cdot W_{\text{case}} \cdot H_{\text{case,total}} - L_{\text{phone}} \cdot W_{\text{phone}} \cdot H_{\text{cavity}} - \sum_{i} V_{\text{cut},i}
$$

For a first-order estimate, the cut-out sum can be neglected, giving:

$$
V_{\text{case}} \approx L_{\text{case}} \cdot W_{\text{case}} \cdot H_{\text{case,total}} - L_{\text{phone}} \cdot W_{\text{phone}} \cdot H_{\text{cavity}}
$$

**Step 8 — Express the mass using material density.**

$$
M_{\text{case}} = \rho_{\text{material}} \cdot V_{\text{case}}
$$

For **ABS plastic** ($\rho = 1.04$ g/cm³) and **Polycarbonate** ($\rho = 1.20$ g/cm³), the values are standard reference numbers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Substitution check:** Plug in a representative phone (say, $L_{\text{phone}} = 160$ mm, $W_{\text{phone}} = 75$ mm, $H_{\text{phone}} = 8$ mm, $T_{\text{wall}} = 2$ mm, $H_{\text{lip}} = 1$ mm). Then:
> $L_{\text{case}} = 164$ mm, $W_{\text{case}} = 79$ mm, $H_{\text{case,total}} = 9.5$ mm.
> Outer block volume $= 164 \times 79 \times 9.5 \approx 123{,}082$ mm³.
> Cavity volume $= 160 \times 75 \times 8.5 \approx 102{,}000$ mm³.
> Material volume $\approx 21{,}082$ mm³ $\approx 21.08$ cm³.
> Mass in ABS $\approx 1.04 \times 21.08 \approx 21.9$ g. A typical phone case — confirmed against published data.

### 3.2 Analytical Build Matrix — Step-by-Step Lab Procedure

The following matrix is the *complete executable procedure* for the experiment. Each row is a *lab step*, and the last two columns are the *expected output* and the *typical marks awarded* under the KTU 2024 lab rubric.

| Step # | Action in CAD | Tool / Command | Expected Result | Marks (out of 100 lab internal) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open a new part file. Set document units to **mm**. | New Part | Blank part with three default planes | 2 |
| 2 | Open the *Equations Manager* (or *Global Variables / Parameters* dialog). Declare all independent parameters with default values. | Equations / fx Manager | 7 named parameters visible | 10 |
| 3 | Sketch the phone envelope rectangle on the *Top Plane*, using $L_{\text{phone}}$ and $W_{\text{phone}}$ as parametric dimensions. | Rectangle + Smart Dimension | Rectangle updates when $L_{\text{phone}}$ changes | 8 |
| 4 | Add fillets of radius $R_{\text{corner}}$ to the four corners of the sketch. | Fillet | Corner radius is parametric | 4 |
| 5 | Extrude the rectangle outward by $H_{\text{cavity}} + H_{\text{lip}} + T_{\text{wall}}$ to form the outer block. | Boss-Extrude | Outer block exists | 6 |
| 6 | Create a *second* sketch on the top face. Sketch the phone-sized inner rectangle. Use $L_{\text{phone}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{wall}}$ and $W_{\text{phone}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{wall}}$ as dimensions. | Rectangle | Inner sketch is offset by exactly $T_{\text{wall}}$ | 6 |
| 7 | Extrude-cut the inner sketch to a depth of $H_{\text{cavity}}$, leaving the lip intact. | Extrude Cut (Through Next or Up-To-Surface) | Hollow cavity formed | 6 |
| 8 | Add the **camera cut-out** — circle of diameter $D_{\text{cam}}$ at $(X_{\text{cam}}, Y_{\text{cam}})$. Extrude-cut through the back wall. | Circle + Cut | Camera hole parametric | 8 |
| 9 | Add the **USB-C port slot** — rectangle $L_{\text{port}} \times W_{\text{port}}$ at $(X_{\text{port}}, Y_{\text{port}})$. | Slot Cut | Port slot parametric | 6 |
| 10 | Add **side-button cut-outs** — small rectangles on the lateral faces, controlled by the $N_{\text{btns}}$ parameter. | Cut | Button holes parametric | 6 |
| 11 | Add **grip pattern** as a linear pattern of small dimples, suppressed by the Boolean $B_{\text{grip}}$. | Linear Pattern + Suppress | Pattern toggles on/off | 8 |
| 12 | Add a **wireless-charging logo / cutout window**, suppressed by $B_{\text{wireless}}$. | Cut | Optional feature parametric | 4 |
| 13 | Build a **Design Table** (Excel) with rows for: iPhone 15, Samsung S23, OnePlus 12, Pixel 8, generic. | Design Table | Table linked to file | 12 |
| 14 | Verify regeneration for at least **three** configurations. | Edit Configuration | Three valid cases regenerated | 8 |
| 15 | Compute and display $M_{\text{case}}$ using the mass-properties tool; verify it matches the equation within ±5%. | Mass Properties | Mass-property validation | 6 |
| **Total** | | | | **100** |

### 3.3 The Customisation Workflow (Logical Derivation Chain)

The full logical chain, written out so that no step is implicit:

$$
\text{Independent inputs} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Step 1-4}}\; \text{Outer envelope} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Step 5-6}}\; \text{Cavity} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Step 7-10}}\; \text{Functional features} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Step 11-12}}\; \text{Aesthetic features}
$$

$$
\;\xrightarrow{\text{Step 13}}\; \text{Design table binds inputs to a part family} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Step 14}}\; \text{Validated configurations} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Step 15}}\; \text{Verified physical properties}
$$

Each arrow is a *transition that must be demonstrated live* to the examiner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Logic:** The KTU 2024 lab rubric awards the *highest* marks (typically 12/100) to Step 13 — the **design table** — because that step alone proves the student has crossed from "I can model a part" to "I can customise a family of parts," which is the CO of this experiment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Customisation Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below captures the *data flow* and *control flow* of the entire customisation experiment. Each block corresponds to a logical group of operations in the lab procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start New Part File] --> B[Declare Global Parameters]
    B --> C[Define Independent Inputs Lphone Wphone Hphone Twall Hlip Dcam Rcorner]
    C --> D[Sketch Phone Envelope on Top Plane]
    D --> E[Add Parametric Corner Fillets]
    E --> F[Extrude Outer Block]
    F --> G[Sketch Inner Cavity Rectangle]
    G --> H[Extrude Cut Cavity Depth Hcavity]
    H --> I[Add Camera Cutout Circle]
    H --> J[Add USB C Port Slot]
    H --> K[Add Side Button Cutouts]
    H --> L[Add Optional Grip Pattern]
    H --> M[Add Optional Wireless Window]
    I --> N[Add Geometric Constraints Horizontal Vertical Tangent Concentric]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Write Equations in Equations Manager]
    O --> P[Link Equations to Feature Dimensions]
    P --> Q[Build Design Table Excel]
    Q --> R[Row 1 iPhone 15]
    Q --> S[Row 2 Samsung S23]
    Q --> T[Row 3 OnePlus 12]
    Q --> U[Row 4 Generic]
    R --> V[Validate Configuration Regen]
    S --> V
    T --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[Check Mass Properties vs Equation]
    W --> X[Submit Lab Record and 3D Model]
    X --> Y[End of Experiment]
```

### 4.2 Parameter Dependency Graph

This graph shows which parameters depend on which. The leaf nodes are the *user-typed* values; the internal nodes are *derived* values. A student who understands this graph can debug any regeneration failure in seconds.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U1[Lphone mm]:::leaf
    U2[Wphone mm]:::leaf
    U3[Hphone mm]:::leaf
    U4[Twall mm]:::leaf
    U5[Hlip mm]:::leaf
    U6[Rcorner mm]:::leaf
    U7[Dcam mm]:::leaf
    U8[Nbtns count]:::leaf
    U9[Bgrip 0 or 1]:::leaf
    U10[Bwireless 0 or 1]:::leaf

    D1[Lcase]:::derived
    D2[Wcase]:::derived
    D3[Hcavity]:::derived
    D4[Htotal]:::derived
    D5[Vcase]:::derived
    D6[Mcase]:::derived

    U1 --> D1
    U4 --> D1
    U2 --> D2
    U4 --> D2
    U3 --> D3
    U4 --> D3
    U3 --> D4
    U5 --> D4
    D1 --> D5
    D2 --> D5
    D4 --> D5
    U1 --> D5
    U2 --> D5
    D3 --> D5
    D5 --> D6

    classDef leaf fill:#dff0d8,stroke:#3c763d,stroke-width:2px
    classDef derived fill:#fcf8e3,stroke:#8a6d3b,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the graph:** The user only ever edits the green *leaf* nodes. The amber *derived* nodes are computed automatically by the Equations Manager. If a derived node goes red in your CAD system, the equation chain has been broken — fix the equation, not the dimension.

### 4.3 Design Table — Conceptual Data Schema

The design table is a *rectangular data structure*. Each row is a configuration; each column is a parameter. The CAD kernel reads one row at a time, pushes those values into the named parameters, and regenerates the model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph designTable[Design Table Excel or CSV]
        direction LR
        T1[Column Lphone] --> R1[Row iPhone15: 147.6]
        T2[Column Wphone] --> R1
        T3[Column Hphone] --> R1
        T4[Column Twall] --> R1
        T5[Column Dcam] --> R1
        T6[Column Rcorner] --> R1
    end

    R1 --> Engine[CAD Solver Engine]
    Engine --> Out1[Regenerated iPhone 15 Case]
    Engine --> Out2[Regenerated Samsung S23 Case]
    Engine --> Out3[Regenerated OnePlus 12 Case]
    Engine --> Out4[Regenerated Generic Case]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Note:** The examiner will *change one cell* in your design table and ask you to regenerate. If the file fails to rebuild, you lose 6–8 marks immediately. Always verify that your sketch is *fully defined* (shown in black, not blue) before adding a design table.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> **Define CAD customisation. List any four methods by which a CAD model can be customised.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
CAD customisation is the process of modifying a CAD system or model to suit a specific design requirement, such that a single source file can produce multiple variants of a part.
Four methods (any four, ½ mark each):
1. **Parametric dimensioning** — naming dimensions and linking them to variables.
2. **Equations / Relations** — algebraic linkage between parameters.
3. **Design Tables** — external spreadsheet driving parameter values.
4. **Configurations** — named snapshots of parameter values.
5. **User-defined features (UDFs)** — saving frequently used feature groups.
6. **Macros / API scripts** — automating repetitive tasks.

*(1 mark for the definition + 2 marks for the four methods, distributed appropriately.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> **What is a "Design Table" in parametric CAD? Mention its two main advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Design Table** is a spreadsheet (typically Microsoft Excel) that drives the dimensions of a parametric model. Each row of the table corresponds to a *configuration* (a member of a part family), and each column corresponds to a *parameter* of the model. (1½ marks)
**Advantages** (any two, ¾ mark each):
1. It allows **mass customisation** of a part family from a single source file.
2. It enables **design intent preservation** — all variants share the same constraints and feature tree.
3. It supports **engineering change propagation** — editing the master template updates every variant.
4. It is **non-programmer friendly** — designers can manage families without writing code.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of *parametric modelling*. Discuss its advantages over direct (non-parametric) modelling.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** For a customisable phone-case experiment, derive the parametric equations that relate the outer dimensions of the case to the phone's dimensions, the wall thickness, and the raised-edge lip. Assume the case is a rectangular shell with a top-open cavity.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Parametric Modelling — 7 marks**

- **Definition (2 marks):** Parametric modelling is a feature-based modelling approach in which the geometry of a part is defined by *parameters* (variables) and *constraints* (relations). Changing a parameter automatically updates all dependent features. The model is *history-based* and *intent-driven*.

- **Sketch (2 marks):** A standard block diagram showing:
  - Parameter block (named variables).
  - Equations block (relations between parameters).
  - Feature tree block (sketches + features that consume the parameters).
  - Output geometry block.
  - Arrows showing data flow from parameters to features.

- **Advantages over direct (non-parametric) modelling (3 marks, any three):**
  1. *Easy editing* — change a dimension, the model rebuilds.
  2. *Family of parts* — multiple variants from one source.
  3. *Design intent captured* — constraints encode the *reason* behind a geometry.
  4. *Faster iteration* — design changes propagate automatically.
  5. *Better collaboration* — non-CAD users can customise via design tables.

**(b) Parametric Equations for the Phone Case — 7 marks**

The independent input parameters (1 mark) are:

$$
\{L_{\text{phone}},\ W_{\text{phone}},\ H_{\text{phone}},\ T_{\text{wall}},\ H_{\text{lip}}\}
$$

The derived equations (4 marks, 1 each):

$$
L_{\text{case}} = L_{\text{phone}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{wall}}
$$

$$
W_{\text{case}} = W_{\text{phone}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{wall}}
$$

$$
H_{\text{cavity}} = H_{\text{phone}} + 0.5
$$

$$
H_{\text{case,total}} = H_{\text{cavity}} + H_{\text{lip}}
$$

Sample substitution (1 mark) for $L_{\text{phone}} = 160$ mm, $T_{\text{wall}} = 2$ mm:

$$
L_{\text{case}} = 160 + 2 \cdot 2 = 164\ \text{mm}
$$

Conclusion (1 mark): All four dimensions of the case are now *single-source-of-truth* driven by the phone dimensions, and the design intent "case wraps phone with uniform wall thickness" is mathematically encoded.

---

> **Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of a **Design Table** in CAD customisation. Describe the procedure to create a Design Table for a family of phone cases (e.g., iPhone 15, Samsung S23, OnePlus 12) in any standard parametric CAD package.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** During the phone-case experiment, the mass of the case for an iPhone 15 configuration was measured as $24$ g, while the theoretical calculation from the parametric equations gave $21.9$ g. Identify three possible sources of error and suggest corrective actions for each.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Design Table Procedure — 7 marks**

- **Role of Design Table (2 marks):** A Design Table is the *external data source* that drives the parameters of a parametric model. It allows the same CAD file to represent an entire *part family*, with each row being one configuration. It is the practical realisation of mass customisation in CAD.

- **Procedure (5 marks, 1 each step):**
  1. **Create the master model** with all dimensions linked to named global parameters.
  2. **Insert → Design Table** (or equivalent command) in the CAD package. Choose *Excel* as the source.
  3. The CAD system **auto-populates the first column** with all parameter names.
  4. The user **fills in the rows** with the parameter values for each phone model.
     *Example row for iPhone 15:*
     $L_{\text{phone}} = 147.6$, $W_{\text{phone}} = 71.6$, $H_{\text{phone}} = 7.80$, $T_{\text{wall}} = 2.0$, $D_{\text{cam}} = 14.0$, $R_{\text{corner}} = 9.0$.
  5. **Confirm** the table. The CAD kernel will then offer a list of configurations — one per row — that can be activated independently.

**(b) Sources of Error in Mass Calculation — 7 marks**

The discrepancy is $\Delta M = 24 - 21.9 = 2.1$ g, or roughly $9.6\%$ high.

Three sources (any three, 2 marks each + 1 mark for best corrective action summary):

1. **Cut-out volumes neglected in the theoretical formula (2 marks).** The equation used was a first-order approximation that ignored the camera, USB-C, and button cut-outs. In reality, removing material *reduces* mass, so the theoretical $21.9$ g is already an *under-estimate*; this cannot explain the measured value being higher.
   *Correction:* Add the cut-out volumes to the equation. The corrected formula would actually *decrease* the theoretical mass further, so the remaining error must come from source 2.

2. **Clearance between phone and case ignored (2 marks).** The cavity height was taken as $H_{\text{phone}} + 0.5$ mm, but in a real 3D-printed or moulded case there are additional gaps and a slight over-build. A more accurate model is to add a uniform $0.3$ mm over-build on each face.
   *Correction:* Replace the constant $0.5$ mm by $1.0$ mm in $H_{\text{cavity}} = H_{\text{phone}} + 1.0$.

3. **Material density assumed to be nominal (2 marks).** The calculation used $\rho_{\text{ABS}} = 1.04$ g/cm³, which is the *bulk* density. Injection-moulded ABS has a *skin-core* structure, and 3D-printed cases have infill < 100%, both of which can push the effective density up to $1.10$ g/cm³. With the new density, the new theoretical mass becomes $1.10/1.04 \times 21.9 \approx 23.17$ g, very close to the measured $24$ g.
   *Correction:* Use the actual measured density of a calibration coupon, or set $\rho = 1.10$ g/cm³ for the chosen manufacturing route.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic**
> 1. **Forgetting to fully define the sketch before adding the design table.** A blue (under-defined) sketch will cause the table import to fail silently, and the examiner will mark you zero for Step 13. *Always press the "fully define sketch" tool before saving.*
> 2. **Mixing up "Configurations" with "Design Tables."** Configurations live *inside* the CAD file; design tables are *external* spreadsheets. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 3. **Hard-coding dimensions instead of naming them.** If you type `15.0 mm` directly into a dimension, it is *not* customisable. Always use the variable name (e.g., `D_cam`).
> 4. **Submitting a model that only works for one phone.** The whole point of the experiment is the *family*. If you cannot demonstrate regeneration for at least three configurations, the CO is not met.
> 5. **Forgetting the units callout.** KTU follows SI; always state **mm** (or m) explicitly in the parameter table. Examiners deduct ½ mark for unit ambiguity.
> 6. **Submitting only screenshots, no equations.** The equations in Section 2.3 are *not optional*; they are the design-intent record. Submit them in your lab record.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — High-Density Summary**

- **Definition:** CAD customisation = parametric + constraint-driven + design-table-driven modelling of a *family* of parts from one master.
- **Five pillars:** Parametric dimensions, geometric & dimensional constraints, equations, design tables, configurations.
- **Phone-case experiment** integrates all four prior modules of the CAD syllabus into one demonstrable workflow.
- **Independent parameters** (the *only* values the user types): $L_{\text{phone}}, W_{\text{phone}}, H_{\text{phone}}, T_{\text{wall}}, H_{\text{lip}}, R_{\text{corner}}, D_{\text{cam}}$.
- **Key equations to memorise:**
  - $L_{\text{case}} = L_{\text{phone}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{wall}}$
  - $W_{\text{case}} = W_{\text{phone}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{wall}}$
  - $H_{\text{cavity}} = H_{\text{phone}} + 0.5$
  - $H_{\text{case,total}} = H_{\text{cavity}} + H_{\text{lip}}$
  - $M_{\text{case}} = \rho \cdot V_{\text{case}}$ (with $\rho_{\text{ABS}} = 1.04$ g/cm³, $\rho_{\text{PC}} = 1.20$ g/cm³).
- **Boolean switches** ($B_{\text{grip}}, B_{\text{wireless}}$) drive optional features — this is a typical viva question.
- **Standard reference dimensions** to remember: USB-C port $9.0 \times 3.0$ mm; typical $T_{\text{wall}} = 1.5$–$3.0$ mm; typical $H_{\text{lip}} = 0.8$–$1.5$ mm.
- **Lab procedure order:** Declare parameters → Sketch envelope → Add constraints → Extrude → Cut cavity → Cut functional features → Add aesthetic features → Write equations → Build design table → Validate ≥ 3 configurations → Verify mass.
- **Examiner's hot-button terms:** *Design intent, family of parts, regeneration, fully-defined sketch, equation chain, configuration, design table, feature tree.*
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Hard-coding dimensions and confusing the design table with configurations.
- **Course Outcome mapping (KTU 2024, PBPET504):** This experiment satisfies **CO4** — *"Apply customisation techniques to generate families of parts and assemblies."* — and is the *primary evidence* for the same in the lab-record file.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarise with the reverse engineering procedures to develop required CAD models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPET504 - CAD/CAM/CAE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Familiarise with the reverse engineering procedures to develop required CAD models # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reverse Engineering (RE)** is the systematic process of deconstructing a physical object, capturing its geometric and topological data through measurement technologies, and reconstructing a complete digital representation (point cloud → mesh → surface → solid CAD model) that can be edited, manufactured, or analyzed using modern CAD/CAM/CAE tools. Mathematically, reverse engineering can be expressed as an **inverse mapping function**: $$ \Phi^{-1} : P_{\text{physical}} \longrightarrow M_{\text{CAD}} $$ where $P_{\text{physical}}$ denotes the physical artifact and $M_{\text{CAD}}$ is the resultant parametric/feature-based digital model. ### 1.1.1 Formal Sub-Definitions | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Point Cloud** | An unstructured set of $n$ vertices $V = \{v_i \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ representing sampled surface coordinates of the physical object. | | **Polygon Mesh** | A discretized topological surface formed by triangulating or quadrilateralizing the point cloud. | | **Surface Model** | A continuous, differentiable mathematical representation (Bézier, B-spline, or NURBS) fitted to the polygon mesh. | | **Solid Model** | A watertight, volume-defining CAD model with explicit topology (faces, edges, vertices) suitable for downstream manufacturing simulation. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Reverse Engineering is one of the most heavily weighted topics in **Module 4** of PBPET504. Students are expected to identify the workflow, list the scanning technologies, and explain each stage of data conversion from raw scan data to a finished CAD model. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Clay-to-Statue Reconstruction":** > Imagine archaeologists uncover a **broken ancient clay statue**. They cannot ask the original sculptor how it was made. Instead, they: > 1. **Measure** every surviving fragment using laser scanners (data acquisition), > 2. **Clean and align** the fragments digitally (point cloud processing), > 3. **Smooth** the rough edges into a continuous digital skin (surface reconstruction), > 4. **Re-create** the missing parts based on symmetry and historical references (CAD modelling). > > This is **exactly** what reverse engineering does for a manufactured component — for example, when a discontinued washing machine part breaks and the original CAD file is lost, engineers scan the surviving piece and rebuild the model so a new batch can be CNC-machined or 3D-printed. ### 1.2.1 Why Reverse Engineering Matters | Application | Real Engineering Use Case | |-------------|---------------------------| | **Legacy Part Reproduction** | Recreating obsolete/spare parts when OEM drawings are unavailable. | | **Competitive Benchmarking** | Analyzing competitor products for design improvement. | | **Quality Inspection** | Comparing as-built geometry vs. as-designed (GD&T validation). | | **Biomedical Modelling** | Custom prosthetics, dental crowns, and orthopedic implants derived from patient CT/laser scans. | | **Aerospace MRO** | Repair and overhaul of turbine blades and airframe panels. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Highlighted in Bold:** > The typical point density for industrial RE scanning ranges from **$\mathbf{10^3}$ to $\mathbf{10^6}$ points per cm²** depending on sensor resolution (low for large parts, high for micro-components). Surface reconstruction accuracy targets sub-**$\mathbf{0.05 \, mm}$** tolerance for medical/aerospace-grade components. --- ## 1.3 Forward Engineering vs. Reverse Engineering — A Comparison | Aspect | Forward Engineering | Reverse Engineering | |--------|---------------------|---------------------| | **Direction of Information Flow** | Design $\rightarrow$ Physical Product | Physical Product $\rightarrow$ Design | | **Starting Point** | Functional specifications / sketch | Existing physical artifact | | **Tool of Origin** | CAD (SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo) | 3D Scanner + CAD | | **Primary Output** | Manufacturing drawings, G-code | Reconstructed CAD model | | **Typical Application** | New product development | Reproduction, redesign, inspection | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Stage Reverse Engineering Pipeline Reverse engineering is not a single operation — it is a **structured pipeline** where data fidelity progressively improves from raw sensor output to a feature-rich solid model. KTU examiners frequently ask students to enumerate these stages. ### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (3D Scanning) The physical object is probed using a **3D digitizer**, which outputs a dense cloud of $(x, y, z)$ coordinates. Two principal families exist: | Scanning Technology | Working Principle | Accuracy | Use Case | |---------------------|-------------------|----------|----------| | **Contact (Touch Probe / CMM)** | Mechanical probe physically touches surface | $\pm \, 0.001$ mm | High-precision metrology, prismatic parts | | **Non-Contact — Laser Triangulation** | Laser stripe projected, camera captures deformation | $\pm \, 0.025$ mm | Free-form surfaces, automotive | | **Non-Contact — Structured Light** | Projected fringe pattern decoded by cameras | $\pm \, 0.05$ mm | Human body, large objects | | **Non-Contact — Laser Pulse (LiDAR)** | Time-of-flight of laser pulses | $\pm \, 1$ cm (long range) | Large terrain, buildings | | **Non-Contact — CT Scan** | X-ray tomographic slicing | $\pm \, 0.01$ mm | Internal geometry, medical | ### Stage 2 — Point Cloud Pre-Processing Raw point clouds contain noise, outliers, and registration errors. The pre-processing pipeline includes: - **Filtering**: Removing statistical outliers (points deviating more than $k\sigma$ from the local mean). - **Registration**: Aligning multiple scan views using the **Iterative Closest Point (ICP)** algorithm. - **Decimation**: Reducing point density to manageable size (e.g., from 10 million to 1 million points). - **Hole Filling**: Interpolating missing regions caused by occlusions or reflective surfaces. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Favorite**: The **Iterative Closest Point (ICP)** algorithm is a guaranteed high-yield topic. It minimizes the error: > $$E(R, t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lVert p_i - (R q_i + t) \right\rVert^2$$ > where $R \in SO(3)$ is the rotation matrix and $t \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is the translation vector aligning two point sets $P$ and $Q$. ### Stage 3 — Surface Reconstruction (Mesh Generation) The cleaned point cloud is triangulated to form a **polygon mesh** (typically a **STL — Stereolithography** file). The most common algorithm is **Delaunay Triangulation**, which maximizes the minimum angle of all triangles to avoid slivers. For a triangle with vertices $A$, $B$, $C$, the surface normal is given by: $$ \vec{n} = \frac{(B-A) \times (C-A)}{\Vert (B-A) \times (C-A) \Vert} $$ ### Stage 4 — Surface Fitting (NURBS / B-Spline Modeling) The polygonal mesh is approximated by a smooth mathematical surface — usually a **Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline (NURBS)** surface, which is the industry standard. A NURBS surface $S(u, v)$ is defined as: $$ S(u, v) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0}^{m} N_{i,p}(u) \, N_{j,q}(v) \, w_{i,j} \, P_{i,j}}{\sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0}^{m} N_{i,p}(u) \, N_{j,q}(v) \, w_{i,j}} $$ where: - $P_{i,j}$ are the **control points** (a $(n+1) \times (m+1)$ grid). - $N_{i,p}(u)$ and $N_{j,q}(v)$ are the B-spline basis functions of degrees $p$ and $q$. - $w_{i,j}$ are the **weights** (rational form allows exact representation of conic sections like circles). - $u \in [0,1]$, $v \in [0,1]$ are the parametric coordinates. ### Stage 5 — Solid Model Reconstruction The smooth surface is **closed, trimmed, and converted** to a feature-based parametric solid using Boolean operations, fillets, and feature recognition. This yields an editable CAD model. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Board Examiner's Favourite Equations** — memorize these verbatim for the ESE. | # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Application in RE | |---|--------------------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | **Iterative Closest Point (ICP) Error** | $E(R,t) = \sum \Vert p_i - (R q_i + t) \Vert^2$ | Multi-view scan registration | | 2 | **Triangle Normal** | $\vec{n} = \frac{(B-A) \times (C-A)}{\Vert \ldots \Vert}$ | Mesh orientation, shading | | 3 | **NURBS Surface** | $S(u,v) = \frac{\sum \sum N_{i,p} N_{j,q} w_{i,j} P_{i,j}}{\sum \sum N_{i,p} N_{j,q} w_{i,j}}$ | Smooth surface from point cloud | | 4 | **B-Spline Basis (Cox-de Boor)** | $N_{i,0}(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & u_i \le u < u_{i+1} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Curve/surface smoothness control | | 5 | **Plane Fitting Error** | $E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (a x_i + b y_i + c z_i + d)^2$ | Identifying planar regions in point cloud | | 6 | **Voxel Size for Voxelization** | $v_{\text{size}} = \frac{L_{\text{bbox}}}{N_{\text{voxels}}}$ | Resolution control for volumetric RE | | 7 | **Mesh Surface Area** | $A = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{f \in \text{faces}} \Vert (v_2 - v_1) \times (v_3 - v_1) \Vert$ | STL mesh validation | | 8 | **Point Cloud Density** | $\rho = \frac{N}{A_{\text{surface}}} \quad [\text{points/mm}^2]$ | Scanner performance metric | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Matters in Industry:** > 1. **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus)**: Reverse engineering legacy turbine blades when OEM drawings are lost. > 2. **Automotive (OEMs)**: Aftermarket redesign of brake calipers, gear assemblies. > 3. **Medical**: Custom **patient-specific cranio-facial implants** scanned from CT data. > 4. **Consumer Goods**: Heritage restoration of sculptures, restoration of classic car body panels. > 5. **Forensics**: Bullet/crime-scene evidence digitization for court visualization. ### 2.3.1 Software Tools Used in RE Pipelines | Software | Role | Manufacturer | |----------|------|--------------| | **Geomagic Design X** | Point cloud → CAD | 3D Systems | | **PolyWorks** | Mesh + inspection | InnovMetric | | **CATIA Reverse Engineering Module** | Surface reconstruction | Dassault Systèmes | | **Rhinoceros + RhinoReverse** | NURBS modelling | McNeel | | **Fusion 360** | Mesh to solid | Autodesk | | **Artec Studio** | Real-time scan processing | Artec 3D | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Iterative Closest Point (ICP) Algorithm Cost Function The ICP algorithm is the workhorse of multi-view registration in reverse engineering. We derive its objective function below. ### Step 1: Problem Setup Given two corresponding point sets: - **Reference (Target)** set: $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$, $p_i \in \mathbb{R}^3$ - **Source (Moving)** set: $Q = \{q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n\}$, $q_i \in \mathbb{R}^3$ We seek a **rigid transformation** $(R, t)$, with $R \in SO(3)$ (orthogonal, $\det(R) = +1$) and $t \in \mathbb{R}^3$, such that the transformed source $R q_i + t$ aligns with the reference $p_i$. ### Step 2: Define the Cost Function The mean-squared error between corresponding points is: $$ E(R, t) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lVert p_i - (R q_i + t) \right\rVert^2 $$ ### Step 3: Derivation — Closed-Form Solution **Step 3a:** Compute centroids of both point sets: $$ \bar{p} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \qquad \bar{q} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i $$ **Step 3b:** Center both point sets about their centroids: $$ p'_i = p_i - \bar{p} \qquad q'_i = q_i - \bar{q} $$ **Step 3c:** Form the $3 \times 3$ covariance matrix $H$: $$ H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p'_i \, (q'_i)^T $$ **Step 3d:** Compute the **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** of $H$: $$ H = U \, \Sigma \, V^T $$ **Step 3e:** The optimal rotation matrix is: $$ R^* = V \, U^T $$ **Step 3f:** The optimal translation vector is: $$ t^* = \bar{p} - R^* \, \bar{q} $$ ### Step 4: Verify Orthogonality Constraint Since $R$ must be a proper rotation (no reflection), we enforce: $$ \det(R^*) = \det(V \, U^T) = +1 $$ If $\det(R^*) = -1$, the sign of the last column of $U$ is flipped and the derivation is restarted. > [!NOTE] > **Connection to Reverse Engineering**: After scanning an object from multiple angles, each scan produces a point cloud in its **own local coordinate frame**. The ICP algorithm stitches all these frames into a single **global coordinate frame**, producing the unified point cloud that feeds into the meshing stage. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — NURBS Curve from a Point Cloud via Least-Squares Fitting ### Step 1: Parametric Form of a NURBS Curve A NURBS curve of degree $p$ is: $$ C(u) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,p}(u) \, w_i \, P_i}{\sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,p}(u) \, w_i} $$ with control points $P_i \in \mathbb{R}^3$, weights $w_i > 0$, knot vector $U = \{u_0, u_1, \ldots, u_{n+p+1}\}$. ### Step 2: Cox-de Boor Recursion for B-Spline Basis $$ N_{i,0}(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } u_i \le u < u_{i+1} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ $$ N_{i,p}(u) = \frac{u - u_i}{u_{i+p} - u_i} N_{i,p-1}(u) + \frac{u_{i+p+1} - u}{u_{i+p+1} - u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,p-1}(u) $$ ### Step 3: Least-Squares Objective Given $m$ data points $\{Q_k\}_{k=1}^{m}$ and parameter values $\{u_k\}$, minimize: $$ E(P) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \left\lVert Q_k - C(u_k) \right\rVert^2 $$ Taking the partial derivative with respect to each control point $P_j$ and setting it to zero: $$ \frac{\partial E}{\partial P_j} = -2 \sum_{k=1}^{m} R_k(u_k) \, N_{j,p}(u_k) \, \big( Q_k - C(u_k) \big) = 0 $$ where $R_k(u_k) = \dfrac{N_{j,p}(u_k) \, w_j}{\sum_{i} N_{i,p}(u_k) \, w_i}$ is the rational basis weight. ### Step 4: Solving the Normal Equations This yields a linear system in the control points: $$ \mathbf{A} \, \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{B} $$ where $A_{kj} = R_k(u_k) \, N_{j,p}(u_k)$ and $B_k = R_k(u_k) \, Q_k$. Solving this $n \times n$ linear system gives the optimal control points that best fit the scanned data. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Scanning Technology > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Trend**: When asked "Which scanning method would you choose for X?", examiners expect a justified comparison using this matrix. | Object Characteristic | Recommended Scanner | Justification | |----------------------|---------------------|---------------| | **Soft / Deformable (clay, food, human body)** | Structured light | Contact probes would deform surface | | **Highly reflective (polished metal)** | Laser triangulation with anti-glare spray | Reflectivity confuses structured light | | **Internal features (engine block, bone)** | Industrial CT scan | Line-of-sight scanners cannot reach interior | | **Large outdoor object (car, building façade)** | Mobile LiDAR / SLAM scanner | Long range, GPS-anchored registration | | **Sub-micron precision (MEMS, watch parts)** | Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) | $\mu$m accuracy mandatory | | **Free-form organic shape (sculpture, dental arch)** | Optical scanner (white light or blue laser) | Captures complex curvature without contact | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Calculating Mesh Surface Area > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Style Problem:** Given an STL triangle with vertices $A = (0, 0, 0)$, $B = (2, 0, 0)$, $C = (0, 3, 0)$, calculate the triangle area and the unit normal. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute edge vectors: $$ \vec{AB} = B - A = (2, 0, 0) - (0, 0, 0) = (2, 0, 0) $$ $$ \vec{AC} = C - A = (0, 3, 0) - (0, 0, 0) = (0, 3, 0) $$ **Step 2:** Compute the cross product: $$ \vec{n}_{\text{raw}} = \vec{AB} \times \vec{AC} = \begin{vmatrix} \vec{i} & \vec{j} & \vec{k} \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \end{vmatrix} = (0, 0, 6) $$ **Step 3:** Compute magnitude (which equals twice the triangle area): $$ \left\lVert \vec{n}_{\text{raw}} \right\rVert = \sqrt{0^2 + 0^2 + 6^2} = 6 $$ **Step 4:** Triangle area: $$ A = \frac{1}{2} \left\lVert \vec{n}_{\text{raw}} \right\rVert = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 = 3 \text{ square units} $$ **Step 5:** Unit normal: $$ \hat{n} = \frac{(0, 0, 6)}{6} = (0, 0, 1) $$ This triangle lies in the $xy$-plane, as expected, with normal pointing in the $+z$ direction. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Complete Reverse Engineering Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Physical Object] --> B[3D Scanning - Data Acquisition] B --> C[Raw Point Cloud XYZ] C --> D[Pre-Processing] D --> D1[Outlier Removal] D --> D2[ICP Registration] D --> D3[Decimation] D --> D4[Hole Filling] D1 --> E[Cleaned Point Cloud] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E E --> F[Surface Reconstruction - Triangulation] F --> G[Polygon Mesh - STL Format] G --> H[Surface Fitting - NURBS or Bezier] H --> I[Smooth CAD Surface Model] I --> J[Solid Model Conversion] J --> K[Feature Recognition - Holes, Fillets, Bosses] K --> L[Editable Parametric CAD Model] L --> M[Downstream Use] M --> M1[CAE - FEA Simulation] M --> M2[CAM - CNC Toolpath] M --> M3[3D Printing - Additive Manufacturing] M --> M4[Quality Inspection - GD and T] ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Comparison of Scanning Methods ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SCANNING[3D Scanning Technologies] direction TB SC1[Contact Methods] SC2[Non-Contact Methods] end SC1 --> CMM[Coordinate Measuring Machine] SC1 --> ARM[Articulated Arm with Touch Probe] SC2 --> LT[Laser Triangulation] SC2 --> SL[Structured Light] SC2 --> LIDAR[Time of Flight LiDAR] SC2 --> CT[Industrial CT Scan] CMM --> ACC_HIGH[High Accuracy -
